description: Comprehensive review of PR #8 adding the authoring funnel subsystem: proto definitions, domain logic (stages, posture, prompts, passes, safety), scanner tiers 1-2, ConnectRPC handler, storage interface + Memgraph implementation, CLI commands, and server wiring.
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

description:
Slug values accepted by all 8 authoring RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are only checked for non-empty. No character allowlist, length limit, or path-component validation is applied.

The most concrete risk is in StoreDecomposeOutput (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:98-122): child slugs are constructed by concatenating the parent slug with a caller-supplied slice ID using fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, sl.ID). A caller submitting a slice ID such as '../../admin' or '../sibling-spec' would cause the child slug to traverse outside the parent's namespace — e.g. parent='myorg/myspec', id='../../admin' produces slug 'myorg/myspec/../../admin' = 'admin'. Because CreateSpec then stores this as a graph node and MATCH queries use the slug as a node key, a crafted slug can collide with or shadow arbitrary existing spec nodes.

Although all Cypher queries use parameterized bindings (so there is no direct Cypher injection via the slug), the unconstrained slug is used as a logical identifier that controls which graph node is created or matched, enabling unauthorized write access to nodes that should be protected by namespace isolation.

Files/lines:
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go:32-55 (Spark — only empty check)
- internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:98, 122 (child slug construction, dep slug construction)

Suggested fix: Define a slug allowlist regex (e.g. ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9\-]*(\/[a-z0-9][a-z0-9\-]*)*$) and maximum length (e.g. 256 chars). Validate at the handler layer in authoring_handler.go before passing to storage. Validate DecomposeSlice.ID with the same regex before constructing child slugs.
title: Unvalidated slug enables path traversal and graph injection via child spec creation

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 92-137

StoreDecomposeOutput performs multiple sequential writes in a loop: storeJSONProperty, then for each slice: CreateSpec (with GetSpec idempotency check), MERGE COMPOSES edge, and MERGE DEPENDS_ON edges. If any step fails mid-loop, a partial set of child specs and edges already exists in the graph.

The GetSpec idempotency check (lines 100-103) only covers CreateSpec, not the edge merges. If MERGE COMPOSES or MERGE DEPENDS_ON fails after a child was created, a retry will skip CreateSpec but will re-attempt the MERGE, which is correct for edges (MERGE is idempotent). However, if the error occurs on the Nth slice after the first M slices succeeded, the result is an inconsistent graph state with some children and edges created and others missing.

More importantly: a failure here is reported back to the handler (line 134 in authoring_handler.go) with a generic 'store decompose output ... transitioned but output not stored' error. The handler has already committed the stage transition. The caller receives CodeInternal with no indication of which slice failed or how many were written, making diagnosis and manual recovery difficult.

Suggested fix: Log which slice index and slug failed when returning the error. Add context to the error message identifying whether the failure was in JSON storage, child spec creation, COMPOSES edge, or DEPENDS_ON edge. At minimum: 'store decompose output: failed at slice %d (%s): %w'.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput partial failure after child spec creation has no rollback

description:
The CLI client (cmd/specgraph/client.go) constructs the server base URL as plain http:// when no explicit remote URL is configured (line 23: fmt.Sprintf("http://%s:%d", cfg.Server.Host, cfg.Server.Port)). The newHTTPClient() function returns http.DefaultClient with no TLS configuration. There is no enforcement that the Remote URL (cfg.Server.Remote) uses HTTPS.

This means spec content — including seeds, contracts, invariants, and safety flag results — is transmitted in cleartext. For a developer tool this is a moderate risk when used against a remote (non-localhost) server.

File: cmd/specgraph/client.go lines 15-28.

Suggested fix:
- Warn or reject when cfg.Server.Remote uses http:// for non-localhost targets.
- Document that production deployments should use cfg.Server.Remote with an HTTPS URL.
- Consider rejecting plain-http remote URLs entirely unless an explicit --insecure flag is supplied.
title: CLI client uses plain HTTP with no TLS, sending data in cleartext to remote servers

description:
Storage memgraph layer has 7 thin wrapper functions that each just call storeJSONProperty with a property name constant:
  - StoreSparkOutput (line 77-78)
  - StoreShapeOutput (line 82-83)
  - StoreSpecifyOutput (line 87-88)
  - StoreRedTeamFindings (line 140-141)
  - StorePeripheralVision (line 145-146)
  - StoreConsistencyIssues (line 150-151)
  - StoreSimplicityFindings (line 155-156)
  - StoreSafetyFlags (line 160-161)
  - StoreConstitutionViolations (line 165-166)

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go

These could be replaced with a single generic StoreProperty method or by having callers invoke storeJSONProperty directly with the property name. This reduces interface surface area while preserving type safety through property name constants.

Trade-off: The wrappers provide semantic clarity (StoreSparkOutput is clearer than StoreProperty(slug, "spark_output", data)). Consider keeping only those called frequently in the authoring flow (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose outputs) and consolidate the others.
title: Consider consolidating thin storage wrapper functions

title: Safety pattern building is sound but could document pattern philosophy

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go, lines 48-50

The fakeAuthoringBackend.StoreDecomposeOutput implementation always returns (nil, nil) regardless of any configured fields on the struct:

    func (f *fakeAuthoringBackend) StoreDecomposeOutput(_ context.Context, _ string, _ *storage.DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error) {
        return nil, nil
    }

Unlike the other store methods (StoreSparkOutput, StoreShapeOutput, StoreSpecifyOutput), StoreDecomposeOutput has no corresponding error field on fakeAuthoringBackend (compare storeSparkOutputErr, storeShapeOutputErr, storeSpecifyOutputErr at lines 25-28). This means:

1. There is no test for the Decompose handler's error path when StoreDecomposeOutput fails (the partial-failure scenario described in the companion finding).
2. The test for Decompose_HappyPath (lines 204-218) passes with a fake that can never represent a real failure, giving false confidence.

The stageError method is covered for Shape/Specify via transitionStageErr, but the analogous store-failure case is completely untested for Decompose.

Suggested fix: Add a storeDecomposeOutputErr field to fakeAuthoringBackend and add a test TestAuthoringHandler_Decompose_StoreOutputError that sets it to a non-nil error and asserts CodeInternal is returned.
title: fakeAuthoringBackend.StoreDecomposeOutput always returns nil preventing tests from covering decompose error path

description:
Several error paths in authoring_handler.go are not tested at the handler level:

1. Spark: nil output field (msg.Output == nil) — only empty slug is tested. The handler has an explicit nil-output guard (line 36) but no test exercises it.
2. Shape: StoreShapeOutput failure path — fakeAuthoringBackend.storeShapeOutputErr is never set in any test. The handler returns CodeInternal when StoreShapeOutput fails after a successful TransitionStage (the half-committed state described in the TODO comment), but no test covers this.
3. Specify: StoreSpecifyOutput failure path — same gap as Shape.
4. Decompose: no error path test for StoreDecomposeOutput failure.
5. Approve: no ErrInvalidStageTransition test (only EmptySlug is covered). Compare with Shape which has TestAuthoringHandler_StageError_InvalidTransition.
6. Amend: no storage error test (only happy-path and empty-slug are covered).

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (missing tests)
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 35-37, 70-74, 102-106, 133-138, 160-162

The fakeAuthoringBackend already has fields storeShapeOutputErr, storeSpecifyOutputErr but they are never used in any test case. This makes those error branches dead from a testing perspective and any regression (e.g., returning wrong connect.Code) would go undetected.
title: Handler: Spark nil-output and StoreShapeOutput/StoreSpecifyOutput errors untested at handler level

close_reason: Out of scope: scanner is CLI-only. Path confinement is only needed if scanner is exposed via RPC (speculative future work).
description:
ScanTier1 and ScanTier2 (internal/scanner/tier1.go:49, tier2.go:40) accept a root string argument with no validation that the path is within an expected boundary. The Scan function in scanner.go similarly calls filepath.WalkDir on the caller-provided dir. The readFile helper enforces a 1 MiB size cap (maxFileSize) but does not restrict which paths are readable.

The skipDir function (scanner.go:178) skips directories starting with '.' and 'vendor'/'node_modules', but does not prevent absolute paths like /etc, symlink traversal, or paths outside the project directory.

In the current codebase the scanner is called from CLI code that supplies a directory from config or flags, so the immediate risk is low. However, if scanner functions are ever exposed via an RPC endpoint (a likely evolution given the service-oriented architecture), a crafted root path could read sensitive files from the server filesystem.

File: internal/scanner/scanner.go:26-42, internal/scanner/tier1.go:49, internal/scanner/tier2.go:40.

Suggested fix: Add a path confinement check — resolve the provided root to an absolute path (filepath.Abs + filepath.EvalSymlinks) and compare it against an allowed base directory before walking. This prevents symlink traversal and absolute path escapes.
title: Scanner accepts arbitrary user-supplied root paths with no confinement, enabling access to sensitive files

description:
internal/authoring/safety.go:33-34 — SafetyFlagResult is declared as a domain type in the authoring package but its Severity field is typed as specv1.FindingSeverity (a generated protobuf enum), not the domain FindingSeverity string type defined in internal/storage/authoring.go.

This breaks the domain/proto boundary: the authoring package now has a hard compile-time dependency on the generated proto package for what is supposed to be a pure domain type. If the proto schema changes or the generated package is regenerated with different enum names, the domain logic breaks.

At the same time, storage.SafetyFlag.Category (line 131) is a raw string instead of the SafetyCategory typed constant, even though SafetyCategory is defined right next to it in safety.go.

Suggested fix:
1. Change SafetyFlagResult.Severity to storage.FindingSeverity (the domain type).
2. Change storage.SafetyFlag.Category to SafetyCategory (or to storage.SafetyCategory once that type is moved there).
3. Add a conversion step in SafetyResultsToProto that maps domain severity to proto severity, analogous to the existing decomposeStrategyMap pattern.
title: SafetyFlagResult mixes proto enum (specv1.FindingSeverity) into a domain type

description:
File: internal/scanner/walk.go, line 23

The WalkDir callback receives an err parameter when a directory entry cannot be read (e.g., permission denied, broken symlink). The current code discards it silently:

    if err != nil {
        return nil //nolint:nilerr // skip unreadable entries
    }

Unlike parse failures (lines 41-44), which are recorded in the skipped slice with a reason, unreadable entries are neither recorded nor reported anywhere. A caller receiving a Tier1Result or Tier2Result has no way to know that parts of the directory tree were silently skipped due to access errors. In a production context (e.g., scanning a project with misconfigured permissions), the scanner would report a clean result over an incomplete dataset.

The nolint comment justifies the suppression but does not make the behavior safe — it only silences the linter.

Suggested fix: Replace 'return nil' with:
    skipped = append(skipped, SkippedFile{Path: path, Reason: err.Error()})
    return nil

This mirrors the existing pattern for unparseable files and ensures callers can inspect or log the skipped entries. The nolint directive can then be removed.
title: walk.go nolint:nilerr on unreadable directory entries silently skips without recording in SkippedFiles

close_reason: Out of scope: safety net is explicitly a first-pass pattern scanner. Pattern obfuscation is a hardening concern for a later security pass.
description:
In internal/authoring/safety.go line 130, the SafetyFlagResult.Description field is populated with the string 'matched pattern: ' followed by sp.re.String() — the full compiled regex. This description flows through SafetyResultsToProto (line 143) into the SafetyFlag proto message and is returned to callers in SparkResponse, ShapeResponse, SpecifyResponse, and DecomposeResponse.

Any API caller (authenticated or not, since no auth layer exists yet) can submit spec content, observe which patterns fired, and iteratively craft input that avoids triggering the patterns. For example, seeing 'matched pattern: \bhardcoded secret\b' reveals the exact word-boundary regex, making it trivial to bypass with 'hardcoded  secret' (double space) or Unicode lookalikes.

This is distinct from a ReDoS risk — the patterns themselves are safe (using QuoteMeta). The issue is intelligence leakage.

File: internal/authoring/safety.go:128-133.

Suggested fix: Replace 'matched pattern: ' + sp.re.String() with a stable human-readable label that describes the concern without disclosing detection logic (e.g., 'potential hardcoded credential detected' or just the category name). The full pattern string should only appear in server-side logs.
title: Safety net regex patterns exposed to callers enable evasion by adversarial input crafting

close_reason: Stale: code has changed since review — verified against HEAD 8bf4eae
description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/authoring/safety.go:14-15

The comment on SafetyInput.Intent says 'Intent accepts any text for pattern scanning — its name reflects the Spark use case, but other handlers pass stage-appropriate text (e.g., risks in Shape).' This comment is incomplete and could mislead developers.

The comment explains the reusage pattern but doesn't clarify WHICH field in the output struct is passed for each stage:
- Spark: seed (line 50 in authoring_handler.go)
- Shape: risks (line 80)  
- Specify: interface_contract (line 109)
- Decompose: slice intents (line 146)

Suggestion: Expand the comment to explicitly list which output fields are scanned in each stage, making it clear the field name 'Intent' is a placeholder/generic name that takes various meanings depending on stage context.
title: SafetyInput.Intent comment is misleading about field reusage across stages

description:
The existing Spec message in proto/specgraph/v1/spec.proto (field 4) uses a free-form string for stage (e.g. 'spark | shape | specify | decompose | approved | in_progress | done'). The new authoring.proto introduces AuthoringStage as a typed enum. These two representations co-exist in the same package but are never linked.

This creates several contract risks:

1. The Spec.stage string comment includes 'in_progress' and 'done' as valid values, but AuthoringStage has no corresponding enum values. The enum is missing at least two documented valid stage values.

2. AmendResponse and ApproveResponse return AuthoringStage (enum), but GetSpec returns Spec with stage as a plain string. Consumers reading stage from GetSpec/ListSpecs get a string; consumers using Amend/Approve get an enum. The same logical field has two incompatible types depending on which RPC is called.

3. The stageToProto map in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 303-309) converts string → enum for ApproveResponse/AmendResponse, but this mapping is one-directional — there is no inverse serialization back into a Spec's string stage field from an AuthoringStage enum value.

Recommended fix: Either (a) migrate Spec.stage to use AuthoringStage enum (breaking change requiring a migration plan for existing stored data), or (b) document explicitly that Spec.stage is the persistence representation and AuthoringStage is the authoring-funnel representation, and add 'IN_PROGRESS' and 'DONE' enum values to AuthoringStage to cover all documented string values. Without resolving this, the API contract is ambiguous for consumers who read and write stage via different RPCs.
title: Spec.stage is a free-form string but AuthoringStage is a typed enum — dual representations create contract ambiguity

description:
internal/storage/authoring.go:131 — storage.SafetyFlag.Category is typed as string even though the SafetyCategory typed string constant (defined in internal/authoring/safety.go:23) exists precisely to describe this field.

The inconsistency means callers can pass arbitrary strings where only 'security' and 'data_loss' are valid, defeating the purpose of having a typed constant. Other similar fields in this file (e.g. Disposition in PeripheralVisionItem, Severity in RedTeamFinding) correctly use their respective typed constants.

Additionally, SafetyCategory is defined in the authoring package while SafetyFlag is in the storage package, so they cannot reference each other directly without a circular import. This suggests SafetyCategory belongs in the storage package alongside SafetyFlag, or that a shared types package is needed.

Suggested fix: Move SafetyCategory to storage/authoring.go and change SafetyFlag.Category from string to SafetyCategory.
title: storage.SafetyFlag.Category is untyped string despite SafetyCategory typed constant existing

description:
The walkGoFiles function in internal/scanner/walk.go handles three important edge cases that have no test coverage:

1. Malformed Go files: walk.go line 43 records unparseable files in SkippedFiles and continues. Neither tier1_test.go nor tier2_test.go verifies that a syntactically invalid .go file ends up in SkippedFiles rather than causing an error return. A regression changing 'return nil' to 'return parseErr' would break silently.

2. Symlinks: WalkDir on macOS/Linux follows symlinks by default on some implementations and can cause infinite loops on circular symlinks. walk.go has no symlink handling. No test exercises a symlink pointing into the scanned tree.

3. Permission errors: walk.go line 23 silently ignores walk errors ('return nil //nolint:nilerr'). No test verifies that a directory with mode 0o000 is gracefully skipped rather than causing the entire scan to abort.

4. ScanTier1: the SkippedFiles field of Tier1Result is populated but never asserted in any test.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/walk.go lines 21-23, 41-44
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/tier1_test.go (missing malformed-file and skipped-file assertions)
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/tier2_test.go (same)

Suggested additions:
- Write a file containing 'package foo\n\nINVALID SYNTAX' and assert result.SkippedFiles is non-empty.
- Create a chmod 0o000 subdirectory and assert ScanTier1 still returns without error.
title: Scanner: malformed Go files, symlinks, and unreadable-path behavior untested

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 278-298

decomposeStrategyMap only contains entries for three explicit strategy values. The map lookup at line 296 uses the zero value of storage.DecompositionStrategy (an empty string "") when the proto strategy is not in the map:

    Strategy: decomposeStrategyMap[p.GetStrategy()],

The Decompose handler validates that the strategy is not DECOMPOSITION_STRATEGY_UNSPECIFIED before calling decomposeOutputToDomain (line 128-130), which prevents the unspecified case. However, this check relies on a proto enum value that happens to be 0. If a new proto strategy value is added in the future and not added to decomposeStrategyMap, the domain object will silently receive an empty/zero strategy string. The caller receives CodeOK with a decompose_output stored that contains an unrecognized strategy, with no warning.

This is a silent data corruption risk as the proto/domain mapping grows.

Suggested fix: Use an explicit ok-check on the map lookup and return a CodeInternal error (or CodeInvalidArgument) if the strategy is not found:

    strategy, ok := decomposeStrategyMap[p.GetStrategy()]
    if !ok {
        return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, fmt.Errorf('unknown decomposition strategy %v', p.GetStrategy()))
    }

Alternatively, move the mapping into a function that panics on unknown values so it is caught at development time.
title: decomposeOutputToDomain silently maps unknown DecompositionStrategy to zero value
AuthoringBackend interface violates Interface Segregation: stage lifecycle and pass storage are unrelated concerns

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-0oj.4 (same non-atomic RPC issue, reported by both errors and api agents).
description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 41-48, the Spark handler:
1. Calls h.backend.CreateSpec (succeeds, spec node created in Memgraph)
2. Calls h.store.StoreSparkOutput (may fail)

If step 2 fails, the spec exists in storage but has no spark output. The handler returns CodeInternal to the caller. On retry, the caller sends another Spark request with the same slug, which will fail at CreateSpec with a duplicate slug error (not CodeAlreadyExists — just CodeInternal wrapping a DB error). The spec is now permanently orphaned with no way to recover through the public API.

This is acknowledged with a TODO comment but the API contract does not document this failure mode and there is no idempotency key, no cleanup endpoint, and no way to overwrite or delete a half-created spec via the AuthoringService.

The Shape, Specify, and Decompose handlers have the same pattern (transition succeeds, store fails), but those are recoverable via Amend. The Spark case is uniquely unrecoverable because there is no Amend path from the initial 'spec exists but no spark output' state — Amend requires the spec to be in a known stage.

Recommended fix: Either (a) make Spark idempotent by checking for existing spec and allowing overwrite of spark output, or (b) add a DeleteSpec or ResetSpec endpoint to handle cleanup, or (c) use a single atomic CreateSpecWithSpark storage method. At minimum, the Spark RPC should return CodeAlreadyExists (not CodeInternal) when the slug already exists, so callers can distinguish a retry scenario from a true internal error.
title: Spark RPC has non-atomic create + store with no recovery path — partial failure leaves orphaned spec

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go StoreDecomposeOutput has two important branches with no test coverage:

1. Idempotent retry path (lines 100-103): when a child spec already exists (GetSpec returns it without error), the code reuses it instead of creating a duplicate. The existing TestStoreDecomposeOutput test only exercises the create-new-child path. A test calling StoreDecomposeOutput twice with the same slices would verify idempotency and catch regressions in the retry logic.

2. Missing parent spec: storeJSONProperty (called first at line 93) returns ErrSpecNotFound when the parent slug does not exist (line 260-262). No test calls StoreDecomposeOutput with a non-existent parent and asserts ErrSpecNotFound is returned. This is the highest-severity data-consistency path — if the guard fails, orphaned child specs could be created in the graph.

3. DEPENDS_ON edge creation: TestStoreDecomposeOutput does include a slice with DependsOn but does not verify the resulting graph edge. A future refactor could silently break the dependency edge without any test failing.

4. AmendSpec missing-spec path: AmendSpec calls GetSpec first and propagates ErrSpecNotFound, but no test covers this. TestAmendSpec only tests the happy path.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go lines 92-137
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (missing cases)
title: Memgraph: StoreDecomposeOutput idempotency and missing-parent cases untested

description:
internal/authoring/passes.go:15-16 — passConfig.pass is typed as string and the passRegistry map key is also string. Pass names ('constitution_check', 'red_team', etc.) and stage names ('spark', 'shape', etc.) can be misspelled with no compile-time error. The StageSpark/StageShape constants exist for stages but there are no equivalent constants for pass names.

A typo in either the passRegistry definition or a downstream caller of PassesForStage/OfferedPasses that matches against the returned strings silently produces wrong behavior instead of a compile error.

Suggested fix: Introduce a PassName typed string constant (or iota-based int) alongside the existing stage constants. Use PassName as the type for passConfig.pass and the return value of PassesForStage/OfferedPasses. This makes mismatches a compile error rather than a runtime gap.
title: passConfig.pass and stage keys in passRegistry are raw strings with no compile-time safety

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-0oj.4 (same non-atomic Spark handler issue, reported by both code and errors agents).
title: Non-atomic Spark handler leaves orphan spec nodes on storage failure

close_reason: Already fixed in batch 2 — StoreDecomposeOutput now returns []string, not []*specv1.Spec
description:
The CLAUDE.md project documentation explicitly states: 'Storage interfaces in internal/storage/ — implementations are in subdirectories (currently only memgraph/). The interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.'

However, in internal/storage/authoring.go line 150:
  StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error)

This method returns []*specv1.Spec (a protobuf type) rather than a domain type. Compare with all other AuthoringBackend methods which return only domain types or error.

Also, AmendSpec at line 158:
  AmendSpec(ctx context.Context, slug, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error)

This also returns *specv1.Spec.

While ConstitutionBackend also returns proto types (it predates this PR), AuthoringBackend was introduced in this PR and the project has a stated convention to use domain types in storage interfaces. Adding proto return types to a new interface extends the inconsistency rather than correcting it.

This matters for the API contract: if the storage interface ever needs to be implemented by a non-Memgraph backend (e.g., SQLite, in-memory for testing), that backend must import and construct proto objects, coupling storage implementations to the protobuf schema version.

Recommended fix: Define domain types for Spec (e.g., storage.SpecRecord) and have StoreDecomposeOutput return []SpecRecord and AmendSpec return *SpecRecord. The handler layer performs the conversion to specv1.Spec as it does for all other domain types.
title: AuthoringBackend.StoreDecomposeOutput returns []*specv1.Spec — storage interface leaks proto types inconsistently

close_reason: Stale: code has changed since review — verified against HEAD 8bf4eae
description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler.go

There are 4 identical TODO comments at lines 47, 73, 105, 137 all saying 'TODO: Add transaction support to make this atomic.'

These TODOs lack sufficient context for future developers:
1. Do NOT explain why transactions are needed (atomic multi-step operations)
2. Do NOT mention severity (this appears to be a known limitation, not urgent)
3. All appear identical despite different contexts (spark, shape, specify, decompose outputs)

The pattern is: [Step1 succeeds] but [Step2 fails] → spec in inconsistent state.

Suggestion: Make each TODO more specific:
- Line 47 (Spark): 'TODO: Wrap CreateSpec + StoreSparkOutput in transaction; currently if StoreSparkOutput fails, spec exists without output'
- Line 73 (Shape): 'TODO: Wrap TransitionStage + StoreShapeOutput in transaction'
- etc.

This helps future maintainers understand the exact risk and potential data inconsistency.
title: TODO comments lack context about transaction support requirements

close_reason: Stale: code has changed since review — verified against HEAD 8bf4eae
description:
internal/storage/authoring.go:146 — TransitionStage(ctx, slug, from, to string) accepts stage names as untyped strings. The from/to arguments at every call site in authoring_handler.go are the stage string constants (authoring.StageSpark, authoring.StageShape, etc.), but the interface signature itself enforces nothing.

A caller passing ('spark', 'approved') — skipping multiple stages — gets a runtime ErrInvalidStageTransition rather than a compile error. More critically, the from argument is not validated by the storage layer at all in some implementations (the memgraph implementation was not reviewed here but the interface contract does not require from-validation).

Suggested fix: Define a Stage type (type Stage string) in the storage package, use it in TransitionStage and AmendSpec signatures, and expose the stage constants as Stage values. The handler's stageToProto / protoToStage maps already do the string-to-typed-value translation, so this would be a clean change.
title: TransitionStage takes stage names as raw strings, bypassing ValidateTransition type safety

description:
internal/authoring/safety.go applies strings.ToLower before pattern matching (line 115), but no test verifies this case-folding contract. A change removing ToLower would break detection silently because all existing tests use lowercase input.

Additionally, the buildPatterns function (lines 47-61) has two distinct regex-building paths: word-boundary anchors for single words (e.g., 'truncate'), and substring match for multi-word/punctuated phrases. No test exercises:

1. Mixed-case input that relies on ToLower to match a pattern (e.g., 'PLAINTEXT', 'Eval(', 'RM -RF').
2. A word embedded in a larger word that should NOT match (e.g., 'truncated' matching the 'truncate' word-boundary pattern). The word-boundary anchor is specifically there to prevent this false positive, but it is not tested.
3. Partial matches for multi-word patterns (e.g., 'drop' without 'table' should not flag data_loss).

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/authoring/safety.go lines 47-61, 115
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/authoring/safety_test.go (missing case-sensitivity and false-positive tests)
title: Safety net: case sensitivity contract and single-word word-boundary behavior untested

description:
File: internal/storage/authoring.go, lines 150 and 158

The CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'Storage interfaces in internal/storage/ — implementations are in subdirectories. The interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.'

Two methods in AuthoringBackend violate this rule by returning *specv1.Spec (a generated protobuf type):

  StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx, slug, output *DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error)
  AmendSpec(ctx, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error)

The storage layer is importing the generated proto package (github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/gen/specgraph/v1), creating a coupling that should not exist at this layer.

Suggested fix: define a domain-level SpecRecord or equivalent struct in the storage package that mirrors the fields of specv1.Spec, and use that as the return type. The handler layer translates domain types to proto types, as it does for all other methods.
title: AuthoringBackend interface leaks protobuf types into the storage layer

close_reason: Out of scope: scanner result types are designed as simple structs per the Slice 3 plan. Richer AST-aware types are a future Tier enhancement.
description:
internal/scanner/tier1.go:18-27, internal/scanner/tier2.go:13-19 — InterfaceInfo.Methods, StructInfo.Fields, and FunctionInfo.Params are all []string. This is a lossy representation:

- InterfaceInfo.Methods loses parameter types and return types, making it impossible for a downstream consumer to distinguish 'Foo(ctx context.Context) error' from 'Foo() string'.
- StructInfo.Fields loses field types entirely — a consumer only knows field names.
- FunctionInfo.Params concatenates name+type as a single formatted string ('ctx context.Context') rather than structured ParameterInfo{Name, Type string}.

For tier1 the loss may be acceptable as a coarse structural index. For tier2's FunctionInfo, which is intended to feed richer analysis, the flat string representation will require string-parsing by consumers, re-introducing the brittleness that structured types are meant to prevent.

Suggested fix for FunctionInfo at minimum:
  type ParamInfo struct { Name, Type string }
  type FunctionInfo struct { ...; Params []ParamInfo; ReturnTypes []string }
title: scanner result types (InterfaceInfo, StructInfo, FunctionInfo) use []string for structured data

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 214-218, GetPrompts rejects AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED (the proto3 zero value) with CodeInvalidArgument. This is correct behavior, but the error path is via a protoToStage map lookup returning !ok, meaning the error message is 'unknown stage 0' rather than 'stage is required'.

More importantly, the Amend handler at line 175 explicitly checks for AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED and returns a clear 'target_stage is required' error, while GetPrompts produces a less informative 'unknown stage AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED' error.

Consumers treating GetPrompts as a discovery endpoint (e.g., CLI tab-completion, tooling) need to handle two different CodeInvalidArgument cases for the same root cause: missing stage. The error strings are inconsistent across the two handlers.

Additionally, AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED has no prompts defined (authoring.PromptsToProto returns empty for approved stage) so GetPrompts will return CodeInvalidArgument for a valid, known stage value. This is undocumented in the proto comments and will confuse consumers who pass a valid enum value and receive an error.

Recommended fix: (1) Add explicit AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED check in GetPrompts matching the Amend pattern; (2) Document in the GetPromptsRequest proto comment that AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED returns no prompts (or return an empty list instead of an error for known stages with no prompts).
title: GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED but other RPCs accept it implicitly — inconsistent validation contract

description:
The PR adds six new CLI commands in cmd/specgraph/: spark.go, shape.go, specify.go, decompose.go, approve.go, and amend.go. None of these have unit tests. Only init.go and serve.go have test files in that package.

The runShape, runSpecify, runDecompose, and runApprove functions have non-trivial logic:
- shape.go lines 37-44 and decompose.go lines 37-44 parse a --json-file flag using protojson.Unmarshal; no test exercises an invalid JSON file, a non-existent file path, or a valid file.
- Output formatting (safety flags, next prompts, slice listing) is untested and could regress silently.
- The authoringClient() helper in client.go is untested against a misconfigured server address.

Since the handler layer is tested via httptest, the most impactful gaps here are the --json-file parse error paths and the output-formatting logic. These are not covered anywhere in the test suite.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/cmd/specgraph/spark.go
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/cmd/specgraph/shape.go lines 37-44
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/cmd/specgraph/decompose.go lines 37-44
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/cmd/specgraph/approve.go
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/cmd/specgraph/amend.go
title: CLI authoring commands (spark, shape, specify, decompose, approve, amend) have no unit tests

description:
File: internal/authoring/safety.go, lines 102-134

RunSafetyNet deduplicates by emitting at most one flag per SafetyCategory. The deduplication uses a seen map keyed on category and marks the category as seen on the FIRST pattern match encountered in the loop. The current allPatternGroups ordering places critical patterns before warning patterns within each category, so critical fires first — which is the desired behavior.

However, this correctness is entirely implicit and depends on the slice ordering:

  var allPatternGroups = []patternGroup{
      {SafetyCategorySecurity, criticalSecurityPatterns},   // critical first
      {SafetyCategorySecurity, warningSecurityPatterns},    // warning second
      ...
  }

If a maintainer adds a new category and accidentally registers warning patterns before critical patterns, a WARNING match will prevent the subsequent CRITICAL match from being emitted. The caller receives a WARNING when the input actually matched a CRITICAL pattern.

Suggested fix: after the full scan, use severity-aware deduplication: for each category, keep only the highest-severity match. For example, collect all matches first, then deduplicate by keeping the entry with the lowest enum value (CRITICAL=1 < WARNING=2 < NOTE=3).
title: Safety net severity deduplication is ordering-dependent and silently suppresses CRITICAL findings

description:
The GetPrompts handler (authoring_handler.go lines 214-226) has two error paths with no test coverage:

1. Unknown stage / UNSPECIFIED (line 216-218): when protoToStage lookup fails, the handler returns CodeInvalidArgument. The only existing test (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts) passes AUTHORING_STAGE_SPARK. No test passes AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED or an unmapped enum value.

2. 'approved' stage produces no prompts (line 220-222): GetPrompts('approved') returns an empty slice, and the handler converts this to CodeInvalidArgument with message 'no prompts defined for stage'. No test exercises this path. The prompts_test.go skips 'approved' but does not test what GetPrompts returns for it at the handler level.

close_reason: Stale: code has changed since review — verified against HEAD 8bf4eae
description:
File: docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md

The file is 2593 lines long. Lines 217 onwards consist almost entirely of repeating bare ```text markers with no closing fence and no content between them — approximately 2300 lines of corrupted blocks that render as broken markdown in every viewer (GitHub PR diff, documentation site, etc.).

The file appears to have been generated or serialised incorrectly: the actual plan content ends around line 216, and everything after that is a mass of ```text open-fence lines that were never closed or populated.

Because this is a planning document that is consumed by Claude Code automations (the CLAUDE.md references it), the corruption could cause tool failures or hallucinated content when an agent reads this file.

Suggested fix: strip all content after line 216 (or the last legitimate content block) and regenerate the remaining skill-content sections from the design doc referenced in the plan header.
title: Slice-7 plan doc is massively corrupted: 2300+ lines of bare backtick-text blocks

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 40-48, 67-74, 99-106, 131-138

Each of the five mutating RPCs performs two sequential storage writes with no transaction. When the second write fails, the database is left in an inconsistent state that cannot be recovered by a simple retry:

- Spark (lines 40-48): CreateSpec succeeds then StoreSparkOutput fails. The spec exists with no output. A retry hits a duplicate-slug error (CreateSpec will fail), making the spec permanently orphaned with no spark output.
- Shape (lines 67-74): TransitionStage advances to 'shape' then StoreShapeOutput fails. The spec is in the shape stage but has no shape data. TransitionStage on retry will fail with ErrInvalidStageTransition because the spec is already at 'shape'.
- Specify (lines 99-106): Same pattern — spec is at 'specify' stage with no specify output stored.
- Decompose (lines 131-138): Same pattern — spec is at 'decompose' stage with no decompose output stored.

The error messages in the return value do document the partial-write (e.g., 'spec %q created but output not stored'), which is good, but there is no compensating rollback, no idempotency key, and no mechanism for the caller to recover without direct database intervention. A retry of Spark will always fail with CodeInternal (duplicate slug) rather than CodeAlreadyExists, so the caller cannot distinguish the corrupted state.

Suggested fix (short-term): Return CodeAlreadyExists (not CodeInternal) from CreateSpec on duplicate slug so callers can detect the orphan case. Add a compensating delete in the Spark error path. For Shape/Specify/Decompose: re-read current stage before transitioning and add an idempotent 'upsert output' path so a retry after partial failure succeeds.

Long-term: the TODO for transaction support is correct; this should be tracked as a blocking issue.
title: Non-atomic create+store in Spark/Shape/Specify/Decompose leaves specs in inconsistent state

description:
Every stage RPC response (SparkResponse, ShapeResponse, SpecifyResponse) includes a next_prompts field that returns PromptTemplate suggestions for the next authoring stage. This establishes a clear progression contract for clients: call a stage RPC, receive the validated output plus prompts for what to do next.

DecomposeResponse in proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto (lines 303-311) does NOT have a next_prompts field. Decompose is the penultimate stage before Approve, so a client following the established pattern would expect next_prompts pointing toward the Approve stage (or at minimum an empty list).

In the handler implementation at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 152, the comment acknowledges: '// TODO: Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check passes.' but there is no TODO for next_prompts, suggesting the omission may be unintentional rather than a known deferral.

This is a RISKY issue because: if next_prompts is added to DecomposeResponse in a future PR, it is a backward-compatible addition for proto3 consumers. However, clients coded against this PR's contract that do not check for next_prompts will silently miss the field. If any CLI or tooling has special-cased DecomposeResponse as the terminal stage response (because it has no next_prompts), adding the field later may break that assumption.

Recommended fix: Add 'repeated PromptTemplate next_prompts = 5;' to DecomposeResponse and wire it in the handler to return authoring.PromptsToProto(authoring.StageApproved), consistent with all other stage RPCs.
title: DecomposeResponse missing next_prompts field — breaks client progression pattern established by all other stage RPCs

close_reason: Out of scope: current code is safe (Go map values are copied). Theoretical concern about future pointer-returning API changes.
description:
internal/authoring/prompts.go:9-12 — Prompt is a plain public struct with exported fields (Name, Template). The registry is private (promptRegistry) and access is mediated through GetPrompts/PromptsToProto, which is good. However, because Prompt.Template is a mutable exported field, a caller that receives a Prompt from GetPrompts can modify the template string in-place (since GetPrompts returns a slice of the map's value, not copies through a constructor).

In Go, map values of struct type are copied on access, so promptRegistry values are safe. However, the type design communicates nothing about immutability intent. If GetPrompts is changed in the future to return pointers, the invariant breaks.

Suggested fix: Either document the immutability invariant explicitly, or make Prompt fields accessible only through accessor methods (less idiomatic in Go but enforces the invariant). At minimum, add a comment to GetPrompts noting that callers must not mutate the returned templates.
title: Prompt struct is fully exported with public mutable fields, but its registry is private

description:
File: internal/storage/authoring.go line 17; internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go

ErrSpecAlreadyApproved is declared in the storage package:

  var ErrSpecAlreadyApproved = errors.New("spec is already approved")

But it is never returned anywhere — neither in TransitionStage, AmendSpec, nor SupersedeSpec. An approved spec can freely be amended (moved backward) or have its stage transitioned forward without any guard. The stageError helper in authoring_handler.go also does not map this error to an appropriate response code.

Impact: a spec in the APPROVED stage can be amended back to any earlier stage silently, defeating the approval gate that the funnel is designed to enforce.

Suggested fix: in TransitionStage and AmendSpec, check whether the current stage is 'approved' before proceeding. If so, return ErrSpecAlreadyApproved so callers can distinguish this case and the handler can map it to CodeFailedPrecondition.
title: ErrSpecAlreadyApproved is declared but never checked — approved specs can be amended without enforcement

close_reason: Out of scope: analytical pass wiring is explicitly deferred (TODO comments). Slice 3 implements the registry; wiring into responses is future work.
description:
Files: internal/server/authoring_handler.go; internal/authoring/passes.go

The proto response messages define rich analytical pass fields (constitution_violations, red_team, peripheral_vision, consistency_issues, simplicity), and the passes.go registry defines which passes auto-run per stage and posture. However, the handler never invokes any of these passes and never populates the corresponding response fields — they are always empty.

The only analytical output wired up is the safety net (RunSafetyNet). All other fields defined in the proto and the pass registry are dead code at runtime. There is one TODO comment in Decompose:

  // TODO: Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check passes.

But the same gap exists for Shape (peripheral_vision), Specify (red_team, consistency_issues), and all stages for constitution_violations.

This is a known deferred feature, but the pass registry (passes.go) and the Storage methods for storing these results (StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, etc.) are fully implemented and unused. This creates a disconnect between the declared contract and the actual behavior, which will confuse callers who receive empty arrays where the service advertises analytical enrichment.

Suggested fix: either stub the passes to return empty results explicitly (no change to behavior, but removes the misleading impression of a complete system), or add a note to each handler that these fields are intentionally deferred to a follow-on slice.
title: Analytical pass results (constitution violations, red team, peripheral vision) are never populated in RPC responses

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, line 76

  scope := append(msg.Output.GetScopeIn(), msg.Output.GetScopeOut()...)

GetScopeIn() returns the slice directly backed by the proto message. If the slice has spare capacity, append will write into that backing array, silently modifying data in the proto message that is later returned to the caller in the response (resp.Msg.Output.ScopeIn may gain extra elements).

While this is unlikely to cause observable problems in practice (proto slices rarely carry spare capacity after unmarshalling), it is a correctness issue: the proto message passed in as req.Msg should be treated as immutable. The proto v2 API documentation recommends against mutating proto messages after construction.

Suggested fix: allocate a new slice explicitly before combining:
  scope := make([]string, 0, len(msg.Output.GetScopeIn())+len(msg.Output.GetScopeOut()))
  scope = append(scope, msg.Output.GetScopeIn()...)
  scope = append(scope, msg.Output.GetScopeOut()...)
title: append in Shape handler may silently mutate the proto message's backing slice

close_reason: Stale: code has changed since review — verified against HEAD 8bf4eae
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 92-136

StoreDecomposeOutput checks whether a child spec already exists before creating it (idempotency for spec nodes). However, for the COMPOSES and DEPENDS_ON edges it uses MERGE, which is idempotent for the edge itself. But storeJSONProperty (line 93) — which writes decompose_output — always overwrites the property regardless of whether the decomposition has changed. More critically, if the slice list in a retry differs from the original (e.g., slices are added or removed), stale COMPOSES edges from the first call remain in the graph pointing to orphaned child specs created in the original call.

There is no cleanup of previously created child specs or edges when a decomposition is re-stored, meaning partial retries produce inconsistent graph state.

Suggested fix: document the non-idempotency limitation explicitly, or implement graph diff logic that removes stale edges and child specs before creating the new ones.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput has no idempotency for COMPOSES edge re-creation, only for child spec nodes

title: Fix(spgr-0oj.4): Add transaction support for atomic handler operations

Extract repetitive slug/output validation in handler RPCs

description:
Multiple CodeInternal error paths in authoring_handler.go wrap and return raw storage-layer error messages directly to the RPC caller. These messages include memgraph-internal context strings such as 'memgraph: store spark_output: ...', database bolt protocol errors, and in partial-failure paths, the full error chain including the spec slug and operation context.

Specific instances (internal/server/authoring_handler.go):
- Line 42: connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, err) — raw storage error from CreateSpec
- Line 48: wraps err with slug and operation context, returned as CodeInternal
- Line 74: same pattern for StoreShapeOutput
- Line 106: same for StoreSpecifyOutput
- Line 138: same for StoreDecomposeOutput
- Line 205: connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, err) — raw error from SupersedeSpec

Additionally, internal/authoring/safety.go:130 includes the compiled regex string in the SafetyFlag description ('matched pattern: ' + sp.re.String()), which reveals implementation internals of the safety detection system to any API caller. An adversary can probe the API to map which exact patterns trigger flags and craft input to route around them.

Suggested fix:
1. In authoring_handler.go, log the detailed error server-side and return a generic 'internal error' message to the caller (or just the sentinel error type, not the full chain).
2. In safety.go line 130, replace the regex string in the Description with a human-readable category label (e.g. 'security concern: hardcoded credentials detected') instead of exposing the regex.
title: Internal storage error details including database layer strings exposed to RPC callers

close_reason: Already fixed in batch 3 (spgr-0oj.18) — nolint directives now have proper justification
description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/walk.go:23 and :44

Two nolint directives appear without explanatory comments:
- Line 23: 'return nil //nolint:nilerr // skip unreadable entries'
- Line 44: 'return nil //nolint:nilerr // error recorded in skipped'

While the inline comments ('skip unreadable entries', 'error recorded in skipped') are brief, they should be expanded to proper godoc-style comments above the code to explain the intentional error suppression pattern:

Current: return nil //nolint:nilerr // skip unreadable entries
Better: 
  // Intentionally return nil to continue walking despite unreadable entries;
  // they are not fatal to the overall scan.
  return nil //nolint:nilerr

This makes the intention clearer to developers who encounter this pattern.
title: Nolint directives lack proper justification in walk.go
Consolidate repeated JSON file loading pattern in CLI commands

description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler.go:151

Line 151 contains: '// TODO: Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check passes.'

This TODO is vague in multiple ways:
1. Does NOT explain what 'wiring' means (should these passes be called, displayed, stored?)
2. Does NOT explain where they should be invoked (in the Decompose handler? in storage?)
3. References 'passes' but the DecomposeResponse proto only has simplicity field, not constitution_violations field
4. Incomplete compared to other stages which include safety and constitution checks

Looking at the proto (line 306-310), DecomposeResponse only returns simplicity findings, not constitution violations. The TODO contradicts the proto schema.

Suggestion: Clarify whether:
- Both passes should be invoked and stored (like Spark/Shape/Specify)
- Only simplicity should be returned in DecomposeResponse
- Constitution check should be added to the response schema

This prevents future developers from implementing in the wrong direction.
title: Inconsistent TODO: Wire passes — incomplete description at authoring_handler.go:151

description: ShapeOutput.Decisions are stored as plain strings in the JSON blob. Per ADR-003 they should become Decision graph nodes with [:DECIDED_IN] edges, enabling cross-referencing, impact analysis, and promotion at Approve time. See internal/storage/authoring.go:52 and internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:93.
title: Promote ShapeOutput.Decisions to Decision graph nodes per ADR-003

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8 adding authoring funnel with 8 RPCs, stage validation, posture detection, prompt templates, safety checks, scanner, storage, CLI commands. 48 files changed, 10469 additions.
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel
AmendSpec propagates raw GetSpec error without context wrapping

description:
The SparkRequest, ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, and DecomposeRequest proto messages all carry a posture field (internal/server/authoring_handler.go, via proto at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto). The authoring package exposes ResolvePosture and PassesForStage which use posture to select which analytical passes run automatically versus offered.

However, the handler never reads req.Msg.Posture — it calls RunSafetyNet unconditionally without posture-aware pass selection. The authoring_handler_test.go has no test that sends a non-UNSPECIFIED posture and verifies different passes or behaviour results.

This is an important gap: the posture field is part of the public API contract, it is documented in the proto, and the authoring package has well-tested posture logic — but there is no test verifying the handler honours it. When the handler is wired to use posture (planned), the absence of a test baseline means the feature could be shipped with the posture silently ignored.

Suggested fix: add at least one test per affected RPC (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) that sets Posture = DRIVE vs SUPPORT and asserts different pass outputs or next_prompts counts, even if the handler currently ignores posture (document the TODO in the test).
title: Spark handler does not verify posture field is threaded into safety/pass selection

description:
The slug field has a 256-character cap (maxSlugLength) and regex validation, but the following free-text RPC fields have no length limit:

- AmendRequest.reason (written to s.amend_reason on the Cypher node)
- SupersedeRequest.reason (written as a property on the SUPERSEDES edge)
- SparkOutput.seed (stored as JSON in spark_output property)
- SpecifyOutput.interface_contract (stored as JSON in specify_output property)
- All repeated string fields in ShapeOutput, SpecifyOutput, DecomposeOutput

Locations:
  internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2707 (Amend reason check — only checks empty)
  internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2733 (Supersede reason — no check at all)
  internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:4031-4037 (AmendSpec writes reason verbatim)

Impact: A caller can submit multi-megabyte strings that are marshalled to JSON and stored as node properties in Memgraph. This can cause:
1. Excessive memory use during JSON marshalling in the server process.
2. Oversized property values in Memgraph nodes.
3. Slow safety-net regex scanning of huge inputs (RunSafetyNet joins all strings and runs several regexes).

Suggested fix: Add a maximum length constant (e.g. maxReasonLength = 4096) and validate all free-text fields at the handler boundary before any storage or safety-net operations.
title: [security] No length cap on free-text RPC fields (reason, seed, interface_contract) — unbounded writes to graph

description:
Two separate FindingSeverity types are defined with different underlying types, same constant names, and same semantic intent:

1. internal/authoring/safety.go: type FindingSeverity int, with SeverityCritical=1, SeverityWarning=2, SeverityNote=3
2. internal/storage/authoring.go: type FindingSeverity string, with SeverityCritical="critical", SeverityWarning="warning", SeverityNote="note"

These are two wholly distinct types despite identical exported names. storage.SafetyFlag.Severity is storage.FindingSeverity (string), while authoring.SafetyFlagResult.Severity is authoring.FindingSeverity (int). The int-based type is used internally for comparison/deduplication logic (m.Severity < existing.Severity), while the string-based type is used for JSON serialisation in storage domain types.

This creates confusion: callers of SafetyResultsToProto must use authoring.FindingSeverity, but SafetyFlag in storage uses storage.FindingSeverity. The types are not interchangeable and require explicit conversion. The severityToProto map in safety.go maps authoring.FindingSeverity (int) to proto, but storage.FindingSeverity (string) is what ends up in persisted and serialised flags.

Suggested fix: consolidate on a single FindingSeverity type. Since it is persisted and serialised, string is the more appropriate underlying type. The int ordering for deduplication can use an explicit rank map (map[FindingSeverity]int) instead of relying on integer value comparison.
title: [types] Duplicate FindingSeverity type with incompatible underlying types across packages

description:
The existing Spec message in proto/specgraph/v1/spec.proto (field 4) uses a free-form string for stage ('spark | shape | specify | decompose | approved | in_progress | done'), while this PR introduces a well-typed AuthoringStage enum. These two representations are now in conflict.

The authoring handler's Amend and Approve RPCs return AuthoringStage enum values, but any caller reading the spec back via SpecService.GetSpec receives a plain string. Clients must now handle two parallel representations of the same domain concept.

Additionally, the storage layer (memgraph/authoring.go line 4002) sets old.stage = 'superseded' directly — a new stage value that is absent from the AuthoringStage enum and not listed in the original Spec.stage comment. This creates a third representation.

Suggested fix: Either (a) change Spec.stage from string to AuthoringStage enum in a coordinated proto change, or (b) add AUTHORING_STAGE_SUPERSEDED to the enum and update the Spec message comment to reference it. Both paths require a migration plan for existing Spec nodes stored with string stage values.
title: [api] Spec.stage field uses free-form string while AuthoringStage enum exists — dual-representation inconsistency
Duplicate stage-to-proto mapping tables in authoring package and server

description:
In internal/scanner/walk.go, the WalkDir callback silently records unreadable paths into SkippedFiles and returns nil:

    if err != nil {
        skipped = append(skipped, SkippedFile{Path: path, Reason: err.Error()})
        return nil //nolint:nilerr // intentional: skip unreadable entries gracefully
    }

The intent is reasonable for file-system permission errors. However, the callers (ScanTier1, ScanTier2) simply return the SkippedFiles slice to their callers. Nobody in the current code path ever checks SkippedFiles or logs its contents. When called from the authoring handler in the future, a scan that silently skips 50% of the codebase due to permission issues will produce misleading constitution output with no indication of the problem.

The nolint directive is appropriate for the skip itself, but there is no mechanism for callers to distinguish 'nothing to scan' from 'everything was skipped due to errors'.

Suggestion: Return or expose a count of skipped-due-error entries separately from skipped-due-test, or require callers to check SkippedFiles and emit at minimum a structured log entry when non-zero. Alternatively, add a helper method SkippedErrors() on Tier1Result/Tier2Result that filters to error-caused skips.
title: Scanner walk errors are swallowed into SkippedFiles with no log or metric

description:
ScanTier2 in internal/scanner/tier2.go (line ~2220) iterates over the caller-supplied dirs slice. For each dir it calls walkGoFiles(absDir, ...). walkGoFiles internally calls filepath.WalkDir which, when the path does not exist, returns an error that walkGoFiles wraps and returns — causing ScanTier2 to return an error to the caller.

The existing tests (internal/scanner/tier2_test.go) cover an empty directory and a normal scan, but not the case where a supplied dir path does not exist. This is a different surface area from the Tier1 non-existent-root test in edge_test.go.

In practice a caller could pass a stale or mis-spelled package path; the current error propagation is correct but untested. A future change that swallows the walk error (e.g. to make ScanTier2 more lenient) would have no test to catch the regression.

Suggested fix: add TestTier2Scan_NonExistentDir that calls ScanTier2(root, []string{'does-not-exist'}) and asserts require.Error(t, err).
title: ScanTier2 with non-existent dir list has no error test; walk silently returns empty result
[security] Internal storage implementation detail (property name) leaks to RPC callers in error responses

description:
The GetPromptsRequest proto comment at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 378 states: 'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' However, the handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 2764 returns CodeNotFound when the stage is valid but has no prompts, which is what happens for APPROVED.

The test at authoring_handler_test.go line 3398 (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage) asserts CodeNotFound, confirming the implementation disagrees with the proto comment.

This is a contract violation: consumers reading the proto documentation will write code expecting INVALID_ARGUMENT (4) for APPROVED, but will receive NOT_FOUND (5). This is a behavioral change risk for any client generated from the proto spec.

Suggested fix: Either (a) update the handler to return CodeInvalidArgument when Stage == AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED before the protoToStage lookup, or (b) update the proto comment to say 'returns NOT_FOUND'. Option (a) is preferred because APPROVED is a known, documented special case, not a missing resource.
title: [api] GetPrompts for APPROVED stage returns CodeNotFound instead of CodeInvalidArgument as documented

description:
internal/authoring/stages.go defines stage constants as untyped string constants (not as AuthoringStage values):

    const (
        StageSpark     = "spark"
        StageShape     = "shape"
        ...
    )

storage/authoring.go defines type AuthoringStage string and storage interfaces take AuthoringStage parameters. However, the authoring package functions ValidateTransition, ValidateAmendTransition, PassesForStage, OfferedPasses, and passRegistry all accept plain string — not AuthoringStage. At the handler boundary, conversion is performed explicitly: storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageSpark). This means the compiler cannot prevent passing an arbitrary string where a typed stage is expected within the authoring package itself.

Additionally, spec.Stage (the field on the domain Spec struct coming from GetSpec) appears to be a plain string (used directly in authoring.ValidateAmendTransition(spec.Stage, ...) without casting), making the type boundary inconsistent.

Suggested fix: either type the constants as storage.AuthoringStage (accepting the import cycle risk which may require moving the type) or introduce an authoring.AuthoringStage type in the authoring package and use it uniformly throughout that package's functions instead of plain string. The goal is compile-time rejection of arbitrary string arguments to stage-sensitive functions.
title: [types] Stage constants are untyped strings; AuthoringStage typed alias not used at authoring package boundary

description:
All 8 RPCs added by this PR (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are registered on the HTTP mux with no authentication or authorization middleware.

Location: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2773-2787 (RegisterAuthoringService), cmd/specgraph/serve.go:274

Impact: Any network-reachable client can approve specs, amend them, or supersede existing work. In the current single-user CLI context this is lower risk, but once the service is exposed on a network interface (the serve command binds on a configurable host/port) this becomes an unauthenticated write-access API.

Suggested fix: Add an authentication interceptor (e.g. ConnectRPC interceptor checking a bearer token from config or a local-only bind restriction) before this PR ships a production-facing deployment. At minimum, document that the server must only be bound to localhost or behind a network boundary that enforces authentication.
title: [security] No authentication on AuthoringService RPCs — all 8 endpoints are unauthenticated

description:
The five new CLI command files (spark.go, shape.go, specify.go, decompose.go, approve.go) share an almost identical structure: var <cmd>Cmd, var <cmd>JSONFile, func init(), func run<Cmd>(). Each file is 41-55 lines of which 30+ lines are structural copy-paste.

The pattern in shape.go, specify.go, and decompose.go is nearly identical:
1. Declare command var
2. Declare jsonFile var
3. init() registers flag and adds command
4. runXxx() calls authoringClient(), conditionally loads JSON, calls client.Xxx(), prints result

Suggestion: Extract a generic helper that handles the common slug+output+json-file pattern, e.g.:

  func runFunnelStage[Req, Resp any](
      slug string,
      output proto.Message,
      jsonFile string,
      call func(context.Context, *connect.Request[Req]) (*connect.Response[Resp], error),
  ) error

This would reduce each command to its unique parts (flag definitions and response printing) while eliminating the repeated client construction, nil-output guard, and error wrapping. The current approach is acceptable for 5 commands but will compound as more stages or operations are added.
title: Five near-identical CLI command files with redundant boilerplate
Supersede handler generic error path does not wrap error with handler context

description:
TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (line ~3056) only exercises AUTHORING_STAGE_SPARK, verifying three specific prompt names. The other valid stages (SHAPE, SPECIFY, DECOMPOSE) have no handler-level GetPrompts test; neither does the error path for AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED (tested separately) cover the stage mapping logic for non-spark stages.

The promptRegistry in internal/authoring/prompts.go is unit-tested via prompts_test.go, but there is no end-to-end assertion that the handler correctly maps proto enums to domain stage strings for shape/specify/decompose and that the response contains the expected prompt names.

If the protoToStage lookup map in authoring_handler.go were to lose an entry (e.g. AUTHORING_STAGE_SPECIFY missing), the handler would return CodeInvalidArgument for valid input, with no test to catch the regression.

Suggested fix: add parameterised subtests for SHAPE, SPECIFY, and DECOMPOSE in TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts, asserting resp.Msg.Prompts is non-empty and that prompts[0].Stage matches the requested stage enum.
title: GetPrompts handler has no test for each individual valid stage returning correct prompt names

description:
The comment on SparkRequest.output at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 229-231 says 'Required by the server; omitting this field returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' The other stage request messages (ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, DecomposeRequest) have no such comment even though the handler enforces the same nil-check for all of them.

In proto3, message fields are always optional at the wire level. The requirement is server-enforced. The inconsistency in comments means:
- SparkRequest documents the requirement
- ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, DecomposeRequest (lines 248-306) do not

Additionally, the validation asymmetry means generated client code and documentation tools may not surface the requirement for the shape/specify/decompose stages consistently.

Suggested fix: Add the same 'Required by the server; omitting this field returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' comment to the output fields of ShapeRequest (line 253), SpecifyRequest (line 277), and DecomposeRequest (line 302). This aligns proto documentation with handler behavior at internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2581-2582, 2618-2619, 2648-2649.
title: [api] SparkRequest.output is documented as required but field nullability is not enforced in proto

description:
The memgraph integration tests in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go call TransitionStage with raw string literals rather than typed constants or named AuthoringStage values:

    err = store.TransitionStage(ctx, "funnel-test", "spark", "shape")
    err = store.TransitionStage(ctx, "funnel-test", "shape", "specify")
    err = store.AmendSpec(ctx, "amend-test", "need to reconsider scope", "shape")

TransitionStage signature is: TransitionStage(ctx, slug string, from, to storage.AuthoringStage). Go allows passing untyped string literals to a string-based named type, so this compiles. However, it means a typo like "shaep" would not be caught at compile time in the test, and the tests fail to demonstrate the typed-constant usage pattern that the rest of the production code follows.

Suggested fix: use storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageShape) or, once the constants are typed as shown in the companion finding, use the constants directly. This makes tests more resilient and exemplifies correct usage.
title: [types] Integration tests bypass typed AuthoringStage by passing raw string literals to TransitionStage
DetectPosture uses float64 cast for integer comparison

description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line ~4975:

The comment on GetPromptsRequest reads:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'

However, the handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage test and the GetPrompts implementation) shows that an APPROVED stage request actually returns CodeNotFound (not CodeInvalidArgument). UNSPECIFIED returns CodeInvalidArgument; APPROVED returns CodeNotFound because it passes the protoToStage lookup but then finds no prompts defined.

This inaccuracy could mislead client authors who implement error handling based on the proto comment and expect INVALID_ARGUMENT from a known-but-empty stage.

Suggested fix: Update the comment to:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns NOT_FOUND.
   The UNSPECIFIED stage returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'
title: [comments] GetPromptsRequest proto doc says APPROVED returns INVALID_ARGUMENT but handler returns NOT_FOUND
ScanTier2 does not pre-validate root existence, producing inconsistent error on non-existent path

description:
TestTransitionStage in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (line ~4133) covers: initial spark->shape, shape->specify, and a skip-forward error (specify->approved). TestTransitionStage_WrongStage covers the wrong-current-stage error path.

Missing integration test cases:
1. TransitionStage called with from = 'approved' (i.e., the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved guard at authoring.go line ~3842) — this guard fires before ValidateTransition and is distinct from the wrong-stage path.
2. TransitionStage on a slug that does not exist (ErrSpecNotFound path via the check query at line ~3864).

Both of these are important error paths that have meaningful storage-level behavior. Without tests, a change that accidentally removes either guard would produce no test failures.

Suggested fix:
- TestTransitionStage_AlreadyApproved: create a spec, advance to approved, call TransitionStage with from='approved', assert ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved).
- TestTransitionStage_SpecNotFound: call TransitionStage on a non-existent slug, assert ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecNotFound).
title: TransitionStage integration test does not verify backward (amend-via-TransitionStage) or approved guard paths
[types] Duplicate stageToProto maps defined independently in authoring package and server handler

description:
The validateSlug function in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (line ~2502) enforces maxSlugLength=256. The handler tests at internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go test empty-slug, path-traversal (..), and invalid-char cases, but no test exercises the 256-char boundary.

Missing cases:
- A slug of exactly 256 characters (should be valid per the current implementation: len(slug) > maxSlugLength means 256 is accepted).
- A slug of 257 characters (should return CodeInvalidArgument).

This matters because the boundary condition is off-by-one: the guard is '>' not '>=' so a 256-char slug passes today. A future change to '>=' would be a silent regression with no test to catch it.

Suggested fix: add two table-driven subtests to TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_* covering exactly-256 and 257-char slugs, asserting the expected error code each time.
title: validateSlug test coverage missing slug-length boundary and exact 256-char slug
[comments] SparkRequest proto field comment omits seed non-empty requirement

description:
The handler for Supersede at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 2742 validates slug and superseded_by with validateSlug() and checks they differ, then calls store.SupersedeSpec(). The storage implementation at memgraph/authoring.go lines 3993-3997 correctly validates both specs exist before proceeding.

However, the handler's stageError helper at line 2899 only maps ErrSpecNotFound to CodeNotFound without distinguishing which slug was not found (old vs new spec). The storage error message does include the slug (e.g. 'new spec %q: %w'), but the connect error wraps it opaquely via CodeNotFound.

More importantly, the test at authoring_handler_test.go line 3258 (TestAuthoringHandler_Supersede_NotFound) only tests the case where the *old* spec is missing. There is no test for the case where superseded_by refers to a non-existent spec. The SupersedeResponse proto at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 369-375 has no error detail field to communicate which slug was missing.

Suggested fix: Add a test for the 'superseded_by spec not found' case. Consider adding a detail field or using connect's error detail mechanism to identify which slug caused the NOT_FOUND, as this is important for client UX.
title: [api] Supersede RPC does not validate that superseding spec exists — asymmetric error behavior
storeJSONProperty character validation is redundant given the allowlist check above it
[comments] Decompose handler TODO missing tracking detail for analytical pass wiring

description:
AmendResponse.version at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 356 is typed as int32. The memgraph implementation at memgraph/authoring.go line 4053 uses a safeInt32(v) helper to convert int64 database values, which implies there is an acknowledged potential for overflow.

This is consistent with the existing Spec.version field (spec.proto line 21, also int32), but the AmendResponse comment says 'monotonically increasing amendment count', which could mislead clients into thinking it is unbounded.

More practically, the Spec.version in spec.proto and AmendResponse.version represent the same underlying counter, but the Spec message is returned by SpecService.UpdateSpec whereas amend operations go through AuthoringService.Amend. There is no way for a client to know from the proto alone that these version fields are synchronized.

Suggested fix: Add a comment to AmendResponse.version noting it mirrors Spec.version and is bounded by int32 (max ~2 billion). For very long-lived specs, consider upgrading to int64 in a future revision, coordinated with the Spec.version field change.
title: [api] AmendResponse.version uses int32 but spec node version can overflow on long-lived specs

description:
Three tests in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go inline the same mux+httptest.NewServer+defer srv.Close() boilerplate instead of using the newAuthoringClient helper defined at line ~3047:

- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts (line ~3056): inlines mux setup, srv := httptest.NewServer, defer srv.Close
- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_UnspecifiedStage (line ~3382): same
- TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage (line ~3398): same

newAuthoringClient already does exactly this and registers cleanup via t.Cleanup. These three tests should use it instead:

  client := newAuthoringClient(t, &fakeAuthoringBackend{}, &fakeBackend{})

This eliminates 6-8 lines of duplicated setup per test and ensures consistent cleanup semantics (t.Cleanup vs defer, which behave differently in subtests).
title: TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts duplicates server/mux setup instead of using newAuthoringClient helper

description:
internal/authoring/safety.go defines SafetyInput as:

    type SafetyInput struct {
        Text       string
        Scope      []string
        Invariants []string
    }

There is no constructor, no validation method, and no documented constraint on when Text may be empty. The RunSafetyNet function accepts *SafetyInput and silently handles empty Text by appending an empty string to parts. In practice the handler for Shape calls RunSafetyNet with Text: strings.Join(msg.Output.GetRisks(), " ") which could be an empty string if no risks are provided. For Decompose, sliceIntents may also be empty.

While benign (scanning empty text returns no flags), the absence of invariant enforcement means the type provides no guarantee that callers are passing meaningful content, and the distinction between 'no content to scan' and 'forgotten to populate' is invisible.
