Suggested fix: add a NewSafetyInput constructor that documents the Text-required contract and returns an error or requires at least one non-empty field, or make the zero-value meaningful by documenting that empty input always returns empty flags.
title: [types] SafetyInput is a plain struct with no validation; empty Text with non-empty Scope/Invariants is silently accepted

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go TransitionStage (line ~3836-3840):

Current Godoc:
  'Returns ErrSpecAlreadyApproved if from is the approved stage.'

The implementation checks:
  if from == authoring.StageApproved {

Here 'from' is storage.AuthoringStage (a named string type) being compared to the string constant authoring.StageApproved. The check fires before any DB query — the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved is returned purely based on the caller-supplied 'from' argument, regardless of what the database actually contains.

The subtle issue: the comment implies the DB is consulted to determine approval state, but it isn't. A caller who passes from=approved always gets ErrSpecAlreadyApproved even if the spec is not actually at that stage in the DB. The Godoc should clarify this is a guard on the caller-supplied argument, not a DB read.

Suggested fix:
  'Returns ErrSpecAlreadyApproved immediately if the caller-supplied from stage is
   the approved stage (no database read is performed for this check).'
title: [comments] TransitionStage Godoc lists ErrSpecAlreadyApproved condition imprecisely

description:
The FindingSeverity enum at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 39-47 is used as the severity field in three distinct message types: SafetyFlag (line 206), RedTeamFinding (line 157), and ConstitutionViolation (line 195). These are semantically distinct concepts:

- SafetyFlag.severity: indicates whether a safety concern requires immediate action
- RedTeamFinding.severity: indicates whether a challenge blocks spec advancement  
- ConstitutionViolation.severity: indicates whether a constraint violation blocks advancement

While the CRITICAL/WARNING/NOTE values happen to map sensibly to all three today, coupling these types to a single shared enum creates brittleness: if the severity model needs to diverge for one type (e.g., safety flags need a FATAL level), changing the enum affects all consumers of all three types.

This is a proto API design concern because enum values in proto3 are shared across the namespace and cannot be independently evolved per message.

Suggested fix: Consider using separate enums per domain concept (SafetyFlagSeverity, FindingSeverity, ViolationSeverity) even if they start with identical values. This follows the proto best practice of preferring redundancy over coupling at the cost of future flexibility.
title: [api] FindingSeverity enum is shared across unrelated message types creating coupling risk
Decompose handler builds sliceIntents with manual loop where strings.Map or a collect helper would suffice

description:
ScanTier2 accepts a caller-supplied slice of directory names and constructs absolute paths via filepath.Join(root, dir) with no verification that the result stays within root.

Location: internal/scanner/tier2.go:2226 (ScanTier2)

Vulnerable code:
  absDir := filepath.Join(root, dir)
  skipped, err := walkGoFiles(absDir, ...)

If a caller passes an absolute path (e.g. '/etc') or a path with leading '../' components (e.g. '../../etc'), filepath.Join will resolve it outside root and the scanner will happily walk that subtree, exposing file paths and package names from arbitrary filesystem locations.

By contrast, ScanTier1 only takes a single root and does not have this issue.

Suggested fix: After computing absDir, verify it is still under root:
  rel, err := filepath.Rel(root, absDir)
  if err \!= nil || strings.HasPrefix(rel, '..') {
      return nil, fmt.Errorf('scanner: dir %q escapes root', dir)
  }

This is the same containment pattern used internally for relPath computation.
title: [security] ScanTier2 does not verify caller-supplied dirs stay under root — path traversal possible
[types] Tier1Result and Tier2Result expose mutable slice fields with only documentation-level immutability contract
[comments] AuthoringHandler Godoc missing from exported RegisterAuthoringService function
runInTxOrSequential returns nil explicitly but the loop body already does so

description:
The Spark RPC at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 2547 calls CreateSpec() as part of its transaction, creating the spec node. All other stage RPCs (Shape, Specify, Decompose) only call TransitionStage() and StoreXOutput() — they assume the spec already exists.

This creates an implicit pre-condition on Shape/Specify/Decompose that is not documented in the proto: callers must have previously called Spark (or created the spec via SpecService.CreateSpec) before calling these RPCs. If the spec does not exist, they receive CodeNotFound.

However, the SparkRequest proto comment (line 225-234) says 'Spark creates a new spec and enters the spark stage', suggesting Spark is the canonical entry point. But clients could reasonably try to call Shape directly on an existing spec that was created via SpecService.CreateSpec.

The behavior when a spec exists at the wrong stage (e.g., calling Shape on a spec at 'decompose') is CodeFailedPrecondition. But the behavior when the spec does not exist at all is CodeNotFound, and Shape's error path goes through h.stageError() which does correctly map ErrSpecNotFound to CodeNotFound.

Suggested fix: Document in the proto service comments that Spark is the required entry point to the authoring funnel. The Shape/Specify/Decompose/Approve/Amend RPCs all require the spec to exist. Add explicit 'Returns NOT_FOUND if the spec does not exist' to each RPC's comment for discoverability.
title: [api] Spark RPC creates spec and advances stage atomically but other stage RPCs do not create the spec — missing symmetry
Amend response returns UNSPECIFIED stage for non-funnel DB values

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput creates child specs and DEPENDS_ON edges in a single sequential loop. The dependency MERGE query:

  MATCH (from:Spec {slug: $from_slug}), (to:Spec {slug: $to_slug})
  MERGE (from)-[:DEPENDS_ON]->(to)

requires both nodes to exist in the DB at the time it runs. If slice B (which depends on slice A) appears before slice A in the output.Slices list, A's child spec has not yet been created when B's DEPENDS_ON edge is attempted. The MATCH finds no rows, MERGE creates nothing, and no error is returned (len(records) == 0 is not checked).

The result is silently missing graph edges — graph queries that traverse DEPENDS_ON will produce incorrect results.

Suggested fix: either (1) enforce that slices are ordered so dependencies always appear before dependents, returning an error if not, or (2) perform edge creation in a second pass after all child specs have been created.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 136-149
title: DEPENDS_ON edges silently not created for out-of-order decompose slices

description:
The proto definition for SparkRequest, ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, and DecomposeRequest all include a 'posture' field (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 4830, 4852, 4876, 4902). The authoring package has full posture detection logic (internal/authoring/posture.go) and pass-selection by posture (internal/authoring/passes.go). However, internal/server/authoring_handler.go never reads req.Msg.Posture and never calls authoring.ResolvePosture or authoring.PassesForStage.

This means:
- Clients cannot influence posture-aware pass selection even though the API contract implies it
- The posture detection and pass infrastructure is unreachable from the live service

If posture wiring is intentionally deferred, a TODO comment should be added in the handler. If unintentional, the handler should call authoring.ResolvePosture(msg.Posture, nil) and use the result to select analytical passes.

Files: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (all stage handlers), internal/authoring/passes.go, internal/authoring/posture.go
title: posture field in authoring RPC requests is accepted but never used

description:
In proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, the comment on GetPromptsRequest (line 4975 in diff) states:

  // The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.

However, the handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 289 returns CodeNotFound (not CodeInvalidArgument) when no prompts are defined for a stage. The test TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage (line 3398) correctly asserts CodeNotFound, consistent with the code but contradicting the proto doc.

The proto comment should be corrected to say 'returns NOT_FOUND' rather than 'returns INVALID_ARGUMENT'. Proto comments serve as the API contract documentation and should be accurate.

File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, GetPromptsRequest comment
title: Proto doc comment for GetPromptsRequest contradicts implementation

description:
In cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go line 198, TestSparkCmd_SeedFlag calls sparkCmd.ResetFlags() on the package-level global sparkCmd variable, then re-registers the --seed flag. This is not safe for parallel execution: if any other test in the same package runs concurrently and uses sparkCmd, the flags will be absent during and after ResetFlags().

Additionally, the test's assertion only checks that the flag can be registered — it does not verify flag behavior. The same test already ran in init(), so this test is essentially a no-op that also introduces a mutation hazard.

Suggested fix: remove TestSparkCmd_SeedFlag or replace it with a table-driven check in TestAuthoringCmds_JSONFileFlag (which already covers --json-file). The --seed flag presence is implicitly covered by that pattern.

File: cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go, line 196-204
title: TestSparkCmd_SeedFlag mutates global sparkCmd in test

description: Fix DEPENDS_ON edge creation for out-of-order slices (two-pass), wrap AmendSpec GetSpec error, close redundant validation as intentional, address Cypher interpolation
title: Fix(spgr-34l.45,.1,.32,.8): memgraph authoring.go fixes

description:
The Supersede handler tests in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go cover error paths well (empty slug, empty superseded_by, not-found, self-supersede, invalid superseded_by slug) but there is no happy-path test that verifies a successful Supersede call returns HTTP 200 with the correct slug/superseded_by fields in the response.

Without a success-path test, the response-body contract (resp.Msg.Slug, resp.Msg.SupersededBy) is never asserted. A silent regression where those fields are swapped or left empty would go undetected.

Suggested fix: add TestAuthoringHandler_Supersede_HappyPath verifying resp.Msg.Slug == 'my-spec' and resp.Msg.SupersededBy == 'new-spec' using the existing fakeAuthoringBackend with supersedeErr: nil.
title: no test for Supersede happy path with valid reason field

description: Add path traversal check to ScanTier2, pre-validate root existence, improve walk error handling, add tier2 error test, protect mutable slice fields on Tier1/Tier2Result
title: Fix(spgr-34l.4,.27,.15,.16,.40): scanner fixes

description: Fix TestSparkCmd_SeedFlag global mutation, reduce CLI command boilerplate across spark/shape/specify/decompose/approve commands
title: Fix(spgr-34l.48,.20): cmd fixes

description: Safety flag persistence, length caps, GetPrompts error code, Supersede wrapping, TODO tracking, manual loop, UNSPECIFIED stage
title: Fix(batch12-handler): authoring_handler.go fixes

description: Slug boundary tests, Supersede happy path, posture threading, per-stage tests, helper dedup
title: Fix(batch12-tests): authoring_handler_test.go fixes

description: FindingSeverity types, duplicate mapping, typed stages, float64 cast, SafetyInput, typed test constants, backward transition, decompose error tests
title: Fix(batch12-pkg): authoring + storage package fixes
storeJSONProperty wraps ErrSpecNotFound but loses caller context when chained

description:
memgraph.StoreDecomposeOutput in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (lines ~3903-3951) validates two constraints before writing:
1. Empty slice ID returns an error (line ~3906).
2. Duplicate slice ID returns an error (line ~3909-3910).
3. A DependsOn referencing a sibling ID not in the current output returns an error (line ~3938-3939).

The integration tests in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go cover the happy path, idempotency, and missing parent, but none of the three validation errors above are exercised against a real Memgraph instance.

If the validation logic were accidentally removed (e.g. during a refactor of the slice-building loop), the integration tests would not catch it. The unit-level handler tests use a fake backend and therefore also miss these paths.

Suggested additions to authoring_test.go:
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_EmptySliceID: output with a slice where ID == ''.
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_DuplicateSliceID: two slices with the same ID.
- TestStoreDecomposeOutput_UnknownDependency: slice with DependsOn pointing to a non-sibling ID.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput duplicate-slice-ID and unknown-dependency errors have no integration test

description:
storeJSONProperty constructs its Cypher query by interpolating the property name directly using fmt.Sprintf because Cypher does not support parameterized property names.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:4085-4089

Vulnerable pattern:
  query := fmt.Sprintf(`
      MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
      SET s.%s = $data, ...
  `, property)

The allowlist (allowedJSONProperties) and character validation are the current defenses. This design is sound as long as:
1. storeJSONProperty is never called from untrusted-input code paths (currently true — all callers pass compile-time constant strings).
2. The allowlist check is always executed before the fmt.Sprintf (currently true).

Risk: If a future refactor adds a caller that derives the property name from user input (e.g. a dynamic pass name from an RPC field), the allowlist is the only gate. A bug in allowlist population would be a direct Cypher injection.

Suggested fix:
- Add a compile-time test or go:generate check that verifies all call sites of storeJSONProperty pass a constant string literal, not a runtime variable.
- Add a code comment at each call site explicitly stating the property is a compile-time constant, making it harder to accidentally introduce a variable.
- Consider extracting a typed PropertyName enum to make non-constant invocations a compile error.
title: [security] Cypher property name interpolated via fmt.Sprintf — allowlist must be sole trust boundary

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, every RPC handler (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) computes safety flags and returns them to the caller:

    safetyFlags := authoring.RunSafetyNet(&authoring.SafetyInput{Text: msg.Output.GetSeed()})
    return connect.NewResponse(&specv1.SparkResponse{
        SafetyFlags: authoring.SafetyResultsToProto(safetyFlags),
        ...
    }), nil

However, StoreSafetyFlags is defined on the AuthoringBackend interface (internal/storage/authoring.go) and the Memgraph implementation exists (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go StoreSafetyFlags), but none of the handlers ever call h.store.StoreSafetyFlags(). The safety analysis runs on every request, the results are returned to the client, but they are never durably recorded in the graph.

This means:
- Audit trails of safety concerns are lost between requests
- Downstream consumers (analytics, constitution check integration) can never read persisted flags
- If a client drops the response, the safety finding is silently gone

The fakeAuthoringBackend in authoring_handler_test.go has StoreSafetyFlags as a no-op (returns nil), confirming this code path is never exercised in the handler.

Fix: After the transaction in each handler, call h.store.StoreSafetyFlags() with the computed flags, wrapping the error and logging it at minimum (or returning it if it should be blocking).
title: Safety flags computed in RPC handlers but never persisted to storage

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8 which adds the authoring funnel (proto, stages, posture, prompts, safety, passes, scanner, storage, handler, CLI)
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 11487: the Supersede handler passes the raw storage error directly into connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, err). This can expose internal details such as storage layer paths, slug context, and memgraph-specific error messages to the RPC caller.

Example: the memgraph layer returns errors like 'memgraph: supersede spec: old spec "foo/bar": memgraph: spec "foo/bar": spec not found' — the full chain is sent to the client.

All other handlers in this file use sanitized error messages (e.g., errors.New("spec not found")), but Supersede deviates on the NotFound path.

Fix: replace 'connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, err)' with 'connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, errors.New("spec not found"))' to match the pattern used in stageError().
title: Supersede RPC passes raw storage error to client, leaking internal details

close_reason: Borderline: Scanner being removed in Slice 3.5. Fixing path canonicalization in code scheduled for deletion.
description:
internal/scanner/tier1.go ScanTier1() (line 10517): the function accepts a root path and passes it directly to os.Stat and then to walkGoFiles without resolving it to an absolute path or verifying it stays within any expected boundary.

By contrast, ScanTier2() (internal/scanner/tier2.go lines 10780-10799) correctly calls filepath.Abs(root) and then validates each sub-directory with filepath.Rel to detect escape via '..'.

If ScanTier1 is called from a server-side context in future (e.g., a constitution scan RPC), a caller supplying '../../etc' as root would traverse outside the intended scan boundary.

Currently ScanTier1 is only called from CLI (cmd/specgraph) where the path comes from the user's own shell, so the risk is low in the current scope. However the asymmetry between Tier1 and Tier2 is a latent defect.

Fix: apply the same filepath.Abs + containment pattern from ScanTier2 to ScanTier1, even though there is no boundary to enforce in the single-root case. At minimum, resolve root to an absolute path with filepath.Abs before passing to walkGoFiles so that error messages and SkippedFile paths are consistent.
title: ScanTier1 accepts arbitrary root path without canonicalization or containment check

description: internal/authoring/stages.go line 10058: Stage.ToStorage() does storage.AuthoringStage(s) — a plain type cast between two distinct string-based types. Both types have the same string values, but the cast is unchecked: an arbitrary Stage value (including the empty string or a typo constant) will silently produce a storage.AuthoringStage with an invalid value. There is no compile-time guarantee that the two sets of constants remain in sync. A wrong Stage constant (e.g. Stage('typo')) would pass through ToStorage and reach the database layer without error. Suggested improvement: add a validation step in ToStorage that checks the converted value against the known storage constants, or consolidate the two types into one canonical type defined in a shared package that both authoring and storage import. The existing ValidateTransition function already validates stage names, but ToStorage bypasses it.
title: Stage and AuthoringStage are parallel string types connected only by unsafe cast

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11421-11424: After TransitionStage succeeds in the Approve handler, GetSpec is called to read back the approved_at timestamp. If GetSpec returns an error, the handler returns CodeInternal (line 11423). This is a user-facing failure mode — the spec was approved in storage but the RPC fails. No test covers this path. The fakeBackend.GetSpec always returns success (line 11935). A test should inject a GetSpec failure after a successful TransitionStage and verify CodeInternal is returned. This is the only Approve error path that is untested.
title: Approve handler GetSpec error path is untested — internal error after successful stage transition

description: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go lines 13014-13026: storeJSONProperty is called at line 13014 to persist the decompose_output JSON blob before the slice ID validation loop runs at lines 13019-13027. If a slice has an empty ID or a duplicate ID, storeJSONProperty has already written the output to the spec node in the graph, leaving it in an inconsistent state (persisted JSON with the invalid data). The fix is to move the slice ID validation (lines 13017-13027 building sliceIDs map plus empty/dup checks) to run before storeJSONProperty at line 13014.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput writes JSON blob before validating slice IDs

description: internal/authoring/safety.go: The godoc on SafetyInput (lines 9569-9579 in the diff) documents stage-specific content guidance ('Spark: seed idea text', 'Shape: risks list (joined)', etc.) but SafetyInput only has three fields: Text, Scope, and Invariants. The mapping of stage names to field usage is partially misleading — Decompose is described as 'slice intents (joined)' but the Text field is populated via a strings.Builder in the handler, not from a named field. More importantly, the godoc implies SafetyInput is a stage-tagged union, which it is not. A future maintainer reading this comment and the struct definition will see a mismatch. The comment should either be removed (since the caller is responsible for populating the fields correctly) or reworded to describe what each field holds, not the per-stage usage policy.
title: SafetyInput godoc lists fields not present on the struct

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go at safetyFlagsToStorage (line 11790-11799), two silent fallbacks exist: (1) categoryToStorage[f.Category] returns "" (empty string storage.SafetyCategory) for unrecognized SafetyCategory values, and (2) authoring.ToStorageSeverity(f.Severity) at safety.go line 11817 explicitly maps unknown values to the empty string. Both are silent: the safety flag is written to storage with an empty category and/or empty severity string, making it unqueryable and meaningless. Unlike SafetyResultsToProto which maps unknowns to _UNSPECIFIED proto enums (visible to callers), the storage path has no diagnostic. Suggested fix: return an error or log a warning when category or severity produces an empty storage value, rather than silently persisting a degraded record.
title: safetyFlagsToStorage silent empty-string on unknown category/severity: flag stored with no category or severity

close_reason: Out of scope: Previously raised as spgr-s34.38 and deferred — type system work not in Slice 3 scope.
description: internal/storage/authoring.go line 12824: ConsistencyIssue.IssueKind is type string with no defined constants. Every other categorical field in the same file uses a typed string constant (DecompositionStrategy, PeripheralDisposition, FindingSeverity, SafetyCategory). IssueKind is the odd one out — callers can assign arbitrary strings, making exhaustive handling impossible. Suggested fix: define type IssueKind string with constants (e.g. IssueKindConflict, IssueKindDuplicate, IssueKindCycle) and change the field to that type. This matches the pattern already established for PeripheralDisposition and DecompositionStrategy in the same file.
title: ConsistencyIssue.IssueKind is untyped string with no validation or constants

close_reason: Out of scope: Slice 3.5 plan explicitly sequences scanner removal AFTER Slice 3 merges. Scanner additions are intentional for this PR.
description: The PR includes docs/plans/2026-03-03-slice-3.5-scanner-cleanup-plan.md which explicitly mandates deleting internal/scanner/tier1.go, tier2.go, tier1_test.go, tier2_test.go, and edge_test.go before any further work. Instead the PR adds all these files as new code (all are marked 'new file' in the diff). internal/scanner/scanner.go is also modified rather than deleted. The plan states: 'The scanner package, the --scan CLI flag, and all documentation references to codebase scanner as a component are removed.' This PR directly contradicts that committed architectural decision. The scanner (Tier 1 / Tier 2 Go AST parser) should be replaced by agent-driven codebase context gathering. Location: internal/scanner/tier1.go (new), internal/scanner/tier2.go (new), internal/scanner/edge_test.go (new), internal/scanner/scanner.go (modified). Fix: either remove the scanner files per the Slice 3.5 plan, or explicitly document that the plan is being superseded by a new decision.
title: Scanner package added despite Slice 3.5 plan requiring its removal

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11507-11508: When GetPrompts is called with AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED, the stage is valid and known (it maps in protoToStage), but promptRegistry has no entry for StageApproved. PromptsToProto returns nil, and the handler returns CodeInvalidArgument with 'no prompts defined for stage approved'. CodeInvalidArgument signals a malformed request, but the request is well-formed — the approved stage simply has no prompts by design. The correct code is CodeNotFound (the resource 'prompts for approved stage' does not exist), or the handler could return a successful empty response. Using CodeInvalidArgument causes clients to treat a legitimate query as a bad request.
title: GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for approved stage instead of CodeNotFound

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11460-11463: The Amend handler checks if stageToProto returns UNSPECIFIED for the stage returned by AmendSpec storage. If so, it returns CodeInternal with an 'unknown stage returned from storage' message. This path is not tested. It would fire if the storage layer returns an unexpected stage string (e.g. due to a migration bug or a new stage not yet in the proto mapping). A test should set fakeAuthoringBackend.amendResult to &storage.AmendResult{Stage: storage.AuthoringStage('bogus')} and verify CodeInternal is returned.
title: Amend returns unknown stage from storage — CodeInternal path is untested

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11471-11495: the Supersede RPC handler validates both slugs but does not enforce a maximum length on req.Msg.Reason before passing it to store.SupersedeSpec(), which stores it verbatim as an edge property in Memgraph.

All other handlers that accept free-text fields enforce maxFieldLen (10000 chars) — e.g. Amend at line 11446-11448, Shape's scope items, Specify's items. Supersede is the only handler that skips this check.

An attacker can supply an arbitrarily large reason string, causing unbounded writes to graph storage and potential memory exhaustion in the Memgraph edge property.

Fix: add 'if len(req.Msg.Reason) > maxFieldLen { return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument, fmt.Errorf("reason exceeds maximum length of %d characters", maxFieldLen)) }' after the self-supersede check, consistent with how Amend validates its reason field.
title: Supersede RPC: reason field has no maximum length cap

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go at stageError (lines 11727-11728), the fallthrough path logs the original error via slog but returns connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, errors.New("internal error")) — wrapping a brand-new static error, not the original. This is intentional from a security perspective (not leaking internal details), but it means: (1) there is no correlation ID or request context in the log entry, making it hard to correlate logs with specific RPC failures at scale; (2) no structured 'slug' field is included in the log, so the log entry is not actionable without cross-referencing other log lines. Suggested improvement: add slug and operation context to the slog call so the log entry is self-contained for on-call debugging, e.g. slog.String("slug", slug) if slug is threaded through.
title: stageError severs error chain for internal errors: original error logged but not propagated

description:
In authoring_handler.go line 11507-11508, GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument when the stage has no prompts (including AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED). AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED is a valid, well-formed enum value — the request is not invalid. CodeInvalidArgument is semantically reserved for malformed requests (missing fields, bad values), not for valid requests that happen to have no associated data.

Per gRPC/ConnectRPC conventions: INVALID_ARGUMENT = client sent a bad request; NOT_FOUND = the requested resource does not exist. Returning INVALID_ARGUMENT for a well-formed request with a valid enum value will mislead clients into thinking they sent a malformed message.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 11507-11508 (diff)
Also: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, GetPromptsRequest comment documents this as INVALID_ARGUMENT, which enshrines the wrong code.

Fix: Return CodeNotFound (or an empty GetPromptsResponse{}) for the no-prompts case. Update the proto comment to match. An empty response with a 200 OK is arguably the cleanest: callers can then check len(prompts) == 0 themselves without needing error handling for a semantically valid query.
title: GetPrompts returns INVALID_ARGUMENT for valid APPROVED stage enum value

close_reason: Borderline: Scanner being removed in Slice 3.5. Type mutability concern moot.
description: internal/scanner/tier1.go lines 10504-10506 and tier2.go lines 10754-10757: Both result types document 'Callers must not modify the returned slices; treat them as read-only' in a comment. This is an invariant enforced solely through documentation — any caller can append to or mutate Packages, Interfaces, Structs, Functions, Imports, TestFiles, or SkippedFiles. This is a classic anemic type anti-pattern. Options: (1) return concrete slice-backed read-only wrapper types, (2) make the fields unexported and expose read-only accessors, or (3) document this as a known trade-off and accept the risk. Option 3 is acceptable for internal scan results that are immediately consumed, but the comment should at least note what happens if the invariant is violated (data corruption in the caller's result set).
title: Tier1Result and Tier2Result mutability invariant enforced only by comment

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go: The comment on the stageToProto local wrapper function reads 'Kept as a local alias so call sites within this file remain unchanged.' This comment documents historical artifact of a refactor, not a design decision. The 'call sites remain unchanged' justification only makes sense during the refactor window and becomes misleading once the refactor settles. A future reader has no way to verify whether the call sites ever used a different form. The comment should either be dropped (the function is self-evident as a delegating wrapper) or replaced with a brief design note if the indirection is intentional.
title: stageToProto local alias comment overstates reason for existence

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11741-11764: runAnalyticalPasses iterates PassesForStage results and dispatches on pass name. PassConstitutionCheck (value 'constitution_check') is registered in passRegistry for every stage (StageSpark, StageShape, StageSpecify, StageDecompose) but has no case in the switch statement. As a result, constitution check results are silently discarded and the constitution_violations fields in SparkResponse, ShapeResponse, SpecifyResponse, and DecomposeResponse (defined in authoring.proto lines 14178, 14202, 14228, 14252) are never populated. A case for authoring.PassConstitutionCheck needs to be added that appends to a constitutionViolations slice and the handlers need to pass that slice back in the response.
title: PassConstitutionCheck missing from runAnalyticalPasses switch — constitution_violations always empty

close_reason: Borderline: Scanner being removed in Slice 3.5. SkippedFiles surfacing moot.
description: internal/scanner/walk.go (lines 11069-11072) intentionally records parse failures in SkippedFiles rather than returning an error. ScanTier1 (tier1.go line 11589) and ScanTier2 (tier2.go line 11861) both propagate SkippedFiles in their result structs. However, no CLI command in cmd/specgraph/ inspects or displays the SkippedFiles field. Any Go file that fails to parse — due to syntax errors, read permission issues, or encoding problems — is silently excluded from the scan result. The caller (CLI user or server-side scan caller) receives a result that looks complete but may be missing significant files. Suggested fix: after scanning, check if len(result.SkippedFiles) > 0 and either log a warning per-file or print a summary count to stderr so users are aware of degraded scan coverage.
title: scanner SkippedFiles never surfaced in CLI: parse and walk errors silently discarded by all CLI commands

description: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go lines 12920-12932: The allowedJSONProperties allowlist guards storeJSONProperty against writing arbitrary property names to the graph. This is a security-relevant path. The allowlist enforcement branch (rejecting a property not in the map) has no test in authoring_test.go. If the allowlist check were accidentally removed or bypassed, no test would catch it. A unit test should call storeJSONProperty (or any Store method that exercises it) with a disallowed property name and verify the error is returned. Note: storeJSONProperty is unexported so the test would need to use a Store method or test via a public wrapper. Alternatively, a test should verify that calling StoreSparkOutput with a deliberate disallowed-property injection in a future refactor would be caught. The char-validation branch (non-alphanumeric/underscore chars in property name) is also untested.
title: storeJSONProperty allowlist enforcement path has no unit test — injection bypass untested

description: authoring.ResolvePosture (internal/authoring/posture.go line 221) provides heuristic posture detection when POSTURE_UNSPECIFIED is passed, but all four stage handlers (Shape, Specify, Decompose in internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11267, 11329, 11397) call runAnalyticalPasses(stage, msg.Posture) directly without going through ResolvePosture. When a client sends POSTURE_UNSPECIFIED, msg.Posture remains POSTURE_UNSPECIFIED at the runAnalyticalPasses call site, which will match no posture in the passRegistry autoIn slices (they only contain DRIVE, PARTNER, SUPPORT). Result: no analytical passes auto-run for clients that do not set an explicit posture. ResolvePosture was implemented specifically to handle this case via heuristic detection, but it is never called from the handlers. Fix: each stage handler should call authoring.ResolvePosture(msg.Posture, nil) (or pass actual messages if available) before passing posture to runAnalyticalPasses.
title: ResolvePosture heuristic dead code — handlers bypass posture detection fallback

description: internal/authoring/safety.go lines 9591-9610: SafetyCategory uses iota+1 so the zero value (0) is not any named constant. SafetyCategory.String() returns 'unknown' for 0. However SafetyFlagResult (line 9613) can be zero-constructed with SafetyCategory(0), and RunSafetyNet does not validate categories before appending results. The categoryToProto map (line 9776) does not include 0, so a zero-value SafetyFlagResult would map to SAFETY_CATEGORY_UNSPECIFIED in SafetyResultsToProto. While the current code never generates a zero-value SafetyCategory (all flags come from allPatternGroups which uses named constants), there is no compile-time or runtime guard preventing it. Suggest adding a SafetyCategoryUnspecified = 0 constant with a corresponding entry in categoryToProto mapping to SAFETY_CATEGORY_UNSPECIFIED, making the zero value explicit and handled.
title: SafetyCategory zero value (0) is unnamed and produces 'unknown' from String()

description:
AmendRequest.target_stage accepts AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED as a valid value at the handler level — protoToStage maps it (line 11682 in diff). The proto comment on AmendRequest says 'Approved specs cannot be amended; use Supersede to replace them instead', but this refers to the SOURCE spec, not the target stage. Nothing in the handler validates that target_stage != APPROVED.

The storage layer (memgraph AmendSpec) calls ValidateAmendTransition which only allows backward transitions. Since APPROVED is the last stage, the only way to set target_stage=APPROVED via amend would be from... nothing (there's no stage after it). ValidateAmendTransition would reject APPROVED as a target from any earlier stage (it would be a forward move, not backward). However, this validation is two layers deep and the behavior is undocumented at the RPC layer.

Risk: If the stage ordering or ValidateAmendTransition logic ever changes, a client could amend a spec directly to APPROVED, bypassing the formal Approve RPC and its side effects (acceptLinkedDecisions, approved_at timestamp). The handler should explicitly reject AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED as a target_stage with CodeInvalidArgument.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Amend handler (~line 11449-11455 in diff)
Proto: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, AmendRequest message

Fix: Add an explicit check in the Amend handler: if req.Msg.TargetStage == AUTHORING_STAGE_APPROVED { return INVALID_ARGUMENT, 'use Approve RPC to advance to approved stage' }
title: Amend RPC accepts APPROVED as target_stage despite proto docs saying approved specs cannot be amended

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11519-11562: The acceptLinkedDecisions method is called from Approve (line 11430) and has non-trivial logic — it queries edges via graphBackend, iterates found decision slugs, fetches each decision, filters for proposed status, then updates to accepted. Errors are intentionally swallowed (best-effort). None of this is tested. Specifically untested: (1) when graphBackend and decisionBackend are wired (via RegisterAuthoringService type assertions), the decisions are actually transitioned; (2) when ListEdges returns an error, the Approve RPC still succeeds; (3) the edge direction disambiguation logic (from_id vs to_id when either equals the spec slug); (4) decisions not in proposed status are skipped. The fakeAuthoringBackend does not implement decision or graph backends, and the test helpers never wire these. A bug in the edge-direction logic (lines 11535-11542) would silently never accept any linked decisions, and no test would catch it.
title: acceptLinkedDecisions is entirely untested — decision acceptance silently skipped on errors

description:
Two locations carry a TODO referencing ADR-003 for promoting ShapeOutput.Decisions to graph nodes, but neither references a bead or issue ID that would make it trackable:

1. internal/storage/authoring.go line ~12738: '// TODO(ADR-003): promote to Decision graph nodes'
2. internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreShapeOutput godoc: 'TODO(ADR-003): ShapeOutput.Decisions are stored as plain strings...'

ADR-003 is a document, not a work item. Without a bead/issue reference, these TODOs have no action path and will silently accumulate. The StoreShapeOutput godoc TODO is particularly at risk of rot — it accurately describes current behavior but the fix path is implicit. Either add a bead ID (e.g., 'TODO(spgr-XXX)') or promote these to tracked work items and link them.
title: TODO(ADR-003) in ShapeOutput.Decisions lacks bead ID or tracking reference

description: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go: The integration tests for AmendSpec cover (a) happy path backward transition, (b) already-approved rejection, and (c) forward-transition rejection. Missing: (1) AmendSpec on a non-existent slug — should return ErrSpecNotFound; (2) AmendSpec where the current stage in storage does not match what ValidateAmendTransition expects. These gaps mean a regression where AmendSpec silently no-ops on missing specs would not be caught at the storage layer (only at the handler layer).
title: memgraph.AmendSpec has no test for empty-reason or missing-spec error paths

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, runAnalyticalPasses (lines 11735-11766) has no error return value. The callers in Shape (line 11267), Specify (line 11329), and Decompose (line 11397) discard the second through fourth return values entirely using blank identifiers (_, _, _, _ or partial blanks). When real LLM-driven pass execution is wired in (noted as 'deferred to a later slice'), any errors from those passes will have nowhere to go — the function signature forces silent failure. The placeholder comment at line 11733-11734 acknowledges this, but the structural issue should be fixed now before the signature becomes load-bearing. Suggested fix: add an error return to runAnalyticalPasses now, even if it always returns nil in the placeholder implementation, so callers are forced to handle it when real logic is added.
title: runAnalyticalPasses has no error return path: future real pass failures will be silently dropped

description: The passRegistry in internal/authoring/passes.go (lines 8993-8995) defines PassConstitutionCheck as auto-running for all postures at the StageSpark stage. However, the Spark handler in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 11149-11205) never calls runAnalyticalPasses and returns a SparkResponse with no constitution_violations field populated. The proto SparkResponse message (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 14178) includes constitution_violations as a repeated field, which is always nil in Spark responses. Shape/Specify/Decompose handlers all call runAnalyticalPasses; Spark is the only stage that does not. This is inconsistent with both the pass registry and the PR description claiming 'Analytical passes with posture-aware scheduling'. Fix: add a runAnalyticalPasses(authoring.StageSpark, msg.Posture) call in the Spark handler and populate SparkResponse.ConstitutionViolations.
title: Spark handler omits runAnalyticalPasses — constitution check never runs for Spark stage

close_reason: Out of scope: Backend.CreateSpec predates this PR on main. Pre-existing architectural pattern.
description:
The AuthoringBackend interface (internal/storage/authoring.go) composes StageWriter + PassWriter + SpecLifecycle, but CreateSpec — which is the first operation of the Spark RPC — lives on the separate Backend interface. The AuthoringHandler holds both store (AuthoringBackend) and backend (Backend) to support the Spark flow.

This split is a leaky abstraction: the authoring funnel's entry point (creating a spec) is not part of the authoring storage contract. Callers who want to implement or test the full authoring flow must implement two separate interfaces. The RegisterAuthoringService function signature makes this awkward (it takes both parameters).

This is not a breaking change since it's new API, but it establishes a precedent that will grow more confusing as Spark is the canonical entry point of the authoring funnel.

File: internal/storage/authoring.go (AuthoringBackend interface), internal/server/authoring_handler.go (RegisterAuthoringService line 11566, AuthoringHandler struct line 11138)

Suggestion: Add CreateSpec to StageWriter or a new SpecCreator sub-interface within AuthoringBackend, so the full authoring lifecycle is represented in one composable interface. This avoids needing two separate backend dependencies in AuthoringHandler for what is logically one workflow.
title: AuthoringBackend missing CreateSpec but Spark handler requires it from a separate Backend interface

description: cmd/specgraph/spark.go, shape.go, specify.go, decompose.go, approve.go: The cmd_test.go only tests cobra flag registration and Args validation — it never calls RunE. The actual run functions (runSpark, runShape, runSpecify, runDecompose) are entirely untested. These functions contain: (1) authoringClient() connection error handling, (2) loadJSONFile error handling (for --json-file commands), (3) RPC call and error propagation, (4) output formatting. A prior review batch (comment id:46 on spgr-s34.50) deferred CLI output/JSON loading tests as 'coverage expansion out of scope', so this is a known gap, but the deferred state should be reflected here. The loadJSONFile function is tested in util_test.go in isolation, which partially mitigates item 2.
title: CLI command run functions (runSpark, runShape etc.) have zero coverage — JSON loading and RPC wiring untested

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11710-11728: stageError maps known storage errors to connect RPC codes. It handles ErrSpecAlreadyApproved (CodeFailedPrecondition), ErrInvalidStageTransition (CodeFailedPrecondition), and ErrSpecNotFound (CodeNotFound), but does not handle ErrSpecSuperseded. When AmendSpec returns ErrSpecSuperseded (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 13151), stageError falls through to the catch-all CodeInternal branch (line 11728), returning a generic 'internal error' response. The fix is to add a case for storage.ErrSpecSuperseded in stageError, mapping it to CodeFailedPrecondition with a clear message like 'spec has been superseded and cannot be amended'.
title: stageError does not handle ErrSpecSuperseded — amend on superseded spec returns CodeInternal

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go: The godoc on runAnalyticalPasses reads 'All returned slices contain placeholder data; real LLM-driven pass execution is deferred to a later slice.' The comment is accurate today but will become incorrect once the real implementation lands — and because the comment says 'later slice' rather than a specific bead or milestone reference, there is no signal in the code about when to update it.

Additionally, the function returns placeholders even when PassesForStage returns no passes (e.g., PassConstitutionCheck is in the registry but has no corresponding case in the switch). The comment mentions 'deferred to a later slice' but does not document that PassConstitutionCheck results are silently dropped. A maintainer adding the real pass logic would need to discover this by reading the switch statement carefully.

Suggested fix: add a TODO with a bead ID pointing at the real pass wiring work, and add a comment noting the unhandled PassConstitutionCheck (or add a case for it that returns nil).
title: runAnalyticalPasses godoc says 'placeholder data' but does not indicate when real implementation arrives

description:
SupersedeRequest has a 'reason' field (field 3) that is documented as 'Human-readable explanation of why this spec is being superseded.' Unlike AmendRequest.reason which is validated as required (line 11443-11444 in diff), the Supersede handler passes req.Msg.Reason to store.SupersedeSpec without any presence check — an empty reason string is silently accepted.

The Amend RPC treats reason as required (returns INVALID_ARGUMENT if empty) because audit trails are important for backward spec movements. Supersession is an equally important lifecycle event (irreversible relationship between specs) and an empty reason provides no audit value.

The inconsistency creates a confusing API contract: one rollback operation requires a reason, the other does not.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Supersede handler (lines 11472-11495 in diff)
Proto: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, SupersedeRequest message (field 3)

Fix: Either (a) validate reason as required in Supersede (matching Amend behavior), or (b) explicitly document in the proto comment that reason is optional for Supersede and update the Amend proto to note it is required. Consistency is the key concern.
title: SupersedeRequest.reason field is optional in proto but passes empty string to storage with no validation

close_reason: Duplicate of prior closure spgr-s34.62. Known design intent — PassWriter methods are scaffolding.
description: The storage.PassWriter interface (internal/storage/authoring.go lines 12872-12879) declares StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, and StoreConstitutionViolations. These methods are implemented in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go. However, the authoring_handler.go never calls any of these methods — runAnalyticalPasses returns placeholder data that is only put into the RPC response, never written to storage. The comment on runAnalyticalPasses (line 11733-11734) says 'real LLM-driven pass execution is deferred to a later slice' but there is no tracking bead or TODO linking to a future slice. The PR body claims 'AuthoringBackend interface with 13 methods + Memgraph impl' as a shipped feature but 6 of those 13 methods (all PassWriter methods) are never called from the handler. This gap should be explicitly documented as a known incomplete wiring with a tracking reference. Location: internal/server/authoring_handler.go runAnalyticalPasses function.
title: PassWriter storage interface entirely unused — analytical pass results never persisted

description: internal/authoring/posture.go lines 9199-9226: DetectPosture and ResolvePosture both return specv1.Posture and accept specv1.Posture as parameters. This couples the domain logic layer directly to the generated proto package. CLAUDE.md and the project convention explicitly require domain types in internal packages, not proto types. The authoring package should define its own Posture domain type (e.g. type Posture int or type Posture string) and expose translation functions at the boundary, the same pattern already used by Stage/AuthoringStage, FindingSeverity, SafetyCategory, etc. Current design means any caller that wants to use DetectPosture must import the generated proto package, defeating the layering goal. Suggested fix: define type Posture int with constants PostureDrive, PosturePartner, PostureSupport, PostureUnspecified in authoring package; change DetectPosture signature to return authoring.Posture; add PostureToProto translation map analogous to stageToProtoEnum.
title: posture.go exposes proto type in domain function signatures

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 11200-11204: The Spark handler constructs its response without calling runAnalyticalPasses. passRegistry[StageSpark] defines PassConstitutionCheck as auto-running for all postures (passes.go line 8994), but the Spark handler never invokes runAnalyticalPasses. As a result, SparkResponse.constitution_violations (proto field 3 in SparkResponse) is always nil regardless of posture, and the constitution check designed for the spark stage never fires. Compare with Shape (line 11267), Specify (line 11329), and Decompose (line 11397) which all call runAnalyticalPasses. The fix is to add a runAnalyticalPasses call in Spark and populate constitution_violations in the response.
title: Spark handler does not call runAnalyticalPasses — StageSpark constitution check never runs

description:
The Spark, Shape, Specify, and Decompose handlers all return the client-supplied output field verbatim (msg.Output) rather than reading back the persisted value. The handlers include a comment noting this is intentional: 'Output is returned as-is from the client request ... has not been enriched server-side.'

The API contract implied by returning a SparkResponse.output / ShapeResponse.output is that the response reflects the authoritative server-side state. If future slices add server-side enrichment (normalisation, default injection, LLM enrichment), the handlers will need to be changed to do a read-back, but clients written against the current contract may already assume the response is the ground truth.

This is a COMPATIBLE change now, but the implicit contract could become a BREAKING change in a future slice if: (1) the server starts mutating or enriching output, and (2) clients rely on the response rather than a separate GetSpec call to obtain the stored state.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go — Spark (~11200), Shape (~11268), Specify (~11330), Decompose (~11398)

Suggestion: Either (a) document in the proto response message comments that output reflects the client-submitted value unmodified and may not reflect server enrichment, or (b) plan a read-back pattern from the start so the contract is consistent when enrichment is added. Option (a) is lower cost now.
title: Spark response echoes client output verbatim without read-back, creating stale-data contract risk

description:
FindingSeverity exists in three incompatible representations that must stay in sync manually:
1. Proto enum FindingSeverity (authoring.proto): FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED=0, _CRITICAL=1, _WARNING=2, _NOTE=3
2. authoring.FindingSeverity (internal/authoring/safety.go): int-based with iota constants for comparison
3. storage.FindingSeverity (internal/storage/authoring.go): string type with values 'critical', 'warning', 'note'

Bridge function ToStorageSeverity() converts (2) to (3). Proto-to-domain mapping happens in SafetyResultsToProto. There is no compile-time enforcement that these three representations stay in sync — if a new severity level is added to the proto enum, both the int constants and the string constants must be updated separately.

This is deferred per prior bead comment (spgr-s34.67) but is worth flagging as a proto API design concern: the proto enum is the external contract; adding a new severity value later is additive, but callers expecting the current three values in storage JSON will receive an unexpected string.

File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto (FindingSeverity enum), internal/storage/authoring.go (FindingSeverity type), internal/authoring/safety.go (FindingSeverity type)

Suggestion: Add a comment to the proto enum documenting that the string storage representations are 'critical'/'warning'/'note' so the mapping is documented at the contract level, not just in implementation.
title: FindingSeverity defined as both int-based enum in proto and string type in storage with no shared canonical representation

description:
internal/authoring/prompts.go: The godoc for GetPrompts reads 'Returns nil if the stage is not found.' This is accurate for the library function itself.

However, the GetPrompts RPC handler in internal/server/authoring_handler.go at line ~11507-11508 calls PromptsToProto (not GetPrompts) and treats an empty/nil result as a CodeInvalidArgument error with message 'no prompts defined for stage %q'. For the approved stage, PromptsToProto returns nil (by design — no prompts after approval), but the handler converts this to an error rather than a successful empty response.

The discrepancy is: the library comment says nil is the normal 'not found' signal, but the handler treats nil as an error. A caller reading the GetPrompts godoc expecting nil to be a benign return would be surprised to receive an RPC error. The handler comment should document that it rejects the approved stage via this path, and the GetPrompts godoc should note that nil is the signal for 'no prompts defined' (which callers should interpret per their context).
title: GetPrompts godoc says 'Returns nil if stage not found' but handler returns CodeInvalidArgument instead

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go: The godoc on TransitionStage (lines ~12934-12939 in the diff) reads 'Returns ErrSpecAlreadyApproved if from is the approved stage.' This documents the early guard at line 12941.

However, the check fires when from == approved, regardless of to. So if a caller passes from=approved, to=decompose (an amend-style call), they still get ErrSpecAlreadyApproved, not ErrInvalidStageTransition. The godoc is accurate but the rationale is missing: why does TransitionStage reject approved→anything rather than letting ValidateTransition handle it? The approved stage is terminal, so the early return is intentional, but a future developer adding a 'reopen approved spec' feature would hit this guard without understanding why it exists separately from the general ValidateTransition logic.

Suggested fix: add a sentence explaining that approved is the terminal stage and cannot be used as a from-stage (as opposed to AmendSpec, which has its own guard).
title: TransitionStage godoc does not document the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved early return

description: internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph.go: The newly added nowRFC3339() helper function has no godoc comment. While it is package-private, the project has a consistent convention of documenting even unexported functions in this package (e.g., txToContext, txFromContext, parseRFC3339, generateID, and fileExists all have comments). Missing the comment here is a minor inconsistency, but the function's purpose is not entirely obvious: it exists to consolidate time formatting and ensure UTC is always used. A one-line comment like '// nowRFC3339 returns the current UTC time formatted as RFC3339.' would match the surrounding convention.
title: nowRFC3339 helper missing godoc

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go, acceptLinkedDecisions (lines ~11532-11542 in the diff):

The inline comment reads 'INFORMS edges: Decision → Spec. ListEdges returns both directions, so the decision slug is the from_id when it differs from the spec slug.'

This comment is partially misleading. The code that follows does NOT simply use from_id as the decision slug — it first checks if from_id equals slug (the spec), and if so, falls back to to_id. This two-step fallback exists because the edge direction in the graph may be Spec → Decision or Decision → Spec depending on how the edge was stored, and ListEdges may return the edge from either perspective.

The comment says 'ListEdges returns both directions' which implies both orderings of the same edge are returned, but what actually happens is that either direction may be stored, so either node may be the decision. The comment should describe the actual ambiguity: 'The edge direction may be Decision→Spec or Spec→Decision depending on how it was created. Check both from_id and to_id to find the non-spec node.'
title: acceptLinkedDecisions INFORMS edge direction comment is partially incorrect

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go: The comment after the Spark transaction block (lines ~11196-11199) reads: 'Output is returned as-is from the client request. The storage layer stores domain-typed stage outputs (via StoreSparkOutput) but does not provide a proto-typed round-trip getter. This is acceptable because the output was just persisted in the same request and has not been enriched server-side.'

The same comment is cross-referenced by Shape, Specify, and Decompose handlers via 'See Spark handler comment.' All four handlers rely on this being true.

The comment documents a current limitation (no proto-typed read-back) and a correctness argument (output is fresh from the same request). If a future slice adds server-side enrichment to any stage output (e.g., AI-generated annotations), returning msg.Output as-is would be silently wrong. The comment should either: (a) reference the bead/slice that will add enrichment so reviewers know to revisit it, or (b) be written as a TODO that prompts future developers to reconsider when enrichment is added.
title: Spark handler comment about no round-trip getter will become stale

description: Replace proto Posture/FindingSeverity with domain types in passes.go, posture.go, safety.go. Fix SafetyCategory zero value.
title: Fix(.7,.4,.42,.28): Replace proto types with domain types in passes, posture, safety

title: Fix(.11): Consolidate Stage/AuthoringStage parallel types

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go: none of the 8 AuthoringService RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) perform any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated caller can create specs, approve them, amend them, or supersede them.

Specific impact:
- Spark: unauthenticated spec creation (line 11150)
- Approve: unauthenticated irreversible approval (line 11411) — also silently accepts linked decisions in the graph
- Supersede: unauthenticated marking of specs as superseded (line 11472)
- Amend: unauthenticated rollback of approved specs is blocked by storage, but all pre-approved stages can be freely amended

RegisterAuthoringService (line 11566) registers on a plain http.ServeMux with no middleware intercept.

The previous review bead spgr-s34.2 was marked 'Deferred: auth/authz not in slice 3 scope' and spgr-34l.2 was closed as out of scope. This finding is re-raised as a critical tracking item since the service is registered and listening with no access control. Even if auth is deferred, this should be tracked as a known risk with a follow-on ticket and ideally a TODO comment in RegisterAuthoringService.

Suggested remediation path: add a ConnectRPC interceptor for token/session validation before slice 4, or at minimum document the unauthenticated surface explicitly in the server registration code and block external exposure via network policy until auth is implemented.
title: All AuthoringService RPCs lack authentication and authorization checks

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go at line 11606, sparkOutputToDomain uses a map lookup on scopeSniffToStorage[p.GetScopeSniff()]. If the proto enum value is SCOPE_SNIFF_UNSPECIFIED or any future unknown value, the map lookup silently returns the zero value (empty string ""). The resulting storage.SparkOutput.ScopeSniff is stored as "" with no warning, no error, and no indication that the field was dropped. This is a silent data loss pattern: the caller believes the scope sniff was persisted, but it was silently discarded. Fix: add an explicit SCOPE_SNIFF_UNSPECIFIED mapping (e.g. to "unspecified") or return an error when the value is unrecognized, similar to how decomposeStrategyMap lookup returns an error at line 11660.
title: scopeSniffToStorage silent zero-value: unrecognized ScopeSniff enum stored as empty string without warning
