description: internal/authoring/passes.go lines 8976-9036: passConfig.autoIn and offeredIn are []specv1.Posture, and PassesForStage/OfferedPasses accept specv1.Posture. This is the same layering violation as posture.go — the pass scheduling registry, which is pure domain logic, is coupled to proto types. If the generated proto enum values or names change, the registry must change too. This also means the allPostures convenience slice (line 8983) must track proto enum values, not domain values. Fix: introduce domain authoring.Posture type (see related finding) and use it throughout passes.go and posture.go. The translation to proto happens only at the RPC handler boundary.
title: passes.go passConfig uses proto Posture in scheduling data structures

close_reason: Borderline: Scanner being removed in Slice 3.5. Test assertion style moot.
description: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go lines 12575-12680: Tests for StoreSafetyFlags errors (TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_StoreSafetyFlagsError, TestAuthoringHandler_Shape_StoreSafetyFlagsError, TestAuthoringHandler_Specify_StoreSafetyFlagsError, TestAuthoringHandler_Decompose_StoreSafetyFlagsError) all use a conditional pattern: 'if err \!= nil { assert code }'. The test comment at line 12583 explains that if no safety flags are raised for the input text, the error path is not hit. This means these tests provide essentially zero coverage of the StoreSafetyFlags error path for benign inputs. The fix is to use inputs known to trigger safety flags (e.g. 'hardcoded secret in config' or 'drop all tables') so that the storeSafetyFlagsErr is always exercised. This is a test quality issue — the tests should be deterministic, not conditional on safety pattern behavior.
title: StoreSafetyFlags error tests use conditional assertions — error path is not guaranteed to be exercised

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go at line 11430, Approve() calls h.acceptLinkedDecisions(ctx, req.Msg.Slug) and immediately discards the result — the function has no return value. The comment (line 11516-11518) explicitly says this is best-effort. However, this means: (1) callers can never tell whether linked decisions were accepted; (2) there is no retry mechanism; (3) the Approve RPC response gives no indication that N decisions were expected to be accepted but failed. In a distributed system this creates a persistent inconsistency: a spec is 'approved' while its linked decisions remain 'proposed' indefinitely. The log warnings are buried in server logs with no surface to the RPC client. Suggested fix: return accepted/failed decision counts in ApproveResponse (even as informational fields), or at minimum emit a structured log event with decision-count context so monitoring can alert on persistent failures.
title: acceptLinkedDecisions fire-and-forget: decision acceptance failures invisible to callers and unretryable

description:
## Decision

The Principle proto message uses `statement` (field 2) instead of `principle` as specified in the Slice 2 plan doc.

## Rationale

- Avoids tautology of `Principle.principle`
- More descriptive: the field holds the principle's statement text
- Consistent with YAML struct `ConstitutionPrinciple.Statement`
- Already propagated correctly through all layers (emitter, CLI show, YAML bootstrap)

## Alternatives Considered

- **Keep 'principle' per plan**: Rejected — tautological naming (`Principle.principle`) is confusing and the plan is a design input, not a binding contract
- **Rename to 'text'**: Rejected — too generic, `statement` better conveys the field's purpose

## Affects

- spgr-gk1.99 (PR #7 review finding about field name divergence)
- proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto — Principle message
- internal/config/config.go — ConstitutionPrinciple.Statement
title: Use 'statement' instead of 'principle' for Principle proto field

description: No auth/authz exists on AuthoringService endpoints. Phase 2+ concern deferred from PR #8 review.
title: Add authentication/authorization to AuthoringService RPCs

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8 adding authoring funnel: proto, stages, posture, prompts, safety net, scanner, storage, handler, CLI
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

close_reason: Out of scope: no existing service has auth/authz. Not in any slice plan — systemic concern for a future security hardening effort.
description:
All eight RPCs in AuthoringService (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are registered on an unauthenticated HTTP mux with no middleware, interceptor, or token check of any kind.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (entire file — no auth checks present)
File: cmd/specgraph/serve.go:85 — RegisterAuthoringService(mux, store, store) with no auth wrapper

The Approve and Amend RPCs are particularly sensitive: any unauthenticated network client can approve or roll back any spec. Because ConnectRPC is plain HTTP/2 over HTTP (not HTTPS) by default (serve.go:87 uses http.Server with no TLS), traffic is also unencrypted in transit.

Suggested fix: Add a ConnectRPC interceptor (connect.UnaryInterceptorFunc) that validates a bearer token or mTLS certificate before dispatching any RPC. Register the interceptor in RegisterAuthoringService. At minimum, document the assumption that the service runs behind a trusted reverse proxy and enforce it with a network policy.
title: No authentication or authorization on any AuthoringService RPC endpoint

description:
The handler tests cover only empty-slug validation for Shape, Specify, Decompose, and Approve. No happy path test exists for any of these four RPCs.

Missing coverage:
- Shape happy path: verify response echoes output, safety flags are returned, next prompts are StageSpecify prompts
- Specify happy path: verify response echoes output, safety flags populated from invariants/interface_contract
- Decompose happy path: verify response echoes output (StoreDecomposeOutput fake always returns nil, never exercised with actual slices)
- Approve happy path: verify response contains slug, stage=APPROVED, and a non-nil approved_at timestamp

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go
Compare: TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_HappyPath exists (line 160) but no equivalent for the other four stages.

A refactor to the safety flag wiring in Shape or Specify (e.g., accidentally passing the wrong field) would produce no failure in the current test suite.
title: Shape/Specify/Decompose/Approve happy paths not tested in handler

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 97-98.

  for i, sl := range output.Slices {
      childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%d", slug, i)

Each DecompositionSlice has an Id field (proto field id, line 133 of authoring.proto) intended as a 'stable identifier for this slice.' However, the implementation ignores sl.Id entirely and generates the child slug as parent-slug/0, parent-slug/1, etc.

This creates a semantic mismatch: the proto contract says Id is the stable identifier, but the stored slug is a positional integer. If slices are reordered or one is deleted from the middle of the list in a subsequent call, the positional indices shift, creating wrong COMPOSES edges and orphaned nodes.

The authoring_test.go TestStoreDecomposeOutput test on line 87-88 asserts:
  require.Equal(t, "slice-1", children[0].Slug)
  require.Equal(t, "slice-2", children[1].Slug)
This test will FAIL because the actual slugs would be 'decomp-parent/0' and 'decomp-parent/1', not 'slice-1' and 'slice-2'.

Suggested fix: use sl.Id (if non-empty) as the child slug suffix, e.g.:
  id := sl.Id
  if id == "" {
      id = fmt.Sprintf("%d", i)
  }
  childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, id)
title: StoreDecomposeOutput discards DecompositionSlice.id and uses positional index as child slug

close_reason: Out of scope: single-user local dev tool. Concurrent stage transitions are not a realistic scenario. No multi-tenant access.
description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:42-73, TransitionStage uses two separate Bolt queries: (1) an atomic conditional SET (lines 47-55) that only updates if s.stage=$from, and (2) a diagnostic read query (lines 61-63) to distinguish 'spec not found' from 'wrong stage' when zero rows are matched. While the first query is itself atomic as a Cypher statement, the second query runs outside any transaction and observes a potentially different snapshot. If two concurrent callers race on the same slug (e.g., two concurrent Shape RPCs for the same spec), both may see zero records matched (because the first already moved the stage) and both will query the diagnostic check. This is low risk today (single-process server), but when horizontal scaling is added the race will cause spurious 'wrong stage' errors that should have been 'success'. More critically, if the diagnostic check itself fails (checkErr \!= nil, line 64), the error returned wraps checkErr only — it does not include the original slug or intent, making the root cause hard to diagnose. Suggested fix: Wrap both queries in a Bolt explicit transaction so the diagnostic read sees the same snapshot, and ensure the diagnostic error path includes slug context.
title: TransitionStage check-then-act race: two queries allow concurrent transitions to corrupt stage

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-5rq.4: same protobuf-in-storage pattern matches existing Backend interface.
description:
CLAUDE.md and the comment on AuthoringBackend itself (line 144) both state: 'Storage interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.' Two methods break this rule:

1. StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx, slug, *DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error) — returns []*specv1.Spec
2. AmendSpec(ctx, slug, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error) — returns *specv1.Spec

Files:
- internal/storage/authoring.go lines 150, 158
- internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go lines 92-136, 198-224

The existing Backend interface (internal/storage/storage.go) also returns *specv1.Spec from CreateSpec/GetSpec/ListSpecs/UpdateSpec, so this is a pre-existing convention inconsistency, but AuthoringBackend's own doc comment promises domain-only types and then breaks the promise on two of its 13 methods.

The core issue is that the storage layer should not depend on the wire format at all. A domain Spec type (or at least a lighter struct carrying just the fields used by callers) should be defined in the storage package and returned by both interfaces. This removes the proto import from storage/, makes mock implementations trivially portable, and keeps the authoring handler as the sole translation boundary.

Suggested fix: define a domain storage.Spec struct mirroring the fields that callers of StoreDecomposeOutput and AmendSpec actually consume (Slug, Stage, Version), and have both methods return that instead of *specv1.Spec. The authoring_handler.go mappers already do proto→domain conversion for inputs; they should do the same for outputs.
title: AuthoringBackend.StoreDecomposeOutput and AmendSpec return protobuf types, violating the storage interface convention

close_reason: Out of scope: no existing service validates slug format. Systemic concern, not Slice 3 specific.
description:
Every RPC handler checks slug != "" but applies no further validation. The slug is stored as a Memgraph node property and used in error messages returned to callers. An arbitrarily long slug could cause denial-of-service via memory allocation in error string construction, and a slug containing special characters (e.g., newlines, null bytes) could corrupt log output or cause unexpected behavior in downstream systems that use slugs as identifiers.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:32-34 (Spark), :61-63 (Shape), :93-95 (Specify), :122-124 (Decompose), :142-143 (Approve), :157-158 (Amend), :173-174 (Supersede)

The child slug at internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:98 is constructed as fmt.Sprintf("%s/%d", slug, i) — if slug is excessively long or contains adversarial characters, this propagates into graph node creation.

Suggested fix: Add a validateSlug helper that enforces: max length (e.g., 255 bytes), allowed character set (alphanumeric, hyphens, slashes for hierarchy), and rejects null bytes and control characters. Call it at the top of each handler before any storage interaction.
title: Slug field has no length or character validation, enabling oversized or special-character inputs

description:
The fakeAuthoringBackend.AmendSpec always returns (nil, amendErr). When amendErr is nil (happy path), the handler dereferences spec.Slug, spec.Stage, and spec.Version at lines 165-167 of authoring_handler.go, which would panic on a nil pointer.

No test exercises the Amend happy path. The test suite has TestAuthoringHandler_Amend_EmptySlug but no success case.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (line 219)
Handler: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 163-168)

If someone ran a happy-path Amend call against the fake today, the test would panic rather than producing an assertion failure, masking the real issue. The fake's amendResult field should be populated in a success test, and the response fields (Slug, Stage, Version) verified.
title: Amend handler: nil result from fake prevents response field verification

close_reason: Fix verified: build passes, tests pass
description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go:172-186, the Supersede handler validates that req.Msg.Slug is non-empty (line 173) but does NOT validate that req.Msg.SupersededBy is non-empty. If a client calls Supersede with an empty superseded_by field, the call propagates to SupersedeSpec (memgraph/authoring.go:169) which will attempt GetSpec with an empty slug. GetSpec with an empty slug will return ErrSpecNotFound (no MATCH), causing a CodeNotFound response — but the error message will be 'spec not found' with no indication that the caller passed an empty superseded_by. This surfaces as a confusing error that looks like a data problem rather than an input validation failure. Suggested fix: Add a validation check for req.Msg.SupersededBy == "" at line 174, returning CodeInvalidArgument with message 'superseded_by is required', matching the pattern used for slug validation.
title: Supersede handler does not validate superseded_by field, allowing silent empty-string edge creation

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 189-203; internal/authoring/prompts.go.

GetPrompts validates the requested stage against AllStages(), which includes 'approved' (all 5 stages). However promptRegistry in prompts.go only has entries for 'spark', 'shape', 'specify', and 'decompose' — 'approved' is absent.

When a client calls GetPrompts with stage='approved', the handler considers it valid (passes the AllStages loop), calls PromptsToProto('approved'), which returns nil (empty), and sends back a 200 OK with an empty Prompts list. This is potentially confusing: clients cannot distinguish 'stage has no prompts' from 'this is an unexpected state.'

The approved stage genuinely has no prompts, but this design means the client can't tell whether an empty list means 'done' or 'something went wrong.'

Suggested fix: Either (a) add an explicit check after validation — if the stage is 'approved', return a documented empty response with a comment, or (b) add a registry entry for 'approved' with an empty slice to make the intent explicit, or (c) treat 'approved' as an invalid stage for GetPrompts and return CodeInvalidArgument. Option (c) is least surprising for callers.
title: GetPrompts returns empty prompts for 'approved' stage without error, despite it being a valid stage

close_reason: Out of scope: SafetyFlagResult uses specv1.FindingSeverity consistently with how the rest of the project uses proto types in domain code.
description:
internal/authoring/safety.go lines 29-33:

  type SafetyFlagResult struct {
      Category    SafetyCategory
      Severity    specv1.FindingSeverity   // <-- proto enum
      Description string
  }

SafetyFlagResult is a pure domain type intended to shield internal logic from proto concerns. Using specv1.FindingSeverity directly re-couples it to the generated proto package. The storage package already defines a parallel FindingSeverity domain string type (internal/storage/authoring.go lines 83-91) with the same values (critical/warning/note).

RunSafetyNet (line 84) hard-codes specv1.FindingSeverity_FINDING_SEVERITY_CRITICAL — meaning the safety net can only ever emit one severity regardless of pattern type, and this can't be tested or changed without regenerating protos.

Suggested fix: Use the storage.FindingSeverity domain type (or define an equivalent in the authoring package to avoid a cross-package import in the wrong direction). SafetyResultsToProto in safety.go is already the correct conversion boundary; it just needs the domain Severity mapped there, not stored in the struct itself.
title: SafetyFlagResult embeds specv1.FindingSeverity, coupling a domain type to the protobuf wire format

description:
TestStoreDecomposeOutput in the integration test creates slices with DependsOn populated (slice-2 depends on slice-1), but the test only checks that the returned children have the expected slugs. It does not verify that the DEPENDS_ON edge was actually written to Memgraph.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (lines 77-88)
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (lines 121-133)

The DEPENDS_ON merge query runs in a loop (line 126) with no result validation. If the query silently matched zero nodes (e.g., dep slug does not match a real spec), no error is returned and the edge is simply not created. A test that queries for the DEPENDS_ON edge after StoreDecomposeOutput would catch this regression.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput: DEPENDS_ON edge creation not integration-tested

close_reason: Out of scope: all existing services use plaintext HTTP. TLS not in any slice plan.
description:
The server binds on plain HTTP with no TLS configuration, and the CLI client unconditionally constructs an http:// base URL when no explicit remote is configured.

File: cmd/specgraph/serve.go:87 — srv := &http.Server{Addr: addr, Handler: mux, ReadHeaderTimeout: 10*time.Second} — no TLS
File: cmd/specgraph/client.go:23 — fmt.Sprintf("http://%s:%d", cfg.Server.Host, cfg.Server.Port)

All spec content (seeds, interface contracts, invariants, constitution data) is transmitted in cleartext. This is a concern even for localhost deployments once remote config is used (serve.go:20 — cfg.Server.Remote can be set to a remote URL that is also constructed without enforcing HTTPS).

Suggested fix: Add TLS support to the server (tls.Config with certificate loading) and validate that Remote URLs use https:// in resolveBaseURL(). At minimum gate plaintext on an explicit --insecure flag.
title: HTTP (plaintext) transport used for both server listener and CLI client

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-5rq.8: same non-atomic write concern.
description:
The Spark handler (and Shape, Specify, Decompose handlers to a lesser degree) performs two separate storage writes without transactional atomicity. If CreateSpec succeeds but StoreSparkOutput fails, a spec node exists in the graph with no output data, and all subsequent Spark retries will fail with a duplicate slug error — permanently locking out that slug.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:40-49
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:67-75 (Shape)
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:99-107 (Specify)
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:128-136 (Decompose)

The TODO comments in the code acknowledge this (e.g., line 47: 'TODO: Add transaction support to make this atomic.'). From a security/integrity perspective, the partial-failure state is also a denial-of-service vector — a caller who observes the error can permanently claim a slug by exploiting the timing window, preventing legitimate future use of that slug.

Suggested fix: Implement Memgraph transaction support (the neo4j-go-driver supports explicit transactions via driver.NewSession + session.BeginTransaction). Wrap CreateSpec + StoreXOutput in a single transaction so both succeed or both roll back.
title: Non-atomic spec creation in Spark handler leaves orphaned nodes on partial failure
GetPrompts stage validation: replace manual loop with slices.Contains

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go:256-277, decomposeStrategyMap maps proto DecompositionStrategy enum values to domain DecompositionStrategy strings. The map does not include DECOMPOSITION_STRATEGY_UNSPECIFIED (proto enum value 0, defined in proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto:54). In Go, a missing map key returns the zero value — an empty string — for DecompositionStrategy. So if a client sends an unspecified strategy (the proto default), decomposeOutputToDomain returns a DecomposeOutput with Strategy="", which is then silently stored in the DB via storeJSONProperty. No error is returned. Downstream readers will encounter an unexpected empty strategy with no context about why. Suggested fix: Add a guard in decomposeOutputToDomain or in the Decompose handler that checks p.GetStrategy() != DECOMPOSITION_STRATEGY_UNSPECIFIED (or alternatively checks that the mapped value is non-empty) and returns CodeInvalidArgument if strategy is unspecified.
title: decomposeStrategyMap lookup silently maps unknown proto enum to empty string strategy

close_reason: Out of scope: string-based stage identifiers are the established project pattern. Type safety improvement would be systemic refactor.
description:
AuthoringBackend.TransitionStage (internal/storage/authoring.go line 146) and all 5 call sites in the handler (authoring_handler.go lines 67, 99, 128, 145, 160) pass raw string literals as from/to stage arguments:

  h.store.TransitionStage(ctx, msg.Slug, authoring.StageSpark, authoring.StageShape)

The constants (StageSpark, StageShape, etc.) exist in the authoring package, so callers do use them. However, the interface and implementing method both accept string — any arbitrary string can be passed without a compile error. ValidateTransition catches invalid strings at runtime, but the type system conveys no constraint to implementors or callers.

Two realistic impact scenarios:
1. A new storage backend implementor writes TransitionStage without reading the domain constants and uses hand-typed literals.
2. A CLI command reads a user-supplied stage string and passes it directly, bypassing the constant.

Suggested fix: Define a domain StageID type (type StageID string) in the authoring or storage package, make the stage constants that type, and update TransitionStage, ValidateTransition, and the stage mapping tables to use StageID. The proto boundary in authoring_handler.go converts proto enums to StageID via the protoToStage map, keeping the conversion isolated.
title: TransitionStage accepts bare strings for stage names with no compile-time validation
Amend handler passes zero-value proto stage to AmendSpec without validating it maps to a real stage

close_reason: Out of scope: safety net is best-effort heuristic. Case sensitivity testing is a hardening concern, not Slice 3 correctness.
description:
RunSafetyNet lowercases the combined input before matching (safety.go line 67: strings.ToLower), which correctly handles 'PLAINTEXT' and 'Plaintext'. This is good. However, there is no test confirming this behaviour - a refactor removing ToLower would not be caught.

Additionally, there is no test for inputs where a pattern appears in the Scope or Invariants fields simultaneously with a clean Intent, verifying multi-field combination detection beyond the existing TestSafetyNet_ScopeFlags and TestSafetyNet_InvariantsFlags.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/authoring/safety_test.go

Suggested test: input with Intent='PLAINTEXT credentials in config' (all-caps) should still flag as security/critical. This is low-effort and protects the ToLower contract explicitly.
title: Safety net: no test for case-variant or mixed-case bypass of security patterns

description:
File: internal/scanner/tier2.go:129

The default case of exprToString returns the literal string "unknown" for any unhandled ast.Expr type:

  default:
      return "unknown"

This silently swallows unrecognized AST node types (e.g. ast.ChanType, ast.FuncType) and produces misleading parameter type strings in scan results. A caller has no way to distinguish a legitimate type named 'unknown' from a parse failure.

Suggested fix — surface the actual Go type name for debugging:

  default:
      return fmt.Sprintf("<%T>", expr)

This preserves all current behavior for handled cases, makes future gaps obvious in output, and requires no additional imports (fmt is already imported). The '<' prefix ensures it cannot be confused with a real Go type name.
title: exprToString default case returns 'unknown' silently, masking unhandled AST node types

close_reason: Out of scope: same error echo pattern as all existing handlers. Not a Slice 3 regression.
description:
GetPrompts echoes the raw stage string from the request back into a connect error message that is returned to the caller.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:199 — fmt.Errorf("unknown stage %q", stage)

While the stage field is a string enum (not directly executable), echoing arbitrary caller-supplied input into error responses is a bad pattern. If stage contains a very long string or control characters, the error message will embed them verbatim in the HTTP response body. This is a low-severity variant of reflected injection.

Suggested fix: Replace the stage string in the error with a generic message: connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument, errors.New("invalid stage")). The valid stage list is small and known; the caller does not need to see their invalid input reflected back.
title: GetPrompts stage parameter echoed verbatim into error response

description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:230-260, storeJSONProperty calls marshalJSON(data). The marshalJSON function (constitution.go:178-184) uses json.Marshal which serializes a nil pointer as the string 'null'. So if any Store*Output method is called with a nil pointer (e.g., StoreSparkOutput(ctx, slug, nil)), the spec node gets its property set to the string 'null' with no error returned. The caller receives a success response. This is not currently triggered by the handler (all handlers create non-nil domain structs from proto getters), but it is a latent silent-failure path. For example, if a new code path calls StoreSparkOutput with a nil SparkOutput to 'clear' the field, the silent 'null' store could be mistaken for a successful write of empty data. Suggested fix: Add a nil-check at the top of storeJSONProperty (or in each Store* method) and return an error if data is nil, making the contract explicit.
title: storeJSONProperty silently stores 'null' when nil pointer data is passed

close_reason: Out of scope: symlink and parse-error edge cases are hardening, not Slice 3 correctness for a local dev tool.
description:
walk.go line 23 suppresses unreadable directory entries with nolint:nilerr and continues walking. Tier1 and Tier2 tests do not exercise two scenarios:

1. Unparseable .go file: the skipped-files collection path (walk.go line 44) is untested. A file with a syntax error should appear in result.SkippedFiles instead of failing the whole scan. This is a documented contract (SkippedFile.Reason) with no test.

2. Symlinks: filepath.WalkDir follows symlinks to directories on supported platforms. A symlink cycle or a symlink to a file outside the temp dir could cause unexpected behaviour. No test verifies the scanner handles symlinks safely.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/walk.go (lines 23, 43-44)
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/scanner/tier1_test.go

The SkippedFiles gap is higher priority: writing a malformed .go file in the test temp dir would verify the graceful-degradation contract explicitly.
title: Scanner: symlink behavior and parse-error skipping not tested

close_reason: Out of scope: all existing handlers pass raw errors to ConnectRPC. Systemic concern, not Slice 3 specific.
description:
Multiple error paths in authoring_handler.go wrap raw storage/driver errors directly into connect.NewError responses and return them to the caller. ConnectRPC propagates the error message string to the HTTP response body.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:42 — connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, err) where err is a raw neo4j/memgraph driver error
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:48 — fmt.Errorf("store spark output (spec %q created but output not stored): %w", msg.Slug, err) — leaks slug AND internal error chain
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:74, 106, 135 — same pattern for shape, specify, decompose
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:178-180 — Supersede passes raw storage error (which contains both slugs) as CodeInternal body
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:297-304 — stageError() wraps and re-wraps storage errors including internal stage/slug details

The stageError helper at line 71 returns ErrInvalidStageTransition errors containing the actual current stage name and the slug (from memgraph/authoring.go:71: fmt.Errorf("memgraph: spec %q at stage %v, expected %q: ...")). This lets unauthenticated callers probe the internal state of arbitrary specs.

Suggested fix: Sanitize CodeInternal errors — log the full error server-side, return only a generic message ("internal error") to the caller. Only propagate user-safe detail in CodeNotFound and CodeFailedPrecondition paths.
title: Internal storage error details leaked verbatim to RPC callers

close_reason: Out of scope: detectInfrastructure is pre-existing Slice 2 code, not changed by this PR.
description: In internal/scanner/scanner.go:132-158, detectInfrastructure calls filepath.WalkDir without capturing its return value, suppressed by //nolint:errcheck. Individually each callback-level error is handled (line 135 returns nil to skip unreadable entries), but a non-nil error from the walk itself (e.g., root directory does not exist, OS-level walk failure) is silently swallowed. The Scan function docs say 'error return is reserved for future use and is currently always nil', so this is consistent with the current design, but means that a scan of a non-existent or unreadable directory succeeds and returns an empty constitution with no indication of failure. If detectInfrastructure is ever called on a bad path, callers get a partial (empty) constitution with no signal. Suggested fix: Capture the WalkDir error and at minimum log it to stderr (matching the pattern used in detectFrameworks at lines 92-93 and 115-117), or promote it to the Scan return value.
title: scanner.go detectInfrastructure drops WalkDir return value with nolint:errcheck suppressing all walk errors

description:
File: internal/authoring/safety.go, lines 80-90.

The scanner concatenates all input fields (intent, scope, invariants) into a single lowercased string and runs strings.Contains for each pattern. Several patterns will produce high false-positive rates in practice:

1. 'exec(' — any spec mentioning 'exec()' in a Go context (e.g. 'calls os/exec(') triggers FINDING_SEVERITY_CRITICAL.
2. 'eval(' — any spec mentioning mathematical evaluation, browser eval API docs, or similar.
3. 'credential' — any spec about credential management, OAuth flows, or secrets vaults.
4. 'truncate' — database pagination specs mentioning TRUNCATE as an operation to avoid.
5. 'purge' — cache invalidation, message queue purge.

These all produce CRITICAL severity findings. Flagging 'implement OAuth credential storage in Vault' as a critical security finding will erode user trust in the tool quickly.

Additionally, the combined string approach means a pattern matched in an innocuous scope_in item cannot be attributed to the actual violating field.

Suggested improvements:
- Use word-boundary checks (regexp) rather than substring match for short/common terms.
- Report which field triggered the flag to help authors understand and dismiss false positives.
- Consider tiered severity: CRITICAL for high-confidence patterns (e.g. 'hardcoded password', 'rm -rf'), WARNING for ambiguous ones (e.g. 'credential', 'exec(').
- Track which pattern matched to avoid duplicate flags for the same category.
title: Safety net scanner produces false positives on substring matches in combined text

description:
internal/storage/authoring.go lines 69-75:

  type DecomposeSlice struct {
      ID        string   // e.g. 'my-spec/0'
      DependsOn []string // slice IDs — but which format? slugs? numeric indices?
      ...
  }

The memgraph implementation (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 98) ignores DecomposeSlice.ID entirely and generates its own slug from the parent slug and loop index:

  childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%d", slug, i)

This means a caller populating DecomposeSlice.ID gets silently ignored; the ID field is dead weight in the domain type. DependsOn entries are treated as spec slugs directly (line 127) but callers writing these IDs have no contract for which slug format to use.

This is an invariant that is enforced only through documentation (the TODO on the field name) — the type does not prevent creating a DecomposeOutput whose DependsOn references a non-existent or incorrectly-formatted ID.

Suggested fix: Either remove ID from the domain type (since the implementation drives slug generation from position), or honour it: validate that all DependsOn values are either relative (matching IDs within the same DecomposeOutput) or absolute (matching existing spec slugs), and fail fast in StoreDecomposeOutput if they are not.
title: DecomposeSlice.DependsOn and DecomposeOutput.Slices.ID use untyped strings for cross-slice references with no graph-consistency guarantee

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:76-82

The Shape handler pre-allocates a scope slice then appends both halves:

  scope := make([]string, 0, len(msg.Output.GetScopeIn())+len(msg.Output.GetScopeOut()))
  scope = append(scope, msg.Output.GetScopeIn()...)
  scope = append(scope, msg.Output.GetScopeOut()...)

This is functional but verbose for a one-time concat passed immediately to RunSafetyNet. The stdlib slices package (already imported elsewhere in the project) provides:

  scope := slices.Concat(msg.Output.GetScopeIn(), msg.Output.GetScopeOut())

Or, if slices.Concat is unavailable in the Go version in use, append is fine but the make can be inlined:

  scope := append(msg.Output.GetScopeIn(), msg.Output.GetScopeOut()...)

Note: the append-without-make form mutates the first slice's underlying array if it has capacity, so slices.Concat is the safest single-line option. Either way reduces 3 lines to 1 without any behavior change.
title: Shape handler builds scope slice with unnecessary make+two-append pattern

close_reason: Fix verified: build passes, tests pass
description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:198-223, AmendSpec calls authoring.ValidateTransition(spec.Stage, targetStage). ValidateTransition (internal/authoring/stages.go:31-65) allows both forward (toIdx == fromIdx+1) and backward (toIdx < fromIdx) transitions. This means AmendSpec — which is documented as 'rolling a spec back to an earlier stage' — will silently accept a forward transition (e.g., Amend on a spark-stage spec targeting shape). The Amend handler in authoring_handler.go (line 160) passes req.Msg.TargetStage directly to AmendSpec with no additional validation. An API caller can use Amend to advance a spec forward, bypassing the normal TransitionStage flow and skipping the associated StoreOutput data write, leaving the spec in a new stage with no output for that stage. Suggested fix: AmendSpec should call a backward-only validator or check that toIdx < fromIdx explicitly, returning ErrInvalidStageTransition if a forward target is supplied.
title: AmendSpec accepts forward transitions, bypassing the funnel's forward-only safety model

close_reason: Out of scope: promptRegistry immutability is a defensive pattern improvement, not a Slice 3 bug. The registry is only written at init time.
description:
internal/authoring/prompts.go lines 9-12:

  type Prompt struct {
      Name     string
      Template string
  }

Issues:

1. promptRegistry (line 15) is a package-level var — it is mutable from any code in the authoring package at test time or via init(). In Go, test files in the same package can reassign it, leading to test-order-dependent behavior. It should be a package-level const-like value (using a function that returns a copy, or a sync.Once-initialized map, or simply being defined as a constant data structure via a function call at init).

2. Prompt has no construction invariant. An empty Name or Template is silently valid. GetPrompts returns nil for unknown stages but does not distinguish 'unknown stage' from 'stage with no prompts'. PromptsToProto propagates that ambiguity to callers.

3. The stage key in promptRegistry is a raw string, not tied to the authoring stage constants (StageSpark, StageShape, etc.) — a typo in the registry key produces a silent no-op where GetPrompts returns nil.

Suggested fix: Define promptRegistry using the StageSpark/StageShape/etc. constants as keys (not string literals). Document the nil-return contract on GetPrompts, or return ([]Prompt, bool) to force callers to handle the unknown-stage case explicitly.
title: Prompt struct has no validation and promptRegistry is a package-level mutable map

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 121-132.

For each slice.DependsOn entry, the code issues a Cypher MATCH/MERGE:
  MATCH (from:Spec {slug: $from_slug}), (to:Spec {slug: $to_slug})
  MERGE (from)-[:DEPENDS_ON]->(to)

Cypher MATCH on both nodes before MERGE means: if $to_slug does not exist in the graph, the MATCH finds no rows and the MERGE is silently skipped. No error is returned. The caller receives a successful response with children, but the dependency edges are absent from the graph.

This is a silent data integrity violation: the graph will show decomp-parent/0 with no dependency on 'missing-dep', but the DecomposeOutput proto claimed it had one. Querying the graph later for execution order will give wrong results.

Note: the DependsOn field in the proto (authoring.proto line 142) contains 'Slice IDs within this spec' — so these should resolve against the sibling slices being created in the same call, but the code passes them to the dep query as-is without prefixing with the parent slug. This means DependsOn: ['slice-1'] will look for a top-level spec with slug 'slice-1' rather than 'parent-spec/slice-1', causing every depends_on edge to be silently dropped (MATCH fails).

Suggested fix:
1. Prefix dep slugs with the parent slug when looking them up: depSlug = fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, dep).
2. After the MERGE, check result.Records length; return an error if 0 (dep not found).
title: StoreDecomposeOutput creates DEPENDS_ON edges to arbitrary slug strings without existence validation

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-5rq.8: the atomicity comment concern is about the same transaction issue already closed.
description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 38-39

Comment: '// CreateSpec sets stage to "spark" as part of spec creation; no separate TransitionStage call is needed because the initial stage is set atomically.'

Issue: The comment is technically correct about CreateSpec being atomic, but misleading in context. The entire Spark() RPC operation is NOT atomic (CreateSpec succeeds, then StoreSparkOutput might fail - as documented in the NOTE below). The comment could lead developers to incorrectly assume the whole handler is atomic.

Suggested fix: Clarify that only the CreateSpec operation itself is atomic, and emphasize that the handler overall is not atomic (as already noted in the TODO/NOTE comments). Rephrase as:

'// CreateSpec atomically sets stage to "spark" as part of spec creation. No separate TransitionStage call is needed for this initial stage. Note: the full Spark handler is not atomic; see the TODO below.'

This maintains accuracy while preventing misunderstanding of the overall operation's atomicity guarantees.
title: Comment misleads about atomicity in Spark handler

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 79-82

Code: SafetyInput{ Intent: strings.Join(msg.Output.GetRisks(), ' '), Scope: scope }

Issue: The field name 'Intent' is semantically confusing. In Spark (line 50), Intent receives the seed (the actual intent). In Shape, Intent receives the risks. The SafetyInput type definition (internal/authoring/safety.go:14) calls this field 'Intent' but in Shape context it contains 'risks', not an intent statement.

Impact: This semantic mismatch could confuse developers reading Shape and Spark handlers about what data is being scanned. While the safety check works (it just looks for patterns in whatever text is provided), the field naming suggests it should contain the spec's intent, not its identified risks.

Suggested: Either (1) clarify with a comment why risks are used as Intent in Shape, or (2) reconsider the field organization to have more semantically meaningful names, or (3) document that SafetyInput.Intent accepts any text to be scanned, not necessarily the spec intent.
title: Shape handler uses risks as Intent in SafetyInput

description:
In proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, GetPromptsRequest.stage (field 1) is typed as string, while the semantically equivalent AmendRequest.target_stage (field 3 of AmendRequest) is typed as AuthoringStage enum. This inconsistency means:

1. GetPromptsRequest consumers can pass any arbitrary string (including invalid values), while AmendRequest consumers are type-safe at the protobuf level.
2. PromptTemplate.stage (field 1) is also string — so the type leaks through to the response schema.
3. The handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2032-2044) must manually validate the string against AllStages(), duplicating what the enum would enforce statically.

Suggested fix: Change GetPromptsRequest.stage to AuthoringStage enum type, and likewise PromptTemplate.stage. The handler can then remove the AllStages() loop and validate via enum != AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED.

File locations:
- proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 3243 (GetPromptsRequest.stage)
- proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 3084 (PromptTemplate.stage)
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2032-2044 (manual validation loop)
title: GetPromptsRequest.stage uses string instead of AuthoringStage enum, inconsistent with AmendRequest.target_stage

close_reason: Not a convention violation: existing Backend interface also uses *specv1.Spec. This is the established project pattern.
description:
CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'Storage interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.' The AuthoringBackend interface in internal/storage/authoring.go (lines 22-33) uses protobuf generated types throughout:

  StoreSparkOutput(ctx, slug, *specv1.SparkOutput) error
  StoreShapeOutput(ctx, slug, *specv1.ShapeOutput) error
  StoreSpecifyOutput(ctx, slug, *specv1.SpecifyOutput) error
  StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx, slug, *specv1.DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error)
  StoreRedTeamFindings(ctx, slug, []*specv1.RedTeamFinding) error
  ... (all 8 Store* methods)
  AmendSpec(ctx, slug, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error)

The return type *specv1.Spec and all *specv1.* input types are generated protobuf types. The existing Backend interface (storage.Backend) also uses *specv1.Spec but that was established prior to this PR — this PR extends the pattern to a new interface, entrenching the violation.

Suggested fix: Define domain structs (e.g. internal/authoring/domain.go) for SparkOutput, ShapeOutput, etc., and have the storage interface accept those. The handler layer maps proto → domain before calling storage, and domain → proto before returning responses. This matches the project's stated convention and decouples storage from the wire format.
title: AuthoringBackend storage interface uses protobuf types, violating project convention

description:
Files: cmd/specgraph/shape.go line 32, cmd/specgraph/specify.go line 31, cmd/specgraph/decompose.go line 31.

The shape, specify, and decompose commands hardcode an empty output struct:
  Output: &specv1.ShapeOutput{}      // shape.go
  Output: &specv1.SpecifyOutput{}    // specify.go
  Output: &specv1.DecomposeOutput{}  // decompose.go

Compare with spark.go which provides a --seed flag (line 25). The shape/specify/decompose commands have no flags to supply any content, making them only useful as 'advance the stage' triggers that store empty data. This means the authoring funnel stores empty spec data for all stages except spark, which defeats the purpose of structured authoring.

The spark command only exposes the 'seed' field via --seed and ignores signal, questions, scope_sniff, and kill_test.

These commands appear to be scaffolding rather than complete implementations, which is fine for an early slice, but they are being added to the command tree and will be exercised by users.

Suggested fix: Either add --json-file / --json flags that accept a JSON payload for the output struct, or add individual flags for key fields, or add a clear 'TODO' notice in the command help text. At minimum, add a note in the command's Short description that content flags are forthcoming.
title: CLI shape/specify/decompose commands always send empty output structs with no flags to populate content

close_reason: Out of scope: scanner result types use []string for simplicity matching the Slice 3 plan. Structural type information is a future enhancement.
description:
internal/scanner/tier1.go lines 19-31, internal/scanner/tier2.go lines 14-21:

  type InterfaceInfo struct { Methods []string }
  type StructInfo    struct { Fields  []string }
  type FunctionInfo  struct { Params  []string }  // e.g. 'ctx context.Context'

All three degrade structural data into flat string lists:
- InterfaceInfo.Methods loses method signatures (only names kept)
- StructInfo.Fields loses field types (only names kept, and only exported/named fields per line 99-103)
- FunctionInfo.Params serialises name+type into a single string ('ctx context.Context') rather than keeping them as separate fields

This makes downstream consumers (e.g. a spec authoring assistant that needs to reason about types, not just names) unable to distinguish 'method that takes a string' from 'method that takes a *Spec'. The information is available in the AST at parse time and simply not preserved.

The issue is mild for Tier 1 (structural inventory) but more significant for Tier 2 (FunctionInfo is the primary value output of tier2.go and its Params are already partially reconstructed via exprToString).

Suggested fix: For InterfaceInfo, add a MethodSignatures []string field (or a MethodInfo sub-struct) that captures the full signature text. For FunctionInfo, split Param into a ParamInfo{Name, TypeStr string} struct. This doesn't require changing the scan logic — exprToString already produces the type string.
title: Scanner result types (InterfaceInfo, StructInfo, FunctionInfo) use []string for method/field/param lists, losing structural information

close_reason: Out of scope: the TODOs document a known systemic limitation. Transaction support not in any slice plan.
description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 45-47, 71-73, 103-105, 132-134

Four identical TODO comments state: '// TODO: Add transaction support to make this atomic.'

These TODOs appear in every stage transition handler (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) where a stage transition is followed by storing output. They document a known architectural limitation but lack tracking.

Issues:
1. No GitHub issue reference - unclear if this is tracked
2. No ticket number - hard to find related work
3. Repeated verbatim - should be DRY'd up with a reference to a central issue
4. Severity unknown - could be critical for data consistency but appears deprioritized

Suggested fix: Create a GitHub issue for 'Add transaction support to AuthoringService handlers' and reference it in all four locations as: '// TODO(#NNN): Add transaction support to make this atomic.' This makes tracking and prioritization explicit.
title: Multiple untracked TODOs for transaction support

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-5rq.24: same Amend zero-value stage issue.
description:
protoToStage in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 2065-2071) does not include a mapping for AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED. When a client sends AmendRequest with target_stage omitted or set to 0, protoToStage[AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED] returns the Go zero value for string (""), which is then passed to AmendSpec as targetStage.

In ValidateTransition (internal/authoring/stages.go), an empty to string is caught by 'if toIdx < 0 { unknowns = append(unknowns, to) }', which produces the error 'unknown stage(s): []' — the empty string renders as nothing inside the brackets, yielding a confusing and unhelpful error message for clients.

This also means ErrSpecAlreadyApproved defined at internal/storage/authoring.go:2390 is declared but never used or checked anywhere in the handler or storage implementation, suggesting incomplete contract enforcement for the Amend operation.

Suggested fixes:
1. Add explicit validation in the Amend handler: if req.Msg.TargetStage == specv1.AuthoringStage_AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED { return CodeInvalidArgument error }
2. Either use ErrSpecAlreadyApproved in AmendSpec logic or remove it to avoid dead code in the public API contract.

File locations:
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2065-2071 (protoToStage map, missing UNSPECIFIED)
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 1998-2011 (Amend handler, no UNSPECIFIED guard)
- internal/storage/authoring.go line 2390 (ErrSpecAlreadyApproved declared but unused)
title: AmendRequest with AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED target_stage produces confusing error via protoToStage zero-value

description:
File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, lines 57-58

Current comment: '// Split work by architectural layer (e.g. storage, API, UI).'

Issue: The comment provides examples but they're vague. 'Storage, API, UI' are very broad and don't clearly convey the layering concept. The examples could be misinterpreted as service boundaries rather than architectural layers.

Suggested clarification: '// Split work by architectural layer (e.g., database schema, API endpoints, client UI). Each layer is completed before the next begins.'

This better clarifies that LAYER_CAKE is sequential (unlike VERTICAL_SLICE which is cross-layer), and uses more concrete layer terminology.
title: DecompositionStrategy LAYER_CAKE comment lacks examples

description:
The proto definition at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto declares DecomposeResponse with three analytical fields:
- repeated SimplicityFinding simplicity (field 2)
- repeated SafetyFlag safety_flags (field 3)
- repeated ConstitutionViolation constitution_violations (field 4)

However, the Decompose handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 1979 constructs the response as:
  return connect.NewResponse(&specv1.DecomposeResponse{Output: msg.Output}), nil

The simplicity, safety_flags, and constitution_violations fields are always empty. Contrast with other stage handlers (Spark, Shape, Specify) which do populate safety_flags and other analytical fields.

The passes.go configuration (internal/authoring/passes.go line 379) shows a simplicity_check pass is defined for decompose stage, so the infrastructure exists but is not wired into the handler.

This creates a contract expectation gap: clients inspecting DecomposeResponse will always receive empty repeated fields even though the service definition implies they would be populated.

Suggested fix: Wire the simplicity pass and safety net into the Decompose handler, consistent with how other stage handlers are implemented.

File locations:
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 1961-1980 (Decompose handler)
- proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 3175-3185 (DecomposeResponse definition)
title: DecomposeResponse declares simplicity, safety_flags, constitution_violations fields but handler never populates them

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 226-229

Current comment: '// storeJSONProperty marshals data to JSON and stores it as a string property on the spec node. The property name is interpolated into the Cypher query via fmt.Sprintf because Memgraph does not support parameterized property names. The allowlist and character validation provide defense-in-depth against Cypher injection.'

Issue: While the comment explains WHY interpolation is used, it could be clearer about the alternative considered. The phrase 'Memgraph does not support parameterized property names' suggests this is a Memgraph-specific limitation, but it's actually a Cypher/Neo4j protocol limitation that Memgraph inherits.

Suggested enhancement: '// storeJSONProperty marshals data to JSON and stores it as a string property on the spec node. Property names must be interpolated into the Cypher query (parameterized property names are not supported by Cypher). The allowlist and character validation provide defense-in-depth against Cypher injection.'

This clarifies the limitation is Cypher-level, not Memgraph-specific.
title: storeJSONProperty documentation missing clarification on Memgraph limitation

close_reason: Out of scope: safety net is a best-effort heuristic for flagging spec text, not a security boundary. Unicode bypass is not a meaningful attack vector for a spec authoring tool.
description:
RunSafetyNet in internal/authoring/safety.go lowercases the combined input and checks for substring matches. This approach has several bypass vectors:

1. Unicode homoglyphs / non-ASCII lookalikes: e.g. 'hardcoded pаssword' (Cyrillic 'a') passes through strings.ToLower undetected because strings.ToLower is not Unicode-normalizing.
2. Embedded whitespace or zero-width characters between pattern tokens: 'drop\u200btable' evades 'drop table'.
3. Leet-speak / character substitution: 'h4rdcoded password' evades 'hardcoded password'.
4. The pattern list itself is incomplete for injection scenarios: 'system(', 'os.exec', 'shell_exec', 'subprocess', 'popen' are not covered; only 'exec(' and 'eval(' are present.
5. Strings scanned are limited to Intent, Scope, and Invariants (safety.go:63-66). The full output structures (interface contracts, risk descriptions, verify criteria, decisions) that flow through Shape/Specify/Decompose are not passed to the safety net in the respective handler paths — only a subset of fields is forwarded (authoring_handler.go:79-82, 108-111).

File: internal/authoring/safety.go:35-59 (pattern lists)
File: internal/authoring/safety.go:62-93 (RunSafetyNet implementation)
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:79-82, 108-111 (partial field forwarding)

Suggested fix: Apply Unicode normalization (golang.org/x/text/unicode/norm) before matching. Use regex with word-boundary anchors. Expand coverage to all output fields. Consider this a warning layer, not a gate — enforcement should happen at the LLM layer or policy enforcement point.
title: Safety net pattern matching is easily bypassed via Unicode, encoding, or spacing

description: Add tests for CreateSpec error and StoreSparkOutput error paths in the Spark handler. Exercise fakeBackend.createSpecErr and fakeAuthoringBackend.storeSparkOutputErr.
title: Fix(spgr-5rq.7): Add error propagation tests for Spark handler

description:
The Spark handler has a documented partial-failure window: CreateSpec can succeed but StoreSparkOutput can fail, leaving the spec created with no output data. Neither failure path is tested in the handler test.

Missing tests:
1. CreateSpec returns an error -> handler should return CodeInternal (fakeBackend.createSpecErr is defined but never exercised in a Spark test)
2. StoreSparkOutput returns an error -> handler should return CodeInternal with the 'spec created but output not stored' message (fakeAuthoringBackend.storeSparkOutputErr is defined but never exercised)

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/.worktrees/slice-3/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go

Without these tests, a regression that silently swallows a CreateSpec error (returning nil) would not be caught. The partial-failure case (spec exists, output missing) is the exact scenario the TODO comment calls out for future transaction support - it should be regression-protected now.
title: Handler error propagation from CreateSpec and StoreSparkOutput not tested

close_reason: Out of scope: transaction support requires systemic Memgraph driver changes across all storage methods. Acknowledged via TODO. Not Slice 3 specific.
description: In Spark handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go:40-49), CreateSpec succeeds and writes a spec node to the DB, then StoreSparkOutput is called. If StoreSparkOutput fails, the spec exists permanently in the 'spark' stage with no output data stored. Any retry of Spark for the same slug will fail with a duplicate-slug error from CreateSpec, leaving the spec permanently stuck and unrecoverable without manual intervention. The same two-step non-atomic pattern exists in Shape (line 67-74), Specify (line 99-106), and Decompose (line 128-135): TransitionStage succeeds and commits, then the Store*Output call may fail, leaving the spec at the new stage with no output data. The TODO comments acknowledge this but the failure mode is severe: retries are blocked because the stage transition already committed. Suggested fix: Either introduce a compensating rollback on Store*Output failure (re-create or delete the spec / reverse the transition), or add idempotency by checking for existing spec in CreateSpec and allowing upsert semantics so retries are safe. Until transactions are available, at minimum the error messages should explicitly tell the operator what manual repair is needed (e.g., 'delete spec X and retry, or call Amend to roll back to prior stage').
title: Non-atomic CreateSpec+StoreSparkOutput leaves spec in broken state on partial failure

close_reason: Out of scope: scanner runs on user's own local codebase. Path traversal is irrelevant for a local dev tool.
description:
filepath.WalkDir (used in walkGoFiles and detectInfrastructure) follows symbolic links to directories on the platforms where the Go stdlib does so. An attacker who controls a directory being scanned (e.g., a malicious repo passed to the CLI scanner) can place a symlink pointing to /etc, /home, or any other path outside the intended root. The scanner will then read, parse, and return information about those files.

File: internal/scanner/walk.go:21 — filepath.WalkDir(root, ...) with no symlink guard
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go:133 — filepath.WalkDir(dir, ...) in detectInfrastructure with no symlink guard

The ScanTier2 function at internal/scanner/tier2.go:46 also takes a caller-supplied dirs []string that is joined with filepath.Join(root, dir) without verifying the result stays within root — a dir value of "../../../etc" would resolve outside root.

File: internal/scanner/tier2.go:46 — absDir := filepath.Join(root, dir)

Suggested fix: (1) Use os.Lstat in the WalkDir callback and skip entries where d.Type()&fs.ModeSymlink != 0. (2) After filepath.Join(root, dir), verify the result with strings.HasPrefix(absDir, filepath.Clean(root)+string(os.PathSeparator)).
title: Scanner follows symlinks, allowing path traversal outside the scanned root

description:
The validateSlug function in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 3013-3024 of the diff) contains security-critical validation logic: it blocks empty slugs, path-traversal via '..', and invalid characters via regex. The handler test file (authoring_handler_test.go) only exercises the empty-slug case for each RPC. There are no tests for:

1. Path-traversal slugs such as '../etc/passwd' — expected to return CodeInvalidArgument
2. Slugs with invalid characters (e.g. 'my spec', 'My-Spec', 'a@b') — expected to return CodeInvalidArgument
3. Single-character slugs that fail the regex (the regex requires at least 2 chars: ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$)
4. Valid slugs containing path separators like 'org/feature' (should pass)

A regression in validateSlug (e.g. loosening the regex) would allow malformed or traversal slugs to reach storage without any test catching it. These are the most security-relevant cases in the entire PR.

Suggested fix: add table-driven tests in internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go covering all branches of validateSlug, or extract validateSlug to its own _test file and test it directly.
title: validateSlug: no tests for path-traversal and invalid-character cases

description:
The stageError helper in internal/server/authoring_handler.go maps three sentinel errors to Connect RPC codes:
- ErrSpecAlreadyApproved → CodeFailedPrecondition
- ErrInvalidStageTransition → CodeFailedPrecondition
- ErrSpecNotFound → CodeNotFound

The existing tests TestAuthoringHandler_StageError_InvalidTransition and TestAuthoringHandler_StageError_NotFound exercise two of these paths via the Shape handler. However, ErrSpecAlreadyApproved is never tested at the HTTP handler level.

The Approve handler (and TransitionStage) can return ErrSpecAlreadyApproved when attempting to transition from the 'approved' stage. If this mapping were accidentally removed or changed, callers would receive an unexpected code (e.g. CodeInternal instead of CodeFailedPrecondition), breaking clients that rely on structured error handling.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go

Suggested fix: Add a test that injects ErrSpecAlreadyApproved via fakeAuthoringBackend.transitionStageErr and asserts CodeFailedPrecondition is returned.
title: Approve handler: no test for ErrSpecAlreadyApproved error mapping

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines ~4345-4349

`storeJSONProperty` uses `fmt.Sprintf` to interpolate the property name directly into the Cypher query string:

    query := fmt.Sprintf(`
        MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
        SET s.%s = $data, s.updated_at = $updated_at
        RETURN s.slug
    `, property)

The code correctly mitigates this with: (1) an allowlist check via `slices.Contains(allowedJSONProperties, property)`, and (2) a character-level validation loop restricting to alphanumeric and underscore. Both controls are applied before the Sprintf. The comment in the code accurately explains why parameterization cannot be used for property names in Cypher.

However, the defence relies on call-site correctness — future callers who add new property names must remember both the allowlist and the character check. The Sprintf pattern is the kind of code that gets cargo-culted without those guards.

Suggestion: Document explicitly in the function signature or godoc that `property` must appear in `allowedJSONProperties` and that the allowlist is the security control. Consider moving allowlist validation to a compile-time check (e.g. a private type or iota) to make it impossible to call with an unapproved property name.
title: storeJSONProperty interpolates property names into Cypher via fmt.Sprintf

description:
The authoring funnel stages are defined as untyped string constants in internal/authoring/stages.go:9-15 (StageSpark, StageShape, etc.). The storage interface then defines storage.AuthoringStage as a distinct string type alias. This design forces repetitive explicit conversions at every storage callsite in internal/server/authoring_handler.go, for example:

  storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageSpark)
  storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageShape)

This pattern appears at least 10 times in authoring_handler.go lines 98, 109, 144, 155, 188, 199, 229, 392-396.

The authoring.StageXxx constants should be typed as storage.AuthoringStage directly (or the stage constants should be the canonical AuthoringStage values), eliminating the need for casting. Alternatively, authoring could expose typed constants:

  const (
    StageSpark     storage.AuthoringStage = 'spark'
    ...
  )

This would make invalid stage values a compile-time error and remove all the casting boilerplate.
title: Stage constants are untyped strings; AuthoringStage wrapper requires repetitive casting at every callsite

description: internal/scanner/walk.go:2954 and :2975 — Both nolint directives carry the comment 'intentional: skip unreadable entries; error recorded in skipped slice'. The first one (line 2954, the WalkDir error callback) is inaccurate: when WalkDir calls the callback with a non-nil err, the code returns nil without adding anything to the skipped slice. The error is silently dropped, not recorded. Only the second case (line 2975, parse failure) actually records the error in the skipped slice. Fix: change the comment on line 2954 to 'intentional: skip unreadable entries gracefully' (matching the pattern from scanner.go line 4418 in this same PR), and correct the second comment to accurately say the parse error IS recorded.
title: walkGoFiles nolint comment reason is inaccurate — error IS recorded in skipped slice

description:
cmd/specgraph/util.go introduces the generic loadJSONFile[T proto.Message] function used by shape, specify, decompose, and spark commands when --json-file is provided. This is a shared utility with three distinct failure modes:

1. File does not exist → os.ReadFile returns error
2. File exists but contains invalid JSON → protojson.Unmarshal returns error
3. File is valid JSON but the wrong schema for the target proto message type

None of these are tested in cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go. The existing tests only exercise cobra argument validation (flag registration and positional arg counts).

While the CLI commands themselves require a running server to fully test, loadJSONFile is a pure function that can be tested in isolation using os.CreateTemp and a proto message. A malformed --json-file silently passes cobra parsing and only fails at runtime when RunE is called.

Suggested fix: Add unit tests in cmd/specgraph/util_test.go (or similar) that:
- Create a temp file with invalid JSON and verify loadJSONFile returns an error containing 'parse'
- Create a temp file with valid JSON for a known proto message (e.g. SparkOutput) and verify successful unmarshal
- Pass a nonexistent path and verify the error contains 'read'
title: loadJSONFile (cmd/specgraph/util.go): no tests for malformed JSON or missing file

close_reason: Fixed proto comments in docs commit
title: SparkRequest.output field required by server but typed optional in proto

close_reason: Already handled: Supersede handler uses errors.Is which matches wrapped ErrSpecNotFound from memgraph. Test TestAuthoringHandler_Supersede_NotFound already covers this.
title: SupersedeSpec does not propagate ErrSpecNotFound from GetSpec validation

description:
storage.DecompositionStrategy (internal/storage/authoring.go:57) is a plain string alias with three well-known constants (vertical_slice, layer_cake, single_unit), but nothing prevents callers from storing arbitrary strings:

  output.Strategy = storage.DecompositionStrategy('anything_goes')

DecomposeOutput.Strategy is persisted directly as JSON to Memgraph with no validation layer. The handler does not validate that Strategy is one of the known constants before passing it to StoreDecomposeOutput.

Similarly storage.PeripheralDisposition (line 99) has the same issue.

Suggested fix: Add a Validate() method or a function IsValidStrategy(s DecompositionStrategy) bool, and call it in the handler before storing. Alternatively, make the type unexported and expose only the constants, or use an iota-based enum with a custom JSON marshaler.
title: DecompositionStrategy type has no validation; invalid string values accepted by storage interface

description:
Files: cmd/specgraph/util.go, cmd/specgraph/shape.go, cmd/specgraph/specify.go, cmd/specgraph/decompose.go

`loadJSONFile` reads an arbitrary path provided by the user via the `--json-file` CLI flag and unmarshals it as a proto message:

    func loadJSONFile[T proto.Message](path string, msg T) error {
        data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
        ...
        if err := protojson.Unmarshal(data, msg); err != nil {
        ...

There is no path restriction (e.g. no check that the path is within a permitted directory, no rejection of absolute paths or path traversal sequences). In a CLI tool where the user supplies the path directly, this is generally acceptable. However, if this utility function is later reused in a context where the path comes from a network request or config file, it becomes a path traversal risk.

Additionally, `protojson.Unmarshal` is used with default options, which by default discards unknown fields rather than rejecting them. This is not a security bug but means extra fields in the JSON file are silently ignored rather than flagged.

Suggestion: Add a comment that `path` must be user-supplied only (CLI context) and should not be used in server-side code paths. For the proto unmarshal, consider using `protojson.UnmarshalOptions{DiscardUnknown: false}` to surface unexpected input.
title: CLI --json-file flag accepts arbitrary filesystem paths without restriction

description: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:4323-4327 — The storeJSONProperty comment says 'The allowlist and character validation provide defense-in-depth against Cypher injection.' However the character validation loop at line 4335 is truly redundant: the allowlist check at line 4331 already guarantees the property is one of the 10 known safe strings. If the allowlist check is removed, the character validation would be a meaningful second layer, but as written it cannot trigger independently. The comment is accurate in spirit but misleading in practice because it implies both checks are load-bearing security layers when the character check is unreachable after a successful allowlist pass. Consider removing the character validation loop and simplifying the comment, or reversing the order (char validation first, then allowlist) so both layers are meaningful.
title: storeJSONProperty comment overstates defense-in-depth: allowlist alone is sufficient

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, line ~3253-3258

In the Supersede RPC handler, `req.Msg.Slug` is validated through `validateSlug()` which enforces the regex `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$` and rejects path traversal. However, `req.Msg.SupersededBy` only receives an empty-string check before being passed to `SupersedeSpec` and into a Cypher parameterized query as `$new_slug`.

Because Cypher parameterization prevents injection, this is not an immediate injection risk. However, it means callers can submit arbitrary strings (e.g. Unicode, control characters, or values that violate slug conventions) as the replacement spec identifier. These values propagate to the graph node lookup (`GetSpec(ctx, supersededBy)`) and appear unvalidated in the `SupersededBy` field of the response.

Suggested fix: apply `validateSlug(req.Msg.SupersededBy)` before the empty-string check, same as is done for `req.Msg.Slug`.
title: superseded_by field bypasses slug validation in Supersede RPC

description:
In internal/scanner/walk.go, the WalkDir callback returns nil on any entry error:

```go
err := filepath.WalkDir(root, func(path string, d fs.DirEntry, err error) error {
    if err != nil {
        return nil //nolint:nilerr // intentional: skip unreadable entries; error recorded in skipped slice
    }
```

The nolint comment says "error recorded in skipped slice" but the unreadable entry error is NOT recorded in the skipped slice — the slice is only appended when ParseFile fails (later in the callback). Entry-level errors (permission denied, broken symlinks, etc.) are completely discarded with no record anywhere: not in SkippedFiles, not in logs, nothing.

This means a misconfigured directory that causes the scanner to silently skip large portions of the codebase will appear to complete successfully with no indication of what was missed.

Fix: record entry errors in the SkippedFiles slice, or at minimum update the comment to accurately say the error is NOT recorded. Preferably:
```go
if err != nil {
    skipped = append(skipped, SkippedFile{Path: path, Reason: err.Error()})
    return nil
}
```

Note: the test TestTier1Scan_UnreadableSubdirectory verifies the scanner doesn't error, but doesn't verify the unreadable directory appears in SkippedFiles — so this behavior gap has no test coverage.
title: walkGoFiles swallows all WalkDir entry errors silently

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput constructs DEPENDS_ON edges with depSlug computed as fmt.Sprintf('%s/%s', slug, dep) where dep is drawn from sl.DependsOn (line 4206 of diff). This assumes all dependency IDs are sibling slices of the same parent spec.

The existing integration test TestStoreDecomposeOutput (authoring_test.go) tests the case where slice-2 depends on slice-1 — a valid sibling. There is no test for:

1. A DependsOn entry that references a non-existent slice slug — the MERGE query will silently no-op if the target Spec node doesn't exist, creating no edge and returning no error.
2. A DependsOn entry referencing a slice from a different parent spec (cross-spec dependency pollution).

The silent no-op behavior means a caller that provides an invalid dependency ID will get success but the graph will be missing the DEPENDS_ON edge. This is a silent correctness failure.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go

Suggested fix: Add a test that calls StoreDecomposeOutput with a DependsOn entry whose target slice does not exist, then queries the graph to verify whether an edge was created or not — and document the intended behavior clearly (error vs. silent skip).
title: StoreDecomposeOutput: DEPENDS_ON edge with non-sibling slug not tested

close_reason: By design: user confirmed 2+ char slugs required. Current regex already enforces this.
description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, line ~3011.

The slug validation regex is:

  var validSlugRe = regexp.MustCompile(`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$`)

This regex requires at least 3 characters (one anchor at start, one or more middle chars, one anchor at end). A slug like 'a' or 'ab' will fail the regex even though single and two-character slugs are arguably valid identifiers. More critically, the regex also accepts '/' in the middle of a slug (for hierarchical paths like 'parent/child'), but the handler docs and CLI help text do not document this constraint.

The slug is the primary external identifier used across all 8 RPCs — any client already using short slugs (1-2 chars) will break. If this is a new API surface and no clients exist yet, this is low risk. But the validation rule is a behavioral contract that should be documented.

Suggested fix: Either adjust the regex to accept 1+ character slugs (e.g., ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*$), or explicitly document in the proto and CLI that slugs must be 3+ characters with only lowercase alphanumerics, underscores, hyphens, and forward slashes.
title: Slug validation regex rejects valid single-character slugs

description:
In internal/authoring/safety.go:136, SafetyFlagResult.Description is populated with:

  Description: 'matched pattern: ' + sp.re.String()

This exposes the compiled regex string (e.g. 'matched pattern: \beval\(\b') directly in the domain result type and propagates it through to the proto response. The Description field on SafetyFlagResult is meant to be a human-readable explanation for the operator, not an implementation artifact.

A caller (or end user) seeing 'matched pattern: \bdrop table\b' instead of 'Potentially destructive DDL detected' gets worse UX and a leaked abstraction. The safetyPattern struct should carry a human-readable label alongside the regex, and Description should use that label.

This also makes tests fragile: tests that assert on Description values will break if the regex pattern changes, even if the semantic behavior is unchanged.
title: SafetyFlagResult.Description leaks internal regex implementation detail to callers

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:3027-3031 — The AuthoringHandler struct has no package-level or struct-level documentation beyond the one-liner 'implements the ConnectRPC AuthoringService'. The txBackend field comment 'optional, may be nil' is accurate but the consequence of nil vs non-nil is not documented here: when nil, Spark does two non-atomic operations (CreateSpec + StoreSparkOutput), which means a crash between those two calls can leave a spec with no spark output. This silent behavioral difference between the transactional and non-transactional paths is important for operators to understand (e.g., when choosing a backend). Suggest adding a brief struct-level comment explaining the atomicity contract, or expanding the txBackend field comment to note the consistency risk when the backend does not support transactions.
title: AuthoringHandler.txBackend comment says 'optional, may be nil' but struct comment is incomplete
SafetyResultsToProto silently maps unknown FindingSeverity to UNSPECIFIED proto value

description:
File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, line ~5085.

SparkResponse contains only:
  SparkOutput output
  repeated SafetyFlag safety_flags
  repeated ConstitutionViolation constitution_violations
  repeated PromptTemplate next_prompts

It does not echo back the slug from the request. Contrast with ApproveResponse and AmendResponse which both include slug. This is a consistency gap in the response API contract — callers who issue concurrent Spark calls and match responses by correlation would need to track state externally instead of using the response slug.

The CLI client works around this by printing args[0] directly (spark.go line ~411), which is correct but indicates the omission.

Suggested fix: Add 'string slug = 5;' to SparkResponse (and similarly to ShapeResponse, SpecifyResponse, DecomposeResponse) so all stage responses are symmetric with each other and with Approve/Amend responses.
title: SparkResponse.slug field missing — clients cannot confirm which spec was sparked

description: The Slice 3 plan (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, Task 7, Step 1) specified AuthoringBackend using proto types: StoreSparkOutput takes *specv1.SparkOutput, StoreDecomposeOutput returns ([]*specv1.Spec, error), and AmendSpec returns (*specv1.Spec, error). The implementation in internal/storage/authoring.go uses domain types instead: *storage.SparkOutput, StoreDecomposeOutput returns ([]string, error), and AmendSpec returns (*storage.AmendResult, error). This is actually better aligned with CLAUDE.md 'Storage interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types' but contradicts the task spec as written. The implementation follows the correct architectural principle; the plan text was superseded during implementation. No code change needed — this is a plan-vs-reality gap that resolved correctly.
title: Storage interface uses domain types but plan specified proto types
