description:
In internal/scanner/walk.go, the walkGoFiles function (line 2952 of diff) has this at the start of its WalkDir callback:

    if err != nil {
        return nil //nolint:nilerr // intentional: skip unreadable entries
    }

This means ALL walk errors are silently swallowed per-entry. The nolint comment documents the intent, but there is no test that verifies the outer walk itself (filepath.WalkDir) can also return a hard error for the root directory (e.g. root does not exist), which propagates via the outer err path.

The existing test TestTier1Scan_UnreadableSubdirectory (edge_test.go) only tests an unreadable *subdirectory*, not the root itself.

Suggested fix: Add a test in edge_test.go that calls scanner.ScanTier1 with a path that does not exist and verifies an error is returned (not nil).
title: scanner/walk.go: no test for walk error from non-root filesystem error

description:
Both passRegistry (internal/authoring/passes.go:41) and promptRegistry (internal/authoring/prompts.go:15) are declared as package-level var maps, not constants. While Go maps cannot be constant, the consequence is that any code in the same package (or via testing hooks) can modify the registry after initialization:

  authoring.passRegistry['spark'] = nil // silently voids all spark passes

The PassesForStage, OfferedPasses, GetPrompts, and PromptsToProto functions all read directly from these mutable globals without any copy, meaning a race condition is possible under concurrent access if these maps are ever modified (e.g. via init() in tests).

Suggested fix:
1. Declare the registries as unexported vars (already done) but wrap all access through functions that return copies (GetPrompts already returns the slice reference directly, not a copy — external mutation of the returned slice changes the registry).
2. Make GetPrompts return a copy of the slice: 'out := make([]Prompt, len(p)); copy(out, p); return out'
3. Consider using sync.Once to initialize a frozen registry if extensibility at startup is needed.

The AllStages() function (stages.go:21) correctly returns a copy — the same pattern should apply to GetPrompts.
title: passRegistry and promptRegistry are mutable package-level vars; external mutation is possible

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, the non-transactional (else) branches for Spark, Shape, Specify, and Decompose swallow the original storage error and replace it with a generic message string, losing the underlying error context.

Lines:
- Spark/else (line ~3063-3064): `connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, fmt.Errorf("failed to store spark output for spec %q", msg.Slug))`
- Shape/else (~3101-3102): same pattern
- Specify/else (~3147-3148): same pattern
- Decompose/else (~3191-3192): same pattern

The actual `err` returned from StoreSparkOutput / StoreShapeOutput / StoreSpecifyOutput / StoreDecomposeOutput is discarded. Only a formatted string with the slug is sent to the caller. This means on-call engineers get no DB error message, no wrapped sentinel error, and no ability to trace the failure.

Contrast with the transactional branches where `err` is correctly wrapped: `fmt.Errorf("store spark output: %w", err)`.

Fix: wrap the actual error in all non-transactional store-error paths, e.g.:
`return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, fmt.Errorf("store spark output for spec %q: %w", msg.Slug, err))`
title: Non-transactional store errors swallow the root cause error
decomposeOutputToDomain uses strategy string fallback that bypasses domain constants

description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 3207-3210: The TODO comment reads 'Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check analytical passes to populate the Decompose response. These passes require the constitution subsystem integration (Slice 4).' The roadmap in CLAUDE.md does not define a numbered Slice 4 — the reference is opaque and likely to become stale. Additionally, the same TODO applies equally to Spark, Shape, and Specify since none wire up constitution_check either, making this TODO incomplete. Suggest either broadening the TODO to cover all stages or removing the Slice 4 reference.
title: TODO comment in Decompose response references Slice 4 which may become stale

description: The Slice 3 plan (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, line 209) defines PromptTemplate.stage as 'string stage = 1'. The actual proto implementation at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto uses 'AuthoringStage stage = 1' (enum type). This is a deliberate improvement — using the enum enforces type safety and prevents invalid stage strings at the proto boundary. However, it creates a discrepancy with the plan and requires PromptsToProto in internal/authoring/prompts.go to map strings to AuthoringStage enum values (the stageToProtoEnum map at line 1583 of the diff). The change is well-implemented and correctly aligned with the rest of the codebase, but diverges from the plan's explicit proto definition. No code change needed; plan should be annotated.
title: PromptTemplate.stage field typed as AuthoringStage enum instead of string as planned

close_reason: By design: Approve only from decompose per sequential funnel rules. User confirmed approved specs cannot be amended.
description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, line ~3218.

The Approve handler hardcodes the expected source stage as 'decompose':

  h.store.TransitionStage(ctx, req.Msg.Slug, storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageDecompose), storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageApproved))

This means calling Approve on a spec that was amended back to 'shape' and never re-decomposed will return CodeFailedPrecondition (ErrInvalidStageTransition). This is intentional business logic, but it is not documented in the ApproveRequest proto comment or the CLI help text ('Mark a spec as approved and ready for execution').

The behavioral contract — that Approve is only valid immediately after Decompose — is invisible to proto consumers. A client that calls Amend then Approve will receive an opaque FailedPrecondition error without guidance.

Suggested fix: Add a proto comment to ApproveRequest stating 'The spec must be at the decompose stage; call Decompose before Approve.' Alternatively, the error message returned by h.stageError should include the expected stage so clients can self-correct.
title: Approve RPC hardcodes transition from decompose only — no path from other stages

description:
storage.AuthoringBackend (internal/storage/authoring.go:182) composes StageWriter, PassWriter, and SpecLifecycle, all of which are write-only interfaces. There is no corresponding read interface for the authoring domain.

As a result, the AuthoringHandler in internal/server/authoring_handler.go holds a separate storage.Backend reference (line 3029) to perform reads (e.g. GetSpec, CreateSpec). This means AuthoringHandler receives two separate backend references that must be the same concrete type at the injection site (serve.go line 302: RegisterAuthoringService(mux, store, store)).

Passing the same store twice is a smell that suggests the interface boundary is not well-drawn. If they diverge (e.g. a read replica for Backend and a write store for AuthoringBackend), the handler's behavior becomes undefined.

Suggested fix: Either add read methods to AuthoringBackend (e.g. GetSpecStage), or compose Backend into AuthoringBackend so a single interface covers all required operations, eliminating the double-store injection.
title: AuthoringBackend interface does not separate read operations from write operations

close_reason: Fixed proto comments in docs commit
title: AmendRequest permits amending from approved stage despite ErrSpecAlreadyApproved guard

close_reason: Fixed proto comments in docs commit
description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 5186 says 'AmendRequest reopens an approved spec and returns it to a specified earlier stage.' but the implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:203 explicitly checks 'if spec.Stage == authoring.StageApproved { return nil, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved }'. The Amend RPC therefore CANNOT amend approved specs — it is only for rolling back specs in intermediate stages. The proto comment is misleading and creates a contractual mismatch between documentation and behavior. A caller who reads the proto expecting to use Amend to reopen an approved spec will receive CodeFailedPrecondition unexpectedly. Fix: update the proto comment to 'AmendRequest rolls a spec in an intermediate stage back to an earlier stage for rework. Approved specs cannot be amended; use Supersede instead.''
title: AmendRequest proto comment contradicts implementation — blocks amending approved specs

description:
Tier1Result (internal/scanner/tier1.go:2486) and Tier2Result (internal/scanner/tier2.go:2724) are plain structs with exported slice fields (Packages, Interfaces, Structs, Functions, Imports, TestFiles, SkippedFiles). These are populated inside ScanTier1/ScanTier2 and returned to callers.

Because the fields are mutable exported slices, callers can accidentally append to or overwrite them:

  result, _ := scanner.ScanTier1(dir)
  result.Interfaces = nil // all interface data silently lost

For a scanning library intended to feed constitution authoring, these results should be treated as immutable data transfer objects. The types are suitable for their current use (simple data containers returned from a function), but if they are ever used in concurrent pipelines or cached, the mutation risk becomes a real bug.

Suggested fix: This is a minor concern given the current usage pattern, but consider adding accessor methods and making fields unexported, or at minimum document that callers must not modify the returned slices. The SkippedFile type (line 2480) is particularly worth protecting since skipped file tracking is a correctness concern.
title: Tier1Result and Tier2Result scanner types expose mutable slices with no encapsulation
DecomposeResponse.next_prompts field added beyond plan spec with unimplemented analytical passes (TODO)

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, the Supersede handler validates req.Msg.Slug via validateSlug() but does not validate req.Msg.SupersededBy. An attacker can pass arbitrary values (e.g., path-traversal strings, control characters) as superseded_by. While the Cypher query uses parameterized values (so no injection risk), the unvalidated value is stored in the database and returned in SupersedeResponse.superseded_by, potentially corrupting data or bypassing slug format invariants the rest of the system relies on. Fix: add 'if err := validateSlug(req.Msg.SupersededBy); err \!= nil { return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument, err) }' after the SupersededBy empty-check at internal/server/authoring_handler.go:268.
title: Supersede RPC does not validate superseded_by slug

close_reason: Fixed proto comments in docs commit
description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto:5186 — The message comment for AmendRequest says "reopens an approved spec and returns it to a specified earlier stage" but the implementation in internal/server/authoring_handler.go and internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go shows that Amend actually rejects approved specs (AmendSpec returns ErrSpecAlreadyApproved when spec.Stage == StageApproved). Amend is for rolling back a non-approved spec (e.g. shape → spark), not for reopening approved specs. Supersede is the correct mechanism for approved specs. Fix: change the comment to something like 'rolls a spec back to an earlier authoring stage; not permitted on approved specs (use Supersede instead)'.
title: AmendRequest comment says 'approved' but Amend works on non-approved specs

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:242 — storeJSONProperty uses slices.Contains to validate the property name against allowedJSONProperties. This is a linear scan over a 10-element slice on every storage write call.

More importantly, the property parameter is typed as plain string with no compile-time constraint. Each of the 10 wrapper methods passes a string literal (e.g. 'spark_output', 'red_team_findings') with no guarantee at compile time that they match the allowlist. A typo in any wrapper (e.g. 'redteam_findings' instead of 'red_team_findings') would only be caught at runtime by the allowlist check.

Suggested fix: Define a JSONProperty type as a string alias with constants for each allowed value, and use that type as the parameter instead of string. The allowlist can then be a map[JSONProperty]struct{} for O(1) lookup. This converts the runtime property name check into a compile-time constraint for all callers within the package.
title: storeJSONProperty uses allowlist as a linear scan rather than a set; property name type is too broad

description: The PR adds ~250 lines of repeated '```text' opening fence markers to docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md (lines 217-617, 380-508, 991-1128, 1399-1527, 2001-2104 in the diff). The plan document content is replaced by blank lines between unclosed code fences, and structured content (Files, Step 1, etc.) is deleted. This is file corruption that should be reverted. The diff shows the original content was removed (lines with '-') and replaced with the bare ```text fences (lines with '+'). The entire docs/plans/ file change should be reverted to HEAD.
title: docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md corrupted with hundreds of spurious backtick-text blocks

description:
ADR-003 (docs/initial-design-session/specgraph-v1.0-draft-adr-003-decisions.md, Authoring Flow Impact section) states: 'Amendment: When a spec is amended, its decisions may need revision.' This implies amendment is specifically an operation on approved specs being reopened. The plan's AmendRequest description (plan line ~281-285) says 'Amend(AmendRequest) — rolling a spec back to an earlier stage.'

The implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go AmendSpec explicitly guards against amending already-approved specs: 'if spec.Stage == authoring.StageApproved { return nil, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved }' (diff line ~4289). This contradicts the spec semantics: amendment is the mechanism to reopen an approved spec. The valid use case 'approved spec needs rework → amend back to shape' is explicitly blocked by this guard.

The ValidateAmendTransition in internal/authoring/stages.go also requires backward transitions only (forward transitions are rejected), which is correct. But the ErrSpecAlreadyApproved guard in AmendSpec runs BEFORE ValidateAmendTransition and rejects all amendments on approved specs without giving the transition validator a chance to evaluate.

Files: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (AmendSpec function, diff lines ~4283-4293).
title: Amend handler does not enforce that only approved specs can be amended per ADR-003 authoring flow

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go Spark(), the else (non-transactional) branch at lines 71-76 calls h.backend.CreateSpec() then h.store.StoreSparkOutput(). If CreateSpec succeeds but StoreSparkOutput fails, the spec node exists in the database at stage='spark' but has no spark_output property — an inconsistent state. The client receives CodeInternal and cannot retry (a second Spark call will fail because CreateSpec will conflict on the slug). The memgraph backend does implement TransactionalBackend so in production the txBackend \!= nil branch is always taken, but if a different storage backend is wired without transaction support, this becomes a real data integrity issue. Fix: either remove the non-transactional else branch and require transaction support for Spark, or add cleanup logic (delete the spec if StoreSparkOutput fails).
title: Spark non-transactional path creates orphaned spec node when StoreSparkOutput fails

description:
File: internal/config/config.go, line ~2298; cmd/specgraph/client.go, line ~106.

A new TLS bool field is added to ServerConfig:

  TLS bool `yaml:\"tls\"`

The default is false (Go zero value), so existing config files without this field continue to use HTTP, which is backward-compatible for local dev. However, the URL scheme selection in client.go now gates on this field:

  scheme := \"http\"
  if cfg.Server.TLS {
      scheme = \"https\"
  }

Before this PR, the URL was always constructed as http://. Any user who has an existing config with Server.Remote pointing to an HTTPS endpoint and no TLS: true in their config will silently continue using HTTP (Remote is used as-is when set, bypassing scheme selection). The subtle issue is that the new TLS field only affects the auto-constructed URL from Host+Port, not the Remote field — this inconsistency is undocumented.

Suggested fix: Add a code comment or config documentation noting that TLS only affects auto-URL construction (Host+Port mode) and has no effect when Remote is set.
title: config.go TLS field addition changes URL scheme behavior without migration documentation

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 22, validSlugRe = regexp.MustCompile(`^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$`). The middle group [a-z0-9_/-]* requires at least one character between the required start/end characters, making the minimum valid slug length 3 (e.g. 'a-b'). Actually the middle group is * (zero-or-more) but the pattern requires two distinct anchored characters, so the minimum length is 2 (e.g. 'ab'). A single-character slug like 'a' will fail validation with 'invalid characters' even though it is a perfectly valid identifier. This is a silent usability bug — no test covers single-char slugs. Fix: change the regex to '^[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9])?$' to allow single-char slugs, or document that single-char slugs are intentionally rejected.
title: validateSlug regex rejects single-character slugs

close_reason: Fixed proto comments in docs commit
title: GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for APPROVED stage — undocumented contract

description: The diff to docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md shows hundreds of added lines consisting entirely of empty backtick code fences (triple-backtick text followed by a blank line, repeated). This appears to be a tool or editor corruption artifact that was accidentally committed. The file is a design plan document and these empty fences add no content -- they corrupt the document structure and make it unreadable. The markdown linter exclusion in Taskfile.yml (docs/plans) hides this from CI checks. This is a data integrity issue with a key design document for Slice 7. The corruption spans hundreds of lines in the diff starting at diff line 521.
title: docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md corrupted with hundreds of empty code fence blocks

description: Fix 5 safety-related findings: 6(regex exposure), 7(duplicate types), 8(comment), 23(description leak), 25(unknown severity). Files: internal/authoring/safety.go, internal/storage/authoring.go
title: Fix(batch-1a): Safety type fixes (6,7,8,23,25)

description: Fix 4 scanner findings: 13(nolint comment), 20(WalkDir errors), 28(walk test), 37(mutable slices). Files: internal/scanner/walk.go, internal/scanner/tier1.go, internal/scanner/tier2.go
title: Fix(batch-1b): Scanner fixes (13,20,28,37)

description:
The Spark handler (and similarly Shape, Specify, Decompose) has two code paths in internal/server/authoring_handler.go:

1. When txBackend == nil: CreateSpec then StoreSparkOutput are called sequentially (non-atomic).
2. When txBackend != nil: both calls are wrapped in RunInTransaction for atomicity.

The test helper newAuthoringClient registers the service with a *fakeBackend that does NOT implement TransactionalBackend, so handler.txBackend is always nil in all handler tests. The transactional path (lines 3047-3066 of the diff) is never exercised by the test suite.

A bug in the transaction-aware branch — e.g. the context not being threaded correctly, or the error mapping being wrong — would go undetected. The integration tests in internal/storage/memgraph/tx_test.go only test the store layer in isolation; the handler + transaction integration is untested.

Failures this prevents:
- If StoreSparkOutput fails mid-transaction, CreateSpec should roll back. Without a test, a missing rollback would leave orphaned Spec nodes in the graph.
- Error mapping from RunInTransaction (wraps with 'memgraph: transaction:') might not be unwrapped correctly to ErrSpecNotFound / ErrInvalidStageTransition.

Suggested fix: add a fake backend that also implements TransactionalBackend (e.g. a simple in-memory sequential executor) and add at least one test where the second operation fails inside a transaction, verifying the first is also rolled back (or at minimum that CodeInternal is returned).
title: Spark handler: no test for transactional path (txBackend != nil) rollback behavior

description: Fix 3 CLI/config findings: 14(loadJSONFile tests), 18(path restriction), 44(TLS docs). Files: cmd/specgraph/util.go, cmd/specgraph/*, internal/config/config.go
title: Fix(batch-1c): CMD + config fixes (14,18,44)

description:
File: internal/authoring/safety.go, line ~1850

The `RunSafetyNet` function sets the `Description` field of each `SafetyFlagResult` to the raw matched regex string:

    Description: "matched pattern: " + sp.re.String()

This string is then serialized into the proto `SafetyFlag.description` field and returned to API callers in `SparkResponse.safety_flags`, `ShapeResponse.safety_flags`, and `SpecifyResponse.safety_flags`.

Exposing internal pattern implementation details is an information disclosure that violates defence-in-depth: it reveals the exact detection logic to an attacker, making it trivial to craft input that triggers safety flags (or avoids them) with knowledge of the internal pattern set.

Suggested fix: Replace the regex string with a human-readable, category-level description that does not reveal the matched pattern. For example: 'Potential security concern detected' rather than 'matched pattern: \\bhardcoded secret\\b'.
title: Safety scanner exposes internal regex patterns in API responses

description:
Two separate type definitions for conceptually identical concepts exist in the codebase:

1. authoring.FindingSeverity (int, values 1/2/3) in internal/authoring/safety.go:15
2. storage.FindingSeverity (string, values critical/warning/note) in internal/storage/authoring.go:82

Similarly:
1. authoring.SafetyCategory (string) in internal/authoring/safety.go:34
2. storage.SafetyCategory (string) in internal/storage/authoring.go:128

The two FindingSeverity types use different underlying kinds (int vs string) with different constant values, making the relationship confusing and requiring a mapping table (severityToProto) to bridge them. There is no compile-time relationship enforced between authoring.SeverityCritical (int=1) and storage.SeverityCritical (string='critical').

This creates a maintenance hazard: adding a new severity level requires editing both type definitions, the conversion map, the proto enum, and constants in two packages. A caller who misuses storage.FindingSeverity where authoring.FindingSeverity is expected will get a type error, but the conceptual duplication is still unnecessary.

Suggested fix: Consolidate FindingSeverity and SafetyCategory into a single canonical location (either the storage package as the persistence contract, or a new internal/domain package). The authoring package's scanner layer should convert from the canonical type to proto rather than defining its own parallel type hierarchy.
title: Duplicate FindingSeverity and SafetyCategory types across authoring and storage packages

description: internal/authoring/safety.go:1737-1743 — The SafetyInput struct comment reads: 'Intent accepts any text for pattern scanning — its name reflects the Spark use case, but other handlers pass stage-appropriate text (e.g., risks in Shape).' This comment is attempting to explain away a naming mismatch rather than resolving it. The field name 'Intent' is actively misleading for Shape (where it receives Risks) and for Decompose (where it receives joined slice intents). This will confuse future contributors adding new stages. Consider renaming the field to 'Text' or 'Content', or documenting the mapping table (Spark→seed, Shape→risks, Specify→interface_contract, Decompose→slice intents) explicitly in the struct comment. The existing inline comment in authoring_handler.go at line 3108 repeats the same workaround explanation.
title: SafetyInput.Intent field comment is misleading for non-Spark use cases
StoreDecomposeOutput silently ignores GetSpec errors during idempotency check

description: Implement the Constitution slice per docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-2-constitution-plan.md. 10 tasks: proto schema, storage interface, Memgraph backend, ConnectRPC handler, Tier 0 scanner, emitter, CLI commands, enhanced init, wiring, verification.
title: Slice 2: Constitution — complete implementation

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8: Authoring proto with 8 RPCs, funnel stages, posture detection, prompt templates, safety net, analytical passes, scanner tiers, storage backend, ConnectRPC handler, CLI commands. 10774 additions across 48 files.
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark (line ~2647), Shape (~2689), Specify (~2728), and Decompose (~2782), the StoreSafetyFlags call happens AFTER the main runInTxOrSequential block completes. This means safety flags are written outside any transaction. If StoreSafetyFlags fails, the handler returns a CodeInternal error to the client, but the stage transition and stage output were already committed — the spec node has been advanced with no safety flags recorded, yet the caller receives an error and may retry, leading to duplicate transitions or confusing state. Additionally, the main transaction wraps only the TransitionStage+StoreOutput operations, while safety flag storage is unconditionally outside. Fix: include StoreSafetyFlags inside the runInTxOrSequential call, or explicitly document and accept the partial-write risk with a comment explaining the fallback behavior.
title: Safety flags stored outside transaction, creating inconsistent state on StoreSafetyFlags failure

description:
The RunSafetyNet function in internal/authoring/safety.go (line 1281) lowercases input with strings.ToLower before regex matching. This handles ASCII case variation but does not Unicode-normalize the combined string.

An attacker submitting spec content with:
- Cyrillic/Greek homoglyphs (е, а, о, с look identical to ASCII e, a, o, c)
- Zero-width joiners (U+200D) or zero-width non-joiners inserted inside flagged phrases
- Fullwidth ASCII variants (U+FF21-U+FF5A)

could cause phrases like 'hardcoded secret' or 'disable auth' to pass all regex patterns undetected.

Example bypass: 'hardcoded\u200Bsecret' (zero-width space between words) would not match the regex `hardcoded secret` because buildPatterns uses regexp.QuoteMeta which preserves the literal space, and the ZWS breaks the match.

Location: internal/authoring/safety.go:1283-1286

Suggested fix: Apply unicode.NFKC normalization and strip zero-width characters (U+200B, U+200C, U+200D, U+FEFF, U+2060) from the combined scan string before regex matching. golang.org/x/text/unicode/norm provides NFKC normalization. This makes the safety net significantly harder to bypass without affecting legitimate spec content.
title: Safety scanner bypassed by Unicode homoglyphs and zero-width characters

description: In internal/authoring/safety.go, SafetyResultsToProto (lines ~1333-1335), when the map lookup for a FindingSeverity fails (ok == false), the code falls back to FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED with no log, no error, and no indication to the caller. The proto comment says FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED is 'not a valid severity'. Returning it silently to clients means a safety finding with a misconfigured or novel severity value disappears from the API surface without trace. Since all severity values are compile-time constants today this is low risk, but any future addition to the domain FindingSeverity enum that lacks a corresponding entry in severityToProto will silently produce UNSPECIFIED findings. Fix: return an error or log a warning when the lookup fails, so a misconfiguration is caught immediately rather than producing invalid proto output.
title: Unknown severity maps to FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED silently in SafetyResultsToProto

description: SafetyInput.Validate() (internal/authoring/safety.go lines 1164-1168) is defined and exported but has zero test coverage. The method returns an error when Text, Scope, and Invariants are all empty. The safety_test.go file tests RunSafetyNet extensively but never calls Validate() directly. If a caller is supposed to invoke Validate() before RunSafetyNet (the handler does not call it; it calls RunSafetyNet directly), a regression introducing a nil-dereference or logic error in Validate() would be undetected. More concretely: the current handler skips Validate() entirely — if a future refactor calls it first, there is no test to confirm it behaves correctly at the boundary. Suggested fix: add table-driven tests for Validate() covering (1) all-empty input returns error, (2) Text-only present returns nil, (3) Scope-only returns nil, (4) Invariants-only returns nil. Location: internal/authoring/safety_test.go (missing — no TestSafetyInputValidate exists).
title: SafetyInput.Validate() has no test coverage

description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go, newTestStore (lines ~4362-4365), t.Cleanup registers func() { store.Close(ctx) } which discards the error return from Close. If Close fails (e.g., a driver resource leak or connection error), the test will not fail and the failure will be invisible. The pattern used in tx_test.go (defer store.Close(ctx)) has the same issue. Fix: use require.NoError(t, store.Close(ctx)) inside the cleanup function, or at minimum log the error. In tx_test.go this is a separate instance of the same pattern.
title: Store.Close() error ignored in newTestStore cleanup

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go TransitionStage (line ~4060):

  if from == authoring.StageApproved {

The parameter from is typed as storage.AuthoringStage (a string typedef), and authoring.StageApproved is an untyped string constant "approved". The comparison compiles and works today, but it bypasses the type system — a caller could pass any storage.AuthoringStage value including a future "approved" alias or misspelling and the guard would silently fail.

More importantly, the guard runs before authoring.ValidateTransition which already rejects transitions from "approved" (because backward transitions from approved are handled only by AmendSpec, not TransitionStage). The pre-check on from == StageApproved therefore only catches a subset of invalid forward transitions from approved; the ValidateTransition call below catches the rest. This dual-check creates an inconsistency: the error returned for from=approved is ErrSpecAlreadyApproved, while the error returned for from=approved via ValidateTransition would be wrapped ErrInvalidStageTransition.

Fix: remove the redundant approved-guard pre-check and rely solely on ValidateTransition, which already returns an error for "approved" → anything forward. Return ErrSpecAlreadyApproved in the ValidateTransition error path when the from stage is detected as approved.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines ~4059-4062
title: TransitionStage approved guard checks from == StageApproved using untyped string comparison

description:
SparkRequest, ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, and DecomposeRequest all declare a 'posture' field (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 233, 255, 278, 303), and the authoring/passes.go PassesForStage() and OfferedPasses() APIs consume posture to select which analytical passes run. However, the handler in internal/server/authoring_handler.go never reads req.Msg.Posture at any call site. The posture is accepted from the client, has no effect on behaviour, and is not stored — but the client has no way to know this. This is a silent contract violation: the API implies posture controls pass selection, but it does not.

RISKY: Any consumer who sends POSTURE_SUPPORT expecting only on-demand passes will still get the same auto-run behaviour as POSTURE_DRIVE. Once the handler is wired to actually honour posture, clients sending POSTURE_UNSPECIFIED (the zero value, i.e. default) will get different pass behaviour from what they experience today.

Fix: Either (a) call authoring.ResolvePosture(msg.Posture, ...) in each stage handler and use the result to gate pass execution, or (b) mark the field as reserved/deprecated in the proto until it is implemented, with a clear comment that it is currently ignored.
title: posture field silently ignored in all stage RPCs

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go enforces maxFieldLen (10000 chars) on a subset of free-text fields: seed (Spark), interface_contract (Specify), reason (Amend), and individual slice.intent (Decompose). However, many fields are uncapped:

SparkRequest (handler ~line 2619):
- output.signal, output.scope_sniff, output.kill_test — no length cap
- output.questions — no element count cap, no per-element length cap

ShapeRequest (handler ~line 2661):
- scope_in, scope_out — no element count or per-element length cap
- approaches[].name, approaches[].description, approaches[].tradeoffs — no bounds
- chosen_approach, decisions — no length cap
- risks — no element count cap, no per-element length cap
- success_must, success_should, success_wont — no element count cap

These fields flow through shapeOutputToDomain and are stored as JSON blobs via storeJSONProperty → SET s.shape_output = $data on Memgraph nodes. A single malicious ShapeRequest with thousands of long scope_in strings could write megabytes to a single graph node and potentially exhaust Memgraph memory.

Suggested fix: Apply maxFieldLen to all free-text scalar fields, add an element-count cap (e.g. maxRepeatedLen = 100) to all repeated string fields in Shape, Spark, Specify, and Decompose handlers. Document the limits in the proto comments.
title: Unbounded repeated string fields in ShapeRequest and SparkRequest allow oversized writes to Memgraph

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description: TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_HappyPath_SafetyFlags (line 3627) verifies that Spark returns safety flags for dangerous input. However, there are no equivalent tests for Shape, Specify, or Decompose. Each handler stage independently runs RunSafetyNet on stage-specific fields: Shape scans Risks and Scope (lines 2685-2688), Specify scans InterfaceContract and Invariants (lines 2724-2727), Decompose scans slice Intents (lines 2779-2781). A regression that accidentally drops the RunSafetyNet call from any of these handlers — or passes the wrong fields — would go undetected. This is particularly concerning for Specify, since InterfaceContract is the most likely field to contain dangerous patterns (e.g. 'disable auth', 'no encryption'). Suggested fix: add analogues of TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_HappyPath_SafetyFlags for Shape (with a dangerous risk string), Specify (with a dangerous interface_contract), and Decompose (with a dangerous slice intent). Location: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (after line 3636).
title: No test for handler safety scan path in Shape, Specify, or Decompose

description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage (line ~4060), the guard 'if from == authoring.StageApproved { return storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved }' compares a storage.AuthoringStage (string typedef) to a plain string constant. This works today because AuthoringStage is a string type, but the comparison is between the literal string 'approved' and the from parameter which is passed as storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageApproved) at the call site. More importantly, in TestTransitionStage_ApprovedGuard (authoring_test.go line ~4618), the test only asserts require.Error(t, err) — it does not assert ErrorIs(t, err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved). This means if the error type changes (e.g., to ErrInvalidStageTransition), the test still passes but the handler's stageError method would route it to a different HTTP status code (CodeInternal instead of CodeFailedPrecondition). Fix: strengthen the test to assert the specific error sentinel, ensuring the error type contract is verified.
title: TransitionStage approved-guard check does not surface ErrSpecAlreadyApproved to caller

close_reason: Fixed: added type Stage string with typed constants
description:
The authoring stage constants in internal/authoring/stages.go (lines 1523-1528) are declared as plain untyped string constants:

  const (
    StageSpark     = "spark"
    StageShape     = "shape"
    StageSpecify   = "specify"
    StageDecompose = "decompose"
    StageApproved  = "approved"
  )

  Meanwhile, storage.AuthoringStage is a typed string (internal/storage/authoring.go line 3972):
    type AuthoringStage string

  This creates an asymmetry: any raw string can be passed where storage.AuthoringStage is expected without compiler error. In internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines 2672, 2716, 2763, 2802), every call to TransitionStage casts via storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageSpark) — a cast that succeeds silently for any string value.

  In memgraph/authoring.go line 4060, the comparison 'from == authoring.StageApproved' works because untyped string constants compare to any string-derived type, but this is subtle.

  Suggested fix: declare a Stage type in the authoring package:
    type Stage string
    const (
      StageSpark Stage = "spark"
      ...
    )
  Then make storage.AuthoringStage = authoring.Stage (or alias it). This makes invalid stage values a compile-time error instead of a runtime one.
title: Stage constants are untyped strings — no compile-time safety for stage values across layers

close_reason: Deferred to spgr-4r8: auth is Phase 2+ concern, not Slice 3 scope
description:
All 8 RPCs registered by RegisterAuthoringService (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are mounted on the HTTP mux with no authentication middleware, no authorization check, and no caller identity requirement.

Location: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:2890-2903 (RegisterAuthoringService), cmd/specgraph/serve.go:271

Any network-accessible caller can:
- Create arbitrary specs (Spark) using any slug
- Advance specs through the funnel (Shape, Specify, Decompose) without owning them
- Approve specs (Approve), finalising them for execution
- Revert specs to earlier stages (Amend)
- Mark specs as superseded (Supersede)
- Read prompt templates (GetPrompts)

This is especially serious for Approve and Amend, which alter spec lifecycle state that downstream execution engines (Gastown) consume.

The existing connect.go handler has no interceptor chain for auth. No JWT validation, API key check, or identity propagation is present anywhere in the handler or serve wiring.

Suggested fix: Add a ConnectRPC interceptor (connect.UnaryInterceptorFunc) in RegisterAuthoringService that validates a bearer token or mTLS identity from the incoming request headers before dispatching to handler methods. Even a simple shared-secret check would eliminate unauthenticated mutation. Longer-term, integrate with the constitution's identity model.
title: No authentication or authorization on any AuthoringService RPC endpoint

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go StoreDecomposeOutput (line ~4117), the first operation is:

  if err := s.storeJSONProperty(ctx, slug, "decompose_output", output); err != nil {

storeJSONProperty returns storage.ErrSpecNotFound (wrapped) when the MATCH returns 0 records. If the parent spec does not exist, this propagates correctly and the test TestStoreDecomposeOutput_MissingParent passes (line ~4538).

However, this only works because storeJSONProperty is the FIRST operation. After the 2-pass child creation loop is introduced (lines ~4131+), if the parent disappears between the storeJSONProperty call and a later MERGE query, the child-creation queries (CreateSpec, COMPOSES MERGE, DEPENDS_ON MERGE) will silently succeed — creating orphaned child spec nodes with no parent COMPOSES edge to a real parent.

Without a transaction wrapping the entire StoreDecomposeOutput operation, partial failure mid-loop leaves the graph in an inconsistent state: some child nodes and edges exist, the parent's decompose_output property may be set, but not all slices are created.

The integration test TestStoreDecomposeOutput_MissingParent tests only the case where the parent never exists, not the partial failure case.

Fix: Document that StoreDecomposeOutput must be called within a RunInTransaction context for full atomicity, and add a note in the interface definition in internal/storage/authoring.go.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput missing-parent detection relies on storeJSONProperty returning ErrSpecNotFound

description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 378-379 documents 'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' The handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 319-333 does return CodeInvalidArgument when no prompts are found, which is consistent with this documentation.

However, INVALID_ARGUMENT is semantically incorrect here. The stage value (APPROVED) is a valid enum member — it is not an invalid argument, it is a valid request for a stage that has no prompts. The more semantically correct code is CodeNotFound (no prompts for this stage) or a dedicated NOT_FOUND response path.

Using INVALID_ARGUMENT for 'valid enum value, empty result' trains consumers to treat this response the same as 'malformed request' (wrong type, missing required field), which conflates two different error conditions. A consumer implementing retry/backoff logic on INVALID_ARGUMENT will behave incorrectly.

Additionally, the proto comment says 'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.' This is incorrect when any unknown stage value hits the else branch — the handler returns 'unknown stage' for unrecognised enum values and 'no prompts defined for stage' for APPROVED. These map to the same error code but with different messages.

Fix: Return CodeNotFound with message 'no prompts available for stage APPROVED' so consumers can distinguish 'you sent bad data' from 'this is valid but empty'.
title: GetPrompts returns INVALID_ARGUMENT for APPROVED stage without a documented fallback

description: passes_test.go (internal/authoring/passes_test.go) tests PassesForStage with DRIVE and PARTNER postures but does not test SUPPORT posture. The passRegistry wires SUPPORT to receive red_team and consistency_check as offered-only in StageSpecify (line 613), and simplicity_check as offered-only in StageDecompose (line 618). PassesForStage(SUPPORT) should return only constitution_check for spark/shape/specify/decompose (since SUPPORT is never in autoIn except via allPostures for constitution_check). OfferedPasses tests SUPPORT only for specify (line 743-746). If a future change accidentally adds SUPPORT to an autoIn list, or removes constitution_check from allPostures, the SUPPORT posture path for PassesForStage would silently regress. Suggested fix: add table entries for SUPPORT posture in TestPassesForStage for shape, specify, and decompose stages. Location: internal/authoring/passes_test.go lines 669-761.
title: PassesForStage and OfferedPasses lack SUPPORT posture coverage

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 4092 formats the actual current stage value retrieved from Memgraph into an error message that propagates through the handler's stageError helper and is returned in the ConnectRPC error detail to callers:

    return fmt.Errorf("memgraph: spec %q at stage %v, expected %q: %w", slug, actualStage, from, storage.ErrInvalidStageTransition)

Similarly, internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 4254 wraps ValidateAmendTransition errors that contain stage names in the returned error.

These messages reach the client as part of the ConnectRPC error message string. Exposing internal state (current stage, expected stage, slug) in error details helps an attacker enumerate spec state without authorization (since there is currently no auth on these endpoints — see sibling finding). Even with auth in place, leaking internal state in error messages is against defense-in-depth principles.

Suggested fix: At the stageError boundary (internal/server/authoring_handler.go:3019-3030), map storage errors to generic client-facing messages. For ErrInvalidStageTransition, return a message like 'spec is not in the required stage for this operation' without echoing the actual or expected stage values. Internal details should be logged server-side only.
title: Internal stage state leaked in error messages returned to RPC callers

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, validateSlug (lines ~2589-2594), two error messages echo the raw slug value directly into client-facing error text: 'slug %q contains path traversal' and 'slug %q contains invalid characters'. While slugs are user-supplied strings and path traversal sequences (e.g. '../etc/passwd') or special characters are not sensitive data themselves, embedding the full raw value in error messages is a pattern that can lead to log injection or information disclosure if the pattern is copy-pasted to contexts where the input is more sensitive (e.g., the reason or seed fields). More concretely, the error text will be forwarded as-is to the ConnectRPC client, which may log it. The path traversal case in particular embeds the verbatim traversal string in the error. Fix: use a generic message like 'slug contains invalid characters or path traversal sequences' without echoing the value, or sanitize the value before including it.
title: validateSlug exposes raw slug value in error messages returned to API clients

close_reason: Fixed: SafetyInput.Validate() now called before ComputeSafetyFlags
description:
SafetyInput defines a Validate() method (internal/authoring/safety.go lines 1161-1168) that returns an error when all three fields (Text, Scope, Invariants) are empty. However RunSafetyNet (line 1281) never calls Validate() before scanning.

  Every call site in the handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2647, 2685-2688, 2724-2727, 2779-2781) constructs a SafetyInput with at least one field populated, so the bug does not manifest in practice today. But the invariant is maintained by convention at call sites rather than enforced at the API boundary.

  Two problems:
  1. RunSafetyNet silently succeeds on an all-empty input, returning no findings rather than an error. A future caller who passes &SafetyInput{} gets a false all-clear.
  2. Validate() is dead API surface that creates a false impression of safety.

  Suggested fix: call s.Validate() at the top of RunSafetyNet and return (nil, error) — changing the signature to ([]SafetyFlagResult, error) — or enforce non-empty construction via a constructor:
    func NewSafetyInput(text string, scope, invariants []string) (*SafetyInput, error)
  Either approach makes the invariant impossible to bypass.
title: SafetyInput.Validate() is never called — invalid empty input silently produces no findings

description: The Amend handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2834-2837) contains a post-storage check: if the stage string returned from storage is not in the stageToProto map, it returns CodeInternal. This branch is not tested. The fakeAuthoringBackend.AmendSpec returns an AmendResult with Stage set by the test, but no existing test supplies a Stage that would fail the stageToProto lookup. This is a distinct error path from the AmendSpec call failing outright. A future change that renames a stage constant in storage without updating stageToProto would cause Amend to silently return CodeInternal after a successful DB write. Suggested fix: add a test that sets fakeAuthoringBackend.amendResult.Stage to an unknown value (e.g. 'invalid_stage') and verifies CodeInternal is returned. Location: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (after line 3302).
title: No test for Amend handler error path when storage returns unknown stage

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, length caps are applied inconsistently across handlers:

- Spark: seed is capped at maxFieldLen (10000) — lines ~2622-2624
- Shape: no length caps on scope_in, scope_out, risks, success_*, decisions, approaches
- Specify: interface_contract is capped — line ~2710 — but verify_criteria and invariants are not
- Decompose: each slice intent is capped — lines ~2752-2756 — but strategy field already validates as enum

The Shape handler has the most free-text content (risks list, scope lists, decisions) and none of it is length-validated before being passed to storeJSONProperty via shapeOutputToDomain. A caller can submit arbitrarily large risks or scope_in slices that bypass the maxFieldLen guard entirely.

This is an incomplete application of the length-cap defense that was explicitly added (the maxFieldLen constant and comment at line ~2580 indicate the intent). The same guard should apply to at minimum: all repeated string fields across all handlers, or document which fields are intentionally uncapped and why.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines ~2660-2699 (Shape handler)
title: Spark handler validates seed length but Shape/Specify handlers do not cap most free-text fields

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 356 declares AmendResponse.version as int32 with the comment 'Monotonically increasing amendment count for this spec.' Using int32 is a future-compatibility trap: int32 overflows at 2,147,483,647 and has no defined behaviour after overflow in the proto contract. More importantly, the comment says 'monotonically increasing amendment count' but does not specify:

1. Whether version 0 is valid (i.e. does the first amend return 1?).
2. What happens on overflow — error, wrap, or undefined.
3. Whether version is a global counter or per-slug.

The handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 288 returns result.Version directly from storage without any overflow check.

For a field representing a counter that consumers will use for optimistic concurrency or audit, uint32 or int64 is more appropriate, and the behaviour on first amend and overflow must be documented in the proto comment.

Fix: Change to int64 (or uint32 with appropriate documentation) and add a proto comment specifying: 'Starts at 1 on first amendment. Undefined behaviour if the counter wraps — treat values <= 0 as an error.' Alternatively, document that this is an opaque version token and should not be compared arithmetically.
title: AmendResponse.version field uses int32 but lacks proto documentation on overflow/rollover semantics

description: The Decompose handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go) accepts a DecomposeOutput with an empty Slices list (Strategy set, no slices). This is arguably invalid — a decomposition with no slices produces no child specs. The handler calls decomposeOutputToDomain which happily returns a DecomposeOutput with an empty slice list, and StoreDecomposeOutput would write the JSON but create no child specs. No test verifies whether an empty slices list is rejected with InvalidArgument or accepted as a degenerate single-unit decomposition. If the contract is that slices must be non-empty, the validation gap allows callers to persist a vacuous decompose node and advance to approved with zero child work items. Suggested fix: add a test with DecomposeOutput{Strategy: VERTICAL_SLICE, Slices: nil} and assert either CodeInvalidArgument (preferred) or document the accepted behavior. Location: internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go.
title: No test for Decompose with empty slices list

close_reason: Fixed: rec.Get() errors now checked and returned
description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, AmendSpec (lines ~4272-4274), the return values from rec.Get('s.slug'), rec.Get('s.stage'), and rec.Get('s.version') each return a second bool indicating whether the key was found. All three boolean return values are silently discarded with blank identifier _. If Memgraph returns a record without one of these fields (e.g., a schema change, projection rename, or driver bug), the code silently constructs an AmendResult with zero/empty values (empty Slug, empty Stage, zero Version) and returns it as success. The caller in authoring_handler.go then uses the Slug and Stage to build a proto response, potentially returning an empty stage to the client. Fix: check the ok booleans for s.slug and s.stage (at minimum), and return an error if they are missing or nil. The version case already has a type switch but misses the key-not-found scenario.
title: Swallowed error from rec.Get() calls in AmendSpec

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
FindingSeverity is defined independently in two packages with different underlying types:

  authoring package (internal/authoring/safety.go lines 1140-1147):
    type FindingSeverity int
    const (
      SeverityCritical FindingSeverity = 1
      SeverityWarning  FindingSeverity = 2
      SeverityNote     FindingSeverity = 3
    )

  storage package (internal/storage/authoring.go lines 3908-3916):
    type FindingSeverity string
    const (
      SeverityCritical FindingSeverity = "critical"
      SeverityWarning  FindingSeverity = "warning"
      SeverityNote     FindingSeverity = "note"
    )

  The int type in authoring is used for severity ordering (lower = more severe). The string type in storage is for human-readable JSON persistence. Both are valid design choices in isolation, but the split means:
  - Two conversion maps are required (severityToProto, severityToStorage in safety.go lines 1323-1360)
  - The constant names (SeverityCritical etc.) collide across packages and require full qualification everywhere
  - A new severity value requires updates in three places (authoring, storage, proto enum) with no compile-time linkage

  Suggested improvement: define FindingSeverity once in the authoring package as the int type (for comparison semantics), and in storage replace the string-typed constants with a conversion function. Or define the canonical type in storage and use it in authoring (reversing the import direction would require a common types package). Document explicitly in a comment why the split is intentional if kept.
title: FindingSeverity defined twice with different underlying types — int in authoring, string in storage

description:
The AuthoringStage RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) accept a posture field in the request message (visible in the proto at proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto). The handler test TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_PostureAccepted (line ~3744) verifies that a non-UNSPECIFIED posture is accepted without error.

However, the posture value from the request is never read, passed to authoring.ResolvePosture, or persisted to storage in any handler. The authoring package contains a fully-implemented posture detection system (posture.go, passes.go) that selects analytical pass sets based on posture — but this is wired up to zero RPC handlers.

This means:
1. The posture parameter is silently ignored for all RPCs even when callers set it explicitly.
2. PassesForStage / OfferedPasses are never called from any handler (dead code in practice).
3. The test TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_PostureAccepted gives false confidence that posture is being handled.

Either the posture should be wired into pass selection and persisted, or the proto field should carry a TODO comment explaining it is not yet used (to prevent callers from relying on it).

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (all stage handlers), internal/authoring/passes.go, internal/authoring/posture.go
title: posture field from SparkRequest is read but never used or persisted

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 77 calls h.backend.CreateSpec(c, msg.Slug, msg.Output.GetSeed(), defaultSpecPriority, defaultSpecComplexity) where defaultSpecPriority='p2' and defaultSpecComplexity='medium' are defined as package-level constants in internal/server/spec_handler.go lines 19-20.

SparkRequest (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 226) has no priority or complexity field. This means:
1. All specs created via the authoring funnel are silently assigned p2/medium with no way for the author to override at creation time.
2. The GraphService CreateSpec RPC (which presumably does expose priority/complexity) has different behaviour from the AuthoringService Spark RPC for what is semantically the same action.

This is a forward-compatibility trap: if priority/complexity need to be author-settable via the funnel in a later slice, adding them to SparkRequest is a COMPATIBLE change, but the existing consumers will not know they can/should set them.

Fix: Either (a) add optional priority and complexity fields to SparkRequest now (with the current defaults as fallback), or (b) add a proto comment to SparkRequest documenting that priority defaults to p2 and complexity to medium, so the omission is intentional and documented in the API contract.
title: Spark RPC creates spec with hard-coded priority/complexity, not exposed in request

description: TestTransitionStage_ApprovedGuard (memgraph/authoring_test.go line 4605) verifies that attempting to transition an approved spec to spark returns an error. However, it does not assert which sentinel error is returned — only that some error occurs. The implementation (authoring.go line 4060-4062) returns ErrSpecAlreadyApproved when from == 'approved', but the test uses require.Error without require.ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved). If a future refactor changes the guard to return ErrInvalidStageTransition or a generic error instead, the test still passes but the handler's stageError mapping would produce a different gRPC code (CodeInternal instead of CodeFailedPrecondition). Additionally, there is no integration test that calls TransitionStage on a spec in the 'superseded' stage to confirm the behavior (error or success) is well-defined. Suggested fix: change line 4618's require.Error to require.ErrorIs(err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyApproved); add a test for transitioning a superseded spec. Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go:4605-4620.
title: TransitionStage integration test missing for Approve guard on superseded spec

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-cmg.40 (same SafetyFlag.category string finding)
description: The SafetyFlag message in proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto (line 5107) defines category as a plain string field: string category = 1. The authoring package defines SafetyCategory as a typed string constant with two values (safety.go lines 1171-1178: 'security', 'data_loss'). At the proto layer all type safety is lost — any string can appear in the category field, and clients have no way to enumerate valid categories from the proto schema. The domain type storage.SafetyCategory (storage/authoring.go line 3955) is also a plain string alias with no validation. Suggested fix: promote SafetyCategory to a proto enum (SAFETY_CATEGORY_UNSPECIFIED, SAFETY_CATEGORY_SECURITY, SAFETY_CATEGORY_DATA_LOSS). This makes category values self-documenting and exhaustively checkable in generated client code.
title: SafetyFlag.category in proto is plain string — loses enum exhaustiveness checking at the proto boundary

close_reason: Backward transitions are intentional by design; ValidateAmendTransition handles amend-specific rules separately
description:
In internal/authoring/stages.go, ValidateTransition (line ~1563) is documented as handling both forward and backward transitions:

  // Backward (amend) transitions are allowed to any earlier stage.

However, the same function is called directly from TransitionStage in memgraph/authoring.go (line ~4063), which is the forward-progression storage method. This means TransitionStage (meant only for forward funnel progression) would accept a backward transition if the Cypher WHERE clause happened to match — because ValidateTransition allows backward transitions.

In practice the WHERE clause in the TransitionStage Cypher query guards against misuse:
  WHERE s.stage =  OR ( = "" AND ...)

But if a caller passes from="shape" to="spark", ValidateTransition succeeds and the Cypher query would set stage to "spark" if the node happens to be at "shape" — which is an unintended backward transition via the forward-only TransitionStage path.

The correct fix is to use a forward-only validator in TransitionStage (rejecting backward transitions outright), and reserve ValidateTransition (which permits backward) for callers that legitimately need both directions. Alternatively, rename ValidateTransition to make clear it permits backward movement.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line ~4063, internal/authoring/stages.go lines ~1563-1586
title: ValidateTransition allows backward transitions via the general forward-transition entry point

close_reason: Fixed: documented that responses return client output (no stored enrichment possible with current proto)
description:
In all stage RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose), the handler returns the response output field populated directly from the request input, not from what was persisted to storage. For example, internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 100-104 (Spark):

  return connect.NewResponse(&specv1.SparkResponse{
      Output:      msg.Output,   // echoed from request, not read back from storage
      ...
  })

This pattern repeats in Shape (line 141), Specify (line 180), and Decompose (line 234).

This creates an API contract ambiguity: consumers cannot tell whether the returned Output represents the canonical stored state (which could be enriched, normalised, or partially failed) or simply a mirror of what they sent. If the server were to normalise, validate-and-correct, or enrich the output before storage (e.g., auto-generating a slug for a DecompositionSlice without an ID), the response would not reflect the actual stored value — creating a silent divergence between the API response and stored truth.

RISKY: If a future implementation adds server-side enrichment (auto-filling questions, normalising scope entries), existing consumers relying on the echo behaviour will start receiving different data without any API version bump.

Fix: After successful storage, either (a) read back the stored output from storage and return that, or (b) add a clear proto comment on each response Output field stating 'This field reflects the input as received; the server does not enrich or modify stage output.' The comment should be on the response message definition in the proto, not just in the handler.
title: Stage RPC responses echo client input rather than returning stored/enriched state

close_reason: Fixed: ConsistencyIssue.issue_kind now uses IssueKind enum
description:
Two sets of values are stringly-typed with no enum or validated type:

  1. ConsistencyIssue.IssueKind (storage/authoring.go line 3943) is a plain string field. The proto comment (authoring.proto line 5077) suggests example values ('overlapping scope', 'conflicting invariant') but they are not enumerated. Any caller can put an arbitrary string here.

  2. Child spec priority/complexity (memgraph/authoring.go lines 4035-4036):
       defaultChildPriority   = "p2"
       defaultChildComplexity = "medium"
     These are plain string constants passed to CreateSpec. The storage.Backend.CreateSpec signature (not shown in this diff) accepts priority and complexity as plain strings. The format 'p2' implies an ordered priority scale (p0-p3?) but this is not enforced by the type system.

  Suggested fix for (1): define a typed IssueKind string type with constants for known kinds, or add an enum to the proto. Suggested fix for (2): define typed Priority and Complexity types in the storage package with validated constructors, matching the existing pattern for DecompositionStrategy.
title: ConsistencyIssue.IssueKind and child spec priority/complexity are stringly-typed with no validation

close_reason: Fixed: SparkOutput.scope_sniff now uses ScopeSniff enum
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 79 declares SparkOutput.scope_sniff as a plain string with the comment 'Early indication of whether the idea is small, medium, or large in scope.' The prompt template in internal/authoring/prompts.go line 963 also suggests 'tiny / small / medium / large / epic'.

Using a free-text string for what is clearly a finite vocabulary (tiny/small/medium/large/epic) is an API contract trap. Each of the following problems will emerge as consumers build tooling around this field:

1. Case sensitivity: 'Small' vs 'small' are different strings.
2. Vocabulary drift: a UI rendering 'epic' vs 'xl' vs 'extra-large' will diverge from the server's intent.
3. Linter/validation logic in storage or downstream tools must handle arbitrary strings rather than a closed enum.

The scope_sniff field parallels the pattern used well elsewhere in the proto (AuthoringStage, Posture, DecompositionStrategy are all typed enums). This field is the notable exception.

Fix: Define a ScopeSize enum (SCOPE_SIZE_UNSPECIFIED=0, TINY=1, SMALL=2, MEDIUM=3, LARGE=4, EPIC=5) and change SparkOutput.scope_sniff to type ScopeSize scope_size. The field rename from scope_sniff to scope_size is a BREAKING change if done after release, which is why it should be fixed before this PR merges. The free-text approach also conflicts with the prompt template which already uses the word 'sniff' informally — the data model should reflect the controlled vocabulary, not informal language.
title: SparkOutput.scope_sniff is untyped free-text where an enum would be more contract-stable

description:
In internal/authoring/passes.go lines 604-621, passRegistry is declared as map[string][]passConfig and keyed with the stage constants:

    var passRegistry = map[string][]passConfig{
      StageSpark:     {...},
      StageShape:     {...},
      StageSpecify:   {...},
      StageDecompose: {...},
    }

  Three issues:
  1. The map key type is string, not a typed Stage type. If StageSpark etc. were a typed Stage type this would be map[Stage][]passConfig, giving compile-time protection.
  2. StageApproved has no entry in passRegistry — this is intentional (approved has no passes) but is silent. A developer adding a new stage would need to know to update this map; there is no compile-time completeness check.
  3. promptRegistry in internal/authoring/prompts.go line 960 has the same pattern, also missing the 'approved' stage entry. These two registries could easily drift independently.

  Suggested fix: if Stage were a typed constant, a linter exhaustiveness check (e.g. exhaustive) could flag missing map entries. Alternatively, define a single stageConfig struct that bundles passConfig and prompts together, ensuring both are always updated together when a stage is added.
title: passRegistry uses plain string stage keys rather than typed Stage constants, weakening exhaustiveness

description: The memgraph integration test suite (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go) has no test for StoreSafetyFlags, StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, or StoreSimplicityFindings. These are thin wrappers over storeJSONProperty, but no test verifies that the JSON round-trip actually persists and that the spec node ends up with the property set. A regression in storeJSONProperty (e.g. wrong field name in the allowlist, marshal failure, or MATCH finding zero records) would go undetected at the storage layer. Given that safety flags are a security-adjacent feature — they gate whether dangerous specs are flagged before advancing — the absence of any persistence test for StoreSafetyFlags is particularly notable. Suggested fix: add at least one integration test that calls StoreSparkOutput (or StoreSafetyFlags), then verifies via a raw Cypher query or a GetSpec call that the property is present on the node. Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (no test covering lines 4207-4213 of diff).
title: No integration test for StoreSafetyFlags in memgraph/authoring_test.go

close_reason: Fixed: SafetyFlag.category now uses SafetyCategory enum
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 201 declares SafetyFlag.category as a plain string with the comment 'Category of safety concern (e.g. "security", "privacy", "compliance").' However, internal/authoring/safety.go lines 1175-1178 shows the implementation defines exactly two typed constants:

  SafetyCategorySecurity SafetyCategory = "security"
  SafetyCategoryDataLoss SafetyCategory = "data_loss"

The proto contract claims 'privacy' and 'compliance' are possible values via the comment, but the implementation does not emit them — only 'security' and 'data_loss' are ever produced. This is a documentation/contract mismatch.

More critically, using string for a field where the server only ever produces two known values (and clients must switch on the value to take action) is an API design regression. Consumers will write string-comparison code that is fragile to any future string change (e.g. 'data_loss' renamed to 'dataloss'). An enum would make additions explicit and additions-only would be COMPATIBLE.

Fix: Define SafetyCategory as an enum (SAFETY_CATEGORY_UNSPECIFIED=0, SECURITY=1, DATA_LOSS=2) and change SafetyFlag.category to use this enum. Update the proto comment to remove the fictional 'privacy' and 'compliance' examples. This is a breaking change if done post-release, so it should be done before this PR merges.
title: SafetyFlag.category uses free-text string in proto, not an enum, despite finite known values

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
AuthoringHandler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go lines 2602-2606) holds three fields:
    store     storage.AuthoringBackend
    backend   storage.Backend
    txBackend storage.TransactionalBackend // optional, may be nil

  The struct is only constructable via RegisterAuthoringService (line 2890), which panics if store or backend are nil and optionally sets txBackend via type assertion. This is a reasonable constructor pattern.

  However, AuthoringHandler is an exported type with exported zero value — nothing prevents external code from constructing &AuthoringHandler{} directly with nil store/backend. The nil check invariant is enforced only through RegisterAuthoringService, not the type.

  The optional txBackend is checked at every call via runInTxOrSequential (line 2997), which is correct. The concern is that the handler struct itself is not sealed.

  Suggested fix: make AuthoringHandler unexported (authoringHandler) since it is only exposed via the ConnectRPC handler registration path and the interface check on line 2608 serves as the compile-time contract. The exported RegisterAuthoringService function is the correct construction boundary.
title: AuthoringHandler exposes txBackend as optional nil field — handler validity not enforced at construction

close_reason: Stale: already addressed in prior fix batches
description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go SupersedeSpec (lines ~4217-4241), the implementation performs two separate GetSpec calls to validate both the old and new spec exist, then runs a combined MATCH+SET+CREATE query:

  GetSpec(ctx, slug)        // check old exists
  GetSpec(ctx, supersededBy) // check new exists
  // ... then:
  MATCH (old:...), (new:...) SET old.stage ... CREATE (new)-[:SUPERSEDES]->(old)

Between the pre-checks and the final combined query, either spec could be deleted by a concurrent operation, causing the MATCH to match only one node (or none). If new-spec disappears, the CREATE edge silently fails because CREATE requires both MATCH nodes. The RETURN old.slug still returns a record from the SET, masking the failed edge creation.

This is a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race. In the current single-user authoring flow the risk is low, but becomes relevant once multi-agent coordination (Phase 3 roadmap) is introduced.

Fix: wrap SupersedeSpec in a transaction (RunInTransaction), or add RETURN both slugs from the combined query and verify both are present in the result.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines ~4216-4241
title: SupersedeSpec has TOCTOU window between existence checks and combined mutation query

close_reason: Fixed: approved_at now stored on spec node via TransitionStage
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 335 declares ApproveResponse.approved_at as a Timestamp with the comment 'Timestamp at which the spec was approved.' The handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go line 255 sets this to timestamppb.Now() at response time.

The storage layer (internal/storage/authoring.go) does not include an approval timestamp in the AmendResult or any stored domain type. The TransitionStage call at line 249 records the stage transition, but the approval timestamp is generated ephemerally in the handler and returned to the client — it is never persisted.

This creates a contract violation: the API response implies the timestamp is the canonical approval time for the spec, but there is no way to retrieve this timestamp later (e.g., via a GetSpec RPC or audit log). If the client loses the response (network error, restart), the approval timestamp is permanently lost.

RISKY: Any consumer who treats approved_at as the authoritative approval record and stores it for audit purposes will have correct data only if the HTTP response was successfully received. A retry of the Approve RPC will return a different (later) timestamp.

Fix: (a) Add an approval_timestamp field to the storage domain type and persist it in TransitionStage or a separate StoreApprovalTimestamp call, so the timestamp survives the RPC, or (b) Change approved_at to documentation-only by adding a proto comment: 'This timestamp is approximate and computed at response time. It is not stored and cannot be retrieved after this response.' until the storage layer is updated.
title: ApproveResponse.approved_at is not stored; computed at response time only

close_reason: Fixed: single StageToProto() in authoring pkg, removed duplicate from handler
description:
Two identical stage-to-proto enum maps exist: stageToProtoEnum in internal/authoring/prompts.go (line 55-61) and stageToProto in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (line 425-431). Both map the same five stage string constants to the same AuthoringStage proto enum values. The handler version uses a plain string key (map[string]specv1.AuthoringStage) while the authoring version is identical but unexported (used only by PromptsToProto). This is outright duplication — any change to stage names or proto values must be made in both places.

Fix: export stageToProtoEnum from the authoring package (rename to StageToProto or similar) and delete the local stageToProto map in authoring_handler.go. The handler already imports the authoring package, so there is no new dependency cost.

Files:
- internal/authoring/prompts.go:55-61
- internal/server/authoring_handler.go:425-431
title: Duplicate stage-to-proto mapping defined in two packages

close_reason: Fixed: SpecifyResponse now populates red_team and consistency_issues from passes
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 287-296 declare SpecifyResponse with red_team (RedTeamFinding[]) and consistency_issues (ConsistencyIssue[]) fields. Similarly, ShapeResponse has peripheral_vision (PeripheralVisionItem[]) and DecomposeResponse has simplicity (SimplicityFinding[]).

However, internal/server/authoring_handler.go:
- Specify handler (line 180): returns SpecifyResponse with SafetyFlags and NextPrompts set, but red_team=nil and consistency_issues=nil always.
- Shape handler (line 141): returns ShapeResponse with peripheral_vision=nil always.
- Decompose handler (line 234): returns DecomposeResponse with simplicity=nil always.

The DecomposeResponse handler even has an explicit TODO at line 238: 'Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check analytical passes.'

The proto response messages imply these fields will contain data that consumers can act on. Consumers building UI around SpecifyResponse.red_team or ShapeResponse.peripheral_vision will see empty arrays in all cases currently, with no indication of whether (a) no issues were found, or (b) the pass was not run.

COMPATIBLE in isolation, but a forward-compatibility trap: once the passes are wired up, consumers that assumed empty = no issues must now handle the case where empty = not run vs empty = clean.

Fix: Add a field to each response indicating which passes were run, e.g., repeated string passes_run = N. Alternatively, populate the existing fields from the pass registry now (even with stub results) so the contract is established before tooling is built around the empty assumption. At minimum, add proto comments on each unimplemented field: 'Currently always empty; analytical pass not yet wired in this release.'
title: SpecifyResponse omits red_team and consistency_issues when analytical passes are not run
Intermediate sliceIntents slice is unnecessary in Decompose handler
Repeated safety-flag store pattern across four RPC handlers

close_reason: Fixed: stageToProto uses typed Stage keys via canonical function
description:
The stageToProto map in internal/server/authoring_handler.go (line 425) uses map[string]specv1.AuthoringStage, keyed by plain strings. The values (authoring.StageSpark etc.) are typed string constants, but the map key type is untyped string, which removes compiler-level protection against typos in key lookups.

    var stageToProto = map[string]specv1.AuthoringStage{
        authoring.StageSpark: ...,
        ...
    }

The lookup on line 254 uses the typed constant directly (stageToProto[authoring.StageApproved]), which is fine, but the lookup on line 281 converts storage.AuthoringStage back to string first: stageToProto[string(result.Stage)]. This indirection suggests the types are misaligned.

If the fix from the duplicate-map finding is applied (exporting stageToProtoEnum from the authoring package), this inconsistency is resolved automatically. If kept local, the map key should at least be documented as expecting authoring stage string constants to aid future readers.

This is informational: if the duplicate-map finding (spgr-cmg.44) is resolved, this issue disappears automatically.
