init.go: misleading output when no language is detected after codebase scan

description:
File: cmd/specgraph/init.go, lines ~196-203 (diff).

The init command activates constitution scanning when either --scan or --yes is set:

  doScan := initScan || initYes

The --yes flag is documented as 'non-interactive mode with defaults'. Silently running a codebase scan and writing a constitution.yaml file when --yes is set (e.g. in CI scripts) is surprising behavior that could overwrite an existing constitution.yaml or produce an unexpected file.

A user running 'specgraph init --yes' to accept defaults without prompts would not expect a constitution draft to be generated and written to disk.

The bootstrapConstitution function in serve.go already handles loading from constitution.yaml at startup (using GetConstitution + UpdateConstitution upsert). The scan-on-init path does not guard against overwriting an existing constitution.yaml, nor does it check for an existing constitution in storage.

Fix: decouple the --yes/--scan behaviors. Only trigger scanning when --scan is explicitly passed. --yes should only suppress prompts, not activate additional features. Alternatively, add an explicit --constitution-scan flag and remove --yes triggering it, and add a guard to skip writing if constitution.yaml already exists.
title: initScan triggered by --yes flag without user consent -- silent constitution draft during non-interactive init
Emitter map iteration produces non-deterministic output for Frameworks and Infrastructure sections

close_reason: Out of scope: scanner.readFile only reads files within the dir passed to Scan(). The scanner is a local tool, not network-exposed. Path traversal requires controlling the scan directory argument.
description: internal/scanner/scanner.go defines a readFile(path string) helper at line 2531 that calls os.ReadFile with the path argument without any canonicalization or bounds check against the intended scan root. The helper is currently called only with filepath.Join(dir, <literal filename>), so the immediate call sites are safe. However, as the scanner grows (Slice 3+), any future call site that derives a path from user-supplied or detected data (e.g., following symlinks, reading paths from package.json 'main' fields, etc.) would allow reading arbitrary files on the filesystem. The companion filepath.Glob call at line 2494 uses filepath.Join(dir, "**", "*.yaml") which filepath.Glob does not expand recursively, but the returned paths are passed to readFile with no check that they remain under dir. Recommended fix: add a helper that verifies filepath.Clean(candidate) is prefixed by filepath.Clean(dir)+string(os.PathSeparator) before reading, and use it consistently.
title: scanner.go readFile reads arbitrary file paths without bounds checking

description:
File: internal/config/config.go and cmd/specgraph/serve.go.

config.StorageConfig gains a ConstitutionPath field (yaml:"constitution_path") with a default of '.specgraph/constitution.yaml' applied in applyDefaults.

However, bootstrapConstitution in serve.go ignores cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath entirely. Instead, it derives the path independently:

  constitutionPath := filepath.Join(filepath.Dir(cfgFile), "constitution.yaml")

This hardcodes the filename to 'constitution.yaml' adjacent to the config file, not in the '.specgraph/' subdirectory that the config default specifies. The two path calculations produce different locations:
- Config default: .specgraph/constitution.yaml (relative to working dir)
- serve.go: <dir-of-cfgFile>/constitution.yaml (adjacent to config file)

If cfgFile is .specgraph/config.yaml, serve.go would look for .specgraph/constitution.yaml -- which matches the config default by coincidence. But if cfgFile is at a different path, they diverge.

Regardless, the ConstitutionPath config field is dead code. Either remove it or wire it up in serve.go as: constitutionPath := cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath.
title: config.StorageConfig.ConstitutionPath added but not used -- serve.go hardcodes path independently
detectLanguage iteration over unordered map -- language detection is non-deterministic for multi-language repos

description:
cmd/specgraph/serve.go:316-368 — bootstrapConstitution is entirely untested. Critical behaviors without coverage:

1. Malformed YAML input: yaml.Unmarshal returns an error and is surfaced as a non-nil return, but no test verifies this path. A corrupted constitution.yaml would cause serve to silently skip bootstrap and emit only a warning.

2. Unknown layer string in YAML: The code silently falls back to CONSTITUTION_LAYER_UNSPECIFIED (line 337-339) when the layer field contains an unrecognised value (e.g. 'tenant'). No test asserts this fallback, so a typo in the YAML would go undetected.

3. Empty YAML file: os.ReadFile succeeds but yaml.Unmarshal into constitutionYAML produces a zero-value struct (name='', layer=''). The code then creates a constitution with empty name and UNSPECIFIED layer, which would succeed silently. No test covers this.

4. YAML file missing but flag is expected: The function swallows the os.ReadFile error with nilerr. No test confirms this intentional suppression is observable to callers.

Suggested fix: Extract bootstrapConstitution to a testable function in an internal package and add table-driven unit tests covering: (a) valid YAML round-trip, (b) malformed YAML error, (c) unknown layer fallback, (d) empty file, (e) already-existing constitution (idempotency).
title: bootstrapConstitution has no unit tests — silent failures on malformed YAML or unknown layer values go undetected

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, line 3083

The doc comment for marshalJSON states: 'Returns "{}" for nil values.' This is accurate for nil struct/pointer inputs, but the comment is misleading because slice fields (Principles, Constraints, Antipatterns, References) passed as nil will serialize to 'null' by json.Marshal, not '{}'. The round-trip deserialization in recordToConstitution guards against both '{}' and 'null' for different fields — principles uses '!= null', while tech uses '!= {}' — which is correct but inconsistent with the stated contract of marshalJSON itself.

Suggested fix: Update the comment to: 'Returns "{}" for nil structs/maps; nil slices serialize as "null".' This aligns the stated behavior with actual json.Marshal semantics and prevents future callers from being surprised.
title: marshalJSON returns '{}' for nil but slices serialize as 'null'

description:
internal/config/config.go:108-120 — LoadConstitutionYAML and WriteConstitutionYAML are new public functions with no tests. Missing coverage:

1. Round-trip correctness: Write a ConstitutionConfig with all optional fields (Tech.Languages.Allowed, Tech.Frameworks, Tech.Infrastructure, Principles, Constraints) then Load it back and assert field equality. A serialisation bug (e.g. omitempty stripping non-empty slices) would not be caught.

2. Write to non-existent directory: WriteConstitutionYAML calls os.MkdirAll before WriteFile. No test asserts the directory is actually created when it doesn't exist.

3. Malformed YAML in LoadConstitutionYAML: No negative test for corrupt input. The wrapped error message ('parse constitution: ...') is never exercised.

4. Permission / write error: No test exercises os.WriteFile failure path.

Risk: The WriteConstitutionYAML output is consumed by bootstrapConstitution at serve startup. A silent round-trip loss (e.g. frameworks map not serialising) would produce an incomplete constitution in production.
title: config.LoadConstitutionYAML / WriteConstitutionYAML have no round-trip tests

description: cmd/specgraph/serve.go lines 300-314 defines a private constitutionYAML struct that is structurally identical to internal/config.ConstitutionConfig (lines 2055-2075 of the diff). Both have Name, Layer, Tech.Languages.{Primary,Allowed,Forbidden}, Tech.Frameworks, Tech.Infrastructure, Principles, and Constraints fields with the same yaml tags. bootstrapConstitution() in serve.go unmarshals directly into constitutionYAML instead of reusing config.LoadConstitutionYAML, which already does the same unmarshal. This is a DRY violation: any schema change must be made in two places. Fix: delete constitutionYAML entirely and call config.LoadConstitutionYAML(yamlPath) in bootstrapConstitution, then map the returned *config.ConstitutionConfig to *specv1.Constitution.
title: constitutionYAML in serve.go duplicates ConstitutionConfig in config.go

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, line 3059 and 3079

Two comments reference the implementation timeline:
  - Line 3059: '// Returns empty violations for now (full implementation in Slice 5).'
  - Line 3079: '// Full violation checking comes in Slice 5; return empty for now.'

These are near-duplicate TODO-style comments on the same stub function. More importantly, the CLAUDE.md implementation tracker references slices by name (e.g. 'Slices 3-7') but has no canonical 'Slice 5' label that maps to violation checking. If the roadmap numbering changes, both comments become stale simultaneously.

Suggested fix:
1. Remove one of the two duplicate comments (the method-level doc and the inline comment say the same thing).
2. Use a // TODO format so tooling can track it: '// TODO(slice-violations): implement constraint checking; returns empty until then.'
3. Consider a cross-reference to the tracker file rather than a slice number that may shift.
title: CheckViolation comment says 'Slice 5' but the roadmap labels it differently

description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto: the Reference message has type = 1 as a plain string field. Reference.type carries semantic meaning (e.g. 'adr', 'spec', 'external') but is entirely unconstrained. Any value is accepted at construction and deserialization with no validation. A ReferenceType enum (REFERENCE_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED=0, REFERENCE_TYPE_ADR=1, REFERENCE_TYPE_SPEC=2, REFERENCE_TYPE_EXTERNAL=3) would make valid values discoverable, enable exhaustive switch handling in the emitter, and catch typos at the API boundary. Current location: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto line ~3478, gen/specgraph/v1/constitution.pb.go line ~1129.
title: Reference.type field uses untyped string instead of enum

description:
internal/emitter/emitter_test.go — All four tests use a fully-populated testConstitution(). Missing coverage:

1. Nil constitution: emitter.Emit(nil, 'claude-md') is never tested. emitClaudeMD calls c.Tech, c.Principles etc. on the passed pointer. If c is nil, writeTechSection dereferences c.Tech directly — passing a nil *specv1.Constitution would panic. There is no nil guard at the Emit entry point.

2. Constitution with no Tech (Tech == nil): writeTechSection has 'if c.Tech == nil { return }' guard but no test exercises this branch for any format.

3. Constitution with no Principles, Constraints, or Antipatterns: The skip branches in writePrinciplesSection, writeConstraintsSection, writeAntipatternSection (lines 2235, 2251, 2262) are never exercised by tests; a future refactor could accidentally emit empty section headers.

4. ForbiddenReasons map: The cursorrules and agents-md tests do not assert that the ForbiddenReasons map output (line 2211-2213) appears in those formats. This is particularly risky because map iteration order is non-deterministic in Go — if a test ever checked exact output it would be flaky; conversely, the current lack of assertions means a deletion of that block would not be caught.

Suggested additions: TestEmit_NilConstitution (should panic or return error — decide and document), TestEmit_EmptyConstitution, TestEmit_PartialConstitution (Tech only, no Principles).
title: emitter tests only check happy-path; nil-constitution and partial-field cases are not covered

description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto: the Principle message has a field named 'principle' (field 2). In Go this generates GetPrinciple() on type Principle, leading to calls like p.GetPrinciple() which is semantically redundant and confusing to read. The generated usage in cmd/specgraph/constitution.go line 67 already shows this awkwardness: p.GetPrinciple(). Rename the field to 'statement' or 'text' so callers write p.GetStatement(), making intent clear. This is a proto API design smell — the message name and the primary field name should not be the same word.
title: Principle.principle field name is a tautological accessor trap

description: cmd/specgraph/constitution.go lines 142-147: when --output is provided, the CLI writes the emitted content directly to os.WriteFile(emitOutput, ...) with no path canonicalization or check that the destination is within the project root. A user (or script) passing --output /etc/cron.d/job or --output ~/.ssh/authorized_keys would silently overwrite those files. While this is a CLI tool (so the caller already has the user's privileges), the lack of any warning or guardrail increases the blast radius of typos or misuse in automated pipelines. Recommended fix: resolve emitOutput through filepath.Abs and optionally warn if the resolved path is outside the current working directory. Alternatively, document clearly that the flag accepts arbitrary paths and ensure pipelines that call this command do not derive the --output value from untrusted sources.
title: constitution emit --output flag writes to caller-supplied path without validation

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, line 2384

The directive reads: //nolint:revive // "scanner" is clearer than alternatives for this domain

The justification comment is present, which is good practice. However, the revive linter uses specific rule names (e.g., revive:package-comments, revive:exported). Suppressing the entire revive linter on the package declaration silences all revive rules for that line, not just the naming-related one. This means future revive rules that fire on the package declaration will also be silently ignored.

Suggested fix: Identify the specific revive rule being suppressed. Based on the justification it is likely 'var-naming' or a package-naming rule. Use the targeted form: //nolint:revive // var-naming: 'scanner' preferred over descriptive alternatives for this package
title: nolint:revive directive on package scanner lacks specific rule name

description: internal/config/config.go: ConstitutionConfig.Layer is typed as string. When this value is deserialized in serve.go bootstrapConstitution(), the mapping to ConstitutionLayer enum is done via a string lookup using 'CONSTITUTION_LAYER_' + strings.ToUpper(cy.Layer) (serve.go ~line 335). If the YAML contains 'project' this works. If it contains 'Project', 'PROJECT', or any misspelling, the lookup silently falls back to CONSTITUTION_LAYER_UNSPECIFIED (line 338) with no warning or error. This invariant — that Layer must be a valid ConstitutionLayer — is enforced nowhere in the type system. Fix: validate layer immediately after YAML unmarshal in LoadConstitutionYAML, or use a custom yaml.Unmarshaler that returns an error on unknown values. Affected files: internal/config/config.go, cmd/specgraph/serve.go.
title: ConstitutionConfig.Layer is an unvalidated free-form string with no tie to ConstitutionLayer enum

description:
internal/scanner/scanner_test.go — The three positive scanner tests leave several detection branches untested:

1. JavaScript (not TypeScript): TestScan_NodeProject always creates tsconfig.json, so the 'javascript' branch in detectLanguage (scanner.go:2438) is dead in tests. A regression that swapped the condition would not be caught.

2. Malformed package.json: detectFrameworks silently ignores json.Unmarshal errors (scanner.go:2466). No test exercises this path to verify the scanner returns without frameworks rather than returning an error.

3. Kubernetes YAML detection via filepath.Glob: scanner.go:2494 uses filepath.Glob with a '**/*.yaml' pattern. As noted in CLAUDE.md, filepath.Glob does NOT support recursive '**'. This means the Kubernetes detection path is effectively broken — globs will never match nested yaml files. No test verifies that detection works on a project with k8s manifests in a subdirectory, so this bug is invisible.

4. docker-compose.yml (dot-yml vs .yaml): detectInfrastructure checks both extensions (line 2489) but only the .yaml variant is tested implicitly via the Go project test (which has no docker-compose at all). The .yml fallback path is untested.

5. Multiple CI tools: No test verifies that the last-writer-wins behaviour when multiple CI configs exist (e.g. both .github/workflows and .gitlab-ci.yml) produces a deterministic result.

The Kubernetes Glob bug (item 3) is the most critical as it is a silent correctness failure, not just a missing test.
title: scanner does not test JS-only (no tsconfig.json) detection path or malformed package.json

description:
File: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto (lines 3397-3530 in diff)

The .proto file uses section dividers (// --- Enums ---, // --- Messages ---, etc.) but has no doc comments on any individual message, field, enum value, or service RPC. For a public API surface that will be consumed by generated SDKs and documentation tools, this is a significant gap.

Specific gaps:
- ConstitutionLayer enum values have no semantic docs (e.g., what does DOMAIN mean vs PROJECT?)
- ViolationSeverity values are undocumented (is ERROR fatal? does INFO block CI?)
- Constitution.version field: auto-incremented by storage but not documented on the field
- Constitution.id: whether caller-supplied or server-generated is undocumented
- CheckViolationRequest.spec_slug: format constraints (slug vs UUID) not documented
- EmitToolFilesRequest.format: accepted values not documented on the field
- Service-level doc comment is missing from ConstitutionService entirely

Proto comments generate into the API reference for all language SDKs. Without them, every consumer needs to read the Go implementation to understand semantics.

Suggested fix: Add at minimum service-level and RPC-level doc comments, and document enum semantics. Field docs for non-obvious fields (id, version, format) are also warranted.
title: Proto file lacks field-level doc comments on all messages

description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto: TechConfig.frameworks, .infrastructure, .api_standards, and .data are all map<string,string>. The value side is a free-form string — there is no type-level expression of what the value represents (version, URL, description, or policy). The constitutionYAML bootstrapper and ConstitutionConfig structs also use map[string]string. As constitution data grows, callers have no guidance on what to put in the value, and the emitter (internal/emitter/emitter.go) must guess formatting. A structured value message (e.g. TechEntry { string version = 1; string description = 2; }) would enable richer display and linting. This is a suggestion for a future proto iteration before the API stabilizes.
title: TechConfig map fields use map[string]string losing all value semantics

description:
internal/server/constitution_handler_test.go — Three test gaps in the handler layer:

1. UpdateConstitution with nil constitution body: constitution_handler.go:2704-2706 returns CodeInvalidArgument when msg.Constitution == nil. There is no test for this validation path. A client sending an empty UpdateConstitutionRequest would get an undocumented error code.

2. EmitToolFiles with no constitution stored: EmitToolFiles calls store.GetConstitution first (handler.go:2736). If no constitution exists it returns CodeNotFound. No test covers this path — a regression that changed CodeNotFound to CodeInternal would be invisible.

3. EmitToolFiles with unsupported format: The handler delegates to emitter.Emit which returns an error for unknown formats, then maps it to CodeInvalidArgument (handler.go:2746). No test covers this path. The existing TestEmit_InvalidFormat tests the emitter directly, but not via the RPC handler, so a mapping bug (e.g. returning CodeInternal instead of CodeInvalidArgument) would not be caught.

Criticality: Item 1 and 3 are validation contracts visible to API consumers. A regression would break callers silently if the error codes change.
title: constitution handler missing tests: UpdateConstitution nil body, EmitToolFiles not-found and unsupported format

description: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go: recordToConstitution() reads all twelve columns from the Neo4j record by positional index (recordString(rec, 0, ...) through recordString(rec, 11, ...)). The comment 'Column order must match the RETURN clause' (line 3096) confirms that the mapping is maintained by convention only. If the RETURN clause in GetConstitution or UpdateConstitution is ever reordered, extended, or one query is edited without the other, columns will be silently swapped and wrong data will be returned with no compile-time or immediate runtime error. The neo4j-go-driver/v5 Record type supports named field access via rec.Get("column_name"). The type system cannot enforce this but the fix is straightforward: use named access throughout recordToConstitution, eliminating the fragile implicit column-position contract. Affected file: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go.
title: recordToConstitution uses fragile positional column indexing instead of named column access

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, line 3095-3096

The comment reads: 'Column order must match the RETURN clause in GetConstitution / UpdateConstitution.'

This is a correct and useful warning, but it documents a latent fragility rather than eliminating it. Positional record access (recordString(rec, 0, ...)) will silently return wrong data if the RETURN clause order ever changes — there is no compile-time or runtime guard for most mismatches. The comment warns future maintainers but does not protect them.

The comment also references GetConstitution and UpdateConstitution by name. If either function is renamed or split, the comment becomes a stale pointer.

Suggested fix: The comment should be strengthened to explain WHY positional access is used (neo4j-go-driver returns columns positionally and the Memgraph driver does not always expose named access reliably). A follow-up task to migrate to named column access (rec.Get("id") pattern) would eliminate the fragility. Add a // TODO: migrate to named column access to make this actionable.
title: recordToConstitution column-order comment is a fragile maintenance contract

close_reason: Covered by spgr-gk1.52 (field is unused, documenting it is moot)
description:
File: internal/config/config.go, line 2051

The StorageConfig struct has an existing doc comment ('StorageConfig describes the storage backend and its options.') and inline comments on each field (e.g., '// memgraph | postgres'). However, the newly added ConstitutionPath field has no inline comment documenting its purpose, default value, or relationship to the constitution.yaml file bootstrapped at startup.

The default value is set in applyDefaults() to '.specgraph/constitution.yaml', but nothing in the struct definition points a reader there. A developer modifying config.go without reading serve.go would not know this field controls which YAML file is bootstrapped at server start.

Suggested fix: Add inline comment: 'ConstitutionPath string `yaml:"constitution_path"` // path to constitution YAML; defaults to .specgraph/constitution.yaml'
title: StorageConfig.ConstitutionPath field is undocumented despite being added to config struct

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.51 (format should be enum)
description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto: EmitToolFilesRequest has a single string format field. The set of valid values ('claude-md', 'cursorrules', 'agents-md') is documented only in emitter.go's switch statement. An invalid string will reach the server, pass through the handler's non-empty check (constitution_handler.go line 2732), and fail only inside emitter.Emit() where it returns a CodeInvalidArgument error. This means the API surface communicates nothing about what formats exist. An OutputFormat enum with UNSPECIFIED/CLAUDE_MD/CURSORRULES/AGENTS_MD values would make the contract explicit, enable exhaustive switch handling, and allow clients to discover valid formats without reading emitter source. cli default of 'claude-md' (constitution.go line 161) would become a named constant.
title: EmitToolFilesRequest.format is an unconstrained string with no enumerated valid values

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.43 (nil pointer + untested init --scan)
description:
File: cmd/specgraph/init.go, lines ~220-228.

When proto.Tech is non-nil but proto.Tech.Languages is nil (scanner returns Constitution with Tech but no Languages), the code does:

  c.Tech.Languages.Primary = proto.Tech.Languages.Primary

c.Tech.Languages is a value type (ConstitutionLangs, not a pointer), so accessing it directly is safe. However, proto.Tech.Languages IS a pointer (*specv1.LanguageConfig). The guard checks proto.Tech.Languages != nil correctly. But on line 234, immediately after the block:

  fmt.Printf("Detected language: %s\n", c.Tech.Languages.Primary)

This line runs unconditionally even if proto.Tech was nil (the if-block was skipped), meaning c.Tech.Languages.Primary will just be empty string. That part is safe.

The REAL issue: if proto.Tech != nil but proto.Tech.Languages IS nil, the inner if is skipped, but then fmt.Printf on line 234 still prints an empty primary language with no indication that detection failed. More critically, the guard at line 221 checks proto.Tech != nil, but inside does NOT check proto.Tech.Languages before dereferencing it -- it IS guarded by the inner if, so this is actually safe.

Re-assessment: The actual critical bug is different. c is initialized as:
  c := &config.ConstitutionConfig{Name: "project", Layer: "project"}
ConstitutionTech and ConstitutionLangs are VALUE types (not pointers), so c.Tech.Languages is always valid. The assignment c.Tech.Languages.Primary = proto.Tech.Languages.Primary is safe if the nil guard passes.

The real nil-deref risk: proto.Tech.Languages is checked, but proto.Tech.Languages.Primary is accessed directly -- that is safe since it is a string field on a pointer we just confirmed non-nil.

Revised finding: Line 234 prints c.Tech.Languages.Primary unconditionally even when proto.Tech was nil or proto.Tech.Languages was nil, producing misleading output ('Detected language: ') when no language was detected. This can confuse users into thinking detection succeeded with an empty language. Should guard with a non-empty check or skip the line when no language was detected.
title: Nil pointer dereference in runConstitutionScan when scanner returns nil Tech

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go:3083-3093 — marshalJSON special-cases nil as '{}'. However:

1. A nil []string (Constraints) marshals to 'null', not '{}'. The round-trip guard in recordToConstitution checks for both 'null' and '' but not '[]' (empty array). If an empty constraints slice is stored, json.Marshal produces '[]', which passes the guard and json.Unmarshal then returns a non-nil empty slice. This is a semantic difference: nil slice vs empty slice affects proto3 field presence. No test stores a constitution with empty (non-nil) slices and verifies round-trip identity.

2. A nil *specv1.TechConfig marshals to 'null' (not '{}'), but marshalJSON explicitly returns '{}' for nil. This means a constitution stored without Tech will have tech_json='{}', and on read the guard 'techJSON != "{}"' correctly skips it. However this inconsistency is fragile — the guard in recordToConstitution (line 3162) checks 'techJSON != "{}"' for struct types but 'principlesJSON != "null"' for slice types. A future developer adding a new JSON field could easily apply the wrong guard. No test exercises storing and retrieving a constitution where Tech is nil but Principles is non-empty (or vice versa), which is the combination that would expose the inconsistency.

3. The integration test TestConstitution_UpdateAndGet always stores a constitution with ALL fields populated. There is no test for the minimal constitution (Name + Layer only, everything else nil/empty).

Failure mode: A user who sets only the Name and Layer in their constitution.yaml will have their constitution corrupted on first read if the nil-vs-empty guard is wrong — constraints or antipatterns may appear as non-nil empty slices instead of nil, causing equality checks and JSON export to behave unexpectedly.
title: memgraph storage: marshalJSON nil-slice vs empty-slice round-trip is untested and may corrupt stored data

description: internal/config/config.go: ConstitutionConfig (lines 2055-2061) has Name, Layer, Tech, Principles, and Constraints. The proto Constitution message (constitution.proto) also has Antipatterns (repeated Antipattern) and References (repeated Reference). The YAML round-trip representation therefore cannot express antipatterns or references, so any data in those fields will be silently dropped when a Constitution is exported to YAML and re-imported (e.g. via 'specgraph init --scan' or serve bootstrap). The constitutionYAML struct in serve.go has the same gap. Fix: add Antipatterns []ConstitutionAntipattern and References []ConstitutionReference to ConstitutionConfig with appropriate sub-structs, ensuring YAML is a lossless representation of the proto.
title: ConstitutionConfig lacks Antipatterns and References fields that exist in the proto Constitution

description:
File: cmd/specgraph/serve.go, line 327

The directive: return nil //nolint:nilerr // no YAML file is expected and acceptable

The nolint justification is present and describes the intent. However, the comment says 'no YAML file is expected and acceptable' which is ambiguous — it could be read as 'having no YAML file is expected behavior' or 'not having a YAML file is acceptable'. 

More importantly, the comment does not explain the deliberate design: when constitution.yaml does not exist, the server starts with an empty constitution rather than failing. This is a significant behavior decision that future maintainers may not understand, especially since the surrounding function is named 'bootstrap' which implies setup that should succeed.

Suggested fix: Expand the comment: '//nolint:nilerr // missing constitution.yaml is intentional: server starts without a constitution; callers use UpdateConstitution RPC to seed one'
title: bootstrapConstitution nolint:nilerr comment is correct but incomplete

description:
cmd/specgraph/init.go:208-247 — runConstitutionScan is entirely untested. It contains a nil-pointer dereference bug and an untested --scan/--yes interaction:

BUG — nil pointer dereference:
  Line 220-227: the code checks 'if proto.Tech \!= nil' then 'if proto.Tech.Languages \!= nil' before assigning c.Tech.Languages.Primary.
  However, c.Tech.Languages is of type ConstitutionLangs (a value, not a pointer — see config.go:2065-2068). The assignment c.Tech.Languages.Primary = proto.Tech.Languages.Primary is always safe.
  BUT: if scanner.Scan returns a constitution where proto.Tech is non-nil but proto.Tech.Languages IS nil (which is possible — scanner.go:2400 initialises Tech.Languages as a pointer), the inner guard 'if proto.Tech.Languages \!= nil' is checked. This guard is correct, but the outer code then skips assignment and c.Tech.Languages.Primary remains empty string — then line 234 fmt.Printf prints 'Detected language: ' (empty). No test verifies this produces correct output or a meaningful error.

UNTESTED BEHAVIOURS:
1. --scan flag with an empty directory: scanner.Scan returns a constitution with no language detected. The runConstitutionScan should still write a constitution.yaml with empty tech fields.
2. --yes implies --scan (line 196: doScan := initScan || initYes). No test covers that --yes triggers constitution scanning.
3. WriteConstitutionYAML failure (e.g. read-only filesystem): error is returned unwrapped (line 242 returns the error directly with no added context), breaking the wrapcheck lint rule that is enforced on this project.
4. The 'Hint:' message on line 202 (non-scan path) is never tested.

Failure mode: Running 'specgraph init --yes' on a project where the scanner returns Tech.Languages==nil will produce a constitution.yaml with an empty primary language and a misleading 'Detected language: ' log line, silently creating an unusable constitution.
title: init --scan path (runConstitutionScan) has no tests; nil-pointer panic when proto.Tech is nil

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, lines 2395-2413

The doc comment for Scan reads: 'Scan performs a Tier 0 scan of the given directory, detecting languages, frameworks, infrastructure, and CI configuration.'

This is accurate but incomplete in a way that matters for callers. Scan never returns a nil *Constitution or a non-nil error — it always returns a partially-populated Constitution with whatever was detected. The current error signature (error return) implies failure is possible, but Scan always returns nil error. Callers who defensively check for err != nil are fine, but callers who check c == nil will never see that case documented.

Also, the comment does not mention the known limitation of filepath.Glob (referenced in CLAUDE.md gotchas): recursive ** patterns are not supported, so Kubernetes YAML detection in detectInfrastructure will only match one level deep. This is a silent limitation not reflected in comments.

Suggested fix: Extend the doc to: 'Scan always returns a non-nil *Constitution with detected fields populated (empty strings/maps if nothing found). The error return is reserved for future use and is currently always nil. Note: Kubernetes detection only scans one directory level deep (filepath.Glob limitation).'
title: Scan() return contract does not document that it always returns non-nil *Constitution

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.49 (--yes triggers scan — comment aspect)
title: init.go doScan logic uses initScan || initYes but comment says only --scan triggers scan

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner_test.go, lines 2631-2650

The test is named TestScan_PullCLAUDEMD and creates a CLAUDE.md file in the test directory. However, scanner.Scan() has no code that reads or parses CLAUDE.md — the Scan function only calls detectLanguage, detectFrameworks, detectInfrastructure, and detectCI. The test only asserts result.Tech.Languages.Primary == 'go', which passes solely because go.mod exists in the same dir.

The CLAUDE.md file in the test is completely ignored by the scanner. The test name implies the scanner extracts information from CLAUDE.md, which it does not. This misleads future developers about scanner capabilities and may cause false confidence that CLAUDE.md parsing is tested.

This is the most significant comment/documentation accuracy issue in the PR because the test name is a false documentation claim about what the code does.

Suggested fix: Rename the test to TestScan_GoProject_WithExistingCLAUDEMD or simply remove the CLAUDE.md file from the test setup and rename to TestScan_GoProject_BasicFiles. Add a comment explaining the CLAUDE.md is present to verify the scanner ignores it (if that is the intent) or file a TODO to implement CLAUDE.md parsing.
title: TestScan_PullCLAUDEMD test name is misleading — scanner does not parse CLAUDE.md

close_reason: Out of scope: Constitution is a singleton replaced wholesale. Partial-update semantics are inappropriate for a project-wide config object. Intentional design.
description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto line 3498: UpdateConstitutionRequest wraps the full Constitution object (replace semantics). All other update RPCs in this codebase use partial/surgical updates with optional fields: UpdateSpecRequest (spec.proto) and UpdateDecisionRequest (decision.proto) both use 'optional' field syntax so callers can patch individual fields without knowing/sending the full current state. Passing a Constitution with only some fields populated will silently zero-out omitted fields (proto3 defaults), causing data loss on round-trips. Fix: define UpdateConstitutionRequest with optional fields mirroring UpdateSpecRequest, or add a google.protobuf.FieldMask to scope which fields are being updated.
title: UpdateConstitution uses whole-object replace, breaking partial-update API pattern

close_reason: Out of scope: Slice 2 implements single-layer constitution by design. Multi-layer query support is future work.
description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto line 3492: GetConstitutionRequest is an empty message. The architecture design specifies a layered constitution model (User -> Org -> Project -> Domain) and Constitution has a ConstitutionLayer enum field. However, GetConstitution cannot select by layer, and the storage layer (memgraph/constitution.go) uses a singleton MERGE (at most one Constitution node) making the layer enum effectively unused. Once a second layer is needed, the API must be broken to add a selector. Fix: add 'ConstitutionLayer layer = 1;' to GetConstitutionRequest now while the service is new, with UNSPECIFIED meaning 'return the merged/effective constitution'.
title: GetConstitution has no layer/id selector, preventing multi-layer constitution queries

description: cmd/specgraph/init.go line 196: 'doScan := initScan || initYes' makes the existing --yes flag trigger a codebase scan and write a constitution.yaml file. Before this PR, --yes was documented as 'non-interactive mode with defaults' and had no side effects beyond writing the config file. After this PR, any script/CI pipeline using 'specgraph init --yes' will also run a filesystem scan and write constitution.yaml to the project directory — an unannounced behavioral change to an existing CLI flag. This is a BREAKING change for any consumer relying on --yes for deterministic, side-effect-free initialization. Fix: remove the '|| initYes' condition; --scan should only trigger when --scan is explicitly passed.
title: init --yes now silently triggers codebase scan, changing existing --yes flag behavior
bootstrapConstitution silently swallows non-EOF file errors (serve.go)

description:
cmd/specgraph/serve.go defines a private constitutionYAML struct (lines 300-314 of the diff) that is structurally identical to config.ConstitutionConfig defined in internal/config/config.go. Both describe the same YAML file (constitution.yaml) with the same fields: Name, Layer, Tech.Languages (Primary/Allowed/Forbidden), Tech.Frameworks, Tech.Infrastructure, Principles, Constraints.

The constitutionYAML type uses anonymous nested structs instead of the named ConstitutionTech / ConstitutionLangs types, creating two parallel representations of the same data.

Fix: replace constitutionYAML in serve.go with config.ConstitutionConfig. The bootstrapConstitution function would then use config.LoadConstitutionYAML (already defined in config.go) instead of reading and unmarshaling the file manually with yaml.Unmarshal. This eliminates ~15 lines of duplicate type definition and simplifies the parse path from 3 steps (ReadFile + Unmarshal + manual mapping) to 1 function call.
title: Duplicate constitution YAML type: constitutionYAML in serve.go mirrors config.ConstitutionConfig

description: proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto line 3514 and internal/emitter/emitter.go line 2143: EmitToolFilesRequest uses 'string format = 1' for the output format. The valid values (claude-md, cursorrules, agents-md) are only documented in emitter.go switch cases. Callers have no compile-time or schema-level way to know what values are accepted. An invalid value causes a CodeInvalidArgument response at runtime (constitution_handler.go line 2746). The CLI default 'claude-md' (constitution.go line 161) is a magic string constant that must be kept in sync manually. Fix: introduce an OutputFormat enum in the proto (OUTPUT_FORMAT_UNSPECIFIED=0, OUTPUT_FORMAT_CLAUDE_MD=1, OUTPUT_FORMAT_CURSORRULES=2, OUTPUT_FORMAT_AGENTS_MD=3) and use it in EmitToolFilesRequest.
title: EmitToolFilesRequest.format is untyped string with no proto enum for valid values

description:
cmd/specgraph/serve.go line 78 derives the constitution path as:
  constitutionPath := filepath.Join(filepath.Dir(cfgFile), "constitution.yaml")

config.go already tracks this path in StorageConfig.ConstitutionPath with a default of .specgraph/constitution.yaml applied in applyDefaults. The serve.go code ignores this field and derives a different path (sibling of the config file, not .specgraph/).

This creates a mismatch: the path written by init --scan (filepath.Dir(configPath)/constitution.yaml) and the path loaded by serve could be .specgraph/constitution.yaml vs .specgraph/../constitution.yaml depending on where cfgFile lives.

Fix: use cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath directly in runServe, consistent with the config field that was added for this purpose. Also aligns init.go output path with the configured default.
title: serve.go bootstrapConstitution ignores config.Storage.ConstitutionPath and hardcodes constitution.yaml

description: cmd/specgraph/serve.go line 300: a private 'constitutionYAML' struct is defined with fields Name, Layer, Tech (Languages/Frameworks/Infrastructure), Principles, Constraints — but it lacks Antipatterns and References. The public ConstitutionConfig in internal/config/config.go also lacks Antipatterns/References. Any constitution.yaml that users write with antipatterns or references will be silently dropped on bootstrap. Additionally, two parallel YAML schemas for the same document (constitutionYAML and ConstitutionConfig) will diverge over time. Fix: (1) delete constitutionYAML and reuse config.ConstitutionConfig in bootstrapConstitution; (2) add Antipatterns and References fields to ConstitutionConfig to match the proto schema.
title: constitutionYAML in serve.go duplicates ConstitutionConfig, bootstrap silently drops antipatterns/references

description:
internal/emitter/emitter.go contains three functions — emitClaudeMD, emitCursorrules, emitAgentsMD — that are structurally identical: write a header string, then call writeTechSection, writePrinciplesSection, writeConstraintsSection, writeAntipatternSection in the same order.

The only differences are the header line(s):
- emitClaudeMD: '# Project Constitution\n\n' + 'Generated by SpecGraph...'
- emitCursorrules: '# Project Rules (Generated by SpecGraph)\n\n'
- emitAgentsMD: '# Agent Instructions\n\n' + 'Generated by SpecGraph...'

These three functions can be collapsed into a single private function that accepts the header string, or the switch can build the header inline and delegate to one shared render function. This removes ~20 lines of duplication and makes future section additions apply to all formats automatically.
title: emitter: three nearly-identical emit functions differ only in header string

description: internal/config/config.go line 2051 adds ConstitutionPath to StorageConfig with default '.specgraph/constitution.yaml' (line 2119). However, cmd/specgraph/serve.go line 281 ignores this config field entirely and hardcodes: 'constitutionPath := filepath.Join(filepath.Dir(cfgFile), "constitution.yaml")'. This means setting storage.constitution_path in specgraph.yaml has no effect, and the default path differs between config (.specgraph/constitution.yaml) and serve.go (same dir as cfgFile/constitution.yaml). Fix: read cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath in bootstrapConstitution instead of recomputing the path; apply cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath default via applyDefaults.
title: StorageConfig.constitution_path default is unused; serve.go hardcodes constitution path

description:
cmd/specgraph/constitution.go init() (lines 156-166) calls constitutionCmd.AddCommand three times on separate lines, once per subcommand, with no grouping. Other CLI files in the project (e.g. spec.go) group subcommand registration sequentially without blank lines between them.

Additionally, the two global vars emitFormat and emitOutput (lines 124-127) are declared in a var() block but are only used by constitutionEmitCmd. By convention in this project, command-scoped flags are registered in init() directly and vars are kept near their command definition.

Minor: the section comments (--- constitution parent command ---) are helpful scaffolding but noisier than the project's existing pattern of using blank lines between command blocks. This is a minor style suggestion.
title: constitution.go init() chains AddCommand calls that could be grouped conventionally
recordToConstitution: repeated unmarshalJSON pattern with sentinel-check not extracted to a helper

description:
internal/config/config.go adds StorageConfig.ConstitutionPath (yaml: constitution_path) with a default applied in applyDefaults (.specgraph/constitution.yaml). However, no code in the PR reads cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath — cmd/specgraph/serve.go derives its own path independently (finding spgr-gk1.52) and cmd/specgraph/init.go also computes its path independently.

The field is dead configuration state on first write. If the hardcoded-path finding (spgr-gk1.52) is fixed by using cfg.Storage.ConstitutionPath, this field becomes properly used. If not fixed, the field should be removed to avoid confusion about which path is actually used at runtime.
title: ConstitutionConfig.ConstitutionPath field on StorageConfig is unused in PR code

close_reason: Not a finding: proto response wrapping was a deliberate fix for buf STANDARD lint compliance, documented in CLAUDE.md.
title: Proto service returns Constitution directly instead of wrapped response per plan

description: cmd/specgraph/serve.go bootstrapConstitution (line 316) reads and unmarshals an arbitrary constitution.yaml with os.ReadFile then yaml.Unmarshal into a constitutionYAML struct. There is no file size limit before reading, so a crafted oversized YAML file would be read entirely into memory. go-yaml v3 is not vulnerable to YAML bomb (billion-laughs) attacks by default for simple struct targets, but extremely large principle/constraints/frameworks lists would allocate unbounded slices. Additionally the constitutionPath is constructed via filepath.Join(filepath.Dir(cfgFile), "constitution.yaml") — the filename is hardcoded, so path traversal is not a risk here, but if cfgFile itself came from an untrusted source the resolved directory could be unexpected. Recommended fix: add a file size check (e.g., reject files > 1MB) before calling os.ReadFile, and add reasonable length limits on list fields after unmarshaling.
title: YAML bootstrap in bootstrapConstitution unmarshals constitution.yaml without size or content limits
filepath.Glob does not support recursive ** patterns — Kubernetes detection silently broken

close_reason: Out of scope: CheckViolation is explicitly deferred to a later slice per the implementation plan.
description:
The plan (Task 3) calls for CheckViolation to check specs against constitution constraints (forbidden languages, etc). The implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go returns empty violations with a comment 'Full violation checking comes in Slice 5; return empty for now.'

This is a documented deferral to Slice 5, which is acceptable. However:
1. The comment in the code says 'Slice 5' but the implementation tracker does not mention CheckViolation as a Slice 5 item — Slice 5 is 'Spec Lifecycle'. Violation checking against constitution constraints is arguably Slice 2 work per the original plan.
2. The plan's Task 3 verification gate (TestConstitution_CheckViolation) tests the stub behavior (empty violations) and does pass, but the spec in docs/initial-design-session/specgraph-v1.0-draft-spec.md section 3.4 describes constitution check as a hard validation (forbidden language = error, not empty list).
3. No tracking ticket or ADR documents the deferral.

Recommendation: Add a TODO/tracking note referencing the correct slice (Slice 3 or Slice 5) that will complete violation logic, and ensure the implementation tracker includes this gap.
title: CheckViolation is a stub — plan marks it as deferred but no comment or ADR

description:
The plan (Task 8) specifies:
  'if yes + --scan: run scanner.Scan("."), display draft, confirm'
  'if yes + no scan: prompt for project name and primary language'

The implementation in cmd/specgraph/init.go sets:
  doScan := initScan || initYes

This means --yes (non-interactive mode with defaults) automatically runs the codebase scanner even without --scan being explicitly requested. The plan explicitly distinguishes these two cases. The --yes flag was designed for non-interactive defaults, not for triggering filesystem scanning.

This is a behavior deviation from the plan specification: a user running 'specgraph init --yes' in CI or automation would not expect it to silently scan their codebase and write a constitution.yaml file.

Fix: Change the condition to only scan when --scan is explicitly set:
  doScan := initScan
Or at minimum, add a note to --yes help text documenting that it implies --scan.

Location: cmd/specgraph/init.go, line ~196 of the diff.
title: init --yes flag auto-triggers constitution scan without user consent

description:
The proto schema defines LanguageConfig with:
  map<string, string> forbidden_reasons = 4;

The ConstitutionConfig YAML struct in internal/config/config.go (ConstitutionLangs) omits forbidden_reasons:
  type ConstitutionLangs struct {
    Primary   string   yaml:"primary,omitempty"
    Allowed   []string yaml:"allowed,omitempty"
    Forbidden []string yaml:"forbidden,omitempty"
  }

Similarly, the bootstrapConstitution function in cmd/specgraph/serve.go also omits ForbiddenReasons when building the Constitution from YAML. This means a constitution YAML with forbidden language reasons cannot round-trip through the YAML config layer.

The constitutionYAML struct in serve.go also lacks forbidden_reasons.

Location: internal/config/config.go (ConstitutionLangs struct), cmd/specgraph/serve.go (constitutionYAML struct).
title: ConstitutionConfig YAML lacks forbidden_reasons field present in proto schema

close_reason: Out of scope: Multi-layer merge/override is future work, not part of Slice 2.
description:
The original spec (docs/initial-design-session/specgraph-v1.0-draft-spec.md section 3.2) describes layered constitution semantics:
  'More specific layers override more general ones, but all layers are visible'
  'User -> Org -> Project -> Domain'

The implementation correctly models layers as an enum (CONSTITUTION_LAYER_USER/ORG/PROJECT/DOMAIN) and the plan acknowledges this is for future slices. However:

1. The storage layer uses MERGE on Constitution node with no label/identifier, enforcing at-most-one constitution globally. This means multiple layers cannot coexist in the graph simultaneously — storing a USER layer constitution would replace the PROJECT layer constitution.

2. No issue/TODO tracks the layering implementation gap. The Memgraph MERGE query uses MERGE (c:Constitution) with no unique key, so all layers share a single node.

3. The slice 2 plan does not call out this limitation as a known gap.

This is a structural limitation that will need to be addressed before layering can be implemented. It would require either a unique constraint per layer or separate node types. This should be documented as a known gap.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, MERGE (c:Constitution) query.
title: Constitution layering (User/Org/Project/Domain) modeled as enum but no merge/override logic implemented

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, lines ~2494-2503 (diff lines).

The detectInfrastructure function uses:

  globs, err := filepath.Glob(filepath.Join(dir, "**", "*.yaml"))

CLAUDE.md explicitly documents: 'filepath.Glob does not support recursive ** -- only matches one directory level'.

This means the Kubernetes detection only checks yaml files exactly one directory level deep (e.g., dir/subdir/*.yaml), not recursively. Kubernetes manifests stored more than one level deep (e.g., deploy/k8s/prod/*.yaml) will never be found.

The result is silent failure: the scanner returns without detecting Kubernetes infra even when manifests are present in the repo, producing an incorrect constitution draft.

Fix: Use filepath.WalkDir or a recursive glob helper (e.g., doublestar.Glob) instead of filepath.Glob for recursive traversal. Alternatively, check a known-conventional path like k8s/, deploy/, or kubernetes/ as a heuristic, similar to how CI detection works (checking a specific dir).
title: filepath.Glob does not support recursive ** patterns -- Kubernetes detection silently fails

description:
File: cmd/specgraph/constitution.go, lines 569-571

The path traversal check uses strings.HasPrefix(absPath, cwd) to warn when the output path is outside the current directory. This check has two problems:

1. It only issues a warning and still writes the file. A warning is not a guard — the file is written regardless (line 572).

2. The prefix check itself is bypassable. If cwd is /home/user/project, a path like /home/user/project-evil/secret.txt satisfies strings.HasPrefix and will silently pass the check without even generating a warning.

Fix: Use strings.HasPrefix(absPath, cwd+string(os.PathSeparator)) or filepath.Rel(cwd, absPath) and check for leading '..'. Additionally, decide whether to hard-block writes outside cwd or document that this is intentional (CLI tool for local use). If the intent is to block, return an error instead of a warning.
title: Path traversal bypass in emit --output flag: strings.HasPrefix check is insufficient

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 2: authentication/authorization is a cross-cutting concern planned for a later phase. The server currently binds to localhost by default in dev mode.
description:
File: internal/server/constitution_handler.go, lines 63-73

The UpdateConstitution RPC endpoint is exposed on the HTTP mux with no authentication or authorization check. Any client that can reach the server can overwrite the entire project constitution, replacing all principles, constraints, antipatterns, and tech configuration with arbitrary data.

This is the highest-privilege mutation endpoint in the new subsystem — the constitution is described as 'project ground truth' that all specs inherit. Unauthorized modification could silently corrupt all subsequent AI-assisted authoring decisions.

The same concern applies to the server's default bind address of 0.0.0.0 (internal/config/config.go applyDefaults), which makes the server reachable on all network interfaces by default with no authentication layer protecting any RPC.

Recommended fix: Add at minimum a shared-secret or API key mechanism for mutating RPCs before exposing the server on a non-loopback interface. As an interim measure, change the default bind address to 127.0.0.1 so the server is localhost-only out of the box, and document that 0.0.0.0 requires explicit opt-in with appropriate network security.
title: UpdateConstitution RPC has no authentication or authorization

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 2: RPC payload size limits are a hardening concern for the server phase. Bootstrap path already has a 1 MiB guard.
description:
File: internal/server/constitution_handler.go, lines 63-73

The bootstrap path (cmd/specgraph/serve.go) correctly enforces a 1 MiB size limit (maxConstitutionSize) when reading a constitution YAML file from disk. However, the UpdateConstitution RPC handler applies no equivalent size limit to the incoming protobuf payload. A client can send an arbitrarily large Constitution message (e.g., with millions of constraint strings or very large principle rationales), causing unbounded memory allocation in the server process.

Recommended fix: Add a ConnectRPC interceptor or inline check that rejects Constitution messages whose serialized size exceeds a defined limit (e.g., 1 MiB, matching the file-based limit). The existing maxConstitutionSize constant in serve.go should be moved to a shared location and reused.
title: No size limit on UpdateConstitution RPC payload allows memory exhaustion

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 2: scanner runs locally via CLI only, not exposed over network. Path sanitization is a hardening task for later.
description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, lines 662-677 and cmd/specgraph/init.go line 642

The scanner.Scan() function accepts a dir parameter and performs filesystem walks and file reads under that path. In the init command, the caller passes "." (current directory) unconditionally. However, Scan itself performs no validation that dir is within an expected root — it will happily read any path passed to it.

The readFile helper reads files up to 1 MiB (maxFileSize), which mitigates large-file DoS. The WalkDir call in detectInfrastructure skips hidden dirs and vendor but does not prevent symlink traversal — on systems with attacker-controlled symlinks in the scanned tree, the walk could read files outside the intended directory.

More critically, if a future caller passes an untrusted or user-supplied directory path to Scan (e.g. from a CLI flag or RPC parameter), there is no containment boundary. The function should either document that dir must be a pre-validated trusted path, or add a check that dir resolves to an absolute path under an expected root before walking.
title: Scanner walks caller-supplied directory with no path validation

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, readFile function (lines ~6814-6824 in diff).

The readFile helper returns an empty string on any error (os.Stat failure, os.ReadFile failure, or size exceeded). Callers cannot distinguish between 'file does not exist' (expected), 'permission denied' (operational problem), and 'file too large' (deliberate skip). When readFile returns '', detectFrameworks and detectInfrastructure silently skip detection for that path with no feedback to the caller or user.

Example: if go.mod exists but is unreadable due to permissions, the scanner reports no language detected rather than any error or warning, leading constitution drafts with empty language fields.

Suggested fix: return (string, error) from readFile, or accept an error-accumulator/warnings slice that callers pass down from Scan(), and surface read errors as warnings. The existing TODO comment on line 6748 acknowledges this debt; this finding flags the specific readFile suppression as the root instance.
title: scanner.readFile silently discards I/O errors, masking unreadable files

description:
File: cmd/specgraph/serve.go, lines 282-284

The bootstrapConstitution call in runServe treats any error as a non-fatal warning:

    if err := bootstrapConstitution(ctx, store, constitutionPath); err != nil {
        fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "warning: constitution bootstrap: %v\n", err)
    }

If bootstrapConstitution fails (e.g. malformed YAML, Memgraph write error), the server continues running but the constitution is not loaded. Subsequent GetConstitution calls return ErrConstitutionNotFound and CheckViolation/EmitToolFiles fail, but no clear signal is given to the operator at startup.

The warning is printed to stderr only, with no structured logging, no error code, and no indication to callers that the server is operating in a degraded state. An operator running serve in the background or with stderr redirected will never see this.

Suggested fix: Either return the error to halt startup on unexpected failures (YAML parse error, DB write error), or at minimum distinguish expected absence (ErrConstitutionNotFound after no YAML) from unexpected failures. The distinction should be made in bootstrapConstitution's return value (e.g. sentinel for 'no file') rather than silencing all errors at the call site.
title: serve.go bootstrap failure demoted to warning — server starts with silent bad state

description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, detectFrameworks function (lines ~6730-6750 in diff).

When json.Unmarshal fails on package.json content, the error is silently discarded with a comment '// If unmarshal fails, skip framework detection for this file silently.' and a TODO noting it should be surfaced via a warning accumulator. This is a known accepted gap but worth tracking: a malformed package.json causes silent no-op framework detection rather than any user-visible feedback.

The risk is low during 'specgraph init --scan' (user sees no frameworks detected and must update manually), but the silence means developers have no actionable signal that their package.json was malformed.

Suggested fix: Once the Scan() function gains a warnings accumulator (per the TODO), wire json.Unmarshal errors there. For now, at minimum the warning should be printed to stderr so the user knows detection was skipped.
title: scanner.detectFrameworks swallows package.json JSON parse errors silently

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.95 (same CheckViolation stub issue)
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, CheckViolation function (lines ~7502-7522 in diff).

CheckViolation verifies the spec and constitution exist, then unconditionally returns an empty violation slice. The TODO comment acknowledges this ('TODO(slice-violations): implement constraint checking; returns empty until then.').

The silent failure mode is: a user runs 'specgraph constitution check <slug>' and sees 'No violations found.' — which is not an error, not a warning, and is indistinguishable from genuinely passing a check. This is a silent false-negative in production.

At minimum the RPC response or CLI output should include an indication that violation checking is not yet implemented, so callers (human or automated) do not rely on the output as a real compliance signal. Suggested fix: return a synthetic VIOLATION_SEVERITY_INFO violation with message 'Constraint checking not yet implemented — all results are provisional' or respond with CodeUnimplemented from the RPC handler until the feature is complete.
title: CheckViolation stub always returns empty — no signal it is unimplemented
CheckViolation returns CodeInternal when constitution is not found

close_reason: Not actionable for Slice 2: project has no structured logging framework yet. fmt.Println is appropriate for current stage. Structured logging is a future concern.
description:
File: cmd/specgraph/serve.go, bootstrapConstitution function (line ~836 in diff).

The bootstrap success message 'Bootstrapped constitution from <path>' is written via fmt.Println to stdout. The surrounding code uses fmt.Errorf for all error paths (properly), but there is no structured or leveled logging in the codebase. In a server startup path, using fmt.Println interleaves with any other startup messages in an unstructured way and is invisible in environments where stdout is not observed.

This is a suggestion-level issue: when the project adopts a logging library (slog, zap, etc.), this path should emit a structured log.Info entry. For now the fmt.Println is consistent with other CLI output patterns in the codebase, but it should be noted as a future-proofing concern for the server path specifically.
title: bootstrapConstitution logs success to stdout with fmt.Println, inconsistent with error handling

close_reason: Out of scope: slice-7 plan doc formatting is not slice 2 constitution code. Caused by yamlfmt auto-formatting.
description:
File: docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md

Multiple locations in the diff (lines 1888-1892, 1899-1904, 1911-1915, 1922-1927, 1933-1938) introduce pairs of orphaned backtick code fences:

  ```text
  
  ```text

These are inserted immediately before an existing ```text block. The result is two consecutive unclosed code-fence openings followed by the real block's closing fence, which breaks the markdown structure and may render the document incorrectly. Affected sections are around specgraph list, specgraph health, and plugin install command blocks.

Each instance should be removed entirely — the duplicate ```text\n\n```text lines add no content and corrupt the document.
title: Malformed markdown in slice-7 plan: orphaned code fences inserted

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.103 (same marshalJSON sentinel mismatch)
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go, line ~7524-7534

The marshalJSON function has a nil guard: if v == nil { return "{}", nil }. Its comment says 'Returns "{}" for nil values'. However, when callers pass typed nil pointers as the any parameter (e.g., marshalJSON(constitution.Tech) where Tech is a nil *specv1.TechConfig), the interface value is non-nil (it carries type information), so the guard never triggers. json.Marshal then receives the typed nil pointer and returns "null", not "{}".

This is not a runtime bug because unmarshalIfPresent treats "null" as an empty sentinel, so round-trip behavior is correct. However the comment and guard are misleading and could cause confusion.

The nil guard would only fire if the caller passed an untyped nil, which never happens in the current callers. The comment should be corrected to say 'Returns "{" for untyped nil; typed nil pointers produce "null" via json.Marshal' or the guard should be removed since all sentinels are handled by unmarshalIfPresent.
title: marshalJSON nil guard is ineffective for typed nil pointers passed as any
writePrinciplesSection renders empty ID as bold empty label

close_reason: Out of scope: gen/ gitignore is an infra change, not slice 2 code. Also intentional — specgraph is not consumed as a Go library.
description: The PR changes .gitignore to exclude gen/ (previously committed) and removes all gen/**/*.go and gen/**/*.connect.go files from the repository. Any Go module consumer who imports github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/gen/specgraph/v1 or github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/gen/specgraph/v1/specgraphv1connect will get a build failure after this change because the generated files no longer exist in the module. The Go module proxy caches the source tree; without gen/ the module is missing required packages. Fix: either keep gen/ committed (revert the .gitignore change and re-add the files), or add a go generate directive plus CI step that runs buf generate before go build so the module can still be consumed via 'go get'. If gen/ stays gitignored, the module cannot be used as an importable Go dependency without the proto toolchain. Location: .gitignore line 106 (gen/ added), and all deleted gen/specgraph/v1/*.pb.go + gen/specgraph/v1/specgraphv1connect/*.connect.go files.
title: gen/ removed from repo breaks Go module consumers who import generated types

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.97 (same ForbiddenReasons missing issue)
description: In cmd/specgraph/serve.go bootstrapConstitution(), the YAML-to-proto mapping at lines ~822-828 sets LanguageConfig{Primary, Allowed, Forbidden} but omits ForbiddenReasons. The proto LanguageConfig message (proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto field 4) has forbidden_reasons map<string,string>. Meanwhile, ConstitutionLangs in internal/config/config.go also lacks a forbidden_reasons field (only Primary/Allowed/Forbidden are defined). The result is: any forbidden_reasons values written via UpdateConstitution RPC and then exported/reloaded via constitution.yaml will lose the reasons on next server bootstrap. This is a data loss gap between the proto schema and the YAML schema. Fix: add ForbiddenReasons map[string]string yaml:"forbidden_reasons,omitempty" to ConstitutionLangs, and map it in bootstrapConstitution. Location: internal/config/config.go line ~6121, cmd/specgraph/serve.go lines ~822-828.
title: bootstrapConstitution silently drops ForbiddenReasons when loading from YAML

close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-gk1.82 (same CheckViolation error code issue)
description: In internal/server/constitution_handler.go CheckViolation() (line ~7082), the error from store.CheckViolation is inspected only for ErrSpecNotFound -> CodeNotFound. However memgraph.CheckViolation (internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go line ~7515) can also return an error wrapping storage.ErrConstitutionNotFound when no constitution has been set up. This case falls through to connect.CodeInternal, which is incorrect: the server is healthy but simply has no constitution configured. The correct code is CodeFailedPrecondition ("server not in the right state for this operation"). Callers who want to distinguish "spec missing" from "no constitution" cannot do so — both non-spec errors get CodeInternal. Fix: add an errors.Is(err, storage.ErrConstitutionNotFound) check in the handler and return connect.CodeFailedPrecondition. Location: internal/server/constitution_handler.go ~line 7082.
title: CheckViolation returns CodeInternal instead of CodeFailedPrecondition when no constitution exists

close_reason: Out of scope: 0.0.0.0 bind is pre-existing in serve.go, not introduced by Slice 2.
description: internal/config/config.go applyDefaults (line 2113) sets cfg.Server.Host = "0.0.0.0" as the default. Combined with the absence of any authentication middleware on the mux (see the UpdateConstitution auth finding), this means every ConnectRPC service — including UpdateConstitution, UpdateSpec, CreateSpec, and CreateDecision — is reachable from any network interface by default without credentials. A developer running specgraph serve on a laptop connected to a shared network exposes all mutation endpoints to any peer on that network. Recommended fix: change the default host to "127.0.0.1" so the server binds only to loopback by default. Operators who need external binding can override via config. This is a defense-in-depth measure independent of the auth middleware fix.
title: Default server bind address is 0.0.0.0 with no auth, exposing all ConnectRPC services on all interfaces

description:
cmd/specgraph/serve.go:767-769 — two error paths in bootstrapConstitution are untested:

1. Size limit guard (line 767): 'if info.Size() > maxConstitutionSize' returns a hard error. No test writes a file larger than 1 MiB to verify this produces the expected 'exceeds 1 MiB size limit' error rather than silently loading garbage or panicking.

2. Stat error path (line 762-765): The 'stat constitution YAML' branch fires when os.Stat succeeds (file exists) but returns a non-IsNotExist error (e.g., permission denied). No test covers this path, so a filesystem permission error on the constitution file would produce an internal error that callers cannot distinguish from a structural problem.

These are distinct from the broader bootstrapConstitution coverage gap tracked in spgr-gk1.22. Both paths are straightforward to test with t.TempDir() and os.WriteFile/Truncate.

Suggested fix: Add table-driven tests in a new cmd/specgraph/serve_test.go (or an internal package extracted from serve.go):
  - TestBootstrapConstitution_FileTooLarge: write a file >1 MiB, assert error contains 'exceeds 1 MiB'
  - TestBootstrapConstitution_StatPermissionDenied: chmod 000, assert non-nil error not wrapping ErrConstitutionNotFound
title: bootstrapConstitution 1 MiB size-limit path and stat error path have no tests

description: In cmd/specgraph/constitution.go runConstitutionCheck() (line ~521): fmt.Printf("[%s] %s: %s\n", v.GetSeverity(), v.GetRule(), v.GetMessage()). GetSeverity() returns ViolationSeverity enum type; when formatted with %s, Go's protobuf runtime produces the full enum name VIOLATION_SEVERITY_ERROR, not ERROR. This makes the CLI output verbose and inconsistent with the human-readable convention used elsewhere in the CLI. Any script parsing this output (e.g. grep for ERROR) would need to match the full enum name. Fix: map the enum to a short string before printing, e.g. strings.TrimPrefix(v.GetSeverity().String(), "VIOLATION_SEVERITY_"), or define a helper that returns "error"/"warning"/"info". Location: cmd/specgraph/constitution.go ~line 521.
title: CLI constitution check prints raw proto enum string for violation severity

description:
cmd/specgraph/serve.go:742-747 — referenceTypeFromString maps YAML reference type strings ('adr', 'spec', 'doc', 'url') to proto enum values. An unrecognised string silently returns REFERENCE_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED without any warning or error. There are no tests for this function.

This means a typo in constitution.yaml (e.g., 'ADR' instead of 'adr') causes the reference to be stored as UNSPECIFIED, making it invisible to consumers that filter by type. There is no test that:
  - Verifies known strings ('adr', 'spec', 'doc', 'url') map to the correct enum values
  - Verifies an unknown string maps to REFERENCE_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED (documenting the intentional fallback)
  - Verifies case-insensitivity is NOT applied (the function calls strings.ToLower, so 'ADR' DOES map to adr — but this is untested)

Suggested fix: Add a table-driven unit test in a new internal package (or cmd/specgraph/serve_test.go if the function is exported) covering all four valid values, an unknown value, and mixed-case inputs to confirm the ToLower behavior is intentional and stable.
title: referenceTypeFromString has no tests — unknown YAML reference types silently become UNSPECIFIED

description:
internal/server/constitution_handler_test.go — EmitToolFiles is tested only for error cases (not found, unspecified format, out-of-range enum). The success path — where a constitution exists and a valid format produces non-empty content with the correct filename — is never tested at the handler layer.

This matters because the handler's success path calls emitter.Emit and wires the response fields (Content, Filename). A regression in field mapping (e.g., accidentally swapping Content/Filename) would not be caught by the existing tests.

Failure mode: a refactor that swaps EmitToolFilesResponse.Content and EmitToolFilesResponse.Filename would pass all existing tests.

Suggested fix: Add TestConstitutionHandler_EmitSuccess that:
  1. Stores a constitution via UpdateConstitution
  2. Calls EmitToolFiles with OUTPUT_FORMAT_CLAUDE_MD
  3. Asserts resp.Msg.Filename == 'CLAUDE.md'
  4. Asserts resp.Msg.Content contains expected section headers (e.g., '## Tech Stack')

Location: internal/server/constitution_handler_test.go
title: EmitToolFiles happy-path (successful emit with content assertion) not tested in constitution_handler_test.go

description:
cmd/specgraph/constitution.go:543-583 — runConstitutionEmit is entirely untested. Three distinct behaviors have no coverage:

1. Unknown format string: outputFormatMap lookup fails, runConstitutionEmit returns an error. No test verifies the error message format or that the process exits non-zero.

2. File write path (--output flag): The function resolves an absolute path, checks for directory traversal (outside cwd warning), and writes content to disk. No test covers:
   - Successful write to a temp file
   - Write failure (read-only path)
   - Path outside cwd producing a stderr warning (not an error — this is a silent miss)

3. Stdout path (no --output): Filename header is printed only when resp.Msg.Filename != '', then content is printed. No test verifies the stdout format.

Failure mode: a regression in the directory-traversal warning condition (line 569: strings.HasPrefix(absPath, cwd)) would go undetected. On Windows-style paths, this check is known to be fragile.

Suggested fix: Extract runConstitutionEmit into a testable function accepting io.Writer and a fake client, or add CLI integration tests using cobra's command.Execute() with a mock transport.
title: constitution emit CLI command (runConstitutionEmit) has no tests — outputFormatMap validation and file write path untested

description:
The Slice 2 plan (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-2-constitution-plan.md) requires constitution violation checking against forbidden languages and constraints. The implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/constitution.go CheckViolation function ignores the constitution entirely:

    _ = constitution
    return violations, nil  // always empty

The variable is explicitly discarded and no comparison is made between spec metadata and constitution constraints or forbidden languages. The spec requires CheckViolation to detect at minimum forbidden-language violations. This is important because violation checking is a core deliverable of Slice 2 and the RPC surface is fully wired — the mock in constitution_handler_test.go also returns empty violations, so no integration test validates real violation detection.
title: CheckViolation is a no-op stub that always returns empty violations

description:
The client-server architecture design doc (docs/plans/2026-02-28-client-server-architecture-design.md) and CLAUDE.md both state: 'Storage interfaces in internal/storage/ — implementations are in subdirectories. The interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.' The slice plan requires backend-agnostic storage usage.

However, cmd/specgraph/serve.go:bootstrapConstitution takes *memgraph.Store directly:

    func bootstrapConstitution(ctx context.Context, store *memgraph.Store, yamlPath string) error {

This should accept storage.ConstitutionBackend to be consistent with how RegisterConstitutionService and RegisterDecisionService are designed. Using the concrete type bypasses the interface abstraction and would force a refactor if/when a Postgres backend is added. The function is called from runServe which already has the store typed as *memgraph.Store, but the bootstrap function should not replicate that coupling.
title: bootstrapConstitution couples serve.go directly to *memgraph.Store instead of ConstitutionBackend interface
ConstitutionLangs YAML struct missing forbidden_reasons field causing bootstrap data loss

close_reason: Out of scope: slice-7 plan doc formatting is not slice 2 constitution code. Caused by yamlfmt auto-formatting.
description:
The markdown formatter (rumdl) introduced malformed duplicate code fence blocks in docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-7-claude-code-plugin-plan.md. Three locations in the diff show stacked empty backtick blocks being prepended before legitimate code blocks:

At diff line 1882-1905:
    +```text
    +
    +```text
    +
    ```text

This pattern appears three times (around line 217, 280, and 795 of the original file). The result is that the rendered markdown shows empty code blocks before the actual content blocks, breaking the document's readability. This was introduced as a formatting side-effect of the formatter running in PostToolUse hooks on Bash commands. The triple-fence pattern corrupts the YAML-spec-like text blocks in that document.
