close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description:
In /internal/server/claim_handler.go:ClaimSpec (line 4647), the handler calls msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration() without checking whether LeaseDuration is nil first. The proto field is optional (not required), so a client omitting it sends a nil *durationpb.Duration. Calling .AsDuration() on a nil pointer panics at runtime.

Similarly, Heartbeat calls msg.ExtendBy.AsDuration() on line 4667 with the same nil-pointer risk.

The storage layer (memgraph/claim.go) handles the zero-duration default correctly, but the handler crashes before reaching storage.

Fix: guard before dereferencing.
  leaseDur := time.Duration(0)
  if msg.LeaseDuration != nil {
      leaseDur = msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration()
  }
  claim, err := h.store.ClaimSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, leaseDur)

Apply the same guard in Heartbeat for ExtendBy.
title: Nil panic in ClaimSpec handler when LeaseDuration is not set
UpdateSpec handler returns CodeNotFound for all storage errors, including non-not-found failures

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
In /internal/storage/memgraph/decision.go:ListDecisions (line 6094), the LIMIT clause is appended to the query string via fmt.Sprintf:

  query += fmt.Sprintf(" LIMIT %d", limit)

The limit value originates from the proto int32 field (ListDecisionsRequest.Limit), converted to int in the handler. Because it is formatted as %d it is safe against injection today, but the pattern is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase which uses parameterised queries for all values. If the format specifier were ever changed to %s, or a similar pattern were replicated for string values, it would be exploitable.

The same pattern appears in UpdateSpec (memgraph.go line 6734) and UpdateDecision (decision.go line 6147) where SET clauses are built with fmt.Sprintf from a slice of hardcoded strings — those are safe because the strings are constants, not user input. The LIMIT case stands out because the value is user-supplied.

Fix: use a parameterised LIMIT. Cypher supports LIMIT $limit:
  query += " LIMIT $limit"
  params["limit"] = limit

This removes the inconsistency and eliminates the risk.
title: ListDecisions LIMIT injected via fmt.Sprintf enables Cypher injection if limit is user-controlled

close_reason: Out of scope: full ADR-003 Decision schema is a future vertical slice; this PR adds foundation
description:
ADR-003 specifies a Decision schema with these required fields: question, chosen, confidence, tags, scope, origin_spec, origin_stage, supersedes, superseded_by, deprecated_reason, and history. The implementation in proto/specgraph/v1/decision.proto only has: id, slug, title, status, decision (text), rationale, superseded_by, created_at, updated_at.

Missing fields:
- question: the question being answered (ADR-003: 'question: Where and how to persist refresh tokens?')
- chosen: the chosen option (separate from 'decision' text — ADR-003: 'chosen: Postgres table with SHA-256 hashed tokens')
- confidence: high|medium|low
- tags: searchable tags (ADR-003: 'tags: [auth, storage, security]')
- scope: project|team|org
- origin_spec: slug of the spec where this decision was made
- origin_stage: which authoring stage (spark|shape|specify etc.)
- supersedes: id of decision this replaces (bidirectional link per ADR-003)
- deprecated_reason: reason if deprecated
- history: version history array

Also, 'decision' field conflates 'chosen' with the decision text. ADR-003 treats 'chosen' as the selected option and 'rationale' as the justification — the current 'decision' field merges these.

Additionally, the Decision model uses a user-supplied slug for identity rather than the hash-based identity scheme in ADR-003 ('d-' prefix + sha256(normalize(title))). ADR-003 states 'two specs that independently decide refresh token storage converge on the same identity' — the current slug-based approach breaks this convergence property.

File: proto/specgraph/v1/decision.proto, internal/storage/memgraph/decision.go, gen/specgraph/v1/decision.pb.go
title: Decision schema missing required fields from ADR-003: question, chosen, confidence, tags, scope, origin_spec, origin_stage, supersedes, history

close_reason: Deferred: lease duration cap is hardening for future multi-agent work
description:
ClaimSpec and Heartbeat accept a caller-supplied lease_duration / extend_by duration with no server-side maximum. A caller can request a lease of years or decades, permanently locking a spec from other agents.

File: internal/server/claim_handler.go, line 4647:
  h.store.ClaimSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration())

File: internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go, ClaimSpec function — no upper bound check before storing lease_expires.

Example: a caller sends LeaseDuration of 876000h (100 years). The spec becomes permanently claimed.

Suggested fix: Add a server-side maximum lease duration constant (e.g., 24 hours) and clamp any requested duration:
  const maxLeaseDuration = 24 * time.Hour
  if leaseDuration <= 0 || leaseDuration > maxLeaseDuration {
      leaseDuration = defaultLeaseDuration
  }
title: No server-side cap on ClaimSpec lease duration enables permanent spec lockout

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description:
The ClaimHandler in internal/server/claim_handler.go uses inconsistent and semantically wrong Connect error codes that leak implementation details into the type contract of the RPC interface:

- ClaimSpec uses CodeFailedPrecondition for both 'spec not found' and 'already claimed' — these are distinct conditions with different appropriate codes (CodeNotFound vs CodeAlreadyExists/CodeFailedPrecondition).
- UnclaimSpec uses CodeInternal for a domain-level error (unclaim failure), which signals infrastructure failure rather than a bad caller request.
- Heartbeat uses CodeNotFound which is correct but the 'not found' and 'lease expired' cases are conflated in the storage layer.

The error code is part of the public type contract of the service (callers branch on it). Using CodeInternal for a domain error misleads clients into thinking a retry is appropriate.

Suggested fix: Map domain errors to semantic codes:
  - Spec not found → CodeNotFound
  - Spec already claimed → CodeAlreadyExists (or CodeFailedPrecondition with message)
  - Storage/driver errors → CodeInternal

This also suggests the ClaimBackend interface should return typed errors (e.g. ErrAlreadyClaimed, ErrNotFound) to let the handler map them correctly, rather than having the handler guess from opaque error strings.
title: ClaimHandler uses wrong error codes for domain errors

readLine returns same value in both branches — dead error branch

close_reason: Deferred: unbounded graph traversal is hardening, not a bug in this slice
description:
The GetTransitiveDeps and GetImpact operations use Cypher variable-length path patterns with no depth limit, allowing any caller to trigger an exhaustive graph traversal:

File: internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go

GetTransitiveDeps:
  MATCH (a {slug: $slug})-[:DEPENDS_ON*]->(b)

GetImpact:
  MATCH (a {slug: $slug})<-[:DEPENDS_ON*]-(b)

GetCriticalPath:
  MATCH p = (a {slug: $slug})-[:DEPENDS_ON*]->(b)

In a graph with many nodes or a long dependency chain, these queries can consume excessive CPU and memory in Memgraph and cause the server to become unresponsive. Combined with unauthenticated access, any caller can trigger this.

Suggested fix: Add a depth limit to all variable-length path patterns:
  [:DEPENDS_ON*..50]   -- limit to 50 hops
Also add query timeouts via context deadlines before calling neo4j.ExecuteQuery.
title: GetTransitiveDeps and GetImpact perform unbounded Cypher graph traversal enabling DoS

close_reason: Out of scope: ADR-003 edge types (decided_in, references) are future work
description:
ADR-003 core architectural decision: 'Decisions connect to specs through two directed edge types: spec --decided_in--> decision and spec --references--> decision.' The current implementation has no mechanism to express these relationships.

The graph.proto EdgeType enum includes EDGE_TYPE_INFORMS (Decision A informs Spec B) but omits the two spec-to-decision edge types explicitly specified in ADR-003:
- EDGE_TYPE_DECIDED_IN (spec made this decision)
- EDGE_TYPE_REFERENCES (spec depends on/references this decision)

Without these edges, the cross-spec referencing workflow described in ADR-003 cannot function. The entire rationale for first-class Decision nodes — 'impact analysis: which specs are affected if we change this decision?' — is impossible without these edges.

The GraphService AddEdge RPC accepts any edge type between any nodes (by slug), but the Decision INFORMS edge type is backwards from what ADR-003 specifies. ADR-003 says edges go FROM spec TO decision; INFORMS goes from decision to spec.

ADR-003 also requires a spec_decisions join table / relationship in storage, and the CLI commands:
  specgraph decision reference <spec-slug> <decision-title>
  specgraph decision impact <decision-title>

Neither the storage interface (internal/storage/decision.go) nor the CLI (cmd/specgraph/decision.go) implement cross-spec referencing.

Files: proto/specgraph/v1/graph.proto, internal/storage/decision.go, cmd/specgraph/decision.go
title: Decision service lacks spec-to-decision edge types (decided_in, references) specified in ADR-003

description:
In /internal/server/decision_handler.go:UpdateDecision (line 4891), the handler calls:

  h.store.UpdateDecision(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Title, msg.Status, msg.Decision, msg.Rationale, msg.SupersededBy)

The storage interface (storage/decision.go) expects (ctx, slug string, title, status, decision, rationale, supersededBy *string), but msg.Title, msg.Status etc. are protobuf optional fields — the generated getter returns a plain string (not *string). As written the call does not compile unless the proto field is directly a *string.

Inspecting the generated code (decision.pb.go line 1852-1856), Title/Status/Decision/Rationale/SupersededBy are actually *string in the proto struct, so the getters return string but the raw fields are *string. The handler calls the getters (which return string), so it passes strings, not *string, causing a type mismatch that will prevent compilation.

Fix: access the raw pointer fields directly (msg.Title, msg.Status etc. are already *string in the generated struct when using proto optional), or use the pointer fields: pass &msg.Title only when changed is relevant. The correct approach is to pass msg.Title (as *string) by reading the struct field rather than the getter method.

If the code compiles today it may be because the getter returns the pointer — verify the generated code accessor signatures are consistent with what the handler passes.
title: UpdateDecision passes string values where *string is expected, losing nil-field semantics

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
storage.NodeRef (internal/storage/graph.go) and specv1.NodeRef (gen/specgraph/v1/graph.pb.go) use a plain string Label field with possible values 'Spec' or 'Decision'. This is documented only by the comment:
  Label string // "Spec" or "Decision"

The graph queries that populate Label do so via labels(n)[0] (memgraph/graph.go line ~6431), which relies on the database returning the correct label string. There is no compile-time or runtime guarantee that only the two valid values appear in the field.

Consequence: Code that branches on NodeRef.Label (e.g., a future renderer or router) must use string comparison with magic constants, making invalid states representable and typos invisible at compile time.

Suggested fix: Define a NodeKind proto enum (NODE_KIND_SPEC, NODE_KIND_DECISION) and replace the Label string in NodeRef. The storage-layer NodeRef can use a Go string internally but should be converted at the handler boundary in graph_handler.go (nodeRefsToProto), where the label-to-enum mapping can be validated and unknown values caught.
title: NodeRef label field is a stringly-typed node kind discriminator
readLine in init.go silently discards io errors, treating partial/failed reads as valid input

description:
Several CLI files split their flag/command registration across multiple init() functions — one per sub-command — when a single init() would be clearer and is the Go convention.

Affected files:
- cmd/specgraph/claim.go: two separate init() blocks (lines 46-51 and 83-87)
- cmd/specgraph/decision.go: four separate init() blocks (lines 173-175, 192-198, 228-231, 270-272)
- cmd/specgraph/edge.go: four separate init() blocks

Each block registers exactly one sub-command plus its flags. Consolidating into a single init() per file makes the full set of sub-commands visible at a glance and matches Go idiomatic style.

Example (cmd/specgraph/claim.go):
Before — two separate init() functions.
After:
func init() {
    claimCmd.Flags().StringVar(&claimAgent, "agent", "", "agent identifier (required)")
    claimCmd.Flags().DurationVar(&claimDuration, "duration", 15*time.Minute, "lease duration")
    cobra.CheckErr(claimCmd.MarkFlagRequired("agent"))
    rootCmd.AddCommand(claimCmd)

    unclaimCmd.Flags().StringVar(&unclaimAgent, "agent", "", "agent identifier (required)")
    cobra.CheckErr(unclaimCmd.MarkFlagRequired("agent"))
    rootCmd.AddCommand(unclaimCmd)
}
title: Multiple init() functions per file — fragmented registration pattern
Wrong author name in copyright headers (Sean Bartell vs Sean Brandt)

close_reason: Deferred: TLS for CLI is not in scope for this vertical slice
description:
The resolveBaseURL function in cmd/specgraph/client.go constructs the server URL using plain HTTP when no remote URL is configured:

  return fmt.Sprintf("http://%s:%d", cfg.Server.Host, cfg.Server.Port), nil

Any traffic between the CLI and a remote server (or a locally-networked server) will be unencrypted. This exposes authentication tokens (once added), spec contents, and decision data to network interception.

The postgres compose template also uses sslmode=disable in the DSN:
  SPECGRAPH_STORAGE_POSTGRES_DSN=postgres://specgraph:specgraph@postgres:5432/specgraph?sslmode=disable
(internal/docker/compose.go, postgresComposeFormat)

Suggested fix:
- Default to https:// for remote connections; only allow http:// for localhost
- Change sslmode=disable to sslmode=prefer or sslmode=require for non-localhost Postgres
- Document that plain HTTP is only safe for loopback use
title: CLI defaults to plain HTTP (no TLS) for server communication

description:
In /internal/server/spec_handler.go:UpdateSpec (line 5587) and /internal/server/decision_handler.go:UpdateDecision (line 4893), all storage errors are mapped to CodeNotFound:

  return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, err)

A database connection failure, a timeout, or a query error will be reported to the client as 404 Not Found. This makes debugging harder and causes clients to retry unnecessarily (404 is not retryable, while CodeInternal or CodeUnavailable signals a transient failure).

The correct pattern (already used in CreateDecision, ListDecisions, etc.) is to return CodeInternal for storage errors and only CodeNotFound when the record is explicitly absent. The two handlers should inspect the error or use a sentinel error value:
  - Use CodeInternal for unexpected storage errors
  - Use CodeNotFound only when the error message/type indicates the entity was not found

Consider introducing a typed error (e.g., storage.ErrNotFound) to allow handlers to discriminate.
title: UpdateSpec and UpdateDecision return CodeNotFound for all errors, hiding internal failures

close_reason: Out of scope: Decision lifecycle CLI ops (accept, deprecate, supersede) are future work
description:
ADR-003 specifies this CLI for decisions:

  specgraph decision accept <title>
  specgraph decision deprecate <title> --reason <...>
  specgraph decision supersede <title> --with <new-title>
  specgraph decision reference <spec-slug> <decision-title>
  specgraph decision impact <decision-title>
  specgraph decision list --spec <slug>
  specgraph decision list --tag <tag>

The implementation in cmd/specgraph/decision.go provides only: create, list (status filter), show, and the UpdateDecision RPC workaround for status transitions.

Missing CLI commands:
- decision accept (sets status to 'accepted')
- decision deprecate (sets status to 'deprecated' with reason)
- decision supersede (sets status to 'superseded', creates new decision with supersedes link, notifies referencing specs)
- decision reference (creates spec-to-decision edge of type 'references')
- decision impact (shows specs referencing a decision)
- decision list --spec (filter by origin or referencing spec)
- decision list --tag (filter by tag)

The supersession operation in ADR-003 is a multi-step atomic operation that must: update old decision status, link superseded_by, link new decision's supersedes, and flag all referencing specs. This requires a dedicated supersede operation, not just UpdateDecision.

File: cmd/specgraph/decision.go
title: Decision CLI missing lifecycle operations specified in ADR-003: accept, deprecate, supersede, impact, reference

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
In internal/server/decision_handler.go line ~4893, UpdateDecision maps all storage errors unconditionally to CodeNotFound:
  return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeNotFound, err)

This means a database connectivity failure, a constraint violation, or any other storage error is returned to clients as CodeNotFound, which:
1. Misleads clients into thinking the decision slug does not exist when the real issue is an infrastructure failure.
2. Causes incorrect client retry behavior (clients will not retry NotFound; they will retry Internal or Unavailable).
3. Leaks the invariant assumption that 'the only failure mode of UpdateDecision is not
title: DecisionHandler.UpdateDecision maps all errors to CodeNotFound regardless of cause

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description: ClaimHandler.ClaimSpec calls msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration() at internal/server/claim_handler.go line 4647 without a nil check. In proto3 all message fields are optional, so a client that omits lease_duration will send nil, causing a nil pointer dereference panic in the server process. The same issue occurs in ClaimHandler.Heartbeat at line 4667 with msg.ExtendBy.AsDuration(). Both LeaseDuration (ClaimSpecRequest field 3) and ExtendBy (HeartbeatRequest field 3) are google.protobuf.Duration message fields that can legally be omitted. Fix: guard with if msg.LeaseDuration != nil { ... } else { use default } before calling .AsDuration(), and similarly for ExtendBy.
title: ClaimHandler panics on nil LeaseDuration/ExtendBy optional proto fields

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description:
The Decision message (proto/specgraph/v1/decision.proto, gen/specgraph/v1/decision.pb.go) uses string for the status field with a comment listing the valid values (proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded). This is the classic invariant-through-documentation anti-pattern. There is no enforcement at the proto layer or Go layer that prevents setting status to an arbitrary string like "active" or "".

The Decision type comment at line ~1538 (gen/specgraph/v1/decision.pb.go) reads:
  Status string // proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded

Suggested fix: Define a DecisionStatus proto enum and replace the string field. This encodes the invariant in the type system and makes the state machine explicit. The transition superseded requires superseded_by to be non-empty—this invariant cannot even be expressed today.

Similarly, the ListDecisionsRequest.Status filter field (proto string) will silently return empty results for any typo; an enum field would be caught at the proto level.
title: Decision.status is an untyped string instead of an enum

close_reason: Deferred: 0.0.0.0 bind is the configured default, operational concern for later
description:
The applyDefaults function in internal/config/config.go sets the server host to 0.0.0.0 when unconfigured:

  if cfg.Server.Host == "" {
      cfg.Server.Host = "0.0.0.0"
  }

This causes the server to listen on all network interfaces by default, including any public or LAN-reachable interfaces. Combined with the absence of authentication (separate finding), any host that can reach the machine can invoke all RPCs.

For a local development tool, the safer default is 127.0.0.1 (loopback only). Users who need external access can explicitly configure the host.

Suggested fix:
  cfg.Server.Host = "127.0.0.1"  // loopback only by default
title: Server default bind address is 0.0.0.0 (all interfaces), unnecessarily increasing attack surface

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go, ClaimSpec method (line ~5825)

    CREATE (s)-[r:CLAIMED_BY {agent: , ...}]->(a:Agent {name: })

This CREATE unconditionally creates a new Agent node on every ClaimSpec call, even if an Agent with that name already exists. Over time this causes accumulating duplicate Agent nodes in the graph. When the claim is later removed (UnclaimSpec), it deletes both the relationship and the Agent node, which could delete a shared node that other claims reference — or leave orphaned Agent nodes if claims are overwritten.

While the immediate runtime error from this is unlikely to surface (Memgraph won't reject the duplicate CREATE), the graph integrity degrades silently over time.

Suggested fix: Replace CREATE with MERGE for the Agent node:
    MERGE (a:Agent {name: })
    CREATE (s)-[r:CLAIMED_BY {...}]->(a)

This is a data-correctness bug that will not produce any error message, making it a silent failure.
title: ClaimSpec Cypher query creates duplicate Agent nodes on every claim due to missing MERGE

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description:
In internal/server/claim_handler.go lines 4647 and 4667 (in the diff), the handler calls msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration() and msg.ExtendBy.AsDuration() unconditionally. Because LeaseDuration and ExtendBy are optional proto3 fields, they will be nil when the client omits them. Calling AsDuration() on a nil *durationpb.Duration panics at runtime, producing a 500 with no error message.

Location: internal/server/claim_handler.go — ClaimSpec() and Heartbeat() methods.

There are no tests exercising a ClaimSpec request without LeaseDuration or a Heartbeat request without ExtendBy. The memgraph layer already handles zero-duration gracefully (defaultLeaseDuration fallback), but the handler never gets there because it panics before calling the backend.

Suggested fix: guard both call sites:
  var d time.Duration
  if msg.LeaseDuration != nil {
      d = msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration()
  }

Tests to add:
- TestClaimHandler_ClaimWithoutDuration: send ClaimSpecRequest with no LeaseDuration; expect a valid claim with 15m expiry.
- TestClaimHandler_HeartbeatWithoutExtendBy: send HeartbeatRequest with no ExtendBy; expect lease extended by default duration.
title: Nil pointer panic when LeaseDuration or ExtendBy is absent in ClaimSpec/Heartbeat RPCs

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
File: internal/storage/decision.go

The DecisionBackend interface has a type-level doc comment but no per-method comments:
  // DecisionBackend defines storage operations for Decision entities.
  type DecisionBackend interface {
      CreateDecision(...)
      GetDecision(...)
      ListDecisions(...)
      UpdateDecision(...)  // optional fields via *string pointers
  }

By contrast, ClaimBackend (internal/storage/claim.go) and GraphBackend (internal/storage/graph.go) both have per-method comments documenting behavior, preconditions, and error semantics. The asymmetry is noticeable and will hurt future implementors.

UpdateDecision in particular has a non-obvious contract (nil pointer = no-op for that field; empty setClauses = fetch-and-return), which is documented in the memgraph implementation but not on the interface. A future Postgres or in-memory backend implementor may miss this contract.

Suggested fix: Add at minimum a comment for UpdateDecision noting the nil=no-op semantics, mirroring the style in claim.go and graph.go.
title: DecisionBackend interface methods lack per-method doc comments
Duplicated client-factory boilerplate across all five CLI files

description:
In /cmd/specgraph/init.go:runInit (line 721), the os.Stat check uses the cfgFile path as-is without creating the parent directory, but cfg.Write already calls os.MkdirAll (config.go line 6432), so this is handled. However, the test in init_test.go (line 797-798) uses path '.specgraph/config.yaml' inside a temp dir, while TestInitAlreadyExists uses 'config.yaml' directly. These two tests use different path depths, so one tests directory creation and the other does not — the coverage is asymmetric.

More importantly, there is no validation of the user-supplied backend or mode values. The interactive path accepts any string:
  cfg.Storage.Backend = backend  // could be 'invalid_backend'
  cfg.Server.Mode = mode          // could be 'invalid_mode'

These invalid values propagate to runServe where the switch on cfg.Storage.Backend falls through to a non-existent case and the server silently fails to register the backend.

Fix: validate backend and mode inputs in runInit against the known valid values (memgraph, postgres for backend; docker, external for mode) and return an error for unknown values.
title: init.go runInit uses global cfgFile without parent directory creation, panics when path does not exist

description: In internal/server/spec_handler.go UpdateSpec handler (line 5588) and internal/server/decision_handler.go UpdateDecision handler (line 4893), all storage errors are mapped to connect.CodeNotFound regardless of error type. If the storage layer returns an internal/infrastructure error (e.g., database connection lost), clients receive a 404-equivalent response and will incorrectly retry as if the resource does not exist rather than treating it as a server-side failure. Fix: distinguish between not-found (CodeNotFound) and other errors (CodeInternal) by checking error type/sentinel from the storage layer, or by having storage return typed errors.
title: UpdateSpec and UpdateDecision use CodeNotFound for all storage errors

close_reason: Deferred: LeaseDuration bounds enforcement is hardening, related to lease cap
description:
ClaimSpecRequest.LeaseDuration and HeartbeatRequest.ExtendBy (proto/specgraph/v1/claim.proto and gen/specgraph/v1/claim.pb.go) accept any google.protobuf.Duration value including zero, negative, or arbitrarily large values. The only invariant is a comment '(default 15m)'.

In internal/server/claim_handler.go lines 4647 and 4667:
  claim, err := h.store.ClaimSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, msg.LeaseDuration.AsDuration())
  claim, err := h.store.Heartbeat(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Agent, msg.ExtendBy.AsDuration())

Note: durationpb.Duration.AsDuration() is nil-receiver safe (returns 0), so no panic. However, a zero or negative duration is silently accepted and passed to the storage layer, which then falls back to the 15m default. This means the request field has no enforced invariant — a caller sending -1s or 0 gets a silent default substitution with no error or warning.

Additionally, no upper bound is enforced. A caller could request a 10-year lease duration, which the storage layer would accept.

Suggested fix: Validate at the handler layer that LeaseDuration/ExtendBy, when non-nil/non-zero, is positive and within a reasonable maximum. Return CodeInvalidArgument if out of range. This makes the invariant explicit and gives callers actionable errors rather than silent substitution.
title: LeaseDuration and ExtendBy proto fields have no minimum/maximum enforcement
Inconsistent copyright header style across new files

close_reason: Deferred: input validation on slugs/status is hardening, future pass
description:
None of the RPC handlers validate the format or content of user-supplied string fields before storing them:

- slug: used as the primary identifier and in Cypher MATCH clauses as a parameterized value (safe from injection), but no length limit, character whitelist, or format check. An arbitrarily long slug can fill storage or cause display issues.
- status (DecisionService): accepts any string including values outside the defined set (proposed|accepted|deprecated|superseded). No server-side enum validation.
- stage (SpecService UpdateSpec): accepts any string, not validated against the authoring funnel stages.
- agent (ClaimService): accepts any string, no length limit.

Affected files:
- internal/server/claim_handler.go
- internal/server/decision_handler.go
- internal/server/spec_handler.go

Suggested fix: Add validation functions at the handler layer before delegating to storage:
- Validate slug format (e.g., ^[a-z0-9-]{1,128}$)
- Validate status against an allowlist
- Validate stage against the defined stage enum
- Enforce maximum string lengths for all free-text fields
title: No input validation on slug, status, or stage fields allows arbitrary string injection into graph nodes

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
In /internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go:AddEdge (line 6340), the Cypher query uses CREATE rather than MERGE:

  MATCH (a {slug: $from}), (b {slug: $to})
  CREATE (a)-[r:RELTYPE]->(b)
  RETURN a.id, b.id

Every call to AddEdge with the same (from, to, type) creates a new duplicate relationship. Subsequent ListEdges, GetDependencies, GetTransitiveDeps, and GetCriticalPath queries will then return or traverse duplicate edges, producing wrong results (e.g., a spec appearing multiple times in dependency lists, or wrong critical-path calculations).

Fix: change CREATE to MERGE on the relationship:
  MATCH (a {slug: $from}), (b {slug: $to})
  MERGE (a)-[r:RELTYPE]->(b)
  RETURN a.id, b.id

This makes AddEdge idempotent and prevents graph corruption.
title: AddEdge creates duplicate edges with no idempotency guard

close_reason: Out of scope: constitution bootstrap is a future vertical slice; this PR adds basic init
description: Spec section 4.2-4.3 and ADR-001 describe specgraph init as a constitution-and-context bootstrap flow. The implementation only creates a server config file. Missing: constitution questions, .specgraph/constitution.yaml creation, .specgraph/context.yaml, --scan flag, and --backend=beads|postgres flag. The init command also uses 'memgraph' as default backend, which is not a backend defined in the spec (only 'beads' and 'postgres' are specified). Roadmap Phase 1 item 2: Constitution schema + bootstrap. File: cmd/specgraph/init.go
title: init command does not implement constitution bootstrap flow from the spec

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go, UnclaimSpec method (line ~5848) and internal/server/claim_handler.go, UnclaimSpec handler (line ~4655)

The UnclaimSpec Cypher query includes the agent parameter in the MATCH pattern:
    MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})-[r:CLAIMED_BY {agent: $agent}]->(a)
    DELETE r, a

If the spec is not claimed by the given agent (or is not claimed at all), the MATCH finds no rows and the DELETE is a no-op. No error is returned. The handler unconditionally responds with 200 OK and prints 'Claim released.'

This means:
1. An agent can call UnclaimSpec with any slug/agent combination and receive a success response regardless of whether a claim was actually released.
2. There is no way for the caller to distinguish 'claim released' from 'no claim existed' or 'wrong agent' — a silent no-op masquerading as success.

Suggested fix: After DELETE, check the number of deleted relationships (using a RETURN clause or summary counters) and return an error if zero were deleted.
title: UnclaimSpec does not verify agent ownership before deleting, silently succeeds for wrong agent

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go implements a non-obvious semantic: the BLOCKS edge type is stored as an inverted DEPENDS_ON (i.e. if A blocks B, the stored edge is B DEPENDS_ON A). This reversal happens in resolveEdge() and silently affects AddEdge, RemoveEdge, and ListEdges.

There is no test case in internal/storage/memgraph/graph_test.go that exercises EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS. Without a test, a future refactor of the direction-swap logic would produce wrong dependency data silently.

Similarly, the server-layer test (internal/server/graph_handler_test.go) only uses EDGE_TYPE_DEPENDS_ON.

Suggested tests to add (integration level in graph_test.go):
- TestBlocksEdgeDirectionReversal: add A-BLOCKS->B, then verify GetDependencies(B) returns A (not B), and GetImpact(A) returns B.
- Verify RemoveEdge with BLOCKS removes the correct stored DEPENDS_ON arc.

Criticality 8: if the reversal logic is broken, the dependency graph silently returns wrong results, breaking GetReady and impact analysis.
title: No test for BLOCKS edge type direction reversal in memgraph graph storage

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
Files: internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go (lines ~6311-6314, ~6502-6514)

The code documents that EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS is stored as an inverse DEPENDS_ON relationship:
  // A blocks B (inverse of depends_on, stored as DEPENDS_ON from B→A)

However, relNameToEdgeType() — used by ListEdges to reconstruct edge types from Cypher results — maps 'DEPENDS_ON' back to EDGE_TYPE_DEPENDS_ON only. It has no case for BLOCKS:
  case "DEPENDS_ON":
      return specv1.EdgeType_EDGE_TYPE_DEPENDS_ON

This means:
- AddEdge(A, B, BLOCKS) stores a B→A DEPENDS_ON relationship
- ListEdges on either A or B will return that edge as EDGE_TYPE_DEPENDS_ON, not EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS
- The BLOCKS type is silently lost on read

The comments correctly describe the storage strategy but fail to warn callers about this read-back asymmetry. A developer using ListEdges and expecting BLOCKS edges to appear as BLOCKS will be surprised.

Suggested fix: Add a comment to relNameToEdgeType() explaining that BLOCKS edges are returned as DEPENDS_ON (or fix the round-trip if that is not intentional), and add a similar note to the ListEdges doc comment.
title: EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS round-trip loss undocumented: ListEdges never returns BLOCKS

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description: In proto/specgraph/v1/decision.proto the Decision.status field (field 4) and UpdateDecisionRequest.status (field 3) are typed as plain string with allowed values documented only in a comment: 'proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded'. This is not enforced at the proto layer or in any handler validation. Clients can write any arbitrary string. This violates schema contract expectations, complicates filtering in ListDecisionsRequest.status (field 1), and makes future migration to a proper enum a breaking change (field renumbering not needed, but string-to-enum wire format changes). Fix: Define a DecisionStatus enum in decision.proto with DECISION_STATUS_UNSPECIFIED=0, DECISION_STATUS_PROPOSED=1, DECISION_STATUS_ACCEPTED=2, DECISION_STATUS_DEPRECATED=3, DECISION_STATUS_SUPERSEDED=4 and use it in Decision and UpdateDecisionRequest.
title: Decision.status modeled as free-form string instead of enum

close_reason: Deferred: rate limiting is operational hardening, not Phase 1 scope
description:
None of the ConnectRPC handlers or the HTTP mux implement rate limiting. Combined with unauthenticated access, any caller can:

- Flood CreateSpec/CreateDecision to exhaust Memgraph storage
- Flood ListSpecs/ListDecisions/ListEdges with high-limit values to generate repeated DB scans
- Repeatedly call GetTransitiveDeps or GetCriticalPath to saturate the graph engine

Files: cmd/specgraph/serve.go — mux registration with no middleware
       internal/server/*.go — no per-handler rate limiting

Suggested fix: Add a rate-limiting middleware (e.g., golang.org/x/time/rate) to the HTTP mux, or use a per-IP connection limiter. For a dev tool, even a simple global rate limit (e.g., 100 req/s) provides meaningful protection.
title: No rate limiting on any endpoint enables brute-force and resource exhaustion attacks

description:
The storage interfaces exhibit an inconsistent coupling pattern between proto types and storage-layer types:

- ClaimBackend (internal/storage/claim.go) returns *specv1.Claim — a proto type — directly from storage.
- GraphBackend (internal/storage/graph.go) returns []storage.NodeRef (a storage-layer type) for query methods, but returns *specv1.Edge (a proto type) for AddEdge/ListEdges.
- DecisionBackend (internal/storage/decision.go) returns *specv1.Decision — a proto type.
- Backend (internal/storage/storage.go) returns *specv1.Spec — a proto type.

This means the storage layer has a mandatory compile-time dependency on the generated proto package (specgraphv1). If the proto shape changes (field additions, renames), the storage interface breaks — even for backends that would otherwise be unaffected.

The NodeRef case (storage.NodeRef being a storage-native type) in GraphBackend is inconsistent with the other backends and requires a manual conversion step in graph_handler.go (nodeRefsToProto). This inconsistency makes it unclear whether storage types should be proto-independent or not.

Suggested fix: Either (a) make all storage interfaces proto-independent by returning storage-native structs throughout, or (b) accept the proto coupling and remove the storage.NodeRef indirection (return *specv1.NodeRef directly from GraphBackend). The current mixed approach is the worst of both worlds: it incurs the indirection cost without achieving the isolation benefit.
title: Storage interface mixes proto types and storage-layer types inconsistently across services

close_reason: Documentation gap, not a code bug — Memgraph choice can be documented separately
description:
ADR-001 specifies exactly two storage backends: Beads+Dolt (Path A) and Postgres+AGE (Path B). The implementation uses Memgraph (a graph database) as its primary backend, which appears nowhere in the spec, ADR-001, ADR-002, or ADR-003. This is a significant architectural deviation from the documented design.

Consequences of this deviation:
1. The Beads backend path is entirely absent, which means Gastown integration (ADR-002) cannot be built on top of this implementation.
2. Postgres+AGE is present in docker-compose templates but has no actual storage implementation.
3. The spec's stated rationale for Beads (Dolt versioning, atomic claims via bd claim, hash-based IDs, branch-per-agent) is not realized.
4. The spec's rationale for Postgres (ACID transactions, SELECT FOR UPDATE claims, LISTEN/NOTIFY) is not realized.
5. Memgraph uses a different ID scheme (sha256 prefix) that differs from both the Beads hash-based IDs and the Postgres short-hash decision IDs specified in ADR-003.

The Memgraph implementation may have been chosen for its native Cypher graph queries, but this is not documented as an ADR or design decision.

Files: internal/storage/memgraph/ (entire directory), cmd/specgraph/init.go (memgraph default), internal/config/
title: Memgraph is used as primary storage backend but is not specified in any design document

close_reason: Deferred: GetCriticalPath tests can follow in a test expansion PR
description:
GetCriticalPath is a graph algorithm exposed as an RPC. The Cypher query in internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go (GetCriticalPath) uses MATCH p = (a {slug})-[:DEPENDS_ON*]->... ORDER BY length(p) DESC LIMIT 1. There is no test for it at either layer:
- internal/storage/memgraph/graph_test.go has no TestGetCriticalPath or TestCriticalPath test.
- internal/server/graph_handler_test.go has no test exercising the GetCriticalPath RPC endpoint.
- The mock implementation (graph_handler_test.go line 5325) returns a trivial single-element list unconditionally, so it cannot catch handler bugs.

Bugs this would catch:
- The query returns the path in wrong order (leaf first vs root first).
- A spec with no outgoing DEPENDS_ON edges returns an empty path rather than just itself.
- The query traverses non-DEPENDS_ON edges.

Suggested test in graph_test.go:
  TestGetCriticalPath: build a linear chain A->B->C; expect GetCriticalPath(A) to return [A, B, C] in order.
  Also test GetCriticalPath on a leaf node (no deps) and on a nonexistent slug.
title: No test coverage for GetCriticalPath in server or storage integration tests

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
title: generateID length-capping logic is incorrect and overly complex

close_reason: Out of scope: auth/authz is Phase 3, not part of this vertical slice
description:
All ConnectRPC service handlers — ClaimService, DecisionService, GraphService, SpecService, and HealthService — are registered on the HTTP mux with no authentication or authorization middleware. Any unauthenticated caller on the network can invoke any RPC, including:

- ClaimSpec / UnclaimSpec: claim or steal any spec from any agent
- CreateDecision / UpdateDecision: create or overwrite architectural decisions
- AddEdge / RemoveEdge: mutate the dependency graph arbitrarily
- UpdateSpec: change intent, stage, priority of any spec

Files:
- internal/server/claim_handler.go: ClaimSpec, UnclaimSpec, Heartbeat — no auth
- internal/server/decision_handler.go: CreateDecision, UpdateDecision — no auth
- internal/server/graph_handler.go: AddEdge, RemoveEdge — no auth
- internal/server/spec_handler.go: CreateSpec, UpdateSpec — no auth
- cmd/specgraph/serve.go: all handlers registered without auth middleware

Suggested fix: Add an authentication interceptor (e.g., shared secret token, mTLS, or bearer token) to the ConnectRPC handler chain before registering services. At minimum gate mutating operations (Create/Update/Delete) behind auth. For a local-only dev tool, bind to 127.0.0.1 by default (see separate finding) to reduce blast radius.
title: No authentication or authorization on any RPC endpoint

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description: In proto/specgraph/v1/graph.proto the EdgeType enum defines EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS=2 with a comment 'inverse of depends_on, stored as DEPENDS_ON from B->A'. The memgraph implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go confirms this: edgeTypeToRel maps EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS to 'DEPENDS_ON' and resolveEdge swaps from/to direction. This causes two API contract problems: (1) ListEdges filtered by EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS will match the same underlying DEPENDS_ON relationships as a DEPENDS_ON filter, making it impossible for callers to reliably distinguish the two semantically, and (2) AddEdge with EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS returns an Edge response with EdgeType=EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS but the data is stored as DEPENDS_ON—a roundtrip read via ListEdges will return EDGE_TYPE_DEPENDS_ON, not EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS. This breaks idempotency and call-response contract symmetry. Fix: Either store BLOCKS as a distinct relationship type (e.g., BLOCKS rel type) or remove EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS from the enum and document that callers should use DEPENDS_ON with reversed node order.
title: EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS stored as inverted DEPENDS_ON leaks storage detail into API contract

close_reason: Out of scope: heartbeat CLI command can be added in next CLI iteration
description: The ClaimService proto (proto/specgraph/v1/claim.proto) defines three RPCs: ClaimSpec, UnclaimSpec, and Heartbeat. The CLI in cmd/specgraph/claim.go implements 'claim' and 'unclaim' commands but there is no 'heartbeat' command. Without a heartbeat command, long-running agents have no CLI-level mechanism to extend their lease; they must implement their own client. This is a capability gap in the CLI-to-RPC surface contract. Fix: Add a 'heartbeat <slug>' CLI command with --agent and --extend-by flags to cmd/specgraph/claim.go.
title: Heartbeat RPC defined in ClaimService but no CLI command exists

close_reason: Design divergence (agent as graph node vs property) — not blocking, track separately if needed
description:
ADR-001 specifies claims via: UPDATE specs SET status='in-progress', owner=$agent, claimed_at=now(), lease_expires=now()+interval, version=version+1 WHERE slug=$slug AND status='approved' AND owner IS NULL AND version=$expected_version. Key elements: optimistic concurrency via version check, status change to 'in-progress', and claim as a property update rather than a separate relationship.

The Memgraph implementation (internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go) stores claims as a CLAIMED_BY graph relationship between a Spec node and an Agent node. This differs from the spec in two ways:
1. It creates a separate Agent node rather than storing the agent as a property on the spec.
2. The spec Cypher query deletes old Agent nodes on unclaim (DELETE r, a), which could have side effects if the same Agent node is referenced by multiple claims.
3. The Spec node's status field is NOT updated to 'in-progress' on claim (the spec model requires this). Only the CLAIMED_BY relationship is created.

The optimistic concurrency version check from ADR-001 is also absent — ClaimSpec does not check or increment the spec version, making concurrent claims possible in theory between the DELETE-expired and CREATE steps.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go lines 5815-5845
title: Claim protocol stores agent as a graph node (Agent label) rather than as a property; spec uses optimistic concurrency with version field

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
The UnclaimSpec handler (internal/server/claim_handler.go) passes slug and agent to the backend, but the mock backend in internal/server/claim_handler_test.go's UnclaimSpec implementation (line 4740) ignores the agent parameter entirely and deletes any claim for the slug:

  func (m *mockClaimBackend) UnclaimSpec(_ context.Context, slug, _ string) error {
      m.mu.Lock()
      defer m.mu.Unlock()
      delete(m.claims, slug)
      return nil
  }

There is no test that verifies that agent-2 cannot unclaim a spec currently claimed by agent-1. Whether enforcement happens in the real Cypher backend is unclear from the diff, but the absence of a test means neither the contract nor the implementation is validated.

Suggested tests:
- TestClaimHandler_UnclaimWrongAgent: agent-1 claims; agent-2 calls UnclaimSpec; expect error (CodePermissionDenied or CodeFailedPrecondition).
- TestClaimAndUnclaim_WrongAgent in memgraph integration tests.

Criticality 7: without this, any agent can steal another agent's claimed spec, breaking the multi-agent coordination protocol.
title: Claim handler does not verify that the unclaiming agent matches the owner

close_reason: Fixed in batch 1: error-returning recordString/recordInt64, generateID hex fix, claim handler nil guards + error codes, Decision status enum, spec_slug naming
description:
File: internal/server/claim_handler.go, ClaimSpec method (line ~4649)

The ClaimSpec handler maps ALL storage errors to connect.CodeFailedPrecondition:

    return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeFailedPrecondition, err)

This means two distinct failure cases — spec not found vs. spec already claimed by another agent — both surface as FailedPrecondition to the client. The underlying storage error messages (e.g., 'spec %q not found or already claimed') disambiguate them in text, but clients cannot distinguish them programmatically without string-parsing the error message.

The Heartbeat handler has the same issue in reverse: it uses CodeNotFound for all errors, which would be wrong if the spec exists but the claim is expired or belongs to another agent.

Suggested fix: Inspect the storage error and select the appropriate Connect code — CodeNotFound when the spec does not exist, CodeAlreadyExists or CodeFailedPrecondition when it is already claimed. Consider sentinel error types or wrapped error values from the storage layer to enable this.
title: ClaimSpec handler uses CodeFailedPrecondition for all claim errors, masking not-found from already-claimed

- cmd/specgraph/graph.go (runDeps, runReady, runCriticalPath, runImpact)
- cmd/specgraph/health.go (runHealth)

init_test.go covers init, but none of the new commands. The only real test in the CLI package is the existing init test.

The edgeTypeMap lookup in edge.go is particularly testable in isolation: an unknown type string (e.g., 'foobar') should return an error, and currently no test validates this path.

Suggested approach: use Cobra's ExecuteC with a test server (httptest.Server with a mock handler) to exercise argument parsing, flag validation, and error handling for each command. At minimum, test the client-free paths such as the edgeTypeMap validation and the CLI error formatting.

Criticality 4: CLI is user-facing and regressions in flag validation or output formatting are visible immediately, but the server-layer tests already cover the underlying logic.
title: No tests for CLI commands: claim, unclaim, decision subcommands, edge, graph queries

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
The Decision type has a structural invariant: when Status == 'superseded', SupersededBy MUST be non-empty (it must reference the slug of the superseding decision). This invariant is documented in the proto comment but not enforced anywhere.

In proto/specgraph/v1/decision.proto:
  string status = 4;           // proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded
  string superseded_by = 7;    // slug of superseding decision, if any

In the UpdateDecision handler (internal/server/decision_handler.go line ~4885-4895), a caller can:
  UpdateDecision(slug='foo', Status='superseded')  // SupersededBy=nil = not changed = still empty

This sets status=superseded while SupersededBy remains empty string, creating an invalid Decision state that is stored and returned to callers.

The storage layer (internal/storage/memgraph/decision.go UpdateDecision) applies nil-field patching, so it will happily store status='superseded' with superseded_by='' if that's what's in the database.

There is no validation at the handler boundary (decision_handler.go) or storage boundary.

Suggested fix: Add a validation check in DecisionHandler.UpdateDecision:
  if msg.Status \!= nil && *msg.Status == 'superseded' {
      if msg.SupersededBy == nil || *msg.SupersededBy == '' {
          return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument,
              fmt.Errorf('superseded_by is required when setting status to superseded'))
      }
  }

A deeper fix would model the Decision state machine explicitly (perhaps as a Go type with a transition method that validates preconditions), but the handler-level check is a practical near-term fix.
title: Decision 'superseded' status invariant not enforced: SupersededBy can be empty when Status is superseded

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
The EdgeType enum (proto/specgraph/v1/graph.proto) documents EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS as 'stored as DEPENDS_ON from B→A'. The storage implementation in internal/storage/memgraph/graph.go (resolveEdge function, line ~6321) swaps from/to when edge type is BLOCKS, correctly storing the inverse DEPENDS_ON in the graph.

However, the AddEdge RPC returns an Edge with from_id and to_id (gen/specgraph/v1/graph.pb.go line ~2133). When the handler calls h.store.AddEdge() and gets back an Edge, the Edge.FromId and Edge.ToId reflect the ACTUAL stored direction (post-swap), not the requested direction. The CLI displays:
  fmt.Printf('Added %s edge: %s → %s\n', edgeAddType, resp.Msg.FromId, resp.Msg.ToId)

So 'edge add spec-a spec-b --type blocks' would display the swapped IDs, showing the storage artifact rather than the semantic edge the user requested. This violates the principle of least surprise: the user said A blocks B but the response says the edge goes from B's ID to A's ID.

Additionally, the Edge message type (proto) has no way to represent EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS semantically — it only stores the stored DEPENDS_ON. Callers receiving an Edge back from AddEdge (blocks) cannot reconstruct the original intent.

Suggested fix: Either (a) return the semantic edge (preserving original from/to with the requested EdgeType), mapping only at storage time, or (b) document clearly in the proto that Edge.from_id/to_id are storage IDs after direction normalization and that BLOCKS will always appear as DEPENDS_ON in responses. A NodeRef-based response that carries slug+direction semantics would be cleaner than raw IDs.
title: EDGE_TYPE_BLOCKS documented as inverse DEPENDS_ON but Edge.from_id/to_id semantics are ambiguous after direction swap

close_reason: Fixed in batch 2: claim MERGE + agent verify, BLOCKS as own type, decision error handling
description:
The ClaimService.UnclaimSpec RPC accepts an arbitrary agent string from the request body and deletes any matching CLAIMED_BY relationship without verifying that the caller is the agent that holds the claim.

File: internal/server/claim_handler.go, lines 4655-4661 (diff)
File: internal/storage/memgraph/claim.go, UnclaimSpec function

The Cypher query:
  MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})-[r:CLAIMED_BY {agent: $agent}]->(a)
  DELETE r, a

This means if agent-A holds a claim on 'my-spec', any unauthenticated caller can send:
  UnclaimSpec { slug: "my-spec", agent: "agent-A" }
and the claim is deleted — with no verification that the caller is actually agent-A.

Similarly, Heartbeat has the same flaw: any caller can extend another agent's lease indefinitely.

Suggested fix: Require a cryptographic token or session credential tied to the agent that proves identity. The simplest approach for a single-node dev tool is a shared secret generated at claim time and returned only to the claiming caller, required for subsequent UnclaimSpec/Heartbeat calls.
title: UnclaimSpec does not verify agent identity, allowing any caller to release another agent's claim

See finding spgr-uqx.1 for full context.
title: Refactor AuthoringBackend to use domain types instead of protobuf

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8 adding the authoring funnel: proto, handler, storage, CLI, business logic
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 3990-3994

The storeJSONProperty function uses fmt.Sprintf to interpolate the property name directly into a Cypher query string:

    query := fmt.Sprintf(`
        MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
        SET s.%s = $data, s.updated_at = $updated_at
        RETURN s.slug
    `, property)

The slug and data values are correctly parameterized, but the property name is interpolated as a string literal into the query itself. Two defenses are in place: an allowlist check (allowedJSONProperties map) and a character validation loop. Both checks happen before the interpolation, so the actual risk of injection through a hot code path is low in the current implementation.

However, the defense is brittle:
1. If a future caller passes a property name that slips past both guards (e.g. a refactor accidentally uses a variable not populated from the allowlist), the query becomes injectable.
2. The allowlist and character check are internal to the function; callers at the storage interface level have no visibility into this constraint.
3. The character check iterates byte-by-byte over a string that has already passed the allowlist — if the allowlist is ever removed or bypassed, the character check alone does not prevent all injection vectors (e.g. a property name ending with a backtick or Cypher keyword boundary).

Recommended fix: Consider restructuring to avoid interpolation entirely by using a lookup table (map[string]string) of pre-built query templates, one per property name. This eliminates the runtime string interpolation path completely and makes the safe-by-construction nature explicit.
title: [security] storeJSONProperty interpolates property name into Cypher query string

description: DecompositionStrategy (internal/storage/authoring.go:58) and PeripheralDisposition (internal/storage/authoring.go:115) are defined as string types with a validStrategies map guard for DecompositionStrategy but no equivalent for PeripheralDisposition. The proto defines both as proper enums (DecompositionStrategy, PeripheralDisposition in authoring.proto:53 and 43). Callers can construct storage.DecompositionStrategy("typo") without any compile-time rejection. ValidateStrategy exists for DecompositionStrategy but it is not called during storage writes — internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go stores the value from DecomposeOutput without calling ValidateStrategy first. PeripheralDisposition has no validation at all. The proto enum already provides exhaustive validation at the transport boundary; consider mapping from proto enum to storage string at the handler layer and trusting that mapping rather than leaving arbitrary strings to flow through. Alternatively, if keeping the domain string types, add a ValidateDisposition mirror and call both validators before writing.
title: [types] DecompositionStrategy and PeripheralDisposition are string types in domain but proto enums, no validation bridge
[simplify] authoring_handler.go: txBackend if/else duplicated across four RPC handlers

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go the Approve handler calls TransitionStage with a hardcoded 'from' of StageDecompose:

  h.store.TransitionStage(ctx, req.Msg.Slug, storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageDecompose), storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageApproved))

This means Approve always fails if the spec is not currently at the decompose stage. However, the AmendRequest proto comment says 'Approved specs cannot be amended; use Supersede to replace them instead', implying users interact with Approve only after Decompose, but the current implementation cannot approve a spec that was amended back to an earlier stage without going through all subsequent stages again.

More critically, the API contract (the proto comment on ApproveRequest) does not document this 'must be at decompose stage' precondition. Clients will receive CodeFailedPrecondition with no actionable hint about which stage the spec needs to be at.

Suggested fix: Either (1) read the current stage from storage before transitioning, accept approval from any non-approved stage, or (2) document the precondition explicitly in the proto comment on ApproveRequest and return a structured error message with the expected and actual stage.
title: Approve RPC hardcodes transition from decompose stage only, preventing approval from earlier stages

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go the Spark handler calls h.backend.CreateSpec and, on failure, unconditionally maps to connect.CodeInternal (lines ~2585-2586 and ~2570-2578 in the diff). When a caller attempts to spark a slug that already exists, the storage layer returns an ErrSpecNotFound-like duplicate error (or a Memgraph constraint error), but the handler surfaces it as 500 Internal instead of 409 CodeAlreadyExists. Callers receive an opaque internal error with no indication that the slug is taken.

Location: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark() non-tx path (~line 79 in final file) and tx path (~line 64).

Suggested fix: Define storage.ErrSpecAlreadyExists, have CreateSpec return it on duplicate, and add an errors.Is check in both the tx and non-tx Spark paths (and in stageError) to return connect.CodeAlreadyExists.
title: Spark CreateSpec duplicate slug mapped to CodeInternal instead of CodeAlreadyExists

description:
The Amend handler (authoring_handler.go:254-259) has two validation branches that lack test coverage:

1. AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED input → CodeInvalidArgument
2. Unknown proto stage value (not in protoToStage map) → CodeInvalidArgument

The existing tests cover: empty slug, happy path, and the backend error path. Neither negative validation branch is exercised.

If a future proto addition creates a stage that isn't in protoToStage, clients would receive a misleading error rather than a clear invalid-argument response.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go (missing tests)
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/server/authoring_handler.go:254

Suggested fix: Add TestAuthoringHandler_Amend_UnspecifiedTargetStage using TargetStage: AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED and verify CodeInvalidArgument.
title: Amend handler: missing test for AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED and unknown target_stage rejection

description: internal/authoring/stages.go declares stage constants as plain untyped string constants (const StageSpark = "spark"). The functions ValidateTransition and ValidateAmendTransition accept plain string, not storage.AuthoringStage. This means any raw string can be passed where a validated stage name is expected. The stages slice (var stages = []string{...}) and indexOf function also work on raw strings. The type system cannot distinguish a stage name from an arbitrary string at any of these call sites. The memgraph implementation then compares storage.AuthoringStage values against authoring.StageApproved (a plain string) using == directly (memgraph/authoring.go:41), which works only because Go string equality is value-based but is semantically confusing — a value of type AuthoringStage should not be compared to an untyped string constant without a cast. Suggested fix: declare stages as type storage.AuthoringStage constants; update AllStages to return []storage.AuthoringStage; update ValidateTransition to accept storage.AuthoringStage parameters.
title: [types] Stage constants in authoring package are untyped strings, bypassing AuthoringStage type safety entirely

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 3824-3862

StoreDecomposeOutput constructs child spec slugs by concatenating the parent slug with the slice ID from the request:

    childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, sl.ID)

The parent slug is validated by validateSlug in the handler before reaching storage. However, sl.ID (DecomposeSlice.ID from the proto message) is never validated. A caller can provide a slice ID such as:
- '../other-spec' — bypasses the parent's slug namespace, constructing a child slug that resolves to a different top-level spec
- '../../etc/passwd' — arbitrary string stored as a node slug in the graph
- An empty string — produces a slug ending in '/' (e.g. 'my-spec/') which may cause unexpected behavior

The Cypher queries use parameterized values for the slug fields, so this is not a Cypher injection risk. However, it is a data integrity and namespace isolation issue: a malicious or buggy client can create spec nodes outside the intended parent namespace, create cross-namespace COMPOSES edges, and potentially reference or shadow existing specs by crafting a childSlug that matches an existing spec's slug.

The DependsOn IDs in sl.DependsOn have the same issue:
    depSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, dep)

Recommended fix: Apply validateSlug (or a slice-specific validator that rejects '/' and '.') to sl.ID and each dep in sl.DependsOn before constructing the child slug. This should happen in the handler (authoring_handler.go decomposeOutputToDomain) so it returns a CodeInvalidArgument before reaching storage.
title: [security] DecomposeSlice.ID is not validated before being used to construct child spec slugs
Shape/Specify/Decompose RPCs hardcode 'from' stage, coupling authoring funnel strictly to sequential ordering
[simplify] authoring/stages.go: ValidateTransition and ValidateAmendTransition duplicate unknown-stage check
[comments] buildPatterns comment inaccurately describes pattern-matching condition

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go and cmd/specgraph/serve.go

All 8 RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede, GetPrompts) are registered with no authentication or authorization middleware:

    // serve.go line 308
    server.RegisterAuthoringService(mux, store, store)

    // RegisterAuthoringService in authoring_handler.go line 2827
    path, h := specgraphv1connect.NewAuthoringServiceHandler(handler)
    mux.Handle(path, h)

Any unauthenticated caller on the network can:
- Create arbitrary spec nodes (Spark) with any slug
- Advance or roll back any spec through the authoring funnel (Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend)
- Mark any spec as superseded (Supersede)
- This is true for all existing services (GraphService, ClaimService, ConstitutionService) as well, but the authoring funnel is new in this PR and expands the attack surface

Severity classified as important (not critical) because this appears to be an early-phase internal tool without documented external deployment, but the pattern should be addressed before any production or shared deployment.

Recommended fix: Add a middleware interceptor (ConnectRPC interceptor pattern) that enforces authentication at minimum — even a simple API key or mutual TLS check — before any handler is invoked. The interceptor can be added once at mux registration time and applied to all services.
title: [security] No authorization/authentication on any AuthoringService RPC

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, AmendSpec retrieves the version field from the Memgraph record with a type assertion on int64:

  retVersion, _ := rec.Get("s.version")
  if v, ok := retVersion.(int64); ok {
      result.Version = safeInt32(v)
  }

If Memgraph returns the version as a different numeric type (e.g. int, float64 — which some neo4j drivers do for untyped integer fields), the type assertion fails silently and result.Version remains 0. The caller (AmendResponse) will show version=0 even after a successful amend. There is no log or error indicating the unexpected type.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, AmendSpec(), lines ~3955-3963 in diff.

Suggested fix: Log a warning or return an error when retVersion is non-nil but not int64. Alternatively, use a more defensive numeric conversion (check both int64 and float64/int).
title: AmendSpec silently returns Version=0 when Memgraph returns non-int64 type for version field

description:
The memgraph AmendSpec implementation (authoring.go:206-244) has two error branches without integration test coverage:

1. Spec is at 'approved' stage → ErrSpecAlreadyApproved (line 211-213). The test TestAmendSpec advances to 'specify' then amends back to 'shape', but never tests amending an approved spec.
2. ValidateAmendTransition fails → ErrInvalidStageTransition (line 214-216). No test attempts an invalid backward transition (e.g. trying to amend spark → shape, which is a forward move).

These are meaningful error paths: if the approved guard is accidentally removed, an approved spec could be amended without version tracking.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (missing tests)
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:206

Suggested fix:
  TestAmendSpec_AlreadyApproved: create spec, advance all stages to approved, call AmendSpec, require ErrorIs ErrSpecAlreadyApproved.
  TestAmendSpec_InvalidTransition: create spec at spark, call AmendSpec with target=shape (forward), require ErrorIs ErrInvalidStageTransition.
title: AmendSpec: no integration test for ErrSpecAlreadyApproved path and invalid amend transition

description: AmendResult (internal/storage/authoring.go:163-168) declares Stage as plain string rather than AuthoringStage. This is inconsistent: the AmendSpec interface method accepts targetStage AuthoringStage but returns a result whose Stage field is untyped. Any code reading AmendResult.Stage must decide how to interpret the string without type guidance. Since storage.AuthoringStage is already defined in the same file, there is no cost to using it here. Similarly, the StoreDecomposeOutput return type []string (internal/storage/authoring.go:176) returns child spec slugs but there is no type alias to document that contract — a []SpecSlug or even a named return would make intent clearer.
title: [types] AmendResult uses bare string for Stage field rather than AuthoringStage
[comments] ScanTier1 godoc omits non-obvious nil-error behavior for non-existent paths

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, lines 2757-2776; internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 3947

The Amend RPC accepts an empty reason string and stores it verbatim on the spec node:

    result, err := h.store.AmendSpec(ctx, req.Msg.Slug, req.Msg.Reason, targetStage)

    // memgraph/authoring.go
    map[string]any{"slug": slug, "stage": string(targetStage), "reason": reason, ...}

No minimum length or content check is performed on req.Msg.Reason. An amend with an empty reason produces a spec node where amend_reason is set to the empty string, making the audit trail useless for understanding why a spec was rolled back.

Similarly, the Supersede RPC (authoring_handler.go line 2790) stores req.Msg.Reason without validation.

This is a minor audit-trail integrity issue: it does not enable injection or privilege escalation, but it undermines the intent of having a mandatory reason field.

Recommended fix: Add a check in Amend and Supersede handlers that reason is non-empty before calling storage. Return CodeInvalidArgument if reason is missing.
title: [security] Amend RPC does not verify the caller supplied a reason; empty reason stored in graph
[simplify] cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go: repeated empty-slug and flag registration tests are copy-paste

description:
SupersedeSpec in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go performs three distinct operations without a transaction:
1. GetSpec(slug) — read to verify source exists
2. GetSpec(supersededBy) — read to verify target exists
3. executeQuery() — SET stage + CREATE SUPERSEDES edge

If the Memgraph write in step 3 fails after both reads succeed, the spec stage is not rolled back and no SUPERSEDES edge exists. The graph is silently left in a partial state. The handler in authoring_handler.go calls this method without wrapping it in RunInTransaction, and there is no compensating logic or logging.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, SupersedeSpec() (~lines 3900-3924 in diff); internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Supersede() (~line 2790).

Suggested fix: Wrap the entire SupersedeSpec operation in a transaction, or at minimum add error-level logging on partial failure. The handler should also attempt to use txBackend if available, matching the pattern used by Spark/Shape/Specify/Decompose.
title: SupersedeSpec is not transactional: partial failure leaves graph in inconsistent state

description:
proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto AmendResponse.version (field 3) is described as 'Monotonically increasing amendment count for this spec.' However, the existing Spec message in spec.proto also has a version field (field 7) that is updated for every UpdateSpec call, not just amendments.

These are the same underlying field in the Memgraph store (s.version + 1), but the proto documentation creates ambiguity:
- Is AmendResponse.version the spec-wide version or amendment-specific?
- Do Spark/Shape/Specify/Decompose calls also bump the version?
- Do clients need to track both?

The memgraph AmendSpec query (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go) does SET s.version = s.version + 1, so it bumps the global version. But the proto comment implies it's amendment-specific.

Suggested fix: Clarify in the proto comment whether this is the same version field as Spec.version or a separate amendment counter. Consider renaming to 'spec_version' or adding an 'amendment_count' field alongside version if they need to be distinct.
title: AmendResponse uses int32 version but Spec message uses int32 version — type is fine but semantics are undocumented

description: In internal/authoring/passes.go:42 and internal/authoring/prompts.go:16, the passRegistry and promptRegistry maps are keyed by plain string. The stage constants (StageSpark etc.) are used as keys at the declaration site, but the public functions PassesForStage and GetPrompts accept plain string parameters. A caller can pass any string and silently get nil back with no indication of the error. If the stage constants were typed (e.g. type Stage string with constants), the map keys and function parameters could also be typed, making invalid stage names a compile error. Additionally, the promptRegistry uses string literals directly ("spark", "shape") as map keys instead of the StageSpark/StageShape constants, creating a maintenance inconsistency: if a stage is renamed, the prompt registry would silently stop matching. File: internal/authoring/prompts.go:17-40 — keys are string literals, not constants.
title: [types] passRegistry and promptRegistry use plain string keys instead of typed stage constants

description:
The Tier1 edge tests (edge_test.go) cover malformed files, symlinks, non-existent paths, and unreadable directories — all good. However, Tier2 (tier2_test.go) only covers the happy path and an empty dir case. The walkGoFiles function used by Tier2 has the same parse-error recording logic as Tier1, but it is never exercised for Tier2.

Specifically untested for ScanTier2:
- Malformed Go file in a scanned dir: should appear in result.SkippedFiles without error
- Dir path that does not exist within root: should not panic
- Multiple dirs in the dirs slice: only single-dir is tested

The concern is that Tier2 relies on walkGoFiles with skipTests=false, and the interaction between test-file tracking (result.TestFiles append) and parse failures is not covered.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/scanner/tier2_test.go
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/scanner/tier2.go:41-108

Suggested fix: Add TestTier2Scan_MalformedGoFile mirroring TestTier1Scan_MalformedGoFile, and TestTier2Scan_MultipleDirs with two dirs to ensure both are scanned.
title: Scanner Tier2: no test for ScanTier2 with malformed Go files or non-existent dirs
[security] Internal error messages include slug values and storage details in CodeInternal responses
[comments] AuthoringStage enum values lack doc comments unlike all other enums in authoring.proto

description:
File: internal/authoring/prompts_test.go (line ~1080) vs internal/authoring/prompts.go (line ~1033)

prompts_test.go declares a local stageToEnum map for test assertions:

    var stageToEnum = map[string]specv1.AuthoringStage{
        "spark":     specv1.AuthoringStage_AUTHORING_STAGE_SPARK,
        "shape":     specv1.AuthoringStage_AUTHORING_STAGE_SHAPE,
        ...
    }

This is structurally identical to the private stageToProtoEnum map in prompts.go. The test needs this for assertions in TestPromptsToProto.

Two options:
1. Export stageToProtoEnum (or add a StageToProtoStage(string) function) so tests don't need to maintain their own copy.
2. If the map should remain private, note that the test duplicates the mapping and will silently drift if prompts.go ever adds a stage without updating the test.

Option 1 is cleaner: export a small lookup function like ProtoStageFor(stage string) (specv1.AuthoringStage, bool) that both tests and handler code can use. This would also eliminate the protoToStage/stageToProto maps in authoring_handler.go that partially replicate the same data.
title: [simplify] authoring/prompts_test.go: stageToEnum duplicates production stageToProtoEnum map

description: SafetyInput (internal/authoring/safety.go:32-36) is a plain struct with no constructor and no validation. RunSafetyNet concatenates Text, Scope, and Invariants before scanning. An empty Text with populated Scope fields will scan only the scope content, which may not align with the documented stage semantics in the comment (Spark: seed idea text; Shape: risks list). There is no enforcement of the per-stage content contract. Additionally, SafetyCategory is a string type (safety.go:39) with only two constants (security, data_loss) but the proto SafetyFlag.category field is a raw string — an implementation could produce a category value not in the proto enum, and the type system would not catch it. Consider a constructor NewSafetyInput(text string, opts ...SafetyInputOption) that enforces at least one non-empty content field, or document the zero-value behavior explicitly.
title: [types] SafetyInput lacks validation at construction; empty Text with non-empty Scope silently produces misleading results
[comments] output field marked Required in SparkRequest but not in Shape/Specify/DecomposeRequest
Spark RPC silently accepts calls with empty SparkOutput fields, creating orphan specs with no meaningful data

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage() performs the stage update as a single Cypher query. When records is empty it runs a second checkQuery to distinguish 'spec not found' from 'wrong stage'. The check query is executed via executeQuery() which, inside RunInTransaction, routes through the managed transaction. However the original update (which returned 0 records) has already completed within that transaction scope; the check query is a separate read under the same transaction, which may not see the same consistent snapshot depending on isolation level.

More critically, when TransitionStage is called *outside* a transaction (the non-tx fallback path in the handler), the primary write and the diagnostic read are completely separate autocommit operations, creating a TOCTOU window where another writer could change the spec's stage between the two queries. In that window, the error returned may incorrectly say 'wrong stage' when the spec was concurrently deleted, or vice versa — leading to a misleading error message that obscures the real failure.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage(), lines ~3783-3795 in diff.

Suggested fix: Remove the diagnostic second query and return a generic 'spec not found or wrong stage' error. The caller already has the expected stage in the error message.
