Alternatively, use a single Cypher query that returns a status discriminator (e.g. CASE WHEN ... THEN 'not_found' ELSE 'wrong_stage' END) so the discrimination happens atomically.
title: TransitionStage check-then-act race: secondary check query runs outside the original transaction scope

description:
TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_HappyPath (authoring_handler_test.go:176) sends a seed of 'some intent' and verifies Output.Seed is echoed, but does not assert anything about SafetyFlags or NextPrompts in the response.

The handler unconditionally runs RunSafetyNet and PromptsToProto (authoring_handler.go:85-90) for every Spark call. If these calls are accidentally removed or their results accidentally dropped before the response, the happy-path test would still pass.

Similarly, TestAuthoringHandler_Shape_HappyPath asserts NextPrompts is non-empty but does not verify the prompts are for the 'specify' stage (the next stage after shape). This means if the stage passed to PromptsToProto were silently changed to 'decompose', the assertion would still pass.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go:176-201
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/server/authoring_handler.go:85-90

Suggested fix: Assert resp.Msg.NextPrompts has prompts for the expected next stage (check p.Stage == AUTHORING_STAGE_SHAPE for Spark response). Use a seed known to trigger a safety flag (e.g. 'hardcoded secret') and assert SafetyFlags is non-empty with expected severity.
title: Spark handler: safety flags presence not asserted in happy-path handler test

close_reason: Wontfix: consolidating stage maps would create import cycles between server and authoring packages
description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (lines ~2901-2917) and internal/authoring/prompts.go (line ~1033)

Three maps independently enumerate all five authoring stages:

  1. stageToProto (handler): string -> specv1.AuthoringStage
  2. protoToStage (handler): specv1.AuthoringStage -> storage.AuthoringStage
  3. stageToProtoEnum (prompts.go): string -> specv1.AuthoringStage (same as #1)

Maps 1 and 3 have the same key/value types and identical entries, but live in different packages. If a new stage is ever added, all three maps must be updated separately.

Suggested fix: Centralise stage-to-proto mapping in the authoring package as an exported function or map, and have authoring_handler.go use it instead of its own copy. This makes the handler a consumer of authoring domain knowledge rather than a re-declarant. The protoToStage map (handler #2) is the inverse and could similarly be derived from the canonical map.
title: [simplify] authoring_handler.go: three separate stage-mapping tables cover the same five stages

description: ConsistencyIssue (internal/storage/authoring.go:131-135) has an IssueKind field of plain string type. The proto comment on issue_kind says 'e.g. overlapping scope, conflicting invariant' but neither the domain type nor the proto define an enum or constant set for this field. Without a closed set of values, serialized data can contain arbitrary strings that clients cannot interpret exhaustively. This is an anemic domain model for the IssueKind concept. A simple type IssueKind string with defined constants (IssueKindOverlappingScope, IssueKindConflictingInvariant, etc.) would give the type system something to work with and make the serialized format self-documenting. The same pattern applies to Approach.Name (ShapeOutput) where chosen_approach is a free string matching one of the Approach.Name values — there is no referential integrity enforced.
title: [types] ConsistencyIssue.IssueKind is untyped string with no defined values or validation
[comments] TODO references 'Phase 1 constitution work' but Constitution (Slice 2) is already implemented

description: storage.AuthoringStage is declared as a bare string type (internal/storage/authoring.go:161) with no typed constants defined in the storage package. The constants that name valid stages live in internal/authoring/stages.go as plain untyped strings (StageSpark = "spark", etc.). Every call site in internal/server/authoring_handler.go must cast: storage.AuthoringStage(authoring.StageSpark). This is repeated 10+ times. The cast is completely unchecked — nothing prevents passing an arbitrary string. The proto layer already defines AuthoringStage as a proper enum; the domain layer should either (a) define typed constants of type AuthoringStage in the storage package itself, or (b) define the stages in internal/authoring/stages.go as AuthoringStage constants rather than plain strings, eliminating the cast entirely. Suggested fix: change stages.go constants to type storage.AuthoringStage and import accordingly, or define const (StageSparkValue storage.AuthoringStage = "spark"; ...) in storage/authoring.go.
title: [types] AuthoringStage string type has no constants, forces unsafe casting at every call site

description:
In internal/scanner/walk.go, walkGoFiles() handles the initial WalkDir error callback by appending to skipped and returning nil. When the root path itself does not exist, WalkDir immediately calls the callback with an error; the path is recorded in skipped and no error is returned to the caller. ScanTier1 therefore returns (non-nil result, nil error) when given a non-existent path.

The test at internal/scanner/edge_test.go:TestTier1Scan_NonExistentPath explicitly asserts this behavior (require.NoError), treating it as correct. However, callers that pass an invalid path get a silently empty result with no indication that the scan root was invalid — only the SkippedFiles slice reveals the failure, and callers may not inspect it.

Location: internal/scanner/walk.go, walkGoFiles() error callback (~line 2471 in diff); internal/scanner/tier1.go, ScanTier1().

Suggested fix: Distinguish between 'error reading a child entry (skip)' and 'error opening the root itself (fail)'. If the initial WalkDir error is for the root path (path == root), return it as a hard error rather than silently skipping it. Document the intentional choice if the current behavior is preferred.
title: scanner.ScanTier1 on non-existent root silently succeeds with empty result instead of erroring
[simplify] memgraph/authoring.go: StoreDecomposeOutput redundant nil-check after ErrSpecNotFound branch

description:
In StoreDecomposeOutput (authoring.go:128-138), when a slice's DependsOn list references a sibling slice ID, a DEPENDS_ON edge is created via MERGE. The dep slug is computed as parent_slug/dep_id. If the referenced dep has not yet been created (e.g. slices are declared out of order, or the dep ID is a typo), the MATCH in the Cypher query silently returns no records — no edge is created, no error is returned.

The integration test TestStoreDecomposeOutput (authoring_test.go:65-88) only tests the case where 'slice-2' depends on 'slice-1' and slice-1 is listed first. The dependency edge creation path for a reference to a non-existent target is not tested.

This is a data-integrity gap: a mistyped DependsOn ID silently produces an incomplete graph with no error signal.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:128-138
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go

Suggested fix: Add TestStoreDecomposeOutput_UnknownDependency that submits a slice with DependsOn referencing a nonexistent ID and asserts either an error is returned OR that the missing edge is detectable post-call. Document current behavior explicitly in the test so regressions are caught.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput: DEPENDS_ON edge to a non-existent sibling slug is not validated or tested

description:
In authoring.proto, the GetPromptsRequest comment says:
  'The APPROVED stage has no prompts; requesting it returns INVALID_ARGUMENT.'

The handler in authoring_handler.go implements this via the protoToStage map — APPROVED maps to storage.AuthoringStage, and PromptsToProto returns nil for 'approved', triggering CodeInvalidArgument.

However, CodeInvalidArgument is semantically wrong for this case. The stage value APPROVED is a valid enum value; the client's request is well-formed. The issue is that no prompts exist for that stage. CodeNotFound or CodeUnimplemented would be more appropriate.

Using CodeInvalidArgument for a valid enum value is confusing for clients: they would normally see INVALID_ARGUMENT for a malformed request, not for a valid request that simply has no data associated with it. This is an API design issue that could lead to incorrect client error handling.

Suggested fix: Either return CodeNotFound('no prompts defined for stage APPROVED') or return an empty GetPromptsResponse{} (zero-length prompts slice) and let clients handle the empty case. The proto comment should be updated to match whichever behavior is chosen. If returning empty is acceptable, update GetPromptsResponse to note that an empty prompts list means no prompts are defined for the stage.
title: GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for APPROVED stage but proto doc says this instead of consistent error code

description:
File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto, DecomposeResponse message.

The proto documents DecomposeResponse.simplicity and DecomposeResponse.constitution_violations as regular fields with positive descriptions. However, the server handler (authoring_handler.go) has a TODO showing these fields are NOT yet populated:

  // TODO(authoring): Wire simplicity_check and constitution_check analytical
  // passes to populate the Decompose response.

The proto comments give no indication that these fields are always empty in the current server implementation. A client developer relying on the proto docs alone would assume these fields are populated.

Similarly, SparkResponse.constitution_violations, ShapeResponse.constitution_violations, SpecifyResponse.constitution_violations are all undocumented as unimplemented — the handler code does not populate constitution_violations for any stage.

Recommendation: add a note like '// NOTE: not yet populated by the server — see TODO in authoring_handler.go' or mark as reserved, until wiring is complete. This prevents client developers from writing code that silently never receives expected data.
title: [comments] DecomposeResponse proto fields simplicity and constitution_violations appear unimplemented but have no caveat in docs

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput iterates over each slice's DependsOn list and constructs a childSlug from the parent slug plus the dependency ID (e.g. 'parent/dep-id'). It then runs a MERGE DEPENDS_ON Cypher query.

However, there is no check that the target dependency spec actually exists before the MERGE. If a slice references a dependency ID that does not correspond to any slice in the current decompose output (a typo or a forward reference to a not-yet-created spec), the MATCH inside MERGE will find no 'to' node and the MERGE will create 0 rows — silently. No error is returned. The DEPENDS_ON edge is never created, the decompose operation succeeds, and the caller has no indication that a dependency reference was dropped.

Location: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput(), DEPENDS_ON block (~lines 3850-3861 in diff).

Suggested fix: After the DEPENDS_ON MERGE query, check if records is empty and return an error identifying the missing dependency slug. Alternatively, validate all DependsOn references against the current slice ID list before executing any Cypher.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput DEPENDS_ON edge creation silently references potentially non-existent sibling slugs

description: The plan (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, architecture section) states: 'Postures (Drive/Partner/Support) and prompt templates are server-side configuration served via a dedicated RPC.' The pass registry (internal/authoring/passes.go) correctly defines posture-aware pass scheduling via PassesForStage/OfferedPasses. However, the AuthoringHandler RPCs (internal/server/authoring_handler.go) never call authoring.PassesForStage or authoring.ResolvePosture using msg.Posture from incoming requests. The posture field is accepted in all requests (SparkRequest, ShapeRequest, SpecifyRequest, DecomposeRequest) but is silently ignored — no posture-aware scheduling occurs at runtime. The passes registry exists but is dead code in the handler path. Fix: call authoring.ResolvePosture(msg.Posture, nil) and authoring.PassesForStage(stage, resolvedPosture) in each handler, then populate the appropriate response fields (red_team, peripheral_vision, consistency_issues, simplicity).
title: [spec] Posture not wired into handler RPC pass scheduling
[comments] RunSafetyNet comment says 'suppressed by an earlier WARNING match' but ordering is irrelevant

description: The plan document (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, Task 5) specifies SafetyInput with field 'Intent string'. The implementation (internal/authoring/safety.go) renamed it to 'Text string'. While the rename is arguably an improvement (Text is more generic and correct for multi-stage use), it is an undocumented deviation from the spec. The plan tests reference 'Intent' directly (e.g. SafetyInput{Intent: "..."}). This is a minor spec drift — the implementation is arguably better, but the plan was not updated to reflect the change. Either update the plan doc or document the intentional deviation.
title: [spec] SafetyInput.Intent renamed to Text without plan update

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 114-117.

The `else if existing == nil` branch after checking `getErr != nil` is unreachable. `GetSpec` either returns a non-nil `*specv1.Spec` on success or an error (including `storage.ErrSpecNotFound`). It never returns `(nil, nil)`. The first branch already handles the not-found case; the second branch can never execute.

This is a logic bug: if somehow the nil path were ever reached, the newly-created spec would be created without the intent from `sl.Intent` because the nil branch duplicates the creation call identically. More importantly, the dead branch suggests a misunderstanding of the API contract.

Fix: Remove the `else if existing == nil` block entirely. If `getErr == nil`, `existing` is guaranteed non-nil and the spec already exists; no action needed.
title: Dead code: GetSpec nil-result branch in StoreDecomposeOutput is unreachable
[security] Slug length is unbounded — no maximum length check in validateSlug

description:
cmd_test.go tests cover cobra arg validation (ExactArgs) and flag registration lookups. These are shallow structural tests — they verify that flags exist but not that --json-file is actually read and passed to the RPC.

The util_test.go covers loadJSONFile in isolation (good). However, there are no tests that exercise the full runShape/runSpecify/runDecompose flow with a --json-file flag wired to a real proto JSON file, even in a fake-server scenario. If the json-file flag variable (shapeJSONFile etc.) were accidentally unlinked from loadJSONFile, the tests would still pass.

Additionally, the approve and supersede commands (approve.go, no corresponding supersede cmd) have no cmd-level tests at all — not even arg validation.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/cmd/specgraph/cmd_test.go
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/cmd/specgraph/approve.go (no test file for approve args)

Suggested fix: Add TestApproveCmd_RequiresSlug following the same pattern as the other commands. For json-file integration, consider a test that writes a valid proto JSON to a tmpfile and calls runShape (or shapeCmd.RunE) with a mock server using httptest.
title: cmd tests only verify flag registration and arg validation, not CLI output or JSON file loading

description:
The existing Spec message in proto/specgraph/v1/spec.proto uses a free-form string for stage:
  string stage = 4;  // spark | shape | specify | decompose | approved | in_progress | done

The new authoring.proto defines a proper AuthoringStage enum with values SPARK, SHAPE, SPECIFY, DECOMPOSE, APPROVED.

This creates a contract inconsistency:
1. SpecService.GetSpec and ListSpecs return Spec with stage as a string — consumers must know valid strings.
2. AuthoringService RPCs (Approve, Amend) return AuthoringStage enum — type-safe.
3. The Spec.stage string includes extra values ('in_progress', 'done') not present in AuthoringStage enum.
4. AmendResponse and ApproveResponse return AuthoringStage enum, but the underlying Spec node uses a string stage.

Clients using both SpecService and AuthoringService will need two different representations of the same concept. This will become a breaking change problem if Spec.stage is ever changed to AuthoringStage enum (it would be a field type change from string to enum, a wire-breaking change in proto3).

Suggested fix: Either (1) add a computed AuthoringStage field to GetSpec responses alongside the string field, clearly documenting that the string is legacy, or (2) plan a migration to use AuthoringStage enum in Spec and document that the string stage field is deprecated. At minimum, document in the proto comment that Spec.stage values are a superset of AuthoringStage enum values.
title: stage field in Spec proto uses free-form string but AuthoringStage enum exists — inconsistency creates contract ambiguity
[comments] AuthoringService RPCs in proto lack method-level doc comments

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go

All 8 integration tests open the store with the same 5-line block:

    boltURI, cleanup := setupMemgraph(t)
    defer cleanup()
    ctx := context.Background()
    store, err := memgraph.New(ctx, boltURI)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    defer store.Close(ctx)

This is repeated verbatim in every test function. tx_test.go has the same pattern for its 2 tests.

Suggested fix: Extract a newTestStore helper used in both files:

    func newTestStore(t *testing.T) *memgraph.Store {
        t.Helper()
        boltURI, cleanup := setupMemgraph(t)
        t.Cleanup(cleanup)
        ctx := context.Background()
        store, err := memgraph.New(ctx, boltURI)
        require.NoError(t, err)
        t.Cleanup(func() { store.Close(ctx) })
        return store
    }

Each test then becomes a single call. Note that t.Cleanup is preferred over defer in test helpers to ensure correct ordering.
title: [simplify] memgraph/authoring_test.go: 8 tests repeat identical 5-line store-setup block

description: The plan document (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, Task 1 proto schema) specifies 'message PromptTemplate { string stage = 1; string name = 2; string template = 3; }'. The implementation in proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto uses 'AuthoringStage stage = 1' (an enum) instead of 'string stage = 1'. This is a wire-breaking change from the plan — the protobuf field number is the same (1) but the type changes from string to enum, which is not binary-compatible. The upgrade is arguably better (type-safe), but it is an undocumented deviation. The internal prompts.go also uses stageToProtoEnum to convert domain strings to the enum for the AuthoringStage field. The plan should be updated or a note added explaining the intentional upgrade.
title: [spec] PromptTemplate.stage field type upgraded from string to AuthoringStage enum

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, lines 128-138.

The DEPENDS_ON MERGE query:
  MATCH (from:Spec {slug: $from_slug}), (to:Spec {slug: $to_slug})
  MERGE (from)-[:DEPENDS_ON]->(to)

will silently produce zero records (and no error) when the dependency spec (`to_slug`) does not yet exist. Child specs are created in slice order within the same loop, so if slice B declares DependsOn: ["slice-a"] and slice-a is processed before slice-b, the dependency node exists. However, if the slice ordering is reversed, or a dependency references a sibling not yet created in this batch, the edge is silently dropped.

The current code has no check after the executeQuery call to verify that the MERGE actually succeeded (i.e. that `len(records) > 0`).

Fix: After the MERGE executeQuery, check if records is empty and return an error indicating that the dependency spec does not exist, giving the caller a clear failure rather than silent data loss.
title: Silent edge-drop: DEPENDS_ON MERGE silently skips if dependency spec not yet created

description:
TestTransitionStage (authoring_test.go:15-41) covers:
1. Valid forward transitions (spark→shape, shape→specify)
2. One invalid transition (specify→approved, skipping decompose) — returns ErrInvalidStageTransition

It does NOT cover:
- Wrong from-stage (spec exists but is at a different stage than the 'from' argument) → should return ErrInvalidStageTransition (the stage mismatch path in authoring.go:62-73)
- Spec does not exist at all → ErrSpecNotFound

The 'check spec stage' secondary query in TransitionStage (authoring.go:62-73) is specifically designed to distinguish 'not found' from 'wrong stage', but the test that exercises the non-transacting path (specify→approved) happens to rely on stage validation before the query, not on this distinction logic.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go
Implementation: /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:61-74

Suggested fix: Add TestTransitionStage_SpecNotFound (call TransitionStage on a nonexistent slug, expect ErrSpecNotFound) and TestTransitionStage_WrongFromStage (create spec at spark, attempt shape→specify, expect ErrInvalidStageTransition).
title: TransitionStage integration test: spec at wrong stage returns ErrInvalidStageTransition but test only checks ErrSpecNotFound path
SupersedeSpec storage error wrapping loses ErrSpecNotFound identity for the superseded_by spec
[comments] TransitionStage godoc says ErrSpecAlreadyApproved when from is approved stage but doesn't document the approved-block behavior at storage layer
Missing self-supersede guard in Supersede RPC handler

description:
Six storage methods on memgraph.Store have no tests in the integration test file:
- StoreRedTeamFindings
- StorePeripheralVision
- StoreConsistencyIssues
- StoreSimplicityFindings
- StoreSafetyFlags
- StoreConstitutionViolations

All six are thin wrappers over storeJSONProperty, but that internal method includes an allowlist check and a character validation loop that is exercised by these wrappers only at runtime. The methods ARE wired into the fakeAuthoringBackend in the handler test, but only as no-op stubs that always return nil.

Files:
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:148-175 (implementations)
- /Volumes/Code/github.com/seanb4t/specgraph/internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go (missing tests)

Suggested fix: Add at minimum one integration test per method (e.g. TestStoreRedTeamFindings) that creates a spec, calls the method, and asserts no error. A single table-driven test covering all six would suffice.
title: Analytical pass store methods (StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, etc.) have zero integration test coverage

description: The plan architecture section states: 'Analytical passes (Red Team, Peripheral Vision, Consistency, Simplicity, Constitution Check) produce typed findings stored on or linked to the spec node.' The AuthoringBackend interface (internal/storage/authoring.go) includes PassWriter methods (StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, StoreSafetyFlags, StoreConstitutionViolations). However, the handler (internal/server/authoring_handler.go) never calls any of these PassWriter methods, and all response fields for analytical results are left empty. Specifically: SparkResponse.constitution_violations is always empty; ShapeResponse.peripheral_vision is always empty; SpecifyResponse.red_team and consistency_issues are always empty; DecomposeResponse.simplicity is always empty. The Decompose handler even has a TODO comment acknowledging this gap (line 2736-2738 of diff). This is a VIOLATION of the spec's core analytical pass requirement — the storage methods and domain types exist but are never invoked.
title: [spec] Analytical pass results not stored to or returned from handler (constitution_violations, red_team, peripheral_vision, consistency_issues, simplicity)

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, line 22.

The regex:
  ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$

requires at least two characters (a mandatory first char, zero-or-more middle chars, a mandatory last char). A single-character slug like 'a' or '1' will always fail validation with 'contains invalid characters' — even though single-character slugs are perfectly valid and short spec IDs are plausible.

This is likely unintentional; the intent appears to be 'must start and end with alphanumeric', not 'must be at least two chars'.

Fix: Use a pattern that supports length-1 slugs:
  ^[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9])?$

This allows single-char slugs while still enforcing alphanumeric start/end for multi-char slugs.
title: Single-character slugs rejected by validateSlug regex

description:
The AuthoringBackend interface in internal/storage/authoring.go includes a PassWriter sub-interface with 6 methods:
  StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, StoreSafetyFlags, StoreConstitutionViolations

All 6 methods are implemented in memgraph/authoring.go and all 6 are implemented in the test fake (fakeAuthoringBackend in authoring_handler_test.go).

However, none of these 6 methods are ever called from the authoring handler. The handler runs safety checks (RunSafetyNet) and returns results in the response, but does not persist them via the storage interface. The Decompose handler even has a TODO comment acknowledging that simplicity_check and constitution_check are not wired up.

This creates an API contract mismatch: the storage interface promises persistence of these findings, clients see the findings in the response, but nothing is stored. If a client later queries a spec expecting to retrieve historical findings, they will not be found.

This is RISKY, not BREAKING (the API is new), but it establishes a misleading contract from day one.

Suggested fix: Either (1) call the PassWriter methods from the handler after computing findings (StoreSafetyFlags, StoreRedTeamFindings, etc.), or (2) remove PassWriter from AuthoringBackend until it is ready to be implemented, to avoid shipping a dead interface. Option 2 is safer for the initial API contract.
title: AuthoringBackend PassWriter methods are stored in storage but never called from handler — dead API surface

description: The plan architecture section explicitly states: 'AuthoringService is a new ConnectRPC service with seven RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede).' The implementation adds an 8th RPC: GetPrompts. The proto file (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto lines 4886-4895 of diff) shows all 8 RPCs in the service definition. GetPrompts is described as serving 'Postures (Drive/Partner/Support) and prompt templates are server-side configuration served via a dedicated RPC' in the architecture section — so the intent was clearly there. The architecture description count of '7 RPCs' in the plan is simply inaccurate. No action needed beyond a minor plan correction.
title: [spec] Plan specifies 7 RPCs but 8 are implemented — GetPrompts undocumented in architecture description
RunSafetyNet returns non-deterministic flag ordering from map iteration

description: The plan's Project Structure section (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md) lists five CLI commands: spark.go, shape.go, specify.go, decompose.go, approve.go. While the plan only lists five, the AuthoringService exposes 8 RPCs including Amend and Supersede. The AuthoringHandler tests (internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go) test Amend and Supersede happy paths and error paths. However, there are no corresponding cmd/specgraph/amend.go or cmd/specgraph/supersede.go CLI command files in the diff. Users have no way to invoke Amend or Supersede from the CLI. The plan did not list these but the handler implements them — consistent with the plan, but creates a usability gap where these RPCs can only be called programmatically or via curl/grpc clients.
title: [spec] CLI missing amend and supersede commands despite being planned in project structure

description:
The DecompositionSlice message (proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto) has:
  string id = 1; // Stable identifier for this slice within the parent spec.

The Decompose handler in authoring_handler.go calls decomposeOutputToDomain which maps slice IDs directly. Then StoreDecomposeOutput in memgraph/authoring.go creates child slugs via:
  childSlug := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", slug, sl.ID)

There is no validation that:
1. sl.ID is non-empty (empty ID → child slug becomes 'parent-spec/')
2. sl.ID values are unique within the output (duplicate IDs → two slices create the same child slug)
3. sl.ID values are valid slug characters (the Decompose handler calls decomposeOutputToDomain before validateSlug for child IDs)

For duplicate IDs: the first call creates the child, the second finds it exists via GetSpec and reuses it — creating a partially wrong dependency graph. For empty IDs: 'parent-spec/' is a syntactically invalid slug that would pass validateSlugRe since it contains '/'.

Wait — actually '/' alone would not pass validSlugRe (^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_/-]*[a-z0-9]$) but 'parent-spec/' ends with '/' which fails the trailing [a-z0-9] requirement. However the slice ID itself is never validated with validateSlug.

Suggested fix: Add validation in the Decompose handler that each DecompositionSlice.id is non-empty, unique within the output, and matches valid slug character rules. Return CodeInvalidArgument with a descriptive message including the problematic slice ID.
title: DecompositionSlice.id field has no validation — empty or duplicate IDs create broken child spec slugs

description: The plan (docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-3-authoring-funnel-plan.md, Task 5) specifies SafetyFlagResult.Severity as 'specv1.FindingSeverity' (the proto enum type). The implementation (internal/authoring/safety.go) defines a separate authoring.FindingSeverity type as an ordered int (SeverityCritical=1, SeverityWarning=2, SeverityNote=3) to enable severity comparison logic (lower value = higher severity). This is an intentional and correct design improvement (the plan's version could not do severity deduplication). The change is a deviation from the plan's type specification. The plan should be updated to reflect this. Location: internal/authoring/safety.go lines 1179-1187 of diff.
title: [spec] SafetyFlagResult.Severity type changed from specv1.FindingSeverity to int-based FindingSeverity without plan update

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, GetPrompts handler (lines 295-308).

When the caller requests prompts for the `approved` stage, the handler looks up prompts via `authoring.PromptsToProto`, finds none (approved has no prompts by design), and returns `CodeInvalidArgument` with 'no prompts defined for stage approved'. 

The error code is semantically misleading. The request is valid — the stage is a known stage that is included in the AuthoringStage enum. The stage simply has no prompts because approval is a terminal state. `CodeNotFound` or an empty 200 response would be clearer.

The test `TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts_ApprovedStage` asserts this returns `CodeInvalidArgument`, so fixing it requires updating the test too.

Fix: Return `CodeNotFound` with a message like 'no prompts for terminal stage approved', or return an empty `GetPromptsResponse` with no error.
title: GetPrompts returns CodeInvalidArgument for approved stage which has no prompts by design

description:
RegisterAuthoringService in internal/server/authoring_handler.go accepts nil for both authoringStore and backend:

  func RegisterAuthoringService(mux *http.ServeMux, authoringStore storage.AuthoringBackend, backend storage.Backend) {
      handler := &AuthoringHandler{store: authoringStore, backend: backend}
      if txb, ok := backend.(storage.TransactionalBackend); ok {
          handler.txBackend = txb
      }
      ...
  }

The test file TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts calls server.RegisterAuthoringService(mux, nil, nil), which works for GetPrompts because that handler doesn't use store or backend.

However, for all other RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede), calling them with a nil store or nil backend will cause a nil pointer dereference panic:
  h.backend.CreateSpec(...)  // panic if backend is nil
  h.store.TransitionStage(...)  // panic if store is nil

The production registration in cmd/specgraph/serve.go passes real instances:
  server.RegisterAuthoringService(mux, store, store)

But the function signature advertises this as safe by accepting interface parameters with no nil guard.

The test TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts explicitly uses nil for both parameters and is in the test suite. This pattern will mislead future test authors into thinking nil is always acceptable.

Suggested fix: Add a nil check at the top of RegisterAuthoringService and panic with a clear message if either required parameter is nil. Alternatively, require non-nil parameters in the function contract documentation. Update the GetPrompts-only test to use fake backends instead of nil.
title: RegisterAuthoringService accepts nil for both parameters but GetPrompts handler panics at nil store dereference

description: FindingSeverity is defined twice with different representations: storage.FindingSeverity (string, internal/storage/authoring.go:98) and authoring.FindingSeverity (int, internal/authoring/safety.go:17). The two are bridged by a manual lookup map (severityToStorage) and conversion function (ToStorageSeverity) in safety.go. This is a DRY violation and a maintenance hazard: adding a new severity level requires updating both type definitions, three constant blocks (storage, authoring, proto), and the bridge map. The justification in the comment ('authoring uses ordered ints for comparison; storage uses strings for JSON') is valid but the same result can be achieved with a single type. Options: (a) consolidate onto the proto enum specv1.FindingSeverity throughout, using its numeric ordering for comparison and proto-json for serialization; (b) keep only one domain type and derive the storage string from it. File locations: internal/storage/authoring.go:97-105, internal/authoring/safety.go:17-24, internal/authoring/safety.go:185-216.
title: [types] Duplicate FindingSeverity type across authoring and storage packages

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, AmendSpec query (line 3942 of diff / line ~120 of file).

The Cypher query in `AmendSpec` writes `s.amend_reason = $reason` onto the spec node, but:
1. The `AmendResult` struct has no `Reason` field, so the value is never returned to the caller.
2. No existing query reads `amend_reason` back from the node.
3. The `AmendResponse` proto message has no `reason` field either.

This means amend reasons are written to the graph but are invisible to any downstream consumer. Either:
- Add `Reason` to `AmendResult`, the `AmendResponse` proto, and the RETURN clause of the query; or
- If amend reasons are intentionally write-only audit data, document this explicitly and verify that Memgraph retains the property across subsequent amendments (it currently gets overwritten, so only the most recent reason is preserved).
title: amend_reason written to spec node but never cleared or read back in any query
AmendRequest missing validation that reason field is non-empty — silent no-reason amendments allowed

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, SupersedeSpec (lines ~170-200 of authoring.go).

`SupersedeSpec` performs two separate `GetSpec` calls to validate both specs exist, then runs the mutating query. Between the existence checks and the mutation, either spec could be deleted by a concurrent operation, resulting in the combined MATCH query returning no records.

In that case the code returns `ErrSpecNotFound`, which is correct — but the preceding error message attributes the failure to the original slug rather than the actual missing one, making diagnosis harder.

More critically, without a transaction wrapping all three operations (two GET + one MUTATE), this is a TOCTOU race. The `RunInTransaction` capability exists on `Store` but is not used here.

Fix: Either wrap `SupersedeSpec` in a `RunInTransaction` call, or collapse the two existence checks into the mutating Cypher query using MATCH with explicit failure handling (e.g. using OPTIONAL MATCH + CASE to detect which spec is missing).
title: SupersedeSpec uses two-phase check-then-act with TOCTOU window between GetSpec calls and query

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark handler (lines 63, 78).

`CreateSpec` is called with `msg.Output.GetSeed()` as the `intent` argument. The `seed` field in `SparkOutput` is described as 'Raw idea or problem statement provided by the author.' — a first-pass free-form input. The `intent` field on `Spec` is the authoritative purpose statement for the spec.

Using seed directly as intent means:
1. If the user provides a well-formed intent via a different mechanism later, the original seed remains as intent.
2. An empty seed creates a spec with empty intent, which may break spec validation in downstream systems.

The `SparkRequest` has no dedicated `intent` field, so the seed-as-intent mapping appears intentional, but this should be explicitly documented. There is also no validation that the seed is non-empty (only `output != nil` is checked).

Fix: Either add an `intent` field to `SparkRequest` separate from the `SparkOutput.seed`, or add a validation that `msg.Output.Seed != ""` before calling CreateSpec.
title: Spark handler uses msg.Output.GetSeed() as spec intent, conflating authoring seed with spec intent

description:
In authoring_handler.go, the Decompose handler calls:
  NextPrompts: authoring.PromptsToProto(authoring.StageApproved),

But in authoring/prompts.go, the promptRegistry has no entry for 'approved':
  var promptRegistry = map[string][]Prompt{
      "spark": {...},
      "shape": {...},
      "specify": {...},
      "decompose": {...},
      // no "approved" entry
  }

PromptsToProto returns nil for missing stages. So DecomposeResponse.next_prompts is always nil/empty.

This is inconsistent with the behavior for Spark (returns shape prompts), Shape (returns specify prompts), and Specify (returns decompose prompts) — all of which return actual next-stage prompts. The Decompose response returning empty next_prompts breaks the established pattern that next_prompts guides the user to the next stage.

The proto comment on DecomposeResponse.next_prompts says 'Prompt templates suggested for the next authoring stage.' If the next stage (Approve) has no prompts, this field should not be included in the response or should be omitted, and the proto comment should document this.

Suggested fix: Either (1) add an approval-guidance prompt to the promptRegistry for the 'approved' stage (e.g., 'Submit for approval'), or (2) omit next_prompts from DecomposeResponse if it will always be empty and document this in the proto. The current state produces a misleading nil in a field that has been non-nil in all prior stages.
title: Decompose RPC returns NextPrompts for APPROVED stage but approved stage has no prompts — clients receive empty next_prompts

description: Add nil check for authoringStore and backend in RegisterAuthoringService. Panic with clear message if nil.
title: Fix(spgr-s34.69): Add nil guard to RegisterAuthoringService

description: Remove else-if existing==nil branch in StoreDecomposeOutput — GetSpec never returns (nil,nil)
title: Fix(spgr-s34.49): Remove dead GetSpec nil-result branch in StoreDecomposeOutput

description: Add method-level doc comments to AuthoringService RPCs, doc comments to AuthoringStage enum values, and validate DecompositionSlice.id field is non-empty
title: Fix(spgr-s34.52,s34.30,s34.66): Proto doc comments and slice ID validation

description:
Files: internal/scanner/walk.go, internal/scanner/tier1.go, internal/scanner/tier2.go

The ScanTier1 and ScanTier2 functions accept a root path that is used directly with filepath.WalkDir without any containment or sanitization:

    // walk.go line 2469
    err := filepath.WalkDir(root, func(path string, d fs.DirEntry, err error) error {

    // tier2.go line 2257
    absDir := filepath.Join(root, dir)
    skipped, err := walkGoFiles(absDir, false, fset, ...)

In ScanTier2, each element of the dirs slice is joined with root — if a caller passes a dirs entry like '../../../etc', the resulting absDir escapes the intended root. The test in edge_test.go covers symlinks and malformed files but does not test directory escape via the dirs parameter.

Current risk is limited because:
- ScanTier1/ScanTier2 are not directly exposed via RPC in this PR
- The scanner is called from CLI context where the user controls the path

However, if scanner functions are later called from an RPC or webhook handler (a reasonable Phase 2 extension), an unvalidated path becomes a server-side directory traversal vulnerability allowing an attacker to read any readable Go file on the server.

Recommended fix: Add a check that absDir in ScanTier2 resolves to a path under root after calling filepath.EvalSymlinks. This is defense-in-depth that prevents future callers from accidentally introducing traversal.
title: [security] Scanner walks arbitrary caller-supplied filesystem path with no containment
Decompose handler: missing test for DECOMPOSITION_STRATEGY_UNSPECIFIED rejection

description: Comprehensive review of PR #8: Slice 3 Authoring Funnel. 2294 additions across 30 files covering authoring proto, funnel stages, posture detection, prompt templates, safety net, analytical passes, scanner tiers, storage backend, ConnectRPC handler, CLI commands, and server wiring.
title: Review: PR #8 — feat: Slice 3 — Authoring Funnel

close_reason: Deferred: plan explicitly specifies protobuf types for AuthoringBackend. Tracked as spgr-q5h for future slice.
description:
CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'Storage interfaces use domain types, not protobuf types.' However, the new AuthoringBackend interface in internal/storage/authoring.go is full of protobuf types:

  StoreSparkOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.SparkOutput) error
  StoreShapeOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.ShapeOutput) error
  StoreSpecifyOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.SpecifyOutput) error
  StoreDecomposeOutput(ctx context.Context, slug string, output *specv1.DecomposeOutput) ([]*specv1.Spec, error)
  StoreRedTeamFindings(ctx context.Context, slug string, findings []*specv1.RedTeamFinding) error
  StorePeripheralVision(ctx context.Context, slug string, items []*specv1.PeripheralVisionItem) error
  StoreConsistencyIssues(ctx context.Context, slug string, issues []*specv1.ConsistencyIssue) error
  StoreSimplicityFindings(ctx context.Context, slug string, findings []*specv1.SimplicityFinding) error
  StoreSafetyFlags(ctx context.Context, slug string, flags []*specv1.SafetyFlag) error
  StoreConstitutionViolations(ctx context.Context, slug string, violations []*specv1.ConstitutionViolation) error
  AmendSpec(ctx context.Context, slug, reason, targetStage string) (*specv1.Spec, error)

File: internal/storage/authoring.go

Every existing storage interface (Backend, GraphBackend, ClaimBackend, ConstitutionBackend) uses domain types. The AuthoringBackend breaks this contract by having specv1 proto types directly in the interface signatures. Domain structs should be defined in the storage package or authoring package, with the proto<->domain conversion happening at the server handler layer (or a dedicated mapper).

Note: The existing Backend interface also returns *specv1.Spec, so there is already precedent for Spec being a 'domain' type that happens to be proto-generated. The real concern is stage-output types (SparkOutput, ShapeOutput, etc.) which are purely transport types and have no reason to bleed into the storage layer boundary.
notes: Plan explicitly specifies protobuf types in AuthoringBackend interface. This is a plan-vs-CLAUDE.md tension to track for future slices, not an implementation bug.
title: AuthoringBackend interface uses protobuf types, violating storage layer convention

description:
Files: internal/storage/authoring.go:20, internal/authoring/stages.go, internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go:47.

TransitionStage(ctx, slug, from, to string) uses raw strings for stage names. The proto already defines AuthoringStage as a proper enum (authoring.proto lines 10-16). The authoring package defines StageSpark/StageShape/etc. constants but these are bare strings, so any caller can pass a misspelled or unknown stage without a compile-time error.

The domain ValidateTransition function returns a runtime error for unknown stages, which is the only guard — but this can be bypassed by callers who do not check errors from TransitionStage, and it pushes a structural invariant to runtime.

Suggested fix:
- Change TransitionStage (and AmendSpec's targetStage argument) to accept specv1.AuthoringStage.
- Convert the domain stage constants to be specv1.AuthoringStage values.
- Remove the string-based stages slice in stages.go and replace indexOf with a simple arithmetic comparison on enum values (which are already ordered 1–5).
title: TransitionStage accepts arbitrary from/to strings instead of the typed AuthoringStage enum already defined in the proto

description: In internal/scanner/tier1.go (line ~1173) and internal/scanner/tier2.go (line ~1442), parser.ParseFile errors are suppressed with 'return nil //nolint:nilerr // skip unparseable files gracefully'. There is no logging, no counter, and no mechanism to surface which files were skipped. When ScanTier1 or ScanTier2 returns a Tier1Result/Tier2Result, the caller has no way to know whether the result is complete or partially empty due to parse failures. This creates a false-negative risk: a codebase with syntax errors (e.g., files requiring build tags, partial checkouts, or generated files mid-regeneration) will produce a silently incomplete scan result that looks successful. Fix: collect skipped files in the result struct (e.g., SkippedFiles []string with the reason), or at minimum log a warning at the point of skipping.
title: Scanner silently drops unparseable Go files with no observability
Duplicated safety-loop pattern in RunSafetyNet could use a shared helper

description:
internal/authoring/posture.go uses exclusive comparisons: avg < 20 → DRIVE, avg > 100 → SUPPORT, else PARTNER. This means avg==20 and avg==100 both resolve to PARTNER.

The tests in posture_test.go use messages clearly below/above/between thresholds but never hit the exact boundary values. A future refactor changing < to <= would silently shift behavior and the existing tests would not catch it.

Boundary cases to add:
- Single message of exactly 20 chars → PARTNER (not DRIVE)
- Single message of exactly 100 chars → PARTNER (not SUPPORT)
- Single message of 19 chars → DRIVE
- Single message of 101 chars → SUPPORT

Example addition:
{name: "avg==20 is PARTNER not DRIVE", messages: []string{strings.Repeat("x", 20)}, want: specv1.Posture_POSTURE_PARTNER},
{name: "avg==100 is PARTNER not SUPPORT", messages: []string{strings.Repeat("x", 100)}, want: specv1.Posture_POSTURE_PARTNER},
{name: "avg==19 is DRIVE", messages: []string{strings.Repeat("x", 19)}, want: specv1.Posture_POSTURE_DRIVE},
{name: "avg==101 is SUPPORT", messages: []string{strings.Repeat("x", 101)}, want: specv1.Posture_POSTURE_SUPPORT},
title: DetectPosture boundary values at thresholds 20 and 100 are untested

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark does two sequential writes without a transaction. CreateSpec can succeed while StoreSparkOutput fails, leaving an orphaned spec node in 'spark' stage with no output. A retry of Spark then fails because the slug already exists (Cypher CREATE is not idempotent). The same pattern recurs in Shape, Specify, and Decompose: TransitionStage succeeds, then Store*Output fails, leaving the spec at the new stage with no output. Suggested fix: wrap both writes in a Memgraph explicit transaction, or document the non-atomicity and provide idempotency semantics.
title: Spark handler non-atomic writes leave orphaned spec on StoreSparkOutput failure

description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 2512: GetPromptsRequest.stage (field 1) is a plain string. The handler at internal/server/authoring_handler.go:1768 passes it directly to authoring.PromptsToProto() which silently returns nil for unknown stage names. There is no validation, no error returned, and no documentation of valid values. Callers passing 'Spark' (capital S) or 'SPARK' get an empty prompts list with HTTP 200, which is indistinguishable from a stage that legitimately has no prompts. The AuthoringStage enum already exists in the same proto file and should be used here: GetPromptsRequest.stage should be AuthoringStage stage = 1. The handler would then validate AUTHORING_STAGE_UNSPECIFIED and return CodeInvalidArgument.
title: GetPromptsRequest.stage is an untyped string with no validation — invalid stages silently return empty

close_reason: Comment fix was included in spgr-uqx.4 fix
description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, storeJSONProperty (diff line ~2076).

The comment says: 'The property name must be in the allowedJSONProperties allowlist.'

This documents the precondition but not the reason, which is non-obvious: the property name is interpolated directly into the Cypher query string via fmt.Sprintf, making it a Cypher-injection vector if an arbitrary string were allowed. Future maintainers might loosen the allowlist check or wonder why it exists without this context.

Suggested addition: '// storeJSONProperty marshals data to JSON and stores it as a string property on the spec node. // The property name is interpolated directly into the Cypher query (fmt.Sprintf), so // only names in allowedJSONProperties are permitted to prevent Cypher injection.'
title: storeJSONProperty comment omits the SQL-injection-style risk and why the allowlist exists

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
File: internal/authoring/prompts.go, lines 10-13.

type Prompt struct {
    Name     string
    Template string
}

Both fields are plain strings with no constructor or validation. An empty Name silently produces a PromptTemplate message with name="" which is indistinguishable from an invalid entry. The promptRegistry entries are all non-empty today, but there is nothing stopping a future maintainer from adding an entry with an empty template.

The GetPrompts function returns the slice from the map directly, so callers also receive a nil slice for unknown stages — returning nil vs empty slice is an observable inconsistency (PromptsToProto handles len==0 but GetPrompts does not normalise).

Suggested fix:
- Add a constructor func NewPrompt(name, template string) (Prompt, error) that validates non-empty fields.
- Or at minimum add a Validate() method so the registry can be checked at init() time via a range loop.
- Normalise GetPrompts to always return a non-nil slice (or document the nil contract).
title: Prompt struct has no validation: empty Name or Template is silently accepted and returned via the API

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, the Shape, Specify, and Decompose handlers all call TransitionStage unconditionally before checking if msg.Output != nil. If msg.Output is nil, the stage is advanced in the database, no output is stored, and RunSafetyNet runs on empty/nil input producing zero safety flags. The spec silently advances through the funnel with no stage output recorded and no safety analysis. The same pattern applies to Spark: CreateSpec succeeds regardless of output, then StoreSparkOutput is skipped when output is nil, but safety net still runs on an empty string.

Fix options:
1. Require non-nil output for each stage transition (return CodeInvalidArgument if nil)
2. Or document explicitly that output is optional but ensure safety net always has meaningful input by guarding the RunSafetyNet call on non-nil/non-empty content.

Affected handlers: Spark (line ~1656), Shape (line ~1674), Specify (line ~1693), Decompose (line ~1715).
title: Stage advanced before output validation — nil output produces no safety analysis

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, TransitionStage validates the transition using authoring.ValidateTransition(from, to), where 'from' is the value supplied by the caller (hardcoded in each handler), not the value currently stored in Memgraph.

The Cypher query does check: WHERE s.stage = $from, so if the DB stage differs the query returns 0 records and ErrSpecNotFound is returned. However, ErrSpecNotFound is semantically wrong — the spec exists but is in the wrong stage. The handler's stageError maps ErrSpecNotFound to CodeNotFound, which misleads the caller into thinking the slug does not exist rather than that the stage precondition failed.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (TransitionStage function)

Suggested fix: When 0 records are returned from a stage-conditional query, distinguish between 'spec not found' and 'spec found but wrong stage' by issuing a separate GetSpec query. Return ErrInvalidStageTransition (CodeFailedPrecondition) when the spec exists in a different stage.
title: TransitionStage ignores actual current stage in DB, allowing out-of-order transitions

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
File: internal/storage/authoring.go, lines 1-34.

AuthoringBackend is a 13-method interface that conflates four orthogonal concerns:
1. Stage lifecycle (TransitionStage, SupersedeSpec, AmendSpec)
2. Stage output persistence (StoreSparkOutput, StoreShapeOutput, StoreSpecifyOutput, StoreDecomposeOutput)
3. Analytical-pass result persistence (StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, StoreSafetyFlags, StoreConstitutionViolations)

Interfaces this wide are hard to mock, hard to satisfy in tests (the handler test passes nil for both store and backend, relying on GetPrompts being stateless), and impossible to implement partially. A new storage backend must implement all 13 methods even if it only needs a subset.

Suggested fix: split into at minimum two interfaces:
  type StageBackend interface { TransitionStage; SupersedeSpec; AmendSpec }
  type PassResultBackend interface { StoreRedTeamFindings; StorePeripheralVision; StoreConsistencyIssues; StoreSimplicityFindings; StoreSafetyFlags; StoreConstitutionViolations }
  type StageOutputBackend interface { StoreSparkOutput; StoreShapeOutput; StoreSpecifyOutput; StoreDecomposeOutput }
Or compose them into AuthoringBackend using interface embedding so callers that need the full set can still use a single type.
title: AuthoringBackend mixes stage logic, output storage, pass-result storage, and lifecycle mutations in one interface

description:
File: internal/authoring/posture.go, DetectPosture (diff line ~517).

The doc comment embeds the literal threshold values: 'Short average (<20 chars) maps to DRIVE, long average (>100 chars) maps to SUPPORT'.

The thresholds are defined as named constants driveThreshold=20 and supportThreshold=100 directly above. If either constant is changed in the future the comment will silently become wrong — a classic comment-rot scenario. The comment should reference the constant names instead of duplicating their values, e.g.: 'Short average (below driveThreshold) maps to DRIVE, long average (above supportThreshold) maps to SUPPORT.' Alternatively, remove the threshold details from the comment and let the constants speak for themselves.
title: DetectPosture comment uses integer literals that will diverge from named constants

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description: internal/server/authoring_handler.go:1666 — the Spark handler returns connect.NewResponse with Output: msg.Output, which is the exact object the client just sent. The server does not enrich, validate, or add any server-side fields (no ID, no created_at, no version). Consumers cannot distinguish a successful create from a no-op echo. This is inconsistent with all other mutation RPCs in the project: CreateSpec (spec.proto) returns the full Spec with id, version, and timestamps; ClaimSpec (claim.proto) returns a Claim with claimed_at and lease_expires. SparkResponse should include at least the created Spec node (or its slug/id/version/created_at) so clients have server-authoritative confirmation. The same issue applies to ShapeResponse, SpecifyResponse, and DecomposeResponse, all of which echo the output back without any server-authoritative field.
title: Spark RPC echoes client-supplied output back verbatim — no server-authoritative response field
ValidateTransition checks forward map then recomputes indexes redundantly

description: In internal/server/authoring_handler.go GetPrompts (line ~1768), when an unknown stage is passed the handler returns an empty prompts list with a 200 OK. There is no CodeInvalidArgument error. Callers (including the CLI) receive a successful response with zero prompts and have no way to distinguish 'valid stage with no prompts' from 'typo in stage name'. Fix: validate that req.Msg.Stage is one of the known stages (spark, shape, specify, decompose, approved) and return connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument, ...) for any unknown value.
title: GetPrompts returns empty success for unknown stage instead of CodeInvalidArgument

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, SupersedeSpec uses a single Cypher query that MATCHes both the old and new spec by slug:

  MATCH (old:Spec {slug: $old_slug}), (new:Spec {slug: $new_slug})
  SET old.stage = 'superseded', ...
  CREATE (new)-[:SUPERSEDES {...}]->(old)
  RETURN old.slug

If new_slug does not exist in Memgraph, the MATCH returns 0 records and the function returns ErrSpecNotFound — but the error message says the old_slug was not found, which is misleading. Worse, there is no separate check to distinguish which slug was missing.

If old_slug does not exist, the same ambiguous error is returned.

Suggested fix: Pre-validate both slugs with GetSpec before the combined query, or inspect the Cypher result to determine which slug was absent and return a descriptive error.
title: SupersedeSpec silently succeeds when either slug does not exist

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
File: internal/authoring/passes.go, lines 21-26.

type passConfig struct {
    pass      string
    autoIn    []specv1.Posture
    offeredIn []specv1.Posture
}

The pass field is a free-form string. The passRegistry uses literals like "constitution_check", "peripheral_vision", "red_team", etc. with no governing constant set. A misspelled pass name in the registry (e.g. "consitution_check") returns silently from PassesForStage and the caller proceeds with the wrong pass list — there is no compile-time or startup-time guard.

The autoIn/offeredIn slices have a meaningful invariant: a posture should not appear in both slices for the same passConfig (otherwise a pass would be both auto-run and offered). This invariant is not expressed by the type or validated anywhere.

Suggested fix:
- Define a PassName type (type PassName string) and constants for each known pass, then change passConfig.pass to PassName.
- Add an init() validation loop over passRegistry that panics on unknown pass names or violated autoIn/offeredIn disjointness.
title: passConfig.pass is an untyped string with no closed set; misspelled pass names fail silently at runtime

description: Two gaps in internal/authoring/safety_test.go. First: SafetyResultsToProto is never tested — if the field mapping were wrong no test would catch it. Second: RunSafetyNet is only called with Intent in tests; the handler calls it with Scope (Shape) and Invariants (Specify) but no test verifies those fields are scanned. Add TestSafetyResultsToProto verifying Category/Severity/Description mapping, plus TestSafetyNet_FlagsFromScope and TestSafetyNet_FlagsFromInvariants.
title: SafetyResultsToProto untested; safety flags from Scope and Invariants fields not exercised

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, line 205.

storeJSONProperty constructs a Cypher query using fmt.Sprintf to interpolate the property name:

  query := fmt.Sprintf(`
      MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
      SET s.%s = $data, s.updated_at = $updated_at
      RETURN s.slug
  `, property)

The property value is validated against an allowlist (allowedJSONProperties) before this point, which prevents current exploitation. However, the pattern creates a structurally unsafe query construction that is one refactor away from a Cypher injection vulnerability — if the allowlist check is removed, moved, or bypassed, the fmt.Sprintf call becomes a direct injection vector.

Recommended fix: Use parameterized property names instead of string interpolation. Memgraph (like Neo4j 5+) does not support parameterized property keys in SET clauses via $params, so the safest alternative is a switch statement that maps each allowed property name to its literal Cypher statement:

  switch property {
  case "spark_output":
      query = `MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug}) SET s.spark_output = $data ...`
  // ...
  }

This makes the query construction safe by construction, not by upstream validation.
title: storeJSONProperty uses fmt.Sprintf to embed property name in Cypher query

description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 2389: ConsistencyIssue message has a field named 'type' (field 1). In Go, the generated getter becomes GetType() and the field is Type — which shadows the built-in 'type' keyword in some contexts and conflicts with common Go linting rules (revive var-naming). In protobuf itself, 'type' is not a reserved word, but it is a keyword in Go and many target languages. This creates friction for all consumers of the generated SDK. Rename to issue_type or kind for clarity and to match the project's patterns (ConstitutionViolation uses 'constraint' and 'violation' instead of 'type').
title: ConsistencyIssue.type field uses proto keyword 'type' — will shadow in generated code

description:
File: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto (entire new file, diff lines ~2276-2530).

None of the 20+ messages, 8 enums, or 8 service RPCs carry any doc comments. Proto files are the primary interface contract for this service — they are consumed by code generators, SDK authors, and other teams. Without field comments, the semantics of fields such as 'scope_sniff' (SparkOutput.4), 'kill_test' (SparkOutput.5), 'success_must/should/wont' (ShapeOutput.6-8), and 'touches' (SpecifyOutput.4, DecompositionSlice.4) are opaque to anyone not reading Go source.

At minimum the service RPC methods and each message type should have a one-line description. Fields with non-obvious semantics (kill_test, scope_sniff, peripheral_vision disposition values, touches) need inline comments.

The AuthoringStage enum is also entirely undocumented despite being the central state machine concept.
title: Proto file has no field-level documentation on any message — critical for a public API contract

description:
internal/server/authoring_handler_test.go contains only one test (TestAuthoringHandler_GetPrompts). The remaining seven RPCs — Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede — have no handler-level tests at all.

Critical gaps:
- Spark with empty slug returns CodeInvalidArgument; this path is never exercised.
- stageError() maps ErrInvalidStageTransition → CodeFailedPrecondition and ErrSpecNotFound → CodeNotFound; neither mapping is verified at the HTTP boundary.
- Supersede is the only RPC that checks errors.Is(err, storage.ErrSpecNotFound) inline (rather than via stageError); this distinct code path is untested.
- Shape/Specify/Decompose/Approve happy-path flows need at least one integration test each.

Suggested fix: Add table-driven handler tests using httptest.NewServer + the real memgraph testcontainer (or a fake AuthoringBackend). At minimum add:
  - TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_EmptySlug — expects CodeInvalidArgument
  - TestAuthoringHandler_Spark_HappyPath
  - TestAuthoringHandler_StageError_NotFound — exercises CodeNotFound mapping
  - TestAuthoringHandler_StageError_InvalidTransition — exercises CodeFailedPrecondition mapping
  - TestAuthoringHandler_Supersede_NotFound — verifies the inline ErrSpecNotFound check
title: AuthoringHandler: only GetPrompts is tested — Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede have no handler tests

description:
internal/scanner/tier1.go and internal/scanner/tier2.go share an essentially identical filepath.WalkDir skeleton:
  - skip unreadable entries with nil error return
  - call skipDir on directory names
  - filter to .go files
  - call parser.ParseFile with the same flags

The only divergence is what they extract from the AST.

A shared walkGoFiles(root string, fn func(path string, f *ast.File, fset *token.FileSet) error) helper in an internal scanner utility file would eliminate ~30 lines of duplication and centralise the nilerr suppression comments. Both ScanTier1 and ScanTier2 would then just supply the per-file callback.

This also makes it easier to add future tiers consistently without copying the boilerplate again.
title: tier1.go and tier2.go duplicate the same WalkDir skeleton with skipDir and parse boilerplate

description:
In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, StoreDecomposeOutput iterates over output.Slices and calls CreateSpec for each child:

  for _, sl := range output.Slices {
      child, err := s.CreateSpec(ctx, sl.Id, sl.Intent, ...)
      ...
      CREATE (child)-[:COMPOSES]->(parent)
  }

CreateSpec uses a Cypher CREATE (not MERGE), so re-running StoreDecomposeOutput with the same slices will create duplicate spec nodes and duplicate COMPOSES edges. There is no idempotency guard.

Also, if creating a COMPOSES edge fails partway through the slice loop, the loop returns an error but previously created child specs and edges are not cleaned up, leaving partial graph state.

File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go (StoreDecomposeOutput function)

Suggested fix: Use MERGE instead of CREATE for child spec creation and COMPOSES edge creation, or check for pre-existing children before creating them. Also consider wrapping the entire loop in a transaction.
title: StoreDecomposeOutput creates child specs without idempotency, duplicates on retry

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
Files: internal/scanner/tier1.go lines 33-39, internal/scanner/tier2.go lines 29-34.

type Tier1Result struct {
    Packages   []PackageInfo
    Interfaces []InterfaceInfo
    Structs    []StructInfo
}
type Tier2Result struct {
    Functions []FunctionInfo
    Imports   []ImportInfo
    TestFiles []string
}

The result structs are initialised with zero-value slices (result := &Tier1Result{}) and populated via append in a WalkDir loop. For large codebases this causes O(log n) re-allocations. More importantly, all slice fields are exported and directly mutable — callers can append to or replace them after construction, violating the intent that ScanTier1/ScanTier2 are the sole producers.

A secondary invariant not expressed by the type: FunctionInfo.IsExported is supposed to be consistent with whether FunctionInfo.Name is exported (starts with uppercase). The type allows these to disagree.

Suggested fix:
- Keep the structs as simple value types (appropriate for data-transfer objects), but document mutability expectations and consider making fields read-only by returning from accessor methods if the structs grow behaviour.
- For the IsExported redundancy: either remove the field (callers can derive it from Name) or validate consistency in ScanTier2 before appending.
title: Tier1Result/Tier2Result are plain structs with no capacity hints; repeated append causes avoidable allocations in large scans
Missing slug validation in Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede handlers

description:
File: internal/authoring/passes.go and internal/authoring/safety.go.

The package-level doc comment ('Package authoring implements the SpecGraph authoring funnel...') lives only in posture.go. In Go, the package doc appears on the first file (alphabetically) that carries it; passes.go and safety.go have no package comment at all. While this is not a compilation error, go doc and pkg.go.dev pick up only the first package-level comment encountered; having it in posture.go rather than a dedicated doc.go means the placement is fragile — if posture.go is ever renamed or removed, the package loses its documentation.

The conventional fix is to move the package doc to a doc.go file in the package, which serves as the stable single source of truth. This also prevents confusion for contributors who open passes.go or safety.go first and see no orientation text.
title: Package authoring has no doc comment in passes.go or safety.go — only posture.go carries the package doc

description: internal/storage/authoring.go line 1863: AuthoringBackend.TransitionStage(ctx, slug, from, to string) requires callers to pass both the current stage (from) and the target stage (to). Each handler in authoring_handler.go hard-codes the expected from value (e.g., Shape hard-codes StageSpark as from, Specify hard-codes StageShape). If a spec is in an unexpected stage the DB query returns 0 rows and the error is ErrSpecNotFound, not ErrInvalidStageTransition, making the error misleading (the spec exists, the stage is just wrong). A storage interface that accepts only (ctx, slug, to) and reads the current stage from the DB would eliminate this caller-side duplication and produce the correct error. Alternatively, the storage layer should return a distinct error (e.g., ErrStageMismatch) when the MATCH finds the node but not with stage=.
title: TransitionStage interface takes from/to as caller-supplied strings — wrong-stage bugs not prevented at storage boundary

description:
cmd/specgraph/spark.go lines 209-211 defines authoringClient(), a helper used by all five authoring CLI commands (spark, shape, specify, decompose, approve). It sits in spark.go purely by accident of being the first file added.

Compare with the project's other client helpers — if any exist in a dedicated client.go or similar, authoringClient should live there too. At minimum it should move to a file whose name reflects its purpose (e.g., client.go or authoring_client.go) rather than being buried in the spark command file, which is the last place a reader would look when wiring up a new authoring command.

File: cmd/specgraph/spark.go line 209
title: authoringClient helper placed in spark.go rather than a shared client file

description:
Three gaps in internal/storage/memgraph/authoring_test.go:

1. TestSupersedeSpec only verifies the happy path. It does not test SupersedeSpec with a non-existent old spec or new spec, which should return ErrSpecNotFound (verified via require.ErrorIs). The server handler relies on this error to return CodeNotFound.

2. TestAmendSpec does not verify spec.Version was incremented. The query increments s.version = s.version + 1 but no assertion checks it — a future regression removing that increment would go undetected.

3. TestStoreSparkOutput does not read back the stored value. The test confirms no error but not that the data was persisted correctly.

Suggested additions:
- TestSupersedeSpec_NotFound: call SupersedeSpec with slug that does not exist, require.ErrorIs(t, err, storage.ErrSpecNotFound)
- In TestAmendSpec: assert spec.Version == 2 after amendment (initial version is 1)
- TestStoreSparkOutput could optionally use GetSpec + JSON field inspection, though that may require a separate query
title: memgraph/authoring_test: SupersedeSpec and AmendSpec missing error path and state verification

description:
In internal/authoring/posture.go, DetectPosture computes an average using integer division:

  avg := total / len(messages)

Integer truncation means a message of 19 characters (which should be below the 20-char DRIVE threshold) rounds down from any fractional average. More concretely: two messages of lengths 19 and 21 yield total=40, avg=20, which is not < driveThreshold (20), so the result is PARTNER rather than DRIVE, even though the intent is likely for the boundary to map to DRIVE.

The docstring says 'Short average (<20 chars) maps to DRIVE' but the code uses strict less-than with integer truncation, so exactly 20 rounds to PARTNER. This may be intentional, but it is inconsistent with the docstring which says '<20'.

File: internal/authoring/posture.go (DetectPosture)

Suggested fix: Clarify whether the threshold is inclusive or exclusive and ensure the docstring and code agree. If fractional message lengths matter, consider using float division. At minimum, add a test case at exactly the boundary value (20 chars average) to make the behavior explicit.
title: DetectPosture uses integer division truncation, boundary messages produce wrong posture
AmendRequest.target_stage is a string in the proto while AuthoringStage enum is defined in the same file

description:
internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 2087 (in the diff) uses fmt.Sprintf to build a Cypher query with the property name interpolated directly into the query string:

  query := fmt.Sprintf(`
      MATCH (s:Spec {slug: $slug})
      SET s.%s = $data, s.updated_at = $updated_at
      RETURN s.slug
  `, property)

While an allowlist check (slices.Contains(allowedJSONProperties, property)) guards this, Cypher property names cannot be parameterized in Neo4j/Memgraph, so the only option is interpolation. However, the allowlist comparison uses string equality against a fixed slice, which is correct.

The real risk is if the allowlist validation were ever bypassed or the function is refactored and the allowlist removed. Additionally, if Memgraph property names ever allowed characters with query injection potential (e.g., backticks), the current allowlist entries are safe but the pattern is fragile.

Severity assessment: The allowlist mitigates actual injection today, but this pattern is explicitly a security-sensitive construction that deserves a code comment explaining why fmt.Sprintf is acceptable here and what invariants must hold. Without that comment, a future developer removing the allowlist would open a Cypher injection vector.

Suggested fix: Add a comment directly above the fmt.Sprintf call documenting that property is always validated against allowedJSONProperties before this point, and that the allowlist must be maintained. Consider also using a switch statement instead of fmt.Sprintf to make the property-to-query mapping explicit and eliminate the string interpolation entirely.
title: storeJSONProperty uses fmt.Sprintf to interpolate property name into Cypher query

description:
File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go, Spark method (diff line ~1655).

The inline comment reads: '// CreateSpec already sets stage to "spark", so no TransitionStage call needed.'

This comment encodes a behavioral assumption about CreateSpec that lives in a different package (memgraph/memgraph.go). If CreateSpec's default stage is ever changed (e.g., to empty string or a different initial state), the Spark handler will silently misbehave because it skips TransitionStage. The comment correctly explains the current logic, but it creates implicit coupling that is easy to miss during a refactor.

A more durable approach would be an assertion or explicit check, or the comment should cross-reference the relevant constant: 'CreateSpec sets stage to memgraph.defaultInitialStage ("spark") — see memgraph/memgraph.go.' This at least points a future reader to the right place to verify the assumption still holds.
title: Spark handler comment says 'no TransitionStage call needed' but this assertion is fragile

description:
internal/authoring/stages_test.go tests valid and common-invalid transitions but misses two distinct error branches in ValidateTransition (stages.go:958-983):

1. Unknown from-stage (non-empty, not in stages list): e.g. ValidateTransition("bogus", "spark") should return 'unknown stage "bogus"'. The current test only covers same-to-same and skip-forward; it never reaches the fromIdx < 0 branch.

2. Unknown to-stage: e.g. ValidateTransition("spark", "bogus") should return 'unknown stage "bogus"'. The toIdx < 0 branch is similarly untested.

These branches exist as explicit error returns but have no test coverage, meaning a future refactor could silently drop them.

Suggested additions to TestValidTransitions table:
  {"bogus", "spark", false},  // unknown from
  {"spark", "bogus", false},  // unknown to
  {"", "bogus", false},       // empty from, unknown to
title: ValidateTransition missing test for unknown from-stage and unknown to-stage error paths

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
File: internal/scanner/scanner.go, line 26; internal/scanner/tier1.go line 45; internal/scanner/tier2.go line 40.

The Scan, ScanTier1, and ScanTier2 functions accept a caller-supplied directory path and walk it with filepath.WalkDir. The skipDir function skips hidden directories and vendor/node_modules, but:

1. Symlinks are not checked — a symlink within a scanned tree can escape the root directory boundary. filepath.WalkDir does not follow symlinks for directories by default, but it does expose symlinked files.
2. The path is not canonicalized (no filepath.EvalSymlinks or filepath.Clean before the walk). A caller supplying a path like /app/../etc could scan outside the intended root.
3. maxFileSize (1 MiB) limits file read size, which is good, but applies only to readFile — the WalkDir in detectInfrastructure calls readFile but the tier1/tier2 walkers use parser.ParseFile directly with no size check before parsing.

Recommended fix:
- Canonicalize the root path with filepath.EvalSymlinks before use.
- In tier1/tier2 walkers, check file size via os.Stat before calling parser.ParseFile to avoid parsing very large or adversarial files.
- Consider bounding the total number of files walked to prevent DoS on deeply nested trees.
title: Scanner accepts arbitrary directory path without symlink or path traversal protection

description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto line 2368: DecompositionSlice.id (field 1) is used directly as the child spec slug in StoreDecomposeOutput (internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go line 1965: s.CreateSpec(ctx, sl.Id, ...)). If a client passes a duplicate slug (either a pre-existing spec slug or a repeated slice ID), CreateSpec will either fail or silently overwrite the existing node, depending on the memgraph MERGE/CREATE semantics. The memgraph CreateSpec implementation (memgraph.go) uses CREATE not MERGE, so it will succeed but produce duplicate nodes with different generated IDs sharing the same slug.
