The field should be renamed to hint_slug or removed entirely, with the server generating the slug from the parent slug and slice index to guarantee uniqueness and namespace safety.
title: DecompositionSlice.id field is caller-supplied slug with no uniqueness guarantee — child spec creation may silently collide

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, the Spark handler calls msg.Output.GetSeed() before checking if msg.Output is nil:

  _, err := h.backend.CreateSpec(ctx, msg.Slug, msg.Output.GetSeed(), defaultSpecPriority, defaultSpecComplexity)

GetSeed() is a proto-generated method that is nil-safe (returns zero value when receiver is nil), so this will not panic. However, RunSafetyNet is also called on the same line pattern:

  safetyFlags := authoring.RunSafetyNet(&authoring.SafetyInput{Intent: msg.Output.GetSeed()})

This is also nil-safe for the same reason. There is no actual panic risk here.

However, the nil check that does appear later (if msg.Output != nil) creates an inconsistency: CreateSpec always runs with GetSeed() (possibly empty string), but StoreSparkOutput only runs when msg.Output != nil. This means a request with a nil Output will create a spec with an empty seed but store no SparkOutput — the spec enters the funnel with no output record. This is a logic gap even if not a panic.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (Spark handler, lines ~1656-1669)

Suggested fix: Either (a) require Output to be non-nil and return InvalidArgument if absent, or (b) consistently use GetSeed() everywhere and always call StoreSparkOutput (with an empty or default output) to maintain a complete output record.
title: Spark handler accesses msg.Output fields before nil check

description:
File: internal/scanner/tier2.go, ScanTier2 function (diff line ~1406).

The doc comment reads: 'ScanTier2 walks the specified directories under root, parses functions and imports, and discovers test files.'

This is accurate but potentially confusing alongside ScanTier1, which explicitly *skips* test files. A reader comparing the two functions would benefit from explicit language that highlights the different handling: Tier1 skips test files entirely, while Tier2 collects them separately into Tier2Result.TestFiles without parsing them for functions/imports. 

Suggested wording: 'ScanTier2 walks the specified directories under root, parses functions and imports from non-test .go files, and separately collects test file paths into Tier2Result.TestFiles (without parsing them). Unlike ScanTier1, test files are discovered rather than skipped.'
title: ScanTier2 doc comment omits that test files are collected rather than skipped

close_reason: Out of scope for Slice 3 — matches plan design or is future work
description:
File: internal/authoring/safety.go, lines 32-86.

The safety net scans spec intent, scope, and invariants using simple case-insensitive exact substring matching:

  if strings.Contains(combined, p) { ... }

Patterns like 'hardcoded secret', 'disable auth', 'exec(' are easily bypassed by inserting spaces, using synonyms, or splitting words across fields. For example, 'hard coded secret' would not match 'hardcoded secret'.

This is a defense-in-depth control (not a security boundary), but its limited effectiveness should be documented so maintainers do not rely on it as a meaningful security gate.

Recommended improvements:
1. Add a docstring explicitly stating the safety net is advisory-only and not a hard security control.
2. Consider regex-based matching to catch common variations (e.g., 'hard.?coded.?secret').
3. Expand patterns to include common bypass forms or synonyms (e.g., 'no auth', 'unauthenticated', 'delete *').

This does not warrant blocking the PR, but the current comment 'RunSafetyNet scans the input for known dangerous patterns' overstates the robustness of the check.
title: Safety net pattern matching is bypassable via trivial obfuscation
ValidateTransition empty-string 'from' edge case returns wrong error for unknown non-empty from

description:
In internal/server/authoring_handler.go, the Shape handler builds its SafetyInput with only scope_in:

  safetyFlags := authoring.RunSafetyNet(&authoring.SafetyInput{Scope: msg.Output.GetScopeIn()})

ShapeOutput also has scope_out, risks, success_must, success_should, success_wont, and chosen_approach fields. The safety net patterns (e.g., 'rm -rf', 'drop all', 'no encryption') could match content in scope_out or risks just as easily as scope_in.

Compare: the Specify handler includes both InterfaceContract and Invariants in its SafetyInput, which is more thorough.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go (Shape handler, around line 1684)

Suggested fix: Include scope_out, risks, and other relevant ShapeOutput fields in the SafetyInput to ensure comprehensive safety scanning at the shape stage.
title: Shape handler runs safety net only on scope_in, ignoring scope_out and risks

description:
The Spark handler validates slug:
    if msg.Slug == "" { return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInvalidArgument, ...) }

But Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, and Supersede do not. An empty slug will propagate to storage and produce a confusing ErrSpecNotFound error (CodeNotFound) instead of a clear CodeInvalidArgument error.

File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go — all handlers except Spark and GetPrompts.

This is consistent with the existing pattern in the codebase: spec_handler.go and decision_handler.go both validate slug before calling storage.

Suggested fix: Add slug non-empty validation to all state-mutating handlers, matching the pattern used in Spark and spec_handler.go.
title: Shape, Specify, Decompose, Approve, Amend, Supersede handlers do not validate slug is non-empty

description:
Files: internal/authoring/safety.go (lines 69-73, 77-80) and proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto (lines 127-131).

The domain type SafetyFlagResult.Category is typed as string, and the proto SafetyFlag.category field is also string. The package defines SafetyCategorySecurity and SafetyCategoryDataLoss constants (lines 70-73) but nothing enforces their use.

Any caller can pass an arbitrary string category. The test at safety_test.go:881 hard-codes the string "security" rather than the constant, showing how easily the invariant is violated.

The proto enum FindingSeverity is properly typed; category deserves the same treatment.

Suggested fix:
- Add a SafetyCategory proto enum (SAFETY_CATEGORY_SECURITY, SAFETY_CATEGORY_DATA_LOSS) so both the proto wire type and the domain type are closed sets.
- Remove the string constants in favour of the enum.
- Update SafetyFlagResult.Category to use the enum type.
title: SafetyFlag.category is an untyped string instead of the SafetyCategoryXxx constants defined in the same package

description: Add InvalidArgument check for nil msg.Output before accessing GetSeed() in Spark handler. File: internal/server/authoring_handler.go
title: Fix(spgr-uqx.44): Require non-nil Output in Spark handler

description: In internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go TransitionStage (line ~1920), ValidateTransition is called with the caller-supplied 'from' string, not the actual stage stored in the database. If a client passes from="spark" but the spec is actually at stage "shape", the Cypher WHERE clause (s.stage = $from) finds 0 records and returns ErrSpecNotFound. The stageError helper then maps ErrSpecNotFound to connect.CodeNotFound, making a wrong-stage transition look like 'spec not found' to callers. The fix is to either: (a) fetch the spec's current stage first and validate against it, then pass the real current stage to the Cypher query; or (b) return ErrInvalidStageTransition with a message indicating the actual vs expected stage when records == 0 and the slug exists.
title: TransitionStage wrong-stage maps to ErrSpecNotFound instead of ErrInvalidStageTransition

description: proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto defines AuthoringStage enum (AUTHORING_STAGE_SPARK etc.) but it is never used in any request or response message. All stage-carrying fields instead use raw string (ApproveResponse.stage field 2, AmendRequest.target_stage field 3, AmendResponse.stage field 2, SupersedeResponse returns no stage). The existing Spec message in spec.proto also uses string for stage (field 4), creating a dual-representation problem. This is a design-time inconsistency that will harden into a permanent wart: consumers must know to compare against string literals rather than enum values for the AuthoringService outputs. Either remove the enum and document stage strings as string constants, or use AuthoringStage in all stage-carrying fields. Given the enum already exists, the right fix is: AmendRequest.target_stage should be AuthoringStage, AmendResponse.stage should be AuthoringStage, ApproveResponse.stage should be AuthoringStage.
title: AuthoringStage enum defined but unused in all 8 RPCs — stage fields are untyped strings

description:
File: internal/storage/memgraph/authoring.go, line 2 of the function (diff line ~1919).

The doc comment reads: 'TransitionStage advances or validates a spec's stage transition.'

The word 'validates' is misleading. The function does validate (via authoring.ValidateTransition), but it also *executes* the transition unconditionally once validation passes — it does not offer a validate-only path. A caller reading 'advances or validates' could reasonably infer this is a conditional that either moves forward or just checks, when in reality it always attempts the write. A clearer comment would be: 'TransitionStage validates and executes a stage transition, updating the spec node in Memgraph. Returns ErrInvalidStageTransition if the transition is not allowed, or ErrSpecNotFound if no spec matches the slug and current stage.'
title: TransitionStage comment describes wrong behavior (advances or validates vs. actual logic)
